topics.title: Review of Semiconductor Physics - Equilibrium and Steady State Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of Semiconductor Physics - Equilibrium and Steady State Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Review of Semiconductor Physics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Equilibrium" vs. "Steady State"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium** is the condition when **no external forces** (no applied electric field, no light, no temperature gradient) act on the semiconductor. The system is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with its surroundings. Net current = 0, net recombination = 0 (every thermally generated carrier eventually recombines, but on average the system is time-invariant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady State** is the condition when **external forces are applied but their effects have stabilized** in time. The carrier concentrations at any point do **not change with time** ($\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = 0$, $\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = 0$), but **net currents and net recombination DO exist**. Steady state is *non-equilibrium* but *time-invariant*.

### 1.2 Formal KTU 2024 Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Thermal Equilibrium** | The state in which no external energy (electric field, magnetic field, optical excitation) is applied; the Fermi level $E_F$ is **constant throughout** the crystal. |
| **Steady State (DC)** | The state in which a constant external excitation is applied and the semiconductor response is independent of time at every point in space. |
| **Quasi-Fermi Level** | An artificial level $F_n$ (for electrons) and $F_p$ (for holes) that re-introduces the Fermi-Dirac statistics when the system is out of equilibrium but remains in steady state. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowded Hallway" Model

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a long, straight hallway representing a semiconductor crystal:
>
> - **Equilibrium** = Empty hallway. People (electrons) drift around randomly, but there is no net flow in any direction. The crowd density is uniform.
> - **Steady State** = A door opens at one end (voltage applied). People now move steadily toward the door, but the *rate* of people entering any point equals the rate leaving that point. The density at each point is *constant in time*, even though people keep flowing.

### 1.4 Physical Constants Used Throughout

- **Boltzmann Constant** $k = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ **eV/K** (or $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**)
- **Elementary Charge** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
- **Thermal Voltage** at 300 K: $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx 0.02585$ **V** (≈ **25.85 mV**)
- **Intrinsic Carrier Concentration** of Si at 300 K: $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ **cm⁻³**
- **Permittivity of free space** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ **F/cm**
- **Relative permittivity of Si** $\varepsilon_r = 11.8$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Band Structure Recap

A semiconductor has:
- **Valence Band (VB)** — fully occupied at 0 K by bound electrons.
- **Conduction Band (CB)** — empty at 0 K, available for free electrons.
- **Forbidden Energy Gap** $E_g$ — no allowed states.
  - Si: $E_g = 1.12$ **eV**
  - Ge: $E_g = 0.67$ **eV**
  - GaAs: $E_g = 1.42$ **eV**

At $T > 0$ K, thermal energy excites electrons from VB to CB, creating **electron-hole pairs (EHPs)**.

## 2.2 Fermi–Dirac Distribution (Thermal Equilibrium)

The probability that an available state at energy $E$ is occupied by an electron at thermal equilibrium:

$$
f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(E - E_F)/kT}}
$$

- $E_F$ = **Fermi level** (energy at which occupancy is exactly 1/2).
- Hole probability: $1 - f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(E_F - E)/kT}}$

## 2.3 Carrier Concentrations at Equilibrium

For a **non-degenerate semiconductor** ($E_c - E_F \gg kT$ and $E_F - E_v \gg kT$):

$$
n_0 = N_c \, e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}
$$

$$
p_0 = N_v \, e^{-(E_F - E_v)/kT}
$$

Where:
- $N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ — **Effective Density of States in CB**
- $N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_p^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ — **Effective Density of States in VB**
- $m_n^*, m_p^*$ = **effective masses** of electrons and holes

## 2.4 The Law of Mass Action

Multiplying $n_0$ and $p_0$:

$$
n_0 \, p_0 = N_c N_v \, e^{-(E_c - E_v)/kT} = N_c N_v \, e^{-E_g/kT} = n_i^2
$$

$$
\boxed{n_0 \, p_0 = n_i^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **valid in equilibrium only**. Under non-equilibrium (e.g., light), the product $np$ may exceed $n_i^2$.

## 2.5 Intrinsic Carrier Concentration

$$
n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \, e^{-E_g/2kT}
$$

For Si at 300 K, $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ **cm⁻³**.

## 2.6 Fermi Level in Intrinsic and Extrinsic Semiconductors

- **Intrinsic**: $E_i = \frac{E_c + E_v}{2} + \frac{3}{4}kT \ln\left(\frac{m_p^*}{m_n^*}\right)$ ≈ mid-gap
- **n-type** (donor doping $N_d$):
$$
E_F = E_c - kT \ln\left(\frac{N_c}{N_d}\right)
$$
- **p-type** (acceptor doping $N_a$):
$$
E_F = E_v + kT \ln\left(\frac{N_v}{N_a}\right)
$$

## 2.7 The Continuity Equation (Steady State Foundation)

The **continuity equation** describes conservation of carriers in space and time.

**For electrons:**
$$
\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{q}\nabla \cdot \vec{J_n} + G_n - R_n
$$

**For holes:**
$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{q}\nabla \cdot \vec{J_p} + G_p - R_p
$$

Where:
- $G_n, G_p$ = **generation rates** (cm⁻³ s⁻¹)
- $R_n, R_p$ = **recombination rates** (cm⁻³ s⁻¹)
- $\vec{J_n}, \vec{J_p}$ = **current densities**

## 2.8 Steady State Condition (DC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady State Definition**: $\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = 0$ and $\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = 0$ at every point in the semiconductor, but net recombination and current can still exist.

The continuity equation **reduces** to:

$$
0 = \frac{1}{q}\nabla \cdot \vec{J_n} + G_n - R_n
$$

$$
0 = -\frac{1}{q}\nabla \cdot \vec{J_p} + G_p - R_p
$$

## 2.9 Minority Carrier Recombination and Lifetime

For **low-level injection**, recombination is linear in excess carriers:

$$
R - G_0 = \frac{\Delta n}{\tau_n} = \frac{n - n_0}{\tau_n}
$$

Where:
- $\tau_n$ = **electron minority carrier lifetime** (seconds)
- $G_0$ = **thermal generation rate** at equilibrium
- $\Delta n = n - n_0$ = **excess electron concentration**

## 2.10 Quasi-Fermi Levels in Steady State

In steady state, the Fermi–Dirac statistics are modified:

$$
n = n_i \, e^{(F_n - E_i)/kT}
$$

$$
p = n_i \, e^{(E_i - F_p)/kT}
$$

$$
np = n_i^2 \, e^{(F_n - F_p)/kT}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **splitting** $F_n - F_p$ measures the deviation from equilibrium. In equilibrium, $F_n = F_p = E_F$.

## 2.11 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 12 equations for KTU ESE.** They are guaranteed appearances in Part A or Part B.

| # | Formula | Description / Condition |
|---|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | $f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(E-E_F)/kT}}$ | Fermi-Dirac distribution |
| 2 | $n_0 = N_c e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}$ | Electron concentration (eq.) |
| 3 | $p_0 = N_v e^{-(E_F - E_v)/kT}$ | Hole concentration (eq.) |
| 4 | $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$ | Law of mass action (eq. only) |
| 5 | $n_i^2 = N_c N_v e^{-E_g/kT}$ | Intrinsic carrier conc. |
| 6 | $E_F = E_c - kT\ln(N_c/N_d)$ | Fermi level (n-type) |
| 7 | $E_F = E_v + kT\ln(N_v/N_a)$ | Fermi level (p-type) |
| 8 | $\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{q}\nabla \cdot \vec{J_n} + G - R$ | Continuity (electrons) |
| 9 | $\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{q}\nabla \cdot \vec{J_p} + G - R$ | Continuity (holes) |
| 10 | $R - G_0 = \frac{\Delta n}{\tau}$ | Linear recombination (LLI) |
| 11 | $n = n_i e^{(F_n - E_i)/kT}$ | Quasi-Fermi for electrons |
| 12 | $p = n_i e^{(E_i - F_p)/kT}$ | Quasi-Fermi for holes |

## 2.12 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The concepts derived here form the **foundation for every active device** — the diode, BJT, MOSFET, solar cell, photodetector, and LED. Understanding equilibrium vs. steady state lets engineers predict **off-state leakage** (equilibrium property) and **forward current flow** (steady state property) in these devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equilibrium Electron Concentration $n_0$

**Starting Point**: The number of electrons per unit volume in the conduction band is the **integral** of (available states) × (probability of occupation) over the entire CB:

$$
n_0 = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g_c(E) \, f(E) \, dE
$$

Where $g_c(E)$ is the density of states near the bottom of the CB:

$$
g_c(E) = \frac{4\pi (2m_n^*)^{3/2}}{h^3} \sqrt{E - E_c}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitution**: We define the substitution $u = (E - E_c)/kT$, so $dE = kT \, du$, and $E - E_F = (E - E_c) + (E_c - E_F) = kT u + (E_c - E_F)$.

$$
n_0 = \frac{4\pi (2m_n^* kT)^{3/2}}{h^3} \int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{u} \, e^{-u} \, e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT} \, du
$$

**Step 2 — Factor out the exponential** (valid in non-degenerate case, $E_c - E_F \gg kT$):

$$
n_0 = \frac{4\pi (2m_n^* kT)^{3/2}}{h^3} \, e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT} \int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{u} \, e^{-u} \, du
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Gamma function**:
$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/2} e^{-u} \, du = \Gamma(3/2) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify**:

$$
n_0 = \frac{4\pi (2m_n^* kT)^{3/2}}{h^3} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}
$$

$$
n_0 = 2 \left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify $N_c$**:

$$
\boxed{n_0 = N_c \, e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}, \quad N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation for $p_0$ is symmetric, replacing $m_n^* \to m_p^*$ and $E_c \to E_v$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Law of Mass Action $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$

**Given**:
$$
n_0 = N_c \, e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}
$$
$$
p_0 = N_v \, e^{-(E_F - E_v)/kT}
$$

**Step 1 — Multiply the two expressions**:

$$
n_0 \, p_0 = N_c N_v \, e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT} \cdot e^{-(E_F - E_v)/kT}
$$

**Step 2 — Combine the exponentials**:

$$
n_0 \, p_0 = N_c N_v \, e^{-[(E_c - E_F) + (E_F - E_v)]/kT}
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $E_F$ from the exponent**:

$$
n_0 \, p_0 = N_c N_v \, e^{-(E_c - E_v)/kT} = N_c N_v \, e^{-E_g/kT}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize this depends ONLY on material, not doping**:

$$
\boxed{n_0 \, p_0 = n_i^2 = N_c N_v \, e^{-E_g/kT}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: This product is **constant at fixed temperature** for any doping level. If you increase $N_d$ → $n_0$ rises → $p_0$ falls by exactly the same factor. This is a frequent 7-mark question.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Continuity Equation (1-D, Holes)

**Starting from the physical principle**: Rate of increase of holes in a small volume = (holes flowing in) − (holes flowing out) + (generation) − (recombination).

**Step 1 — Consider a thin slab** of width $\Delta x$ between $x$ and $x + \Delta x$.

The hole current density entering at $x$: $J_p(x)$.
The hole current density exiting at $x+\Delta x$: $J_p(x+\Delta x)$.

**Step 2 — Net hole accumulation rate per unit volume**:

$$
\text{Accumulation} = -\frac{1}{q} \cdot \frac{J_p(x+\Delta x) - J_p(x)}{\Delta x} + G_p - R_p
$$

**Step 3 — Take the limit** $\Delta x \to 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{q} \frac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} + G_p - R_p
$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to 3-D using divergence**:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{q} \nabla \cdot \vec{J_p} + G_p - R_p}
$$

**Step 5 — Steady State** ($\partial p/\partial t = 0$):

$$
\boxed{0 = -\frac{1}{q} \nabla \cdot \vec{J_p} + G_p - R_p}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Minority Carrier Lifetime and Diffusion Length

**Scenario**: Excess carriers injected at $x = 0$ in a long semiconductor bar.

**Starting equation** (steady state, no electric field, low-level injection):

$$
0 = D_p \frac{d^2 (\Delta p)}{dx^2} - \frac{\Delta p}{\tau_p}
$$

(where $D_p$ is the diffusion coefficient).

**Step 1 — Rearrange**:

$$
\frac{d^2 (\Delta p)}{dx^2} = \frac{\Delta p}{D_p \tau_p}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the diffusion length** $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$:

$$
\frac{d^2 (\Delta p)}{dx^2} = \frac{\Delta p}{L_p^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the second-order ODE** with boundary conditions $\Delta p(0) = \Delta p_0$ and $\Delta p(\infty) = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\Delta p(x) = \Delta p_0 \, e^{-x/L_p}}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical meaning**: $L_p$ is the **average distance** a minority carrier diffuses before recombining.

> [!NOTE]
> This exponential decay is the **basis for the diffusion capacitance** in p-n junction diodes and is a hallmark KTU derivation question.

## 3.5 📊 Analytical Matrix: Equilibrium vs. Steady State

| Property | Thermal Equilibrium | Steady State |
|----------|--------------------|--------------|
| External excitation | None | Constant (DC bias, light) |
| $\partial n/\partial t$, $\partial p/\partial t$ | 0 | 0 |
| Net current | 0 | **Non-zero** |
| Net recombination | 0 | **Non-zero** ($=G_{excess}$) |
| Fermi level | Single $E_F$ throughout | **Quasi-Fermi levels** $F_n \neq F_p$ |
| $np$ product | $n_i^2$ | $np > n_i^2$ possible |
| Charge neutrality | Global + local | **Local** only |
| $J_n, J_p$ | Both 0 | Drift + diffusion present |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Band Diagram of a Semiconductor at Equilibrium

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CB["CONDUCTION BAND - Ec"]

    end

        B2["EF here indicates Fermi level position"]
    end
    subgraph VB["VALENCE BAND - Ev"]
        C1["Fully filled at 0 K; holes appear at T>0"]
    end

```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Carrier Dynamics in Steady State

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Stimulus: Voltage or Photon] --> B[Generation Source G]
    B --> C[Conduction Band Electrons n]
    B --> D[Valence Band Holes p]
    C --> E[Recombination Center]
    D --> E
    E -->|R = n0 + dN over tau| F[Heat / Photon Emission]
    C --> G[Drift and Diffusion Currents]
    D --> G
    G --> H[External Circuit / Load]

    style A fill:#FFCC99,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#D0F0D0,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#F0E0A0,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#C0C0FF,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Flowchart: Equilibrium → Non-Equilibrium → Steady State

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Equilibrium
    Equilibrium: Thermal Equilibrium
    Equilibrium: EF constant
    Equilibrium: np equals ni squared
    Equilibrium: no net current
    Equilibrium --> Transient: Apply external excitation at t=0
    Transient: Carriers adjusting
    Transient: EF splits into Fn and Fp
    Transient: time dependent
    Transient --> SteadyState: After relaxation time tau
    SteadyState: Time invariant
    SteadyState: Fn minus Fp equals constant
    SteadyState: Currents flow
    SteadyState: np greater than ni squared
    SteadyState --> Equilibrium: Remove excitation
    note right of SteadyState: KTU ESE focus area
```

## 4.4 Carrier Concentration vs. Position (Excess Minority Carriers)

```mermaid

    P1 -->|Decay| P2["x = Lp: Delta p equals 0.368 Delta p0"]
    P2 -->|Decay| P3["x = 2 Lp: Delta p equals 0.135 Delta p0"]
    P3 -->|Decay| P4["x = infinity: Delta p equals 0"]

    style P0 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333
    style P1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define thermal equilibrium in a semiconductor. State the role of the Fermi level at equilibrium.**

**Model Answer:**

Thermal equilibrium in a semiconductor is the state in which no external energy (no applied voltage, no light, no thermal gradient) is applied, and the system is at uniform temperature.

**Roles of the Fermi level $E_F$ at equilibrium:**

1. The Fermi level is **constant throughout** the crystal (same value in p-region, n-region, and any junction).
2. It sets the **probability of state occupation** via $f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(E-E_F)/kT}}$.
3. It determines carrier concentrations: $n_0 = N_c e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}$ and $p_0 = N_v e^{-(E_F - E_v)/kT}$.
4. At equilibrium, $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$ regardless of doping.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Distinguish between thermal equilibrium and steady state condition in a semiconductor.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Thermal Equilibrium | Steady State |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------|
| External stimulus | None | Constant (DC bias, light) |
| Time dependence | $\partial n/\partial t = 0$ | $\partial n/\partial t = 0$ |
| Net current | Zero | Non-zero |
| Fermi level | Single $E_F$ | Split: $F_n$, $F_p$ |
| $np$ product | $= n_i^2$ | $\geq n_i^2$ |

> Steady state is **not equilibrium** — it is a time-invariant *non-equilibrium* state.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Derive the expressions for the thermal equilibrium electron and hole concentrations in a non-degenerate semiconductor. Hence obtain the law of mass action.**

#### **Part (a) — Derive $n_0$ and $p_0$ (7 Marks)**

Starting from the density of states in the conduction band:

$$
g_c(E) = \frac{4\pi (2m_n^*)^{3/2}}{h^3} \sqrt{E - E_c}
$$

The electron concentration is:

$$
n_0 = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g_c(E) f(E) dE
$$

For the **non-degenerate case**, $E - E_F \gg kT$ in the CB, so $f(E) \approx e^{-(E-E_F)/kT}$:

$$
n_0 = \frac{4\pi (2m_n^*)^{3/2}}{h^3} e^{E_F/kT} \int_{E_c}^{\infty} \sqrt{E - E_c} \cdot e^{-E/kT} dE
$$

Substitute $u = (E-E_c)/kT$, $dE = kT \, du$:

$$
n_0 = \frac{4\pi (2m_n^*)^{3/2}}{h^3} (kT)^{3/2} e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT} \int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{u} \, e^{-u} du
$$

Using $\int_0^{\infty} u^{1/2} e^{-u} du = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$:

$$
n_0 = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT} = N_c e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}
$$

By symmetry:

$$
p_0 = N_v e^{-(E_F - E_v)/kT}, \quad N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_p^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Law of Mass Action (7 Marks)**

Multiplying:

$$
n_0 p_0 = N_c N_v e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT} \cdot e^{-(E_F - E_v)/kT}
$$

$$
n_0 p_0 = N_c N_v e^{-(E_c - E_v)/kT} = N_c N_v e^{-E_g/kT}
$$

Defining:

$$
n_i^2 \equiv N_c N_v e^{-E_g/kT}
$$

We obtain the **Law of Mass Action**:

$$
\boxed{n_0 p_0 = n_i^2}
$$

**Key Property**: $n_0 p_0$ depends only on **material and temperature**, not on doping. If doping raises $n_0$ tenfold, $p_0$ falls by the same factor.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**State and derive the continuity equation for holes in a semiconductor. Apply the steady state condition and explain the concept of minority carrier lifetime.**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation of Continuity Equation (7 Marks)**

Consider holes in a 1-D slab between $x$ and $x + \Delta x$. Holes enter at rate $J_p(x)/q$ (per unit area) and leave at rate $J_p(x + \Delta x)/q$.

**Rate of accumulation per unit volume**:

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \left[-\frac{J_p(x + \Delta x) - J_p(x)}{q \, \Delta x}\right] + (G - R)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{q} \frac{\partial J_p}{\partial x} + (G - R)
$$

In 3-D:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{q} \nabla \cdot \vec{J_p} + G_p - R_p}
$$

**For electrons** (positive charge carrier convention, $J_n$ is electron current):

$$
\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{q} \nabla \cdot \vec{J_n} + G_n - R_n
$$

#### **Part (b) — Steady State and Minority Carrier Lifetime (7 Marks)**

**Steady state**: $\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = 0$ and $\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = 0$ at every point.

$$
0 = -\frac{1}{q}\nabla \cdot \vec{J_p} + G_p - R_p
$$

**At thermal equilibrium**, $G = R$, and $R - G = \frac{p - p_0}{\tau_p}$ (linear recombination, low-level injection).

**Definition of lifetime $\tau_p$**: It is the **average time** a minority hole exists before recombining with a majority electron.

The recombination rate under low-level injection:

$$
R = G_0 + \frac{\Delta p}{\tau_p}, \quad \text{where } \Delta p = p - p_0
$$

For 1-D diffusion-only, steady state, uniform $G$:

$$
D_p \frac{d^2 (\Delta p)}{dx^2} - \frac{\Delta p}{\tau_p} + G_L = 0
$$

Where $G_L$ is the **excess generation rate** (e.g., due to light).

For $G_L = 0$:

$$
\Delta p(x) = \Delta p(0) \, e^{-x/L_p}, \quad L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}
$$

Where $L_p$ is the **minority carrier diffusion length**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Sign convention trap**: In the continuity equation for *holes*, the divergence term has a **negative** sign: $-\frac{1}{q}\nabla \cdot J_p$. For *electrons*, it is **positive**: $+\frac{1}{q}\nabla \cdot J_n$. Students frequently get this reversed — **lose 2 marks**.
>
> 2. **"Steady state = equilibrium" misconception**: Steady state is *not* equilibrium. In steady state, $F_n \neq F_p$ and currents flow. Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for this confusion.
>
> 3. **$n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$ ONLY at equilibrium**: Many students incorrectly apply this under non-equilibrium (light, forward bias). The correct general expression is $np = n_i^2 e^{(F_n - F_p)/kT}$.
>
> 4. **Dropping the units of $N_c, N_v$**: Always state that $N_c, N_v$ have units of **cm⁻³**. $n_i$ for Si at 300 K is **1.5 × 10¹⁰ cm⁻³**, not per m³.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the $3/4 kT \ln(m_p^*/m_n^*)$ correction in $E_i$**: In intrinsic semiconductors, $E_i$ is not exactly at mid-gap. Examiners reward this detail for full marks in derivations.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Equilibrium & Steady State**

### ⭐ Must-Know Concepts
- [x] **Thermal equilibrium** = no external stimulus, single Fermi level, $E_F$ constant.
- [x] **Steady state** = constant external stimulus, **time-invariant but non-equilibrium**.
- [x] **Fermi-Dirac distribution** governs state occupancy.
- [x] **Carrier concentrations**: $n_0 = N_c e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}$, $p_0 = N_v e^{-(E_F - E_v)/kT}$.
- [x] **Law of Mass Action**: $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$ — **valid in equilibrium only**.
- [x] **$n_i$** depends only on $T$ and material; for Si at 300 K, $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ **cm⁻³**.
- [x] **Fermi level position**:
  - n-type: $E_F$ moves toward $E_c$.
  - p-type: $E_F$ moves toward $E_v$.
  - intrinsic: $E_F \approx E_i$ near mid-gap.

### ⭐ Must-Know Continuity Framework
- [x] **Continuity equation**: charge conservation in time and space.
- [x] **Steady state simplification**: $\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = 0$.
- [x] **Minority carrier lifetime $\tau$** — average time before recombination.
- [x] **Diffusion length** $L = \sqrt{D\tau}$ — average distance diffused.
- [x] **Quasi-Fermi levels** $F_n, F_p$ replace $E_F$ in non-equilibrium steady state.

### ⭐ Must-Know Numerical Values
- [x] $kT/q$ at 300 K = **0.02585 V**
- [x] $E_g$ (Si) = **1.12 eV**, (Ge) = **0.67 eV**, (GaAs) = **1.42 eV**
- [x] $n_i$ (Si, 300 K) = **1.5 × 10¹⁰ cm⁻³**

### ⭐ Must-Know Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
- ❌ Don't write $np = n_i^2$ outside equilibrium.
- ❌ Don't confuse steady state with equilibrium.
- ❌ Don't reverse the sign of $\nabla \cdot J$ in continuity equations.
- ❌ Don't forget the **3/4 kT correction** for $E_i$ in detailed derivations.
- ❌ Don't write $N_c$ without units (cm⁻³).

---

*End of Module 1 Notes — Equilibrium and Steady State Conditions*
*Total Study Time Recommended: 6–8 hours | Practice Problems: 10+ for fluency*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Effective Mass and Fermi Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Effective Mass and Fermi Level

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Concept of Effective Mass

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> The **effective mass ($m^*$)** of an electron (or hole) in a crystalline solid is a quantity that accounts for the influence of the crystal lattice's periodic potential on the motion of charge carriers. It is defined through the curvature of the energy-momentum ($E$–$k$) dispersion relation near band extrema and allows the carrier to be treated as a *quasi-free particle* governed by Newtonian-like equations of motion.

Mathematically, the effective mass is defined as:

$$m^{*} = \frac{\hbar^{2}}{\dfrac{d^{2}E}{dk^{2}}}$$

where $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck's constant ($1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s), $E$ is the electron energy, and $k$ is the wave vector.

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Analogy — "The Swimmer in a Pool"**
> Imagine an electron as a swimmer. In empty water (vacuum), the swimmer pushes against pure water and moves with a certain acceleration. Now, place the swimmer in a swimming pool with lane dividers, waves, and floating toys (the crystal lattice). The swimmer's *apparent* inertia changes — he is slowed or accelerated by interactions with the pool structure. The crystal lattice does the same thing to the electron. We don't need to track every wave; instead, we just say the electron has a new "swimming mass" — the **effective mass**. It can be **larger**, **smaller**, or even **negative** compared to the free electron mass $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg.

### 1.1.1 Physical Origin of Effective Mass

In a free space (no lattice), the energy of an electron is:

$$E = \frac{\hbar^{2}k^{2}}{2m_{0}}$$

But inside a crystal, the electron experiences a **periodic potential** $V(\vec{r}) = V(\vec{r} + \vec{R})$ due to the regularly arranged ions. Bloch's theorem tells us the wavefunctions take the form of plane waves modulated by a periodic function, and the energy-momentum relationship becomes **non-parabolic** in general. The curvature of $E(k)$ near band edges dictates $m^*$.

### 1.1.2 Why Effective Mass Matters

| Property | Free Electron (Vacuum) | Electron in Crystal |
|---|---|---|
| Inertia | Fixed ($m_0$) | Variable ($m^*$) |
| Energy–Momentum | Strictly parabolic | Curved by lattice potential |
| Velocity group | $v_g = \hbar k / m_0$ | $v_g = (1/\hbar)\,dE/dk$ |
| Acceleration law | $F = m_0 a$ | $F = m^* a$ |

---

## 1.2 Concept of the Fermi Level

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> The **Fermi Level ($E_F$)** is the energy level at which the probability of finding an electron is exactly $\mathbf{1/2}$ (or 50%) at any temperature above absolute zero, as described by the **Fermi–Dirac distribution function**. At $T = 0$ K, $E_F$ represents the **highest occupied energy state** in a system of non-interacting fermions.

The Fermi–Dirac distribution function is:

$$f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)}$$

where $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant ($1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K) and $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin.

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Analogy — "The Stadium with Seats"**
> Picture a stadium with rows of seats at different heights (energy levels). At $T = 0$ K, every seat *below* a certain row (the Fermi level) is filled, and every seat *above* is empty. When you heat the stadium (raise $T$), people start shuffling — a few move up, a few leave empty seats below. The **Fermi level** is the "waterline" dividing the occupied seats from the empty ones. In **metals**, this waterline cuts through the bleachers (conduction band) because the stadium is mostly full. In **insulators**, the waterline sits in the empty aisle (forbidden gap) — and only a few brave souls jump across.

### 1.2.1 Position of $E_F$ in Different Materials

| Material Type | Position of $E_F$ | Conductivity Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Metal / Conductor** | Lies *inside* the conduction band | High carrier density; $E_F \sim$ few eV |
| **Intrinsic Semiconductor** | Lies *near mid-gap* between valence and conduction bands | Conductivity increases with $T$ |
| **n-type Semiconductor** | Lies *closer to conduction band edge* ($E_C$) | Electrons are majority carriers |
| **p-type Semiconductor** | Lies *closer to valence band edge* ($E_V$) | Holes are majority carriers |
| **Insulator** | Lies deep inside a wide bandgap | Negligible carriers at room $T$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Effective Mass — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 The Logic Behind Effective Mass

1. **Electron in a Crystal** — The electron moves through a periodic potential $V(\vec{r})$ created by ion cores. Its wavefunction is a **Bloch wave**: $\psi_k(\vec{r}) = u_k(\vec{r}) e^{i\vec{k}\cdot\vec{r}}$.
2. **Group Velocity** — The actual velocity of the electron wave packet is the group velocity:
$$v_g = \frac{1}{\hbar}\frac{dE}{dk}$$
3. **Semiclassical Equation of Motion** — Under an applied external force $\vec{F}$ (e.g., from an electric field), the crystal momentum $\hbar k$ changes:
$$\hbar \frac{dk}{dt} = F$$
4. **Acceleration** — Differentiating velocity with respect to time:
$$a = \frac{dv_g}{dt} = \frac{1}{\hbar}\frac{d^{2}E}{dk^{2}}\frac{dk}{dt} = \frac{1}{\hbar^{2}}\frac{d^{2}E}{dk^{2}}\,F$$
5. **Effective Mass Identification** — Comparing with $a = F/m^{*}$, we get:
$$m^{*} = \frac{\hbar^{2}}{\dfrac{d^{2}E}{dk^{2}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Physical Insight (Why "Negative" Mass?)**
> At the **top of a band**, $E(k)$ curves *downward* — so $\dfrac{d^{2}E}{dk^{2}} < 0$ and $m^{*} < 0$. A negative mass means: when you push the electron in one direction, it *accelerates the opposite way*. Physically, this unphysical behavior is reinterpreted as a **positively charged hole** moving in the *same* direction as the force. This is the **foundation of hole theory** in semiconductors.

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why Bloch waves?** Because the lattice is periodic; only certain wave shapes survive.
- **Why group velocity?** The electron behaves as a wave packet, not a point particle; its motion is the motion of the packet's envelope.
- **Why differentiate twice?** Acceleration is the *second derivative* of position, but here we use $E$–$k$ to get acceleration with respect to $k$ — and curvature is precisely $\dfrac{d^2 E}{dk^2}$.

### 2.1.3 Effective Mass Tensor (3D Crystals)

In anisotropic crystals (e.g., silicon has elliptical constant-energy surfaces), the effective mass is a **tensor**:

$$m^{*}_{ij} = \hbar^{2}\left[\frac{\partial^{2}E}{\partial k_{i}\,\partial k_{j}}\right]^{-1}$$

For **silicon** and **germanium**, the conduction band minima are along $\langle 100 \rangle$ and $\langle 111 \rangle$ directions respectively, giving **longitudinal ($m_l^*$)** and **transverse ($m_t^*$)** effective masses.

### 2.1.4 Density of States Effective Mass

When counting states in a band, we use a **geometric average**:

$$m^{*}_{dos} = (m_1^{*} m_2^{*} m_3^{*})^{1/3}$$

For Si: $m^{*}_{dos} = 6^{2/3}(m_l m_t^2)^{1/3}$ (with 6 equivalent valleys).

---

## 2.2 Fermi Level — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 The Logic Behind the Fermi–Dirac Distribution

1. **Indistinguishable particles** — Electrons are fermions; the Pauli exclusion principle forbids two electrons in identical quantum states.
2. **Statistical mechanics** — The grand canonical ensemble gives the probability of a state at energy $E$ being occupied:
$$f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(E - \mu)/k_B T}}$$
where $\mu$ is the **chemical potential**, identified as $E_F$ in solid-state physics.
3. **At $T = 0$ K** — Exponential term becomes $\infty$ for $E > E_F$ and $0$ for $E < E_F$ → step function.
4. **At $T > 0$ K** — States near $E_F$ become partially occupied over a window of width $\sim k_B T$.

### 2.2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a step function at 0 K?** No thermal energy is available to push electrons above $E_F$ — they fill from the bottom up.
- **Why does $E_F$ shift with doping?** Doping changes electron/hole density, which shifts the chemical potential to maintain charge neutrality.

### 2.2.3 Fermi Energy for a 3D Free Electron Gas (Metal)

By integrating the density of states up to $E_F$ and equating to the electron density $n$:

$$E_F = \frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m_{0}}\left(3\pi^{2}\,n\right)^{2/3}$$

For typical metals, $E_F \sim 1$–$10$ eV (Fermi velocity $v_F \sim 10^{6}$ m/s).

### 2.2.4 Fermi Level in Intrinsic Semiconductors

$$E_F = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3}{4}k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{m^{*}_{h}}{m^{*}_{e}}\right)$$

If $m^{*}_{h} = m^{*}_{e}$, the Fermi level lies exactly at the midgap.

### 2.2.5 Fermi Level in Doped Semiconductors

For an **n-type** semiconductor (donor concentration $N_D$, acceptor $N_A$):

$$E_F = E_C + k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{n}{N_C}\right)$$

where $N_C = 2\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi m^{*}_{e} k_B T}{h^{2}}\right)^{3/2}$ is the effective density of states in the conduction band.

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Symbol Key | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m^{*} = \dfrac{\hbar^{2}}{d^{2}E/dk^{2}}$ | Effective mass from band curvature | kg |
| 2 | $v_g = \dfrac{1}{\hbar}\dfrac{dE}{dk}$ | Group velocity of electron wave packet | m/s |
| 3 | $a = \dfrac{F}{m^{*}}$ | Semiclassical acceleration in crystal | m/s² |
| 4 | $f(E) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right]}$ | Fermi–Dirac distribution | dimensionless |
| 5 | $E_F = \dfrac{\hbar^{2}}{2m_{0}}\left(3\pi^{2} n\right)^{2/3}$ | Fermi energy in 3D free electron gas | J (or eV) |
| 6 | $E_F = \dfrac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \dfrac{3}{4}k_B T \ln\!\left(m^{*}_{h}/m^{*}_{e}\right)$ | Intrinsic Fermi level | eV |
| 7 | $E_F = E_C + k_B T \ln(n/N_C)$ | n-type semiconductor Fermi level | eV |
| 8 | $E_F = E_V - k_B T \ln(p/N_V)$ | p-type semiconductor Fermi level | eV |
| 9 | $N_C = 2\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi m^{*}_{e} k_B T}{h^{2}}\right)^{3/2}$ | Effective DOS — conduction band | m⁻³ |
| 10 | $N_V = 2\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi m^{*}_{h} k_B T}{h^{2}}\right)^{3/2}$ | Effective DOS — valence band | m⁻³ |
| 11 | $n = N_C \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right]$ | Electron concentration in CB | m⁻³ |
| 12 | $p = N_V \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right]$ | Hole concentration in VB | m⁻³ |
| 13 | $n_{i}^{2} = N_C N_V \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{E_g}{k_B T}\right]$ | Mass-action law ($n_i$ = intrinsic carrier conc.) | m⁻⁶ |
| 14 | $m^{*}_{dos} = (m^{*}_{1} m^{*}_{2} m^{*}_{3})^{1/3}$ | Density-of-states effective mass | kg |
| 15 | $m^{*}_{c} = (m^{*}_{l} (m^{*}_{t})^{2})^{1/3}$ | Conductivity effective mass (Si-like) | kg |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Effective mass** determines **mobility** ($\mu = q\tau/m^{*}$), which dictates transistor speed.
- **Low effective mass materials** (e.g., InSb, graphene) are used in **high-speed photodetectors** and **HEMTs**.
- **Fermi level engineering** is the cornerstone of **p–n junction design**, **MOSFET threshold voltage tuning**, and **heterojunction solar cells**.
- **Quasi-Fermi level splitting** under illumination governs **solar cell open-circuit voltage**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Effective Mass from the E–k Relationship

> **Objective:** Show that the effective mass emerges naturally from the semiclassical dynamics of an electron in a periodic potential.

### Step 1: Bloch Wave Assumption

In a periodic potential $V(\vec{r}) = V(\vec{r} + \vec{R})$, the electron's stationary states are **Bloch waves**:

$$\psi_k(\vec{r}) = u_k(\vec{r})\,e^{i\vec{k}\cdot\vec{r}}$$

where $u_k(\vec{r})$ is a periodic function with the lattice periodicity. Each Bloch state is characterized by a crystal wavevector $\vec{k}$ and has a definite energy $E_n(\vec{k})$ (band index $n$).

### Step 2: Energy in the Nearly-Free-Electron Limit

In the nearly-free-electron model, the electron energy is nearly that of a free particle but perturbed by the lattice. Near a band minimum at $\vec{k} = \vec{k}_0$, expand $E(\vec{k})$ in a Taylor series to second order:

$$E(\vec{k}) \approx E(\vec{k}_0) + \frac{1}{2}\frac{d^{2}E}{dk^{2}}\bigg|_{\vec{k}_0}(\vec{k} - \vec{k}_0)^{2}$$

Higher-order terms are neglected because the band minimum is parabolic to first approximation.

### Step 3: Comparison with Free Electron

For a free electron of mass $m_0$:

$$E_{\text{free}}(k) = \frac{\hbar^{2}k^{2}}{2m_{0}}$$

Rewriting our Taylor-expanded form (with $\vec{q} = \vec{k} - \vec{k}_0$):

$$E(q) - E(\vec{k}_0) = \frac{1}{2}\frac{d^{2}E}{dk^{2}}\,q^{2}$$

This is structurally identical to the free-electron formula if we identify:

$$E(q) - E(\vec{k}_0) = \frac{\hbar^{2} q^{2}}{2 m^{*}}$$

Equating coefficients:

$$\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2 m^{*}} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{d^{2}E}{dk^{2}}$$

### Step 4: Final Result

$$\boxed{\,m^{*} = \frac{\hbar^{2}}{\dfrac{d^{2}E}{dk^{2}}}\,}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The second derivative of the band curve is the *curvature*. A sharply curved band → small $m^*$ (electron responds easily). A flat band → large $m^*$ (electron sluggish). An inverted band (top of valence band) → negative $m^*$ (hole).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Negative Effective Mass and the Hole Concept

> **Objective:** Demonstrate that a missing electron near the top of a band behaves as a positively charged particle.

### Step 1: Set Up at Valence Band Maximum

Let the valence band maximum occur at $k = 0$ with energy $E_V$. Near the top:

$$E(k) = E_V - A k^{2}, \quad A > 0$$

Compute the curvature:

$$\frac{d^{2}E}{dk^{2}} = -2A < 0$$

Therefore:

$$m^{*} = \frac{\hbar^{2}}{-2A} = -m_{h}^{*}$$

The electron has **negative effective mass** at the top of the band.

### Step 2: Show the Hall Effect for a Nearly Full Band

Consider a nearly full valence band with one missing electron (a vacancy at $\vec{k}_h$). The current density of a filled band is **zero** (sum of $\vec{v}_g$ over filled states vanishes by symmetry). Removing one electron at state $\vec{k}_h$ leaves:

$$\vec{J} = -(-e)\vec{v}_g(\vec{k}_h) = +e\vec{v}_g(\vec{k}_h)$$

The "missing electron" current acts as if it were a **positive charge** $e$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}_g(\vec{k}_h)$.

### Step 3: Show the Equation of Motion for the Hole

The missing electron obeys $\hbar\,d\vec{k}_h/dt = -e(\vec{E} + \vec{v}_h \times \vec{B})$ (force on a *negative* charge). Define hole wavevector $\vec{k}_{\text{hole}} = -\vec{k}_h$ and hole velocity $\vec{v}_h = \vec{v}_g(\vec{k}_h)$. The hole equation becomes:

$$\hbar\frac{d\vec{k}_{\text{hole}}}{dt} = +e(\vec{E} + \vec{v}_h \times \vec{B})$$

This is exactly the Lorentz force on a **positive charge** $e$ with crystal momentum $\hbar\vec{k}_{\text{hole}}$ and **positive** effective mass $m_h^* = +|\hbar^2/(d^2E/dk^2)|$.

> [!NOTE] **Conclusion:** A vacancy in a nearly full band (valence band) behaves identically to a positively charged particle with **positive effective mass** $m_h^*$ and **positive charge** $+e$. This fictitious particle is the **hole**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Fermi Energy for a 3D Free Electron Gas

> **Objective:** Derive $E_F = \dfrac{\hbar^{2}}{2m_{0}}\left(3\pi^{2} n\right)^{2/3}$ for a metal.

### Step 1: Density of States (DOS) in 3D

For a free electron in 3D, counting states in $\vec{k}$-space with periodic boundary conditions in volume $V$:

$$g(k)\,dk = \frac{V}{2\pi^{2}}\,k^{2}\,dk$$

Transforming to energy using $E = \hbar^{2}k^{2}/(2m_0)$:

$$k = \frac{\sqrt{2m_0 E}}{\hbar}, \quad dk = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2m_0}{E}}\,\frac{dE}{\hbar}$$

Substituting:

$$g(E)\,dE = \frac{V}{2\pi^{2}}\left(\frac{2m_0 E}{\hbar^{2}}\right)\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2m_0}{E}}\,\frac{dE}{\hbar}$$

$$g(E) = \frac{V}{2\pi^{2}}\left(\frac{2m_0}{\hbar^{2}}\right)^{3/2}\frac{\sqrt{E}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,g(E) = \frac{V}{2\pi^{2}}\left(\frac{2m_0}{\hbar^{2}}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}\,}$$

> (Often written as $g(E) = C\sqrt{E}$ with $C = \dfrac{V}{2\pi^{2}}\left(\dfrac{2m_0}{\hbar^{2}}\right)^{3/2}$.)

### Step 2: Total Number of Electrons at $T = 0$ K

At absolute zero, all states up to $E_F$ are filled:

$$N = \int_{0}^{E_F} g(E)\,dE = C \int_{0}^{E_F}\sqrt{E}\,dE = C\cdot\frac{2}{3}E_F^{3/2}$$

### Step 3: Solve for $E_F$

Using $n = N/V$ and the constant $C/V = \dfrac{1}{2\pi^{2}}\left(\dfrac{2m_0}{\hbar^{2}}\right)^{3/2}$:

$$n = \frac{1}{3\pi^{2}}\left(\frac{2m_0 E_F}{\hbar^{2}}\right)^{3/2}$$

Solving for $E_F$:

$$E_F^{3/2} = \frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m_0}\left(3\pi^{2} n\right)^{2/3}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_F = \frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m_0}\left(3\pi^{2} n\right)^{2/3}\,}$$

> **Numerical check:** For copper, $n \approx 8.5 \times 10^{28}$ m⁻³, giving $E_F \approx 7.0$ eV ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Fermi Level in an Intrinsic Semiconductor

> **Objective:** Show that $E_F$ sits near midgap and derive the small correction.

### Step 1: Charge Neutrality in Intrinsic Semiconductor

For an intrinsic semiconductor, $n = p = n_i$. From the carrier concentration formulas:

$$n_i = N_C \exp\!\left[-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right]$$

$$n_i = N_V \exp\!\left[-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right]$$

### Step 2: Equate the Two Expressions

$$N_C \exp\!\left[-\frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T}\right] = N_V \exp\!\left[-\frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}\right]$$

Take the natural logarithm:

$$\ln N_C - \frac{E_C - E_F}{k_B T} = \ln N_V - \frac{E_F - E_V}{k_B T}$$

Collect $E_F$ terms:

$$\frac{2E_F}{k_B T} = \frac{E_C + E_V}{k_B T} + \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

$$E_F = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{k_B T}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

### Step 3: Substitute DOS Expressions

Since $N_C \propto (m_e^*)^{3/2}$ and $N_V \propto (m_h^*)^{3/2}$:

$$\frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right) = \frac{3}{4}\ln\!\left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)$$

Hence the standard textbook result:

$$\boxed{\,E_F = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3}{4}k_B T\,\ln\!\left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)\,}$$

> **Key insight:** If $m_h^* = m_e^*$, then $E_F$ is exactly at midgap. Any asymmetry in effective masses shifts $E_F$ slightly toward the band with the *lighter* carriers.

---

## 3.5 📊 Analytical Matrix: Effective Mass Values for Common Semiconductors

| Material | $m_e^* / m_0$ (electron) | $m_{lh}^*/m_0$ (light hole) | $m_{hh}^*/m_0$ (heavy hole) | $E_g$ (eV) | Band Structure |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Si** | 0.26 ($m_t$), 0.98 ($m_l$) | 0.16 | 0.49 | 1.12 | Indirect |
| **Ge** | 0.082 ($m_t$), 1.64 ($m_l$) | 0.043 | 0.34 | 0.67 | Indirect |
| **GaAs** | 0.067 | 0.082 | 0.45 | 1.42 | Direct |
| **InSb** | 0.014 | 0.015 | 0.40 | 0.18 | Direct |
| **CdTe** | 0.096 | 0.12 | 0.60 | 1.56 | Direct |
| **InP** | 0.077 | 0.12 | 0.60 | 1.35 | Direct |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why GaAs is faster than Si:** $m_e^*(\text{GaAs}) = 0.067\,m_0$ is much smaller than $m_e^*(\text{Si}) = 0.26\,m_0$ (transverse). This gives GaAs a much **higher electron mobility** — making it the material of choice for high-frequency / high-speed devices like HEMTs and laser diodes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Effective Mass as Band Curvature — Block-Level Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *logic flow* connecting the crystal's band structure to the effective mass concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Crystal Lattice with Periodic Potential V(r)"] --> B["Electron experiences Bragg reflections and band formation"]
    B --> C["E-k dispersion relation E(k) is non-parabolic"]
    C --> D["Take second derivative d-squared-E by d-k-squared"]
    D --> E["Compute effective mass m-star equals hbar squared over curvature"]
    E --> F{"Sign of curvature?"}
    F -- "Positive: bottom of conduction band" --> G["m-star greater than 0: free-electron-like behavior, treated as electron"]
    F -- "Negative: top of valence band" --> H["m-star less than 0: empty state treated as a HOLE with positive mass and positive charge"]
    G --> I["Carrier contributes to current as a negative charge"]
    H --> J["Carrier contributes to current as a positive charge"]
    I --> K["Device physics: n-type conduction, electron mobility"]
    J --> L["Device physics: p-type conduction, hole mobility"]
```

> **Reading guide:** The diagram traces the chain: *crystal potential → band structure → curvature → effective mass → sign decision → physical carrier identification → device behavior*.

---

## 4.2 Fermi Level Position in Different Materials

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Metal["METAL: E_F inside conduction band"]
        M1["Valence band: completely filled"] --- M2["Conduction band: partially filled"]
        M3["E_F line cuts through conduction band"] -.-> M2
    end

    subgraph Intrinsic["INTRINSIC SEMICONDUCTOR: E_F near midgap"]
        I1["Valence band: full"] --- I2["Empty bandgap E_g"]
        I2 --- I3["Empty conduction band"]
        I4["E_F sits at midgap"] -.-> I2
    end

    subgraph Ntype["N-TYPE: E_F shifted up toward E_C"]
        N1["Valence band"] --- N2["Bandgap"]
        N2 --- N3["Conduction band with donor electrons"]
        N4["E_F close to E_C"] -.-> N3
    end

    subgraph Ptype["P-TYPE: E_F shifted down toward E_V"]
        P1["Valence band with acceptor holes"] --- P2["Bandgap"]
        P2 --- P3["Conduction band empty"]
        P4["E_F close to E_V"] -.-> P1
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Parabolic E–k Band Showing Effective Mass

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ConductionBand["Conduction band: parabola opens upward"]
        C1["d-squared-E by d-k-squared greater than 0"]
        C2["m-star equals positive quantity"]
        C3["CURVATURE: large → small m-star: highly mobile electron example GaAs"]
    end
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3

    subgraph ValenceBand["Valence band: parabola opens downward"]
        V1["d-squared-E by d-k-squared less than 0"]
        V2["m-star equals negative quantity: physically reinterpreted as HOLE"]
        V3["CURVATURE: flat → large effective mass: heavy hole example silicon"]
    end
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3

    ConductionBand -. "Energy gap E_g separates them" .-> ValenceBand
```

---

## 4.4 Fermi–Dirac Distribution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Energy axis E increasing upward"] --> B["Three reference energies: E_V, E_F, E_C"]
    B --> C{"Compare E with E_F"}
    C -- "E much less than E_F" --> D["f of E approaches 1: state almost certainly filled"]
    C -- "E equals E_F" --> E["f of E equals 0.5: state half occupied"]
    C -- "E much greater than E_F" --> F["f of E approaches 0: state almost certainly empty"]
    D --> G["Physical: valence band deep states always full"]
    E --> H["Physical: near E_F states can be flipped thermally"]
    F --> I["Physical: conduction band deep states always empty at low T"]
```

> **Engineering takeaway:** Only electronic states within a window of width $\sim k_B T$ ($\approx 26$ meV at room temperature) around $E_F$ can be thermally excited. This is why **shallow dopants** (energy levels within $k_B T$ of a band edge) are effective, but **deep levels** (far inside the bandgap) are not.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🎯 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1.** Define the term *effective mass* of an electron in a crystal. How is it related to the E–k diagram? `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The effective mass $m^*$ of an electron in a crystal is the mass that an electron appears to possess when moving through the periodic potential of the lattice. It is not a real mass but a **parameter** that incorporates the effect of the crystal's periodic potential on the electron's motion. It is defined from the curvature of the energy-wavevector ($E$–$k$) dispersion curve:

$$m^{*} = \frac{\hbar^{2}}{d^{2}E/dk^{2}}$$

At the **bottom of the conduction band**, the $E$–$k$ curve is concave upward, so $m^{*} > 0$. At the **top of the valence band**, the curve is concave downward, so $m^{*} < 0$, which physically corresponds to a **hole**.

---

### **Q2.** What is the Fermi level? Explain its position in (i) an intrinsic semiconductor and (ii) an n-type semiconductor. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Fermi level ($E_F$)** is the energy at which the probability of occupation by an electron is exactly $\mathbf{1/2}$ at finite temperature, governed by the Fermi–Dirac distribution $f(E) = 1/(1 + e^{(E-E_F)/k_BT})$.

**(i) Intrinsic semiconductor:** $E_F$ lies very close to the **middle of the bandgap**. The exact position is

$$E_F = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3}{4}k_BT \ln(m^{*}_{h}/m^{*}_{e})$$

If $m_h^* = m_e^*$, then $E_F$ is exactly at midgap.

**(ii) n-type semiconductor:** Doping with donors shifts $E_F$ **upward**, closer to the conduction band edge $E_C$. At moderate doping,

$$E_F = E_C + k_BT \ln(n/N_C)$$

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> Each Part B question provides two options. **Answer ANY ONE.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### **(a)** Derive an expression for the effective mass of an electron in a crystal. Explain with an E–k diagram why the effective mass is **negative** at the top of a band. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Semiclassical starting point.** An electron in a crystal under an external force $\vec{F}$ obeys

$$\hbar \frac{d\vec{k}}{dt} = \vec{F}$$

**Step 2 — Group velocity.** The electron wave packet's velocity is

$$v_g = \frac{1}{\hbar}\frac{dE}{dk}$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration.**

$$a = \frac{dv_g}{dt} = \frac{1}{\hbar}\frac{d^{2}E}{dk^{2}}\cdot\frac{dk}{dt} = \frac{F}{\hbar^{2}}\cdot\frac{d^{2}E}{dk^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with $a = F/m^*$:**

$$m^{*} = \frac{\hbar^{2}}{d^{2}E/dk^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Sign analysis with E–k diagram:**

```
   E
   |        ___________  ← Top of band (negative curvature)
   |       /           \
   |      /             \
   |     /               \
   |____/_________________\______ k
        0                 k_top
```

At the top of the band, the curve is **concave downward** → $d^{2}E/dk^{2} < 0$ → $m^* < 0$.

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation:** A negative effective mass means the electron accelerates *opposite* to the applied force. This is unphysical; therefore, the **vacancy** (missing electron) is reinterpreted as a **hole** — a positively charged particle with **positive effective mass** $m_h^* = |m^*|$.

---

#### **(b)** Calculate the Fermi energy of aluminum, given that it has a free electron density of $n = 1.81 \times 10^{29}$ m⁻³. Take $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg and $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s. Comment on the result. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 1.81 \times 10^{29}$ m⁻³, $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s.

**Formula:**

$$E_F = \frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m_{0}}\left(3\pi^{2} n\right)^{2/3}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $3\pi^{2} n$:**

$$3\pi^{2} n = 3 \times 9.8696 \times 1.81 \times 10^{29} = 5.358 \times 10^{30}\,\text{m}^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Raise to power 2/3:**

$$(5.358 \times 10^{30})^{2/3} = (5.358)^{2/3} \times 10^{20}$$

$$(5.358)^{2/3} = (5.358^{1/3})^{2} = (1.747)^{2} = 3.052$$

$$(3\pi^{2} n)^{2/3} = 3.052 \times 10^{20}\,\text{m}^{-2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute prefactor $\hbar^{2}/(2m_0)$:**

$$\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m_0} = \frac{(1.054 \times 10^{-34})^{2}}{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31}} = \frac{1.111 \times 10^{-68}}{1.822 \times 10^{-30}} = 6.097 \times 10^{-39}\,\text{J·m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$E_F = 6.097 \times 10^{-39} \times 3.052 \times 10^{20} = 1.861 \times 10^{-18}\,\text{J}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to eV** (divide by $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$):

$$E_F = \frac{1.861 \times 10^{-18}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 11.6\,\text{eV}$$

**Comment:** This is high but consistent with aluminum's three valence electrons per atom. The Fermi temperature is $T_F = E_F/k_B \approx 1.34 \times 10^{5}$ K — meaning **even at room temperature ($T \ll T_F$)**, the electron gas is highly degenerate. This validates the use of Fermi–Dirac statistics (not classical Maxwell–Boltzmann) for metals at ordinary temperatures.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

#### **(a)** With the help of the Fermi–Dirac distribution function, explain the concept of the Fermi level. Discuss the variation of $E_F$ with temperature and doping in semiconductors. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Distribution Function:**

$$f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right]}$$

- At $T = 0$ K: $f(E) = 1$ for $E < E_F$ and $f(E) = 0$ for $E > E_F$ — a step function. $E_F$ is the **highest filled state**.
- At $T > 0$ K: $f(E)$ smears around $E_F$ over a window $\sim k_B T$. At $E = E_F$, $f = 1/2$ exactly.
- For $E - E_F \gg k_B T$: $f(E) \approx e^{-(E - E_F)/k_B T}$ → **Maxwell–Boltzmann tail** (classical limit).

**Step 2 — Definition of $E_F$:** The Fermi level is the energy at which the occupation probability is **50%** at any finite temperature. It is the **chemical potential** of electrons in the solid.

**Step 3 — Temperature variation in intrinsic semiconductor:**

$$E_F(T) = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3}{4}k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{m_h^{*}}{m_e^{*}}\right)$$

The second term is small (depends on mass ratio), so $E_F$ stays close to midgap and shifts slightly as $T$ rises.

**Step 4 — Doping variation:**

- **n-type doping** (donors $N_D$): excess electrons push $E_F$ **up** toward $E_C$. At low $T$, $E_F$ lies between $E_D$ (donor level) and $E_C$. At high $T$, all donors ionized, $E_F$ moves back toward midgap.
- **p-type doping** (acceptors $N_A$): excess holes push $E_F$ **down** toward $E_V$. At low $T$, $E_F$ lies between $E_V$ and $E_A$ (acceptor level).

**Step 5 — Engineering significance:** By controlling doping type and concentration, $E_F$ can be positioned anywhere in the bandgap — this is the foundation of all **modern semiconductor device design** (diodes, transistors, solar cells, LEDs).

---

#### **(b)** The electron effective mass in GaAs is $0.067\,m_0$ and the hole effective mass is $0.45\,m_0$. The bandgap is $E_g = 1.42$ eV. Calculate the intrinsic Fermi level position relative to the midgap at $T = 300$ K. Take $k_B T = 0.0259$ eV. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $m_e^* = 0.067\,m_0$, $m_h^* = 0.45\,m_0$, $E_g = 1.42$ eV, $T = 300$ K, $k_BT = 0.0259$ eV.

**Formula:**

$$E_F - E_{\text{midgap}} = \frac{3}{4}k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{m_h^{*}}{m_e^{*}}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Compute the mass ratio:**

$$\frac{m_h^{*}}{m_e^{*}} = \frac{0.45}{0.067} = 6.716$$

**Step 2 — Compute the natural log:**

$$\ln(6.716) = 1.905$$

**Step 3 — Compute the shift:**

$$E_F - E_{\text{midgap}} = \frac{3}{4} \times 0.0259 \times 1.905$$

$$= 0.75 \times 0.0259 \times 1.905 = 0.0194 \times 1.905$$

$$= 0.0370\,\text{eV}$$

**Step 4 — Direction of shift:** Since $m_h^* > m_e^*$, the log is positive, so $E_F$ is shifted **above** the midgap, closer to the conduction band.

**Step 5 — Final position:**

$$E_F = E_{\text{midgap}} + 0.0370\,\text{eV}$$

In other words, $E_F$ lies **0.037 eV above the midgap**, or equivalently at $E_V + 0.747$ eV (since midgap is at $E_V + 0.71$ eV for GaAs).

**Comment:** This small shift ($\sim k_BT$) reflects the slight asymmetry in effective masses. Because GaAs has a *direct* bandgap and small $m_e^*$, its high electron mobility dominates the device physics, making it ideal for optoelectronic applications.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include $1/2$ in the Taylor expansion** of $E(k)$ — the correct expansion is $E \approx E_0 + \frac{1}{2}(d^2E/dk^2)(k-k_0)^2$. Dropping the $1/2$ gives a factor-of-2 error in $m^*$.
> 2. **Sign confusion in the hole concept** — a negative $m^*$ belongs to the *missing electron*; the **hole** itself has *positive* mass and *positive* charge. Examiners specifically deduct for mixing these up.
> 3. **Mixing Fermi energy ($E_F$ at 0 K) and Fermi level ($E_F$ at finite $T$)** — they are usually the same in metals, but in semiconductors they can differ noticeably with doping and temperature.
> 4. **Forgetting the units** in $E_F$ calculations. KTU examiners expect J → eV conversion explicitly. (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.)
> 5. **Applying Maxwell–Boltzmann statistics** to a metal at room temperature. The condition $k_BT \ll E_F$ means you **must** use Fermi–Dirac — this is a full 2-mark penalty if missed.
> 6. **Not justifying the position of $E_F$ with a band diagram** in qualitative questions — KTU examiners want a labeled diagram for full credit.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before your exam!):**

- ⭐ **Effective mass** is *not* a real mass — it is a **band-curvature parameter** that lets us treat a Bloch electron as a quasi-free particle.
- ⭐ Formula: $m^* = \hbar^{2}/(d^{2}E/dk^{2})$ — *curvature in the denominator*.
- ⭐ **Conduction band bottom** → $m^* > 0$ → electrons (negative charge, positive mass).
- ⭐ **Valence band top** → $m^* < 0$ → reinterpreted as **holes** (positive charge, positive mass $m_h^*$).
- ⭐ Fermi–Dirac distribution: $f(E) = 1/[1 + e^{(E-E_F)/k_BT}]$.
- ⭐ At $T = 0$ K, $E_F$ = **highest occupied energy** (step function).
- ⭐ At finite $T$, $E_F$ = energy of **50% occupation probability**.
- ⭐ Fermi energy of free electron gas: $E_F = (\hbar^{2}/2m_0)(3\pi^{2}n)^{2/3}$.
- ⭐ **Intrinsic semiconductor**: $E_F$ near midgap, $E_F = (E_C + E_V)/2 + (3/4)k_BT \ln(m_h^*/m_e^*)$.
- ⭐ **n-type**: $E_F$ shifts up toward $E_C$; **p-type**: $E_F$ shifts down toward $E_V$.
- ⭐ **Quasi-Fermi levels** (not in syllabus today, but mentioned) — used under non-equilibrium conditions like illumination or forward bias.
- ⭐ **Density of states effective mass**: $m^*_{dos} = (m_1^* m_2^* m_3^*)^{1/3}$.
- ⭐ **Conductivity effective mass** for Si-like multi-valley bands: $m^*_{c} = (m_l^* (m_t^*)^2)^{1/3}$.
- ⭐ **Key constants to memorize:** $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K = 8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K, 1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.
- ⭐ **Practical memory hook:** GaAs has $m_e^* = 0.067\,m_0$ — *small mass, high mobility, fast devices*. Si has $m_e^* = 0.26\,m_0$ — *larger mass, slower electrons, but mature technology*.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** When a question asks "**explain**" effective mass or Fermi level, *always* pair your explanation with a **sketch of the E–k curve** or the **energy band diagram with $E_F$ marked**. A diagram earns you 1–2 easy marks that words alone cannot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Density of States & Effective Density of States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Density of States & Effective Density of States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Density of States (DOS)?

The **Density of States (DOS)**, denoted mathematically as `g(E)` or `Nc(E)`, is a fundamental quantum mechanical function in solid-state physics. It formally represents **the number of available electron states per unit volume per unit energy at a given energy `E`** within a crystalline solid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> The Density of States `g(E)dE` is defined as the number of permissible energy states available to be occupied by electrons in a unit volume of the semiconductor, lying within the energy interval between `E` and `E + dE`.

### The Two Critical DOS Functions in Semiconductors

1. **Conduction Band DOS — `gc(E)`**:
   Defined for `E ≥ Ec`, where `Ec` is the conduction band minimum.
2. **Valence Band DOS — `gv(E)`**:
   Defined for `E ≤ Ev`, where `Ev` is the valence band maximum.

Both functions describe the **available (empty) states** — i.e., states that electrons can occupy.

## 1.2 What is the Effective Density of States (`Nc` and `Nv`)?

The **Effective Density of States** is a scalar constant obtained by integrating the DOS function over an entire energy band. It condenses the entire band structure into a single equivalent "effective" density parameter:

- **`Nc`** = Effective Density of States in the **Conduction Band** (units: **cm⁻³** or **m⁻³**)
- **`Nv`** = Effective Density of States in the **Valence Band** (units: **cm⁻³** or **m⁻³**)

> [!NOTE]
> The physical meaning of `Nc` is profound: it represents the **equivalent concentration of states** if *all* allowed conduction band states were concentrated at a single energy level at the conduction band edge `Ec`.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Parking Lot" Model

Imagine a multi-story parking garage representing a semiconductor crystal:

- **Each parking slot** = one allowed quantum state.
- **The "floor" the slot is on** = the energy level `E`.
- **Density of States `g(E)`** = *How many parking slots exist on a particular floor per unit floor area*. Floors closer to the "ground floor" (band edge) have fewer slots, while higher floors have progressively more slots because the "floor area" (constant energy surface in k-space) grows.

- **Effective Density of States `Nc`** = *If we collapsed all parking slots in the entire garage onto a single imaginary "ground floor" reference level, how many slots would we count?*

This is why `Nc` carries the units of concentration (per cm³) — it is an **integrated total**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants Used (Memorize These)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Units |
|----------|--------|-------|-------|
| Reduced Planck's Constant | `ℏ` | `1.0546 × 10⁻³⁴` | J·s |
| Planck's Constant | `h` | `6.626 × 10⁻³⁴` | J·s |
| Boltzmann Constant | `k` | `1.381 × 10⁻²³` | J/K |
| Electron Rest Mass | `m₀` | `9.109 × 10⁻³¹` | kg |
| Thermal Voltage @ 300 K | `VT` | `0.0259` | V |

> [!TIP]
> At room temperature (**T = 300 K**), `kT ≈ 0.0259 eV`. This value is the cornerstone of all semiconductor carrier statistics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of the DOS — The Quantum Mechanical Foundation

The derivation of DOS begins from the **Particle-in-a-3D-Box** (Infinite Potential Well) model applied to a crystalline solid of volume `V = L³`. The Schrödinger equation yields allowed energy eigenvalues:

$$E = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m^*}$$

where `k² = kx² + ky² + kz²` and `m*` is the **effective mass** of the carrier.

### Stepwise Logic Flow

1. **Quantization of `k`**: For a cube of side `L`, the boundary condition `ψ = 0` at walls requires `k = πn/L` where `n` is a positive integer. This discretizes k-space into a lattice of spacing `π/L` per dimension.

2. **Number of States in k-Space Volume**: One quantum state occupies a volume `(π/L)³` in k-space. Therefore, the number of states in a k-space volume `d³k` is:
   $$\text{States} = \frac{d^3k}{(\pi/L)^3} = \frac{V \cdot d^3k}{\pi^3}$$

3. **Spherical Symmetry in k-Space**: A constant energy surface is a sphere of radius `k`. The volume between `k` and `k + dk` is `4πk² dk`. Substituting:
   $$g(k) dk = 2 \cdot \frac{V \cdot 4\pi k^2 \, dk}{(2\pi)^3} = \frac{V k^2}{\pi^2} dk$$
   *(The factor `2` accounts for spin degeneracy — two electrons per state, one spin-up and one spin-down.)*

4. **Conversion to Energy Domain**: Using `k = √(2m*E)/ℏ` and differentiating to get `dk`:
   $$dk = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2m^*}}{ℏ \sqrt{E}} \, dE$$

5. **Final DOS Expression** (per unit volume):
   $$g(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E}$$

## 2.2 DOS in the Conduction and Valence Bands

When we shift the zero of energy to the band edge:

**Conduction Band DOS** (energy measured from `Ec`):
$$g_c(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2 m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_c} \quad \text{for } E \geq E_c$$

**Valence Band DOS** (energy measured downward from `Ev`):
$$g_v(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2 m_p^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E_v - E} \quad \text{for } E \leq E_v$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the parabolic `√E` dependence — this is a **direct consequence of the parabolic band approximation** (`E ∝ k²`). It is the single most tested fact in KTU board exams.

## 2.3 Derivation of Effective Density of States

The effective density `Nc` is obtained by integrating `gc(E)` from `Ec` to `∞`, weighted by the **Boltzmann occupation probability** `f(E) = exp(-(E - Ec)/kT)` (Boltzmann approximation):

$$N_c = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g_c(E) \cdot e^{-(E - E_c)/kT} \, dE$$

Substituting and evaluating the standard integral `∫√u · e^(-u/kT) du`:

$$N_c = 2 \left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* k T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

Similarly:

$$N_v = 2 \left(\frac{2\pi m_p^* k T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM-CRITICAL FORMULAS — MEMORIZE THIS TABLE VERBATIM**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $g(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E}$ | DOS per unit volume per unit energy | J⁻¹ m⁻³ |
| 2 | $g(E) dE = \frac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E} \, dE$ | Number of states in `[E, E+dE]` for volume V | dimensionless |
| 3 | $N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ | Effective DOS — Conduction Band | m⁻³ or cm⁻³ |
| 4 | $N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_p^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ | Effective DOS — Valence Band | m⁻³ or cm⁻³ |
| 5 | $N_c = 2.5 \times 10^{19} \left(\frac{m_n^*}{m_0}\right)^{3/2} \left(\frac{T}{300}\right)^{3/2}$ | Simplified Nc @ T (Si) | cm⁻³ |
| 6 | $N_v = 2.5 \times 10^{19} \left(\frac{m_p^*}{m_0}\right)^{3/2} \left(\frac{T}{300}\right)^{3/2}$ | Simplified Nv @ T (Si) | cm⁻³ |
| 7 | $n_0 = N_c \cdot e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}$ | Thermal equilibrium e⁻ concentration | cm⁻³ |
| 8 | $p_0 = N_v \cdot e^{-(E_F - E_v)/kT}$ | Thermal equilibrium hole concentration | cm⁻³ |
| 9 | $n_i^2 = N_c N_v \cdot e^{-E_g/kT}$ | Mass-action law | cm⁻⁶ |

## 2.5 Why Is This Important in Engineering?

- **MOSFET Design**: Threshold voltage `Vth` depends directly on `Nc`. Engineers tune `m*` via doping to control carrier density.
- **Solar Cell Engineering**: Open-circuit voltage `Voc = (kT/q) \ln(Jsc/(J_0))` where `J_0 ∝ N_c N_v e^{-Eg/kT}`. Better solar cells require understanding DOS.
- **LED & Laser Diodes**: Carrier injection and recombination rates depend on `Nc, Nv`.
- **CMOS Scaling**: As devices shrink to the nanoscale, the DOS itself becomes discretized (quantum confinement), fundamentally changing device behavior.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of `g(E)` — The Density of States

### Step 1: Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation for a 3D Box

For an electron of effective mass `m*` confined in a cube of side `L` with infinite potential walls:

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*} \nabla^2 \psi = E \psi$$

The boundary condition is `ψ = 0` at `x, y, z = 0` and `L`. Using separation of variables, the allowed wavevectors are quantized:

$$k_x = \frac{n_x \pi}{L}, \quad k_y = \frac{n_y \pi}{L}, \quad k_z = \frac{n_z \pi}{L}$$

where `nx, ny, nz` are positive integers `(1, 2, 3, ...)`.

### Step 2: Energy Eigenvalue

The energy corresponding to quantum numbers `(nx, ny, nz)` is:

$$E = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2m^* L^2} (n_x^2 + n_y^2 + n_z^2)$$

Equivalently, in terms of wavevector magnitude `k = √(kx² + ky² + kz²)`:

$$E = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2m^*}$$

### Step 3: Number of States in a Spherical Shell of k-Space

Each allowed state occupies a k-space volume of:

$$\Delta V_k = \left(\frac{\pi}{L}\right)^3 = \frac{\pi^3}{V}$$

The total number of k-points with magnitude between `k` and `k + dk` lies in a spherical shell of volume `4πk² dk`. With spin degeneracy factor `gs = 2`:

$$N(k) dk = g_s \cdot \frac{4\pi k^2 \, dk}{\Delta V_k} = 2 \cdot \frac{4\pi k^2 \, dk}{\pi^3 / V} = \frac{8\pi V k^2}{\pi^3} dk = \frac{8 V k^2}{\pi^2} dk$$

### Step 4: Transformation from k to Energy

We require `N(E) dE = N(k) dk`. Compute `dk/dE`:

$$E = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2m^*} \implies k = \frac{\sqrt{2 m^* E}}{\hbar}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to `E`:

$$\frac{dk}{dE} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2 m^*}}{\hbar \sqrt{E}} = \frac{1}{2\hbar} \sqrt{\frac{2m^*}{E}}$$

Therefore:

$$N(E) dE = \frac{8 V k^2}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{dk}{dE} \, dE = \frac{8 V}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{2 m^* E}{\hbar^2} \cdot \frac{1}{2\hbar} \sqrt{\frac{2m^*}{E}} \, dE$$

Simplify term by term:

$$N(E) dE = \frac{8 V}{2\pi^2 \hbar^3} \cdot (2m^*)^{3/2} \cdot \sqrt{E} \, dE = \frac{4 V}{\pi^2 \hbar^3} (2m^*)^{3/2} \sqrt{E} \, dE$$

Wait — let me recheck the spin factor. In many KTU textbooks (Neamen, Streetman), the standard convention gives `N(E) = (V/2π²)·(2m*/ℏ²)^(3/2)·√E`. This comes from integrating over **one octant** of k-space (using `gs = 1` for one spin and dividing by 8) or equivalently using **gs = 2 with the full sphere**. Let me reconcile:

For a cube of side `L`, the integer quantum numbers `nx, ny, nz ∈ {1, 2, 3, ...}` span the **positive octant** of k-space. So:

- Number of states per unit k-space volume in the positive octant: `1/(π/L)³ = V/π³`
- The full sphere volume is `4πk² dk`. The positive octant is `1/8` of this: `(4πk²/8) dk = (πk²/2) dk`
- Total states (with spin gs = 2): `N(k) dk = 2 · (V/π³) · (πk²/2) dk = V k²/π² dk`

This gives the standard form. So:

$$N(E) dE = \frac{V k^2}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{dk}{dE} \, dE = \frac{V}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{2m^* E}{\hbar^2} \cdot \frac{1}{2\hbar} \sqrt{\frac{2m^*}{E}} \, dE$$

$$N(E) dE = \frac{V}{2\pi^2 \hbar^3} (2m^*)^{3/2} \sqrt{E} \, dE$$

### Step 5: Final DOS Expression

$$\boxed{N(E) dE = \frac{V}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E} \, dE}$$

The **DOS per unit volume** is therefore:

$$\boxed{g(E) = \frac{N(E)}{V} = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Check**: Substituting `ℏ = h/(2π)`, this reduces to the Neamen textbook form: `g(E) = (4\pi(2m*)^{3/2}/h³) √E`. Verify: `(1/2π²)·(2m*/ℏ²)^(3/2) = (1/2π²)·(2m*)³/² / (h³/(2π)³) = (1/2π²)·(8π³(2m*)³/²)/h³ = 4π(2m*)³/²/h³`. ✓

## 3.2 Full Derivation of `Nc` — Effective DOS in Conduction Band

### Step 1: Carrier Concentration Integral

The total electron concentration in the conduction band is the integral of the **occupied DOS**:

$$n_0 = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g_c(E) \cdot f_F(E) \, dE$$

where the Fermi-Dirac function is:

$$f_F(E) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(E - E_F)/kT}}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Boltzmann Approximation

For `E - EF ≫ kT` (i.e., the non-degenerate semiconductor condition, `Ec - EF > 3kT`):

$$f_F(E) \approx e^{-(E - E_F)/kT}$$

This is the **MOST IMPORTANT** approximation in semiconductor statistics.

### Step 3: Substitute the DOS

$$n_0 = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2 m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_c} \cdot e^{-(E - E_F)/kT} \, dE$$

### Step 4: Substitution to Simplify

Let `u = E - Ec`, so `du = dE`. Lower limit: `u = 0`. Upper limit: `u = ∞`. The exponential becomes `e^(-(u + Ec - EF)/kT) = e^((EF - Ec)/kT) · e^(-u/kT)`.

$$n_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2 m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} e^{(E_F - E_c)/kT} \int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{u} \cdot e^{-u/kT} \, du$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the Standard Integral

The integral is a Gamma function form. Recall the general result:

$$\int_0^{\infty} u^{n} e^{-u/\alpha} du = n! \cdot \alpha^{n+1}$$

For `n = 1/2` and `α = kT`:

$$\int_0^{\infty} \sqrt{u} \cdot e^{-u/kT} du = \Gamma(3/2) \cdot (kT)^{3/2} = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} (kT)^{3/2}$$

*(Using `Γ(3/2) = √π/2`.)*

### Step 6: Assemble and Factor

$$n_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2 m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} (kT)^{3/2} \cdot e^{(E_F - E_c)/kT}$$

Using `ℏ = h/(2π)`, so `ℏ³ = h³/(8π³)`. Substituting:

$$n_0 = \frac{(2 m_n^*)^{3/2}}{2\pi^2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot \frac{8\pi^3 (kT)^{3/2}}{h^3} \cdot e^{(E_F - E_c)/kT}$$

$$n_0 = 2 \left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} \cdot e^{(E_F - E_c)/kT}$$

### Step 7: Final Result

$$\boxed{N_c = 2 \left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}}$$

$$\boxed{n_0 = N_c \cdot e^{(E_F - E_c)/kT} = N_c \cdot e^{-(E_c - E_F)/kT}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams**: The factor `2` in front of `Nc` always comes from **spin degeneracy** `(2 spin states per orbital)`. If you forget it, you'll be off by a factor of 2 in the answer!

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Typical KTU-Style Problem

**Problem**: For Silicon at **T = 300 K**, the effective mass of electrons is `mn* = 1.08 m₀` and the bandgap is `Eg = 1.12 eV`. Calculate `Nc` and `nv` assuming `mp* = 0.56 m₀`. (Use `m₀ = 9.1 × 10⁻³¹ kg`, `h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s`, `k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K`.)

### Solution

**Step A: Compute the prefactor for Nc.**

$$\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2} = \frac{2\pi \times (1.08 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31}) \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2}$$

Numerator:
$$2\pi \times 1.08 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300$$
$$= 2\pi \times 1.08 \times 9.1 \times 1.38 \times 300 \times 10^{-54}$$
$$= 2\pi \times 4064.3 \times 10^{-54} = 25541 \times 10^{-54} = 2.554 \times 10^{-50}$$

Denominator:
$$(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2 = 43.90 \times 10^{-68} = 4.390 \times 10^{-67}$$

Ratio:
$$\frac{2.554 \times 10^{-50}}{4.390 \times 10^{-67}} = 5.817 \times 10^{16} \, \text{m}^{-2}$$

**Step B: Raise to the 3/2 power.**

$$(5.817 \times 10^{16})^{3/2} = (5.817)^{3/2} \times 10^{24}$$

Computing `(5.817)^(3/2)`:
- `√5.817 = 2.412`
- `5.817 × 2.412 = 14.03`

So: `(5.817 × 10¹⁶)^(3/2) = 14.03 × 10²⁴ = 1.403 × 10²⁵ m⁻³`

**Step C: Multiply by 2.**

$$N_c = 2 \times 1.403 \times 10^{25} = 2.806 \times 10^{25} \, \text{m}^{-3} = 2.806 \times 10^{19} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$$

**Step D: Compute Nv with `mp* = 0.56 m₀`.**

Since `Nv/Nc = (mp*/mn*)^(3/2) = (0.56/1.08)^(3/2)`:
- `0.56/1.08 = 0.5185`
- `(0.5185)^(3/2) = 0.5185 × √0.5185 = 0.5185 × 0.7201 = 0.3734`
- `Nv = 2.806 × 10¹⁹ × 0.3734 = 1.048 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³`

**Final Answer**:
- **`Nc ≈ 2.8 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³`** ✓ (Matches standard KTU textbook value)
- **`Nv ≈ 1.04 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³`** ✓
