KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023] Define the term "built-in potential" of a p-n junction. Write the expression for $V_{bi}$ in terms of doping concentrations.** **Model Answer:** The built-in potential $V_{bi}$ is the internal electrostatic potential difference that develops across a p-n junction under open-circuit, thermal equilibrium conditions. It arises from the separation of charge due to carrier diffusion across the junction and is the potential barrier that must be overcome for current to flow. $$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$ It cannot be measured directly by a voltmeter because the contact potentials at the metal probes cancel it out. For silicon at 300 K with typical doping of $10^{15}$–$10^{17}$ cm⁻³, $V_{bi}$ ranges from 0.6 V to 0.9 V. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] Sketch the charge density, electric field, and potential distributions across an abrupt p-n junction in equilibrium. Label all critical points.** **Model Answer:** The three distributions for an abrupt (step) p-n junction are: 1. **Charge Density $\rho(x)$:** A rectangular (boxcar) function: $-qN_A$ for $0 \le x \le x_p$, $+qN_D$ for $-x_n \le x \le 0$, and zero outside the depletion region. 2. **Electric Field $\mathcal{E}(x)$:** A triangular function: linearly rising from 0 at $x = -x_n$ to a maximum $\mathcal{E}_{max}$ at $x = 0$, then linearly falling to 0 at $x = +x_p$. The field points from n to p. 3. **Potential $V(x)$:** A quadratic (parabolic) function: starts at 0 at $x = -x_n$, increases quadratically through the junction, and reaches $V_{bi}$ at $x = +x_p$. Critical points: depletion edges at $x = -x_n$ and $x = +x_p$, peak field at $x = 0$, total potential drop = $V_{bi}$. **(3 marks)** --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the electric field distribution across an abrupt p-n junction in equilibrium. State clearly the boundary conditions used. **(b) [7 Marks]** For a silicon p-n junction at 300 K with $N_A = 5 \times 10^{16}$ cm⁻³, $N_D = 10^{15}$ cm⁻³, and $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, calculate the built-in potential, total depletion width, and the maximum electric field. Take $\epsilon_s = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm. **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Electric Field Distribution [7 Marks]:** Starting with Poisson's equation in 1-D: $$\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\epsilon_s}$$ Equivalently, $\dfrac{d\mathcal{E}}{dx} = \dfrac{\rho(x)}{\epsilon_s}$. **Region 1: n-side, $-x_n \le x \le 0$, $\rho = +qN_D$:** $$\frac{d\mathcal{E}}{dx} = \frac{qN_D}{\epsilon_s}$$ Integrating and applying BC $\mathcal{E}(-x_n) = 0$: $$\mathcal{E}(x) = \frac{qN_D}{\epsilon_s}(x + x_n)$$ **Region 2: p-side, $0 \le x \le x_p$, $\rho = -qN_A$:** $$\frac{d\mathcal{E}}{dx} = -\frac{qN_A}{\epsilon_s}$$ Integrating and applying BC $\mathcal{E}(x_p) = 0$: $$\mathcal{E}(x) = \frac{qN_A}{\epsilon_s}(x_p - x)$$ At $x = 0$, continuity gives $\mathcal{E}_{max} = \dfrac{qN_D x_n}{\epsilon_s} = \dfrac{qN_A x_p}{\epsilon_s}$, leading to the charge neutrality condition $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$. **(7 marks — full marks with diagram and BCs)** **(b) Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]:** **Step 1: Built-in potential.** $$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = 0.02585 \text{ V at 300 K}$$ $$V_{bi} = 0.02585 \times \ln\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{16} \times 10^{15}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right) = 0.02585 \times \ln(2.22 \times 10^{11})$$ $$V_{bi} = 0.02585 \times 26.13 = 0.6755 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Total depletion width.** $$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.6755}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}\left(\frac{1}{5 \times 10^{16}} + \frac{1}{10^{15}}\right)}$$ $$W = \sqrt{1.377 \times 10^{7} \times (0.02 \times 10^{-15} + 1.0 \times 10^{-15})}$$ $$W = \sqrt{1.377 \times 10^{7} \times 1.02 \times 10^{-15}} = \sqrt{1.404 \times 10^{-8}}$$ $$W \approx 1.185 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 1.185 \text{ μm}$$ **Step 3: Maximum electric field.** Using $x_n \approx W \times \dfrac{N_A}{N_A + N_D} = 1.185 \times 10^{-4} \times \dfrac{5 \times 10^{16}}{5.1 \times 10^{16}} = 1.162 \times 10^{-4}$ cm: $$\mathcal{E}_{max} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{15} \times 1.162 \times 10^{-4}}{1.04 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.787 \times 10^4 \text{ V/cm}$$ **Final Answers:** $V_{bi} \approx 0.676$ V, $W \approx 1.19$ μm, $\mathcal{E}_{max} \approx 1.79 \times 10^4$ V/cm. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the total depletion width $W$ of an abrupt p-n junction in terms of built-in potential and doping concentrations. **(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon p⁺n junction has $N_A = 10^{18}$ cm⁻³ and $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³. Calculate the depletion width extending into the n-side and the junction capacitance per unit area at zero bias. Given: $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, $\epsilon_s = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm, $T = 300$ K. **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Total Depletion Width [7 Marks]:** The potential at the n-edge ($x = -x_n$) is zero, and at the p-edge ($x = +x_p$) is $V_{bi}$. Integrating the electric field: $$V_{bi} = \int_{-x_n}^{+x_p} (-\mathcal{E})\, dx$$ Using the triangular field profile, the area of the triangle is: $$V_{bi} = \frac{1}{2} \mathcal{E}_{max} \cdot W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{qN_D x_n}{\epsilon_s} \cdot (x_n + x_p)$$ Using $N_A x_p = N_D x_n \Rightarrow x_p = \dfrac{N_D}{N_A} x_n$: $$V_{bi} = \frac{qN_D x_n^2}{2\epsilon_s}\left(1 + \frac{N_D}{N_A}\right) = \frac{q x_n^2}{2\epsilon_s}(N_A + N_D)$$ Solving for $x_n$ and using $x_p$ similarly: $$x_n = \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D} \cdot \frac{N_A}{N_A + N_D}}, \quad x_p = \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_A} \cdot \frac{N_D}{N_A + N_D}}$$ Adding: $$W = \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$ **(7 marks — include substitution steps and final form)** **(b) Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]:** **Step 1: Built-in potential.** $$V_{bi} = 0.02585 \times \ln\left(\frac{10^{18} \times 10^{16}}{2.25 \times 10^{20}}\right) = 0.02585 \times \ln(4.44 \times 10^{13})$$ $$V_{bi} = 0.02585 \times 31.73 = 0.8202 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Depletion width on n-side (p⁺n junction).** Since $N_A \gg N_D$, the depletion region is almost entirely in the n-side: $$x_n \approx W = \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D} \cdot \frac{N_A}{N_A + N_D}}$$ $$\approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.8202}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{16}} \times \frac{10^{18}}{1.01 \times 10^{18}}}$$ $$= \sqrt{1.066 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.99} = \sqrt{1.055 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.027 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}$$ $$x_n \approx 1.03 \text{ μm}$$ **Step 3: Junction capacitance per unit area.** $$C_j/A = \frac{\epsilon_s}{W} = \frac{1.04 \times 10^{-12}}{1.027 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.013 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F/cm}^2$$ $$= 10.13 \text{ nF/cm}^2 = 101.3 \text{ pF/mm}^2$$ **Final Answers:** $x_n \approx 1.03$ μm, $C_j/A \approx 10.13$ nF/cm². **(7 marks)** --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks — KTU 2024 Pattern:** > > 1. **Wrong Thermal Voltage:** Using $V_T = 26$ mV (approximate) is acceptable, but writing $V_T = 25$ mV (without justification) is penalized in numericals requiring 4-significant-figure accuracy. Always state $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K. > > 2. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** When given $N_A, N_D$ in cm⁻³, $\epsilon_s$ in F/cm, and $q$ in C, the formula yields $W$ in **cm**, not μm. Failing to convert loses the answer mark. Always convert to μm at the end: $1 \text{ cm} = 10^4$ μm. > > 3. **Forgetting the Built-in Potential in Applied Bias:** The general formula uses $V_{bi} - V$ where $V$ is the applied voltage (positive for forward, negative for reverse). For equilibrium, $V = 0$ and only $V_{bi}$ appears. > > 4. **Confusing $x_p$ and $x_n$:** Students often write $x_n = W \cdot \dfrac{N_A}{N_A + N_D}$ — this is **wrong**. The correct relation is $x_n = W \cdot \dfrac{N_A}{N_A + N_D}$ only when $W$ is computed from the full expression, but the depletion width extends **more into the lighter side**. Always double-check using $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$. > > 5. **Skipping Boundary Conditions:** Examiners specifically look for stating the boundary conditions (e.g., $\mathcal{E} = 0$ at depletion edges, $V = 0$ at the reference edge). Omitting these costs 1–2 marks per derivation question. > > 6. **Misuse of $n_i$ in the formula:** For silicon at 300 K, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³. For germanium, $n_i = 2.4 \times 10^{13}$ cm⁻³. Using the wrong value yields incorrect $V_{bi}$. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Three Pillars of Junction Physics:** Charge density (boxcar), Electric field (triangle), Potential (parabola). All three are mathematically related through Poisson's equation. - **Built-in Potential $V_{bi}$:** $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$. Increases with doping, decreases with temperature. Not directly measurable with a voltmeter. - **Charge Neutrality:** $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$. The depletion region extends into the **lightly doped** side more than the heavily doped side. - **Depletion Width Formula:** $W = \sqrt{(2\epsilon_s V_{bi}/q)(1/N_A + 1/N_D)}$. For one-sided junctions, simplify using the lighter doping. - **Maximum Field:** Occurs at the metallurgical junction ($x = 0$): $\mathcal{E}_{max} = qN_D x_n / \epsilon_s$. - **Boundary Conditions (MUST state in derivations):** - $\mathcal{E} = 0$ at $x = -x_n$ and $x = +x_p$. - $V(-x_n) = 0$, $V(x_p) = V_{bi}$. - $\mathcal{E}$ and $V$ are continuous at $x = 0$. - **Step vs. Linearly Graded:** KTU problems assume **step (abrupt)** junctions unless stated otherwise. Linearly graded junctions have a different charge profile (triangular, not boxcar). - **Depletion Approximation Validity:** The depletion approximation (mobile charges neglected in SCR) holds for reverse bias and low forward bias (typically $V < 0.5$ V). At high forward bias, the approximation breaks down. - **Permittivity Values to Remember:** Si: $\epsilon_s = 11.7 \epsilon_0$; Ge: $\epsilon_s = 16 \epsilon_0$; GaAs: $\epsilon_s = 13.1 \epsilon_0$. - **Junction Capacitance:** $C_j = \epsilon_s A / W$ — the depletion region acts like a parallel-plate capacitor. This is the operating principle of **varactor diodes**.

topics.title: Biasing and Energy Band Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Biasing and Energy Band Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Biasing of a P-N Junction?

**Biasing** is the controlled application of an external DC voltage across a P-N junction diode to manipulate its energy band structure and, consequently, its conduction behavior. The external voltage shifts the relative position of the conduction band ($E_C$), valence band ($E_V$), and the Fermi level ($E_F$) on the N-side with respect to the P-side, thereby either encouraging or suppressing carrier flow across the junction.

In the **2024 KTU syllabus**, the term "biasing" specifically refers to the three canonical operating conditions of a P-N junction:

| Bias Type | External Polarity | Depletion Region | Current Flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zero / Equilibrium** | None | Built-in width $W_0$ | Net current = **0 A** |
| **Forward Bias (FB)** | P to +ve, N to −ve | Narrows ($W < W_0$) | Large current flows |
| **Reverse Bias (RB)** | P to −ve, N to +ve | Widens ($W > W_0$) | Negligible saturation current |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2, you are expected to draw and interpret the **Energy Band Diagrams (EBDs)** — plots of $E_C$, $E_F$, and $E_V$ versus position $x$ — for all three bias conditions. This is a guaranteed 7-mark sub-question in the End Semester Exam (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a P-N junction as a **water reservoir with a one-way valve (a check valve)**:
- The **depletion region** is the **closed valve** — it has no free carriers and acts as a barrier.
- The **built-in potential ($V_{bi} \approx 0.7$ V for Si)** is the **natural spring tension** holding the valve shut. This barrier exists even without any battery.
- **Forward bias** is like **pushing water in the correct direction** — your applied pressure overcomes the spring and forces the valve open. Electrons and holes flood across the junction → current flows.
- **Reverse bias** is like **pushing water against the closed valve** — the spring compresses tighter, the valve shuts harder, the barrier widens. Only a tiny trickle (saturation current $I_0$) leaks through.

The **Energy Band Diagram** is essentially a **topographical map of this "electrical hill"** that carriers must climb. Biasing simply changes the **height and slope** of this hill.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants (Must Memorize)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Thermal Voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx$ **$0.0259$ V $\approx 26$ mV**
> - **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
> - **Electronic charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
> - **Intrinsic carrier concentration (Si at 300 K):** $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ **cm$^{-3}$**
> - **Built-in potential (typical Si junction):** $V_{bi} \approx$ **0.6 – 0.9 V**

---

## 1.4 What is an Energy Band Diagram?

An **Energy Band Diagram (EBD)** is a 2D plot with:
- **X-axis:** Spatial position $x$ across the P-N junction (in cm or $\mu$m).
- **Y-axis:** Electron energy $E$ (in eV).

It depicts the spatial variation of three critical energy levels:
1. **$E_C(x)$** — Bottom of the conduction band.
2. **E_F** — Fermi level (a single constant line in equilibrium; splits into quasi-Fermi levels under bias).
3. **E_V(x)** — Top of the valence band.

The vertical separation $(E_C - E_V) = E_g$ (the **bandgap**, **1.12 eV** for Si) is preserved across the entire structure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Case 1 — Zero Bias (Thermal Equilibrium)

When no external voltage is applied, the junction is in **thermal equilibrium**.

### Key Theoretical Points:
- A single, flat **Fermi level** spans the entire device (constant energy reference).
- The bands on the N-side are **lower** in energy than the P-side because the work function of N-type Si is smaller.
- A **built-in potential barrier** $V_{bi}$ exists across the depletion region:

$$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

- The depletion width is:

$$W_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$

- The net current is **zero** because the **drift current** (due to built-in field) exactly cancels the **diffusion current** (due to concentration gradients).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the band bend?** At the junction, electrons diffuse from N → P (downhill in concentration), and holes diffuse from P → N. This leaves behind ionized donors (+ve) on the N-side and ionized acceptors (−ve) on the P-side, creating a **space-charge region**. The resulting electric field tilts the bands — the P-side bands shift up, N-side bands shift down — until equilibrium is reached.

---

## 2.2 Case 2 — Forward Bias

The positive terminal of the battery is connected to the **P-side** and the negative to the **N-side**.

### Theoretical Analysis:
- The applied voltage $V_F$ **opposes** the built-in potential, effectively reducing the barrier from $V_{bi}$ to $(V_{bi} - V_F)$.
- **Depletion width shrinks:**

$$W_{FB} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_F)}{q}\left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$

- The **Fermi level splits** into two **quasi-Fermi levels**: $E_{FP}$ on the P-side and $E_{FN}$ on the N-side, separated by $qV_F$:

$$E_{FP} - E_{FN} = q V_F$$

- The N-side quasi-Fermi level is **higher** than the P-side quasi-Fermi level (when electrons fall from N → P, they release energy).
- Minority carrier **injection** occurs: electrons injected into P-side, holes injected into N-side.
- Current follows the **Shockley diode equation**:

$$I = I_0 \left(e^{V_F / V_T} - 1\right) \approx I_0 \, e^{V_F / V_T} \quad (\text{for } V_F \gg V_T)$$

---

## 2.3 Case 3 — Reverse Bias

The positive terminal is connected to the **N-side** and the negative to the **P-side**.

### Theoretical Analysis:
- The applied voltage $V_R$ **adds** to the built-in potential, increasing the barrier to $(V_{bi} + V_R)$.
- **Depletion width widens:**

$$W_{RB} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q}\left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$

- Quasi-Fermi levels still split, but now $E_{FP}$ is **above** $E_{FN}$ by $qV_R$.
- Minority carrier **extraction** (sweeping out) occurs — the small reverse saturation current $I_0$ flows.
- The current is bounded and approximately:

$$I \approx -I_0 \quad (\text{for } V_R \gg V_T)$$

- The junction acts as a **voltage-controlled capacitor** (varactor) with capacitance:

$$C_j = \frac{\epsilon_s A}{W} = A \sqrt{\frac{q \epsilon_s}{2(V_{bi} + V_R)} \cdot \frac{N_A N_D}{N_A + N_D}}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 0.0259$ V | Thermal voltage at 300 K | V |
| 2 | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$ | Built-in potential | V |
| 3 | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \epsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q}\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)}$ | Depletion width under bias $V$ (use $V = +V_F$ for FB, $V = -V_R$ for RB) | m |
| 4 | $E_{max} = \dfrac{2(V_{bi} - V)}{W}$ | Peak electric field at junction | V/m |
| 5 | $C_j = \dfrac{\epsilon_s A}{W}$ | Junction capacitance | F |
| 6 | $I = I_0\left(e^{V/V_T} - 1\right)$ | Shockley diode equation | A |
| 7 | $I_0 = qA\left(\dfrac{D_p n_i^2}{L_p N_D} + \dfrac{D_n n_i^2}{L_n N_A}\right)$ | Reverse saturation current | A |
| 8 | $E_{FP} - E_{FN} = qV$ | Quasi-Fermi level splitting | eV |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TRICK:** In the depletion width formula, the sign convention is universal — **subtract** $V$ for forward bias and **add** $V$ for reverse bias. KTU examiners often test this with a one-sided junction (e.g., $N_A \gg N_D$, so $\frac{1}{N_A} \approx 0$) — be ready to simplify.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar Cells (Photovoltaics):** Operate near forward bias in the **fourth quadrant** of the I-V curve.
- **Rectifiers:** Use forward bias to convert AC → DC in power supplies.
- **Zener / Avalanche Diodes:** Exploit heavy reverse bias to enter breakdown.
- **Varactor Diodes:** Use the voltage-dependent depletion capacitance in RF tuning circuits.
- **LEDs and Laser Diodes:** High forward bias causes radiative recombination across the bandgap.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Built-in Potential $V_{bi}$

We start from the law of mass action and the position of the Fermi level relative to the intrinsic level $E_i$.

**Step 1 — Carrier concentrations in terms of Fermi level:**
The electron and hole concentrations at any point are given by:

$$n = n_i \, \exp\left(\frac{E_F - E_i}{kT}\right)$$

$$p = n_i \, \exp\left(\frac{E_i - E_F}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply in the neutral N-region (far from junction):**
Deep in the N-side, $n \approx N_D$ (donor concentration) and $E_F$ is close to $E_C$:

$$N_D = n_i \, \exp\left(\frac{E_{F,N} - E_{i,N}}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply in the neutral P-region:**
Deep in the P-side, $p \approx N_A$ and $E_F$ is close to $E_V$:

$$N_A = n_i \, \exp\left(\frac{E_{i,P} - E_{F,P}}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 4 — In equilibrium, $E_{F,N} = E_{F,P} = E_F$ (single Fermi level).**
Subtracting the two equations to find the difference in intrinsic levels:

$$\frac{E_{i,P} - E_{i,N}}{kT} = \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Relate intrinsic level shift to potential:**
The intrinsic level shift across the depletion region is exactly $q V_{bi}$ (charge $\times$ built-in potential):

$$q V_{bi} = E_{i,P} - E_{i,N}$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{V_{bi} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right) = V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Depletion Width $W$

**Step 1 — Poisson's equation in the depletion region:**
Assume a step junction with ionized donors on the N-side and ionized acceptors on the P-side.

In the depletion region ($-x_p \le x \le 0$ on P-side, $0 \le x \le x_n$ on N-side):

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\epsilon_s} = -\frac{q N_A}{\epsilon_s} \quad (x \in [-x_p, 0])$$

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = \frac{q N_D}{\epsilon_s} \quad (x \in [0, x_n])$$

**Step 2 — Integrate once to get the electric field $E(x) = -dV/dx$:**

$$E(x) = \frac{q N_A}{\epsilon_s}(x + x_p) \quad (x \in [-x_p, 0])$$

$$E(x) = \frac{q N_D}{\epsilon_s}(x_n - x) \quad (x \in [0, x_n])$$

**Step 3 — Charge neutrality condition:**
Total positive charge on N-side must equal total negative charge on P-side:

$$q N_D x_n = q N_A x_p \implies N_D x_n = N_A x_p$$

**Step 4 — Integrate again to get potential $V(x)$:**
Applying boundary conditions $V(-x_p) = 0$ and $V(x_n) = V_{bi} - V$ (where $V$ is applied bias):

$$V_{bi} - V = \frac{q}{2\epsilon_s}(N_A x_p^2 + N_D x_n^2)$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of total depletion width $W = x_n + x_p$:**
Using $x_n = W N_A / (N_A + N_D)$ and $x_p = W N_D / (N_A + N_D)$:

$$\boxed{W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem [KTU 2024 Model]:** A silicon P-N junction at 300 K has $N_A = 10^{18}$ cm$^{-3}$ and $N_D = 10^{15}$ cm$^{-3}$. Given $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$ and $\epsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm. Calculate $V_{bi}$ and the depletion width at equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Compute $V_{bi}$:**

$$V_{bi} = 0.0259 \times \ln\left(\frac{10^{18} \times 10^{15}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right)$$

$$= 0.0259 \times \ln\left(\frac{10^{33}}{2.25 \times 10^{20}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(4.44 \times 10^{12})$$

$$= 0.0259 \times 29.12 = 0.754 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $W_0$ (one-sided approximation: $N_A \gg N_D$):**
Since $N_A \gg N_D$, the depletion region lies almost entirely in the N-side:

$$W_0 \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.035 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.754}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{15}}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{1.56 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{9.75 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 9.87 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} = 0.987 \text{ μm}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the exact formula gives the same result (within rounding):**

$$W_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.035 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.754}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \left(\frac{1}{10^{18}} + \frac{1}{10^{15}}\right)}$$

$$= \sqrt{9.75 \times 10^{-9} \times (10^{-18} + 10^{-15})} = \sqrt{9.75 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.001 \times 10^{-15}}$$

$$= \sqrt{9.76 \times 10^{-24}} = 9.88 \times 10^{-12} \text{ cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Wait — correction!** The units of $\epsilon_s$ are F/cm = C/(V·cm). When using cm-based inputs, $W$ comes out in cm. The result $W_0 \approx 0.987$ μm is the **correct physical value** for a $10^{15}$ cm$^{-3}$ doping level.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture: Energy Band Diagram Across a P-N Junction

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of band structures under different bias conditions. Since physical EBDs are 2D plots, this block representation maps the key energy levels and their relative shifts.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Zero["ZERO BIAS - Thermal Equilibrium"]
        Z1["E_C on P-side: HIGH"]
        Z2["E_F: FLAT single line across junction"]
        Z3["E_C on N-side: LOW"]
        Z4["Built-in barrier V_bi present"]
        Z5["W_0: Depletion width at equilibrium"]
        Z1 --> Z2 --> Z3
        Z3 --> Z4
        Z4 --> Z5
    end

    subgraph Forward["FORWARD BIAS - P to plus, N to minus"]
        F1["E_C on P-side: SHIFTS DOWN"]
        F2["E_FN and E_FP SPLIT by qV_F"]
        F3["E_C on N-side: SHIFTS UP"]
        F4["Barrier reduced to V_bi - V_F"]
        F5["Depletion width NARROWS: W less than W_0"]
        F6["LARGE current flows: I equals I_0 exp of V_F by V_T"]
        F1 --> F2 --> F3
        F3 --> F4
        F4 --> F5
        F5 --> F6
    end

    subgraph Reverse["REVERSE BIAS - P to minus, N to plus"]
        R1["E_C on P-side: SHIFTS UP"]
        R2["E_FP above E_FN by qV_R"]
        R3["E_C on N-side: SHIFTS DOWN"]
        R4["Barrier increased to V_bi plus V_R"]
        R5["Depletion width WIDENS: W greater than W_0"]
        R6["TINY saturation current I_0 flows"]
        R1 --> R2 --> R3
        R3 --> R4
        R4 --> R5
        R5 --> R6
    end

    Zero --> Forward
    Zero --> Reverse
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic Representation: Energy Levels & Their Shifts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Pside["P-SIDE: p-type region"]
        PEC["E_C_P - Conduction band edge on P-side"]
        PEF["E_FP - Quasi-Fermi level on P-side"]
        PEV["E_V_P - Valence band edge on P-side"]
        PEC --- PEF
        PEF --- PEV
    end

    subgraph Jx["JUNCTION: Depletion region"]
        JBD["Built-in field tilts bands"]
        JBD --> BARRIER["Barrier height equals q times V_bi minus V"]
    end

    subgraph Nside["N-SIDE: n-type region"]
        NEC["E_C_N - Conduction band edge on N-side"]
        NEF["E_FN - Quasi-Fermi level on N-side"]
        NEV["E_V_N - Valence band edge on N-side"]
        NEC --- NEF
        NEF --- NEV
    end

    Pside --> Jx
    Jx --> Nside
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Bias Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Apply External Voltage V to P-N Junction"] --> B{Polarity Check}
    B -- P connected to plus, N to minus --> C["FORWARD BIAS"]
    B -- P connected to minus, N to plus --> D["REVERSE BIAS"]
    C --> E["Barrier: V_bi minus V_F"]
    E --> F["W decreases"]
    F --> G["I large, exponential with V_F"]
    D --> H["Barrier: V_bi plus V_R"]
    H --> I["W increases"]
    I --> J["I equals negative I_0, saturation"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawing Tip for KTU Exams:** When drawing the EBD, always show:
> 1. A **continuous $E_g$ bandgap** (the gap between $E_C$ and $E_V$ is constant).
> 2. The **bending only inside the depletion region** (flat bands in the neutral regions).
> 3. The **relative position of $E_F$** — closer to $E_V$ on P-side, closer to $E_C$ on N-side.
> 4. Under bias, **label the quasi-Fermi levels** $E_{FP}$ and $E_{FN}$ separately.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define built-in potential in a P-N junction. Write its expression.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **built-in potential** ($V_{bi}$) is the internal potential barrier that exists across a P-N junction at thermal equilibrium, caused by the space-charge region formed due to carrier diffusion.

**Expression:**

$$V_{bi} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right) = V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

For Si at 300 K, $V_{bi} \approx 0.7$ V. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for typical value/units.)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Differentiate between forward bias and reverse bias in terms of depletion width and barrier potential.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Forward Bias | Reverse Bias |
|---|---|---|
| Barrier potential | $V_{bi} - V_F$ (decreases) | $V_{bi} + V_R$ (increases) |
| Depletion width | Decreases ($W < W_0$) | Increases ($W > W_0$) |
| Current | Large, exponential in $V_F$ | Small, saturates at $I_0$ |
| Quasi-Fermi splitting | $E_{FN} > E_{FP}$ by $qV_F$ | $E_{FP} > E_{FN}$ by $qV_R$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU 2024 Model ESE, Module 2]**

#### (a) With the help of a neat energy band diagram, explain the behavior of a P-N junction under (i) zero bias, (ii) forward bias, and (iii) reverse bias. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Zero Bias (Equilibrium) — 2 marks:**
- At thermal equilibrium, a **single Fermi level** $E_F$ exists across the entire device.
- The conduction band $E_C$ and valence band $E_V$ **bend** in the depletion region.
- The P-side bands are at a **higher energy** than the N-side bands by $qV_{bi}$.
- Depletion width is $W_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$.
- Net current is zero; drift current cancels diffusion current.

**(ii) Forward Bias — 3 marks:**
- Positive terminal of battery → P-side, negative → N-side.
- Applied voltage $V_F$ **opposes** built-in potential.
- Effective barrier reduces to $(V_{bi} - V_F)$.
- Fermi level **splits** into $E_{FP}$ (P-side) and $E_{FN}$ (N-side) with $E_{FN} - E_{FP} = qV_F$.
- Depletion region **narrows**; majority carriers are **injected** across the junction.
- Current is large: $I = I_0 (e^{V_F/V_T} - 1) \approx I_0 e^{V_F/V_T}$.

**(iii) Reverse Bias — 2 marks:**
- Positive terminal of battery → N-side, negative → P-side.
- Applied voltage $V_R$ **adds** to built-in potential.
- Effective barrier increases to $(V_{bi} + V_R)$.
- Quasi-Fermi levels split with $E_{FP} - E_{FN} = qV_R$.
- Depletion region **widens**; minority carriers are **extracted**.
- Only a tiny reverse saturation current $I_0$ flows.

**Diagram Required:** Three side-by-side EBDs showing the conduction band, Fermi level (or quasi-Fermi levels), and valence band for each case. (Award **1 mark** for each correctly drawn diagram; students often lose marks by drawing the bandgap with varying width — **it must be constant**.)

---

#### (b) Derive the expression for the depletion width $W$ of an abrupt P-N junction under an applied bias $V$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write Poisson's equation in the depletion region. Assume complete ionization and step junction profile:

$$\frac{d^2 V(x)}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\epsilon_s}$$

For $-x_p \le x \le 0$: $\rho = -qN_A$; for $0 \le x \le x_n$: $\rho = +qN_D$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Integrate once to find electric field, with boundary conditions $E(-x_p) = 0$ and $E(x_n) = 0$:

$$E(x) = \frac{qN_A}{\epsilon_s}(x + x_p), \quad E(x) = \frac{qN_D}{\epsilon_s}(x_n - x)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply charge neutrality: $N_A x_p = N_D x_n$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Integrate again and apply the boundary condition that the total potential drop across the junction is $(V_{bi} - V)$:

$$V_{bi} - V = \frac{q}{2\epsilon_s}\left(N_A x_p^2 + N_D x_n^2\right)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve using $W = x_n + x_p$ and the neutrality condition:

$$\boxed{W = \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Sign Error:** Students often write $V_{bi} + V$ for forward bias — **WRONG**. Forward bias always uses **$-V$** in the formula.
> 2. **Units Mismatch:** Mixing cm and μm — KTU strictly expects SI base units. $\epsilon_s$ for Si = $11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
> 3. **One-Sided Approximation:** If $N_A \gg N_D$, the term $\frac{1}{N_A} \approx 0$ — students often forget to mention this approximation.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice**

#### (a) Explain the concept of quasi-Fermi levels. Show that under forward bias, the splitting of quasi-Fermi levels equals $qV_F$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (3 marks):**
Under non-equilibrium conditions (when external bias is applied), the carrier concentrations deviate from their equilibrium values, and the single Fermi level is no longer valid. We define **quasi-Fermi levels** $E_{FN}$ (for electrons) and $E_{FP}$ (for holes) such that:

$$n = n_i \exp\left(\frac{E_{FN} - E_i}{kT}\right), \quad p = n_i \exp\left(\frac{E_i - E_{FP}}{kT}\right)$$

**Derivation (4 marks):**
In the neutral N-region, $n \approx N_D$ (essentially unchanged). In the neutral P-region, $p \approx N_A$ (essentially unchanged). The deviation occurs in the quasi-neutral regions near the junction edges.

In the N-side neutral region (using electron quasi-Fermi level):

$$N_D = n_i \exp\left(\frac{E_{FN} - E_{i,N}}{kT}\right)$$

In the P-side neutral region (using hole quasi-Fermi level):

$$N_A = n_i \exp\left(\frac{E_{i,P} - E_{FP}}{kT}\right)$$

Multiplying these two equations:

$$N_A N_D = n_i^2 \exp\left(\frac{(E_{FN} - E_{i,N}) + (E_{i,P} - E_{FP})}{kT}\right)$$

But we also know from the equilibrium relation:

$$N_A N_D = n_i^2 \exp\left(\frac{E_{i,P} - E_{i,N}}{kT}\right) = n_i^2 \exp\left(\frac{qV_{bi}}{kT}\right)$$

Equating the exponents and rearranging:

$$E_{FP} - E_{FN} = E_{i,P} - E_{i,N} - qV_{bi} - (E_{i,P} - E_{i,N}) + qV_{bi} \cdot \text{(correction)}$$

**The cleaner approach (standard KTU method):**
Apply the **law of mass action** under non-equilibrium:

$$np = n_i^2 \exp\left(\frac{E_{FN} - E_{FP}}{kT}\right)$$

At the edges of the depletion region, in the P-side, $p \approx N_A$ and $n$ is the **injected minority carrier concentration**:

$$n_p = \frac{n_i^2}{N_A} \exp\left(\frac{qV_F}{kT}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$N_A \cdot \frac{n_i^2}{N_A} \exp\left(\frac{qV_F}{kT}\right) = n_i^2 \exp\left(\frac{E_{FN} - E_{FP}}{kT}\right)$$

$$\boxed{E_{FP} - E_{FN} = qV_F}$$

---

#### (b) A Si P-N junction has $N_A = 5 \times 10^{17}$ cm$^{-3}$ and $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$. Calculate $V_{bi}$, $W_0$, and the peak electric field $E_{max}$. Given $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$, $V_T = 0.0259$ V, $\epsilon_s = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Built-in potential (2 marks):**

$$V_{bi} = 0.0259 \times \ln\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{17} \times 10^{16}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right)$$

$$= 0.0259 \times \ln\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{33}}{2.25 \times 10^{20}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(2.22 \times 10^{13})$$

$$= 0.0259 \times 30.74 = 0.796 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Depletion width (3 marks):**

$$W_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.796}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \times \left(\frac{1}{5 \times 10^{17}} + \frac{1}{10^{16}}\right)}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{1.656 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \times (2 \times 10^{-18} + 10^{-16})}$$

$$= \sqrt{1.035 \times 10^{7} \times 1.02 \times 10^{-16}} = \sqrt{1.056 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$W_0 \approx 3.25 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} = 0.325 \text{ μm}$$

**Step 3 — Peak electric field (2 marks):**
Using the one-sided approximation ($N_A \gg N_D$), the peak field is:

$$E_{max} = \frac{2 V_{bi}}{W_0} = \frac{2 \times 0.796}{3.25 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 4.9 \times 10^{4} \text{ V/cm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $N_A, N_D$ are in cm$^{-3}$ — keep $\epsilon_s$ in F/cm. Mixing cm and m costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong logarithm argument:** Always $\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}$, **never** $\frac{N_A + N_D}{n_i}$.
> 3. **Not simplifying:** When $N_A \gg N_D$, you **must** state the one-sided approximation explicitly to earn full marks.
> 4. **No units in final answer:** KTU examiners deduct **half a mark** if units are missing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 2: Biasing & Energy Band Diagrams**

- **Equilibrium:** Single flat $E_F$; barrier $V_{bi}$; depletion width $W_0$.
- **Built-in Potential Formula:** $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$.
- **Forward Bias:** Barrier **decreases** to $(V_{bi} - V_F)$; $E_{FN} - E_{FP} = qV_F$; current is **exponential** in $V_F$.
- **Reverse Bias:** Barrier **increases** to $(V_{bi} + V_R)$; $E_{FP} - E_{FN} = qV_R$; current saturates at $-I_0$.
- **Depletion Width Formula:** $W = \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_s(V_{bi} - V)}{q} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$ — universal for all bias with proper sign of $V$.
- **One-Sided Junction Shortcut:** If $N_A \gg N_D$, depletion region lies almost entirely in N-side, and $W \approx \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_s(V_{bi} - V)}{qN_D}}$.
- **Constant Bandgap:** The vertical gap $E_C - E_V = E_g$ must be drawn as **constant** in the EBD.
- **Quasi-Fermi Levels:** Always label as $E_{FN}$ and $E_{FP}$ — never as a single $E_F$ under non-equilibrium.
- **Junction Capacitance:** $C_j = \epsilon_s A / W$ — useful for varactor diode problems.
- **Units Vigilance:** Si: $\epsilon_s = 11.7\epsilon_0 = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm or $1.04 \times 10^{-10}$ F/m.
- **Exam Drawing:** Always show: (1) flat bands in neutral regions, (2) bending in depletion region, (3) constant $E_g$, (4) labeled axes ($E$ in eV vs $x$ in μm).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ideal Diode Equation. Bipolar Junction Transistor - Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ideal Diode Equation. Bipolar Junction Transistor - Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Ideal Diode Equation (Shockley Diode Equation)

The **Ideal Diode Equation**, also known as the **Shockley Diode Equation**, is the fundamental mathematical model that describes the exact current-voltage (I-V) relationship across a p-n junction diode under forward and reverse bias conditions. It was formulated by William Shockley in 1949 and remains the cornerstone of semiconductor device analysis.

Mathematically, the ideal diode equation is expressed as:

$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{\frac{V_D}{nV_T}} - 1 \right)$$

Where:
- **$I_D$** = Diode current (Amperes, A)
- **$I_S$** = Reverse saturation current (Amperes, A), typically in the range of **$10^{-12}$ to $10^{-6}$ A**
- **$V_D$** = Voltage applied across the diode (Volts, V)
- **$V_T$** = Thermal voltage = $\frac{kT}{q}$ ≈ **25.85 mV at room temperature (300 K)**
- **$n$** = Ideality factor (also called emission coefficient), typically between **1 and 2**
- **$k$** = Boltzmann's constant = **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**
- **$q$** = Electron charge = **$1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C**
- **$T$** = Absolute temperature in Kelvin (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The ideal diode equation is the foundation for analyzing ALL semiconductor devices in this course, including BJTs, MOSFETs, and solar cells. Mastery of this equation is non-negotiable for KTU examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Check Valve" Understanding

Imagine a **water check valve** in a plumbing system:
- Water can flow in **only one direction** (forward bias) when sufficient pressure is applied.
- In the **reverse direction**, the valve is sealed shut — no water flows, no matter how hard you push.
- A real valve has a small "leak" (reverse saturation current), just as a real diode has a tiny leakage current.
- The "spring tension" inside the valve represents the **barrier potential** (built-in voltage) that must be overcome.

Similarly, an ideal diode equation tells us:
- **Forward bias ($V_D > 0$):** Current flows exponentially as voltage increases beyond the threshold.
- **Reverse bias ($V_D < 0$):** Only a tiny saturation current $I_S$ flows in the opposite direction.
- **Thermal voltage $V_T$** acts as the "sensitivity knob" — higher temperature means the diode conducts more easily.

### 1.3 The Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) — Working

A **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** is a three-terminal semiconductor device consisting of two p-n junctions formed by sandwiching either a thin p-type layer between two n-type layers (**NPN transistor**) or a thin n-type layer between two p-type layers (**PNP transistor**). The three terminals are named **Emitter (E)**, **Base (B)**, and **Collector (C)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A BJT is a current-controlled current device in which a small current flowing between the base and emitter terminals controls a larger current flowing between the collector and emitter terminals, enabling signal amplification and switching functions.

The word **"Bipolar"** signifies that **both electrons and holes** participate in the current conduction process, distinguishing BJTs from unipolar devices like FETs.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy: The BJT as a "Water Faucet"

Think of a BJT as a **faucet/tap system**:
- The **Base terminal** is like the **faucet handle** — a small twist (small base current) controls a large water flow.
- The **Collector terminal** is the **main water inlet** (high current path).
- The **Emitter terminal** is the **water outlet** (combined current exits here).
- Even a tiny adjustment at the handle (base) results in a large change in water flow (collector current). This is **amplification**!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus Point:** The BJT in the **active region** (forward-biased B-E junction, reverse-biased B-C junction) is the primary operating mode for amplifiers. The other regions (saturation and cutoff) are used for switching applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of the Ideal Diode Equation

The ideal diode equation emerges from the **semiconductor physics of the depletion region** in a p-n junction. Let's analyze the physics step by step:

1. **Built-in Potential ($V_{bi}$):** When p-type and n-type materials join, a depletion region forms with a built-in electric field. This creates a **barrier potential** ($V_{bi} \approx 0.7$ V for Si, **0.3 V for Ge**).

2. **Bias Application:** When an external voltage $V_D$ is applied:
   - **Forward bias** ($V_D > 0$): The barrier is reduced → majority carriers diffuse across the junction → exponential current increase.
   - **Reverse bias** ($V_D < 0$): The barrier is increased → only minority carriers cross → tiny saturation current $I_S$.

3. **Carrier Injection:** Under forward bias, the concentration of minority carriers at the junction edges increases exponentially with applied voltage, governed by the **Boltzmann relation**: $n = n_0 \cdot e^{V/V_T}$.

4. **Diffusion Current:** The injected minority carriers diffuse away from the junction, creating a diffusion current proportional to the carrier concentration gradient.

5. **Law of the Junction:** The boundary condition at the depletion region edges gives rise to the exponential term $e^{V_D/V_T}$.

### 2.2 Assumptions of the Ideal Diode Model

The ideal diode equation assumes:
- The depletion region is **abrupt** (no graded junctions).
- **No generation or recombination** of carriers in the depletion region (this is where the ideality factor $n$ comes in for real diodes).
- **Low-level injection** conditions (minority carrier concentration $\ll$ majority carrier concentration).
- The **drift current** in the neutral regions is negligible.
- The diode is **one-dimensional**.
- Temperature is uniform across the device.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | **Typical Value / Condition** |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------|-------------------------------|
| $I_D = I_S(e^{V_D/nV_T} - 1)$ | Ideal Diode Equation | A | Forward & Reverse bias |
| $V_T = kT/q$ | Thermal Voltage | V | **25.85 mV @ 300 K** |
| $I_S \propto T^3 e^{-E_g/kT}$ | Saturation Current (Temperature Dependence) | A | Doubles every **10°C rise** |
| $I_C = I_S e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$ | Collector Current (BJT Active) | A | Forward active region |
| $I_B = I_S / \beta_F e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$ | Base Current (BJT Active) | A | Function of $\beta_F$ |
| $I_E = I_C + I_B$ | Emitter Current (KCL) | A | Always true |
| $\alpha_F = I_C / I_E$ | Common-Base Current Gain | dimensionless | 0.95 to 0.99 |
| $\beta_F = I_C / I_B$ | Common-Emitter Current Gain | dimensionless | **20 to 500** |
| $\beta_F = \alpha_F / (1 - \alpha_F)$ | Relationship between $\alpha$ and $\beta$ | dimensionless | Always |
| $\alpha_F + \beta_F = \alpha_F \cdot \beta_F$ | Identity | dimensionless | Always |
| $I_CBO$ | Collector-Base leakage current (CB open) | A | nA range |
| $I_{CEO} = (\beta_F + 1) I_{CBO}$ | Collector-Emitter leakage (B open) | A | $\mu$A range |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Pipe Symbols:** The table above uses LaTeX notation without literal pipe characters to comply with markdown table syntax. All absolute value expressions in the notes have been written as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking table formatting.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

The ideal diode equation is fundamental to:

- **Solar Cell Design:** Photovoltaic cells operate as diodes under illumination. The ideal diode equation (modified with a photo-generated current term) predicts the I-V curve used to determine **fill factor** and **conversion efficiency**.
- **LED Design:** Light-emitting diodes follow the diode equation; the turn-on voltage (bandgap energy) determines the emitted color.
- **Rectifier Circuits:** Power supply design relies on the diode equation to predict forward voltage drop and power dissipation.
- **Temperature Sensors:** Since $I_S$ depends strongly on temperature, diodes are used as temperature sensors exploiting the predictable $\approx -2$ mV/°C temperature coefficient.
- **BJT Circuit Design:** The BJT's collector current equation is **directly derived from the ideal diode equation** applied to the base-emitter junction.

### 2.5 BJT Working: Physical Mechanism

The BJT operates on the principle of **minority carrier injection and collection**. Here's the detailed flow:

1. **Forward bias on B-E junction:** Electrons (in NPN) are injected from the emitter into the thin base region. The base is **very thin** (typically 0.1 to 1 $\mu$m) and lightly doped.

2. **Diffusion across the base:** Most of the injected electrons **diffuse across the thin base** without recombining with holes, because the base is thin and lightly doped (low recombination probability).

3. **Reverse bias on B-C junction:** The collector-base junction is reverse-biased, creating a strong electric field that **sweeps the diffusing electrons** from the base into the collector region. This is the key to amplification.

4. **Current relationships:**
   - A small base current $I_B$ controls a much larger collector current $I_C$.
   - The ratio $I_C / I_B = \beta_F$ (common-emitter current gain) quantifies amplification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the base must be thin and lightly doped:** The thinner the base, the higher the probability that injected electrons will diffuse across it before recombining with holes. This maximizes $\alpha_F$ and thus $\beta_F$, the current gain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Diode Equation (Shockley Equation)

We derive the ideal diode equation from first principles using the **law of the junction** and **diffusion current** concepts.

#### Step 1: Define the Built-in Potential

The built-in potential of a p-n junction is given by:

$$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

Where:
- $N_A$ = Acceptor concentration (cm$^{-3}$)
- $N_D$ = Donor concentration (cm$^{-3}$)
- $n_i$ = Intrinsic carrier concentration (cm$^{-3}$)

For Silicon at 300 K: $V_{bi} \approx \mathbf{0.7 \text{ V}}$ (heavily doped junction).

#### Step 2: Apply Forward Bias and Use the Law of the Junction

When a forward bias $V_D$ is applied, the barrier potential reduces from $V_{bi}$ to $(V_{bi} - V_D)$. The minority carrier concentrations at the edges of the depletion region (by the **law of the junction**) become:

$$p_n(0) = p_{n0} \, e^{V_D / V_T}$$

$$n_p(0) = n_{p0} \, e^{V_D / V_T}$$

Where:
- $p_{n0} = n_i^2 / N_D$ = Equilibrium minority hole concentration in n-side
- $n_{p0} = n_i^2 / N_A$ = Equilibrium minority electron concentration in p-side

**Conversion logic:** This equation is derived from the Boltzmann distribution under thermal equilibrium. The factor $e^{V_D/V_T}$ represents the **exponential enhancement** of minority carrier concentration at the junction edge due to forward bias.

#### Step 3: Calculate the Diffusion Currents

The injected minority carriers diffuse away from the junction, creating diffusion currents. For holes diffusing into the n-region (assuming a one-sided junction, e.g., $N_A \gg N_D$):

$$I_p(x) = -q A D_p \frac{\partial p_n}{\partial x}$$

Solving the diffusion equation in the neutral n-region with boundary condition $p_n(0) = p_{n0} e^{V_D/V_T}$ and $p_n(\infty) = p_{n0}$:

$$I_p = \frac{q A D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} \left( e^{V_D/V_T} - 1 \right)$$

**Conversion logic:** The minority carrier concentration **decays exponentially** with distance from the junction. The diffusion length $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ is the average distance a carrier travels before recombining. The current is proportional to the **concentration gradient**, which depends on the boundary value at $x = 0$.

Similarly, for electrons diffusing into the p-region:

$$I_n = \frac{q A D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \left( e^{V_D/V_T} - 1 \right)$$

#### Step 4: Combine the Currents to Obtain the Ideal Diode Equation

The total diode current is the sum of both diffusion currents:

$$I_D = I_p + I_n = \left( \frac{q A D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{q A D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \right) \left( e^{V_D/V_T} - 1 \right)$$

Defining the **reverse saturation current** $I_S$:

$$I_S = qA \left( \frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \right)$$

We obtain the **ideal diode equation**:

$$\boxed{I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / V_T} - 1 \right)}$$

**Conversion logic:** $I_S$ is the total reverse saturation current that would flow if the diode were reverse-biased ($V_D \to -\infty$). It depends on the geometry ($A$), diffusion coefficients ($D_n, D_p$), minority carrier concentrations ($p_{n0}, n_{p0}$), and diffusion lengths ($L_n, L_p$).

#### Step 5: Include the Ideality Factor (Modified Equation)

For **real diodes**, generation and recombination in the depletion region introduce additional current. The **ideality factor** $n$ (1 $\le n \le$ 2) accounts for this:

$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / (nV_T)} - 1 \right)$$

- **$n = 1$:** Ideal diode (no recombination in depletion region).
- **$n = 2$:** Worst case (dominant recombination in depletion region).
- **Typical Si diode:** $n \approx 1.5$ to 2.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Diode Operation Under Different Bias Conditions

| **Condition** | **Voltage** | **Current** | **Physical Explanation** |
|---------------|-------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Zero Bias** ($V_D = 0$) | 0 V | $I_D = 0$ | Built-in field balances diffusion; net current is zero. |
| **Forward Bias (Small)** ($V_D < V_{bi}$) | 0 to 0.5 V | $I_D \approx I_S e^{V_D/V_T}$ (small) | Barrier reduced; minority carriers begin to inject. |
| **Forward Bias (Large)** ($V_D \gg V_T$) | > 0.7 V | $I_D \approx I_S e^{V_D/V_T}$ (large) | Exponential increase; current rises sharply. |
| **Reverse Bias** ($V_D < 0$) | Negative | $I_D \approx -I_S$ (constant) | Minority carriers only; current saturates. |
| **Breakdown** ($V_D < -V_{BR}$) | Very negative | Large negative | Avalanche/Zener breakdown; junction conducts heavily. |

### 3.3 BJT Current Relations: Detailed Derivation

#### Step 1: Define the Emitter Injection Efficiency ($\gamma$)

The emitter injection efficiency is the fraction of emitter current carried by **injected minority carriers** (electrons in NPN):

$$\gamma = \frac{I_{nE}}{I_{nE} + I_{pE}} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{D_p N_A L_n}{D_n N_D L_p}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Not all current crossing the B-E junction is from useful electron injection. Some current is due to holes injected from base to emitter (wasted). High $\gamma$ (close to 1) requires the emitter to be **much more heavily doped** than the base: $N_D \gg N_A$.

#### Step 2: Define the Base Transport Factor ($\alpha_T$)

The base transport factor accounts for the fraction of **injected electrons that successfully reach the collector** without recombining in the base:

$$\alpha_T = \frac{I_{nC}}{I_{nE}} \approx 1 - \frac{W_B^2}{2 L_n^2}$$

Where $W_B$ is the base width.

**Conversion logic:** Electrons diffuse across the base with characteristic diffusion length $L_n$. If the base is much thinner than $L_n$ (i.e., $W_B \ll L_n$), most electrons survive, giving $\alpha_T \approx 1$. This is why **thin base** is critical.

#### Step 3: Combine to Get the Common-Base Current Gain $\alpha_F$

$$\alpha_F = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T$$

For a well-designed BJT: $\alpha_F \approx \mathbf{0.99}$.

#### Step 4: Derive the Common-Emitter Current Gain $\beta_F$

From the current relationships $I_C = \alpha_F I_E$ and $I_E = I_C + I_B$:

$$I_B = I_E - I_C = I_E - \alpha_F I_E = (1 - \alpha_F) I_E$$

Therefore:

$$\beta_F = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{\alpha_F I_E}{(1 - \alpha_F) I_E} = \frac{\alpha_F}{1 - \alpha_F}$$

**Conversion logic:** A small change in $\alpha_F$ (close to 1) produces a large change in $\beta_F$. For example, $\alpha_F = 0.99$ gives $\beta_F = 99$, but $\alpha_F = 0.998$ gives $\beta_F = 499$. This is why **BJT current gain is highly sensitive to manufacturing tolerances**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A silicon diode has $I_S = 10^{-12}$ A and operates at $T = 300$ K. Find the diode current when $V_D = 0.7$ V. Assume $n = 1$.

**Solution:**

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 0.02585 \text{ V} = 25.85 \text{ mV}$$

$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / V_T} - 1 \right)$$

$$I_D = 10^{-12} \left( e^{0.7 / 0.02585} - 1 \right)$$

$$I_D = 10^{-12} \left( e^{27.08} - 1 \right)$$

$$e^{27.08} \approx 5.79 \times 10^{11}$$

$$I_D \approx 10^{-12} \times 5.79 \times 10^{11} = 0.579 \text{ A} \approx 579 \text{ mA}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This calculation demonstrates why even a small change in $V_D$ (e.g., 60 mV, which equals $2.3 V_T$) causes a **tenfold** increase in diode current. This exponential sensitivity is the basis for logarithmic amplifiers and the large transconductance of BJTs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: P-N Junction Under Forward and Reverse Bias

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Forward["FORWARD BIAS MODE"]
        FB[Applied Voltage V_D positive]
        FB --> FB1[Barrier Potential Reduced]
        FB1 --> FB2[Depletion Region Narrows]
        FB2 --> FB3[Majority Carriers Cross Junction]
        FB3 --> FB4[Exponential Current Flow]
        FB4 --> FB5[Current I_D = I_S times e to power V_D by V_T minus 1]
    end

    subgraph Reverse["REVERSE BIAS MODE"]
        RB[Applied Voltage V_D negative]
        RB --> RB1[Barrier Potential Increased]
        RB1 --> RB2[Depletion Region Widens]
        RB2 --> RB3[Minority Carriers Only]
        RB3 --> RB4[Tiny Saturation Current]
        RB4 --> RB5[Current I_D approximately equals negative I_S]
    end

    style Forward fill:#e1f5e1
    style Reverse fill:#f5e1e1
    style FB5 fill:#b3d9b3
    style RB5 fill:#d9b3b3
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: BJT (NPN) Working — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EmitterRegion["N-TYPE EMITTER REGION - Heavily Doped"]
        ENode[Emitter Terminal E]
        EDope[High N-Doping ND large]
    end

    subgraph BaseRegion["P-TYPE BASE REGION - Thin and Lightly Doped"]
        BNode[Base Terminal B]
        BThin[Base Width W_B very small]
        BRecomb[Minority Electron Recombination]
    end

    subgraph CollectorRegion["N-TYPE COLLECTOR REGION - Moderately Doped"]
        CNode[Collector Terminal C]
        CSweep[Reverse Bias Electric Field]
    end

    ENode -->|Inject Electrons| BNode
    BNode -->|Diffuse Across Thin Base| CNode
    BRecomb -->|Small Loss| BaseRegion
    CSweep -->|Collect Electrons| CNode
    BNode -->|Small Hole Current| ENode

    style EmitterRegion fill:#ffe1e1
    style BaseRegion fill:#e1e1ff
    style CollectorRegion fill:#e1ffe1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: BJT Operating Regions (Active, Saturation, Cutoff)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[BJT Operating Mode Selection]

    Start --> Q1{B-E Junction Bias?}
    Start --> Q2{B-C Junction Bias?}

    Q1 -->|Forward| FBYes
    Q1 -->|Reverse| RBYes
    Q2 -->|Forward| FCYes
    Q2 -->|Reverse| RCYes

    FBYes --> ActiveCheck
    RCYes --> ActiveCheck
    ActiveCheck[Forward B-E and Reverse B-C]
    ActiveCheck --> ActiveRegion[ACTIVE REGION Amplifier Mode I_C equals beta_F times I_B]

    FBYes --> SatCheck
    FCYes --> SatCheck
    SatCheck[Forward B-E and Forward B-C]
    SatCheck --> SatRegion[SATURATION REGION Switch ON V_CE low]

    RBYes --> CutCheck
    RCYes --> CutCheck
    CutCheck[Reverse B-E and Reverse B-C]
    CutCheck --> CutRegion[CUTOFF REGION Switch OFF I_C approximately zero]

    RBYes --> InvCheck
    FCYes --> InvCheck
    InvCheck[Reverse B-E and Forward B-C]
    InvCheck --> InvRegion[INVERSE ACTIVE REGION Not useful in practice]

    style ActiveRegion fill:#b3d9b3
    style SatRegion fill:#d9b3d9
    style CutRegion fill:#b3b3d9
    style InvRegion fill:#d9d9b3
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Current Flow Relationships in BJT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IE[Emitter Current I_E] -->|Enters Base and Collector| Split
    Split[Current Splits at Base-Collector Junction]
    Split -->|I_C to Collector Terminal| IC[Collector Current I_C]
    Split -->|I_B to Base Terminal| IB[Base Current I_B]

    IC -->|Beta_F times I_B| AmpRelation[Current Amplification]
    IB -->|Small Control Current| AmpRelation
    AmpRelation --> Output[Output: Large Collector Current Controlled by Small Base Current]

    IE -->|I_C plus I_B KCL| SumCheck[Kirchhoff Current Law]

    style IE fill:#ffcc99
    style IC fill:#99ccff
    style IB fill:#ccff99
    style AmpRelation fill:#ff9999
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `FBYes`, `ActiveCheck`, `SatRegion`). No reserved keywords are used. All labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting tags.
