<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State and explain the ideal diode equation. What is the physical significance of the reverse saturation current $I_S$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The ideal diode equation (Shockley equation) gives the current-voltage relationship of a p-n junction diode:

$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / nV_T} - 1 \right)$$

**Physical significance of $I_S$:** The reverse saturation current $I_S$ is the small reverse current that flows when the diode is reverse-biased. It arises from the **drift of minority carriers** across the junction. $I_S$ depends on:
- **Temperature** (doubles every 10°C rise)
- **Doping concentrations** ($N_A, N_D$)
- **Diode area** ($A$)
- **Carrier diffusion coefficients and lifetimes** ($D_n, D_p, L_n, L_p$)

Typical values: $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-6}$ A.

*(Award: 1 mark equation, 1 mark significance, 1 mark factors/values)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the three operating regions of a BJT and state the bias conditions for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Region** | **B-E Junction** | **B-C Junction** | **Application** |
|------------|------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Active** | Forward | Reverse | Amplifier |
| **Saturation** | Forward | Forward | Switch ON |
| **Cutoff** | Reverse | Reverse | Switch OFF |

In the **active region**, the BJT operates as a linear amplifier with $I_C = \beta_F I_B$. In **saturation**, both junctions are forward-biased, $V_{CE}$ is small (~0.2 V), and the transistor acts as a closed switch. In **cutoff**, no current flows, and the transistor acts as an open switch.

*(Award: 1 mark per region with bias conditions)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the ideal diode equation starting from the law of the junction. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon diode has $I_S = 5 \times 10^{-13}$ A at 300 K. Calculate:
   - (i) The diode current at $V_D = 0.65$ V (assume $n = 1$).
   - (ii) The diode current at $V_D = 0.70$ V.
   - (iii) Comment on the change in current when voltage increases by 50 mV. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Derivation of the Ideal Diode Equation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Abrupt junction with one-dimensional geometry.
2. No generation/recombination in the depletion region.
3. Low-level injection in neutral regions.
4. Drift current in neutral regions is negligible.
5. Uniform temperature.

**Derivation Steps (6 marks):**

**Step 1:** Under forward bias $V_D$, the minority carrier concentration at the depletion region edge (n-side) is given by the **law of the junction**:

$$p_n(0) = p_{n0} \, e^{V_D / V_T}$$

**Step 2:** The excess minority carriers diffuse away from the junction, decaying exponentially. The hole diffusion current in the n-region is:

$$I_p = \frac{q A D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} \left( e^{V_D / V_T} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 3:** Similarly, the electron diffusion current in the p-region is:

$$I_n = \frac{q A D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \left( e^{V_D / V_T} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 4:** Total diode current:

$$I_D = I_p + I_n = \left( \frac{q A D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{q A D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \right) \left( e^{V_D / V_T} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 5:** Define the reverse saturation current:

$$I_S = qA \left( \frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \right)$$

**Step 6:** Substituting:

$$\boxed{I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / V_T} - 1 \right)}$$

This is the **ideal diode equation** (Shockley equation).

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $I_S = 5 \times 10^{-13}$ A, $T = 300$ K, $n = 1$.

**Step 1:** Calculate thermal voltage:

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.025875 \text{ V}$$

**(i) At $V_D = 0.65$ V (3 marks):**

$$I_D = 5 \times 10^{-13} \left( e^{0.65 / 0.025875} - 1 \right)$$

$$\frac{0.65}{0.025875} = 25.121$$

$$e^{25.121} = 7.95 \times 10^{10}$$

$$I_D = 5 \times 10^{-13} \times 7.95 \times 10^{10} = 3.975 \times 10^{-2} \text{ A} \approx 39.75 \text{ mA}$$

**(ii) At $V_D = 0.70$ V (3 marks):**

$$I_D = 5 \times 10^{-13} \left( e^{0.70 / 0.025875} - 1 \right)$$

$$\frac{0.70}{0.025875} = 27.053$$

$$e^{27.053} = 5.43 \times 10^{11}$$

$$I_D = 5 \times 10^{-13} \times 5.43 \times 10^{11} = 0.2715 \text{ A} \approx 271.5 \text{ mA}$$

**(iii) Comment (1 mark):**

The voltage increased by 50 mV (from 0.65 V to 0.70 V), and the current increased by a factor of approximately **6.83** (from 39.75 mA to 271.5 mA). This demonstrates the **exponential sensitivity** of diode current to applied voltage. The factor is approximately $e^{50\text{mV}/V_T} = e^{1.93} \approx 6.89$, which is close to the calculated ratio. This property makes the diode useful in **logarithmic amplifiers** and explains the sharp turn-on characteristic.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an NPN BJT in the **active region**. Derive the relationship between $\alpha_F$ and $\beta_F$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a BJT with $\alpha_F = 0.98$ and $I_{CBO} = 5$ $\mu$A, find:
   - (i) The value of $\beta_F$.
   - (ii) If $I_B = 100$ $\mu$A, calculate $I_C$ and $I_E$.
   - (iii) If the base is open-circuited, find the leakage current $I_{CEO}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) NPN BJT Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction (2 marks):**
An NPN BJT consists of a thin, lightly doped p-type **base** region sandwiched between two n-type regions: a heavily doped **emitter** and a moderately doped **collector**. The three terminals are Emitter (E), Base (B), and Collector (C).

**Working in Active Region (Forward B-E, Reverse B-C) (3 marks):**

1. **Forward bias on B-E junction:** Electrons are injected from the n-type emitter into the p-type base.
2. **Diffusion across base:** The thin, lightly doped base allows most electrons to **diffuse across** without recombining with holes.
3. **Reverse bias on B-C junction:** The strong electric field in the reverse-biased B-C depletion region **sweeps the diffusing electrons** into the collector.
4. A small base current $I_B$ controls a much larger collector current $I_C$, giving amplification.

**Derivation of $\alpha_F$ and $\beta_F$ relationship (2 marks):**

From $I_C = \alpha_F I_E$ and $I_E = I_C + I_B$:

$$I_B = I_E - I_C = I_E (1 - \alpha_F)$$

$$\beta_F = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{\alpha_F I_E}{(1 - \alpha_F) I_E} = \frac{\alpha_F}{1 - \alpha_F}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_F = \frac{\alpha_F}{1 - \alpha_F}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\alpha_F = 0.98$, $I_{CBO} = 5$ $\mu$A.

**(i) Calculate $\beta_F$ (2 marks):**

$$\beta_F = \frac{0.98}{1 - 0.98} = \frac{0.98}{0.02} = 49$$

**(ii) Calculate $I_C$ and $I_E$ when $I_B = 100$ $\mu$A (3 marks):**

$$I_C = \beta_F I_B = 49 \times 100 \, \mu\text{A} = 4900 \, \mu\text{A} = 4.9 \text{ mA}$$

$$I_E = I_C + I_B = 4.9 \text{ mA} + 0.1 \text{ mA} = 5.0 \text{ mA}$$

**(iii) Calculate $I_{CEO}$ (base open) (2 marks):**

$$I_{CEO} = (\beta_F + 1) I_{CBO} = (49 + 1) \times 5 \, \mu\text{A} = 250 \, \mu\text{A} = 0.25 \text{ mA}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget the "-1" in the ideal diode equation when $V_D \gg V_T$. Always include it for mathematical rigor, even if its value is negligible at high forward bias.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing $I_S$ (reverse saturation current, nA to $\mu$A range) with the actual forward current (mA range). The exponential term amplifies $I_S$ by a factor of $\sim 10^{10}$ to $10^{12}$.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Using $V_T = 26$ mV vs. the more accurate 25.85 mV. KTU expects 25.85 mV at 300 K. Using the wrong value loses a half mark.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** In BJT problems, students often calculate $I_E = I_C - I_B$ instead of $I_E = I_C + I_B$. Remember KCL: $I_E$ is the **sum** (input current).
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting to convert $I_{CBO}$ units (typically $\mu$A or nA) when computing $I_{CEO}$. Always check units first!

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ideal Diode Equation & BJT Working**

#### **Ideal Diode Equation Essentials:**

- $\square$ The ideal diode equation is $I_D = I_S(e^{V_D/nV_T} - 1)$, derived by **William Shockley** in 1949.
- $\square$ **Thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q = \mathbf{25.85 \text{ mV}}$ at 300 K (room temperature).
- $\square$ The **ideality factor** $n$ ranges from 1 (ideal) to 2 (recombination-dominated).
- $\square$ **Reverse saturation current** $I_S$ doubles for every **10°C rise** in temperature.
- $\square$ A 60 mV increase in $V_D$ causes a **10× increase** in $I_D$ (since $60 \text{ mV} \approx 2.3 V_T$).
- $\square$ Under forward bias, $I_D \approx I_S e^{V_D/V_T}$ when $V_D \gg V_T$.
- $\square$ Under reverse bias, $I_D \approx -I_S$ (constant saturation).
- $\square$ Built-in potential $V_{bi} \approx 0.7$ V for Si, 0.3 V for Ge.

#### **BJT Working Essentials:**

- $\square$ A BJT is a **three-terminal, two-junction** device with terminals: **E**mitter, **B**ase, **C**ollector.
- $\square$ **NPN**: N-E | P-B | N-C; **PNP**: P-E | N-B | P-C.
- $\square$ The base is **thin and lightly doped** to maximize $\beta_F$.
- $\square$ The emitter is **heavily doped** to maximize injection efficiency $\gamma$.
- $\square$ **Active region**: B-E forward, B-C reverse → amplifier mode.
- $\square$ **Saturation region**: both junctions forward → switch ON.
- $\square$ **Cutoff region**: both junctions reverse → switch OFF.
- $\square$ **Current relationships**: $I_E = I_B + I_C$, $\beta_F = I_C/I_B$, $\alpha_F = I_C/I_E$.
- $\square$ **Key identity**: $\beta_F = \alpha_F / (1 - \alpha_F)$ or equivalently $\alpha_F = \beta_F / (\beta_F + 1)$.
- $\square$ **Leakage current**: $I_{CEO} = (\beta_F + 1) I_{CBO}$ — always specified with the base open.
- $\square$ BJT is a **current-controlled current source (CCCS)** in the active region.
- $\square$ Typical $\beta_F$ values: 20 to 500 (discrete BJTs), 50 to 200 (integrated BJTs).

#### **Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid:**

- $\square$ Do NOT confuse $I_S$ (saturation current) with $I_{CBO}$ (collector-base leakage).
- $\square$ Do NOT use $V_T = 26$ mV in formal derivations; use 25.85 mV.
- $\square$ Do NOT forget the ideality factor $n$ in real diode problems.
- $\square$ Do NOT mix up $\alpha_F$ and $\beta_F$ definitions.
- $\square$ Do NOT assume BJT is in active region without checking bias conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When solving numerical problems, **always state the formula first**, then substitute the values, and show intermediate steps. KTU examiners award **partial marks** for correct methodology even if the final answer has a small numerical error. Good luck with your exams!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transistor Action
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transistor Action

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transistor Action:** The controlled flow of majority charge carriers from one pn-junction to another within a three-terminal, two-junction semiconductor device (BJT), achieved by appropriately biasing the **Emitter-Base** junction in the forward direction and the **Collector-Base** junction in the reverse direction. This action results in current amplification, the foundational principle behind all modern analog and digital electronics.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAET412 - Solid State Devices)**, Module 2 focuses on the **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)**, a sandwich-like three-layer structure comprising either:
- **NPN Transistor:** a thin p-type semiconductor sandwiched between two n-type regions.
- **PNP Transistor:** a thin n-type semiconductor sandwiched between two p-type regions.

The three terminals are named:
1. **Emitter (E):** Heavily doped region that *emits* charge carriers into the base.
2. **Base (B):** Very thin and lightly doped central region.
3. **Collector (C):** Moderately doped region that *collects* the emitted charge carriers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Faucet & Hose" Model

Imagine a **water tap (faucet)** connected to a high-pressure water pipe. The flow of water out of the tap is determined by how much you turn the handle — but the handle itself requires only a *small* twisting effort to control a *large* water stream.

Mapping this to transistor action:
- **Water Pipe (High Pressure Source) = Emitter Terminal:** This is where the charge carriers (electrons in NPN) originate. It is heavily doped to supply a large quantity of carriers.
- **Faucet Handle (Small Control Knob) = Base Terminal:** A small current applied here controls a much larger current flowing from Emitter to Collector. The base is thin and lightly doped precisely so that a small change in base current can dramatically influence the collector current.
- **Water Outlet (Final Stream) = Collector Terminal:** The carriers that successfully cross the base region are "collected" here, producing a much larger output current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The transistor is *not* a device that creates energy out of nothing. It is a **current-controlled current source** — a small base current (control signal) regulates a much larger collector-emitter current (output signal), with the additional power supplied by an external DC bias source (the battery).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Si NPN) |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal Voltage at 300 K | $V_T$ | **≈ 25.85 mV** (often approximated as **26 mV**) |
| Electron Charge | $q$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
| Intrinsic Si Carrier Concentration | $n_i$ | **1.5 × 10¹⁰ cm⁻³** at 300 K |
| Common-Base Current Gain | $\alpha$ | **0.95 – 0.99** (dimensionless) |
| Common-Emitter Current Gain | $\beta$ | **50 – 300** (dimensionless) |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** $\alpha$ is *always* less than 1 (since it represents a fraction of emitter carriers reaching the collector), whereas $\beta$ is *always* greater than 1 (current amplification).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

For a standard **NPN transistor in the Active Region** (the regime where true amplification occurs):

**Step 1 — Forward Bias the Emitter-Base (EB) Junction**
- The n-type emitter is connected to the *negative* terminal of $V_{EE}$, and the p-type base to the *positive* terminal.
- The depletion region at the EB junction *narrows*, and a huge number of **electrons** from the heavily doped emitter are injected into the thin, lightly doped base.

**Step 2 — Reverse Bias the Collector-Base (CB) Junction**
- The p-type base is connected to the *negative* side (relative to the collector) of $V_{CC}$, and the n-type collector to the *positive* terminal.
- The depletion region at the CB junction *widens*, creating a strong electric field pointing from the n-collector toward the p-base.

**Step 3 — Diffusion and Collection Across the Base**
- The injected electrons diffuse through the thin base (typically **≈ 1 μm** thick). Because the base is so thin, *almost all* electrons (typically **> 95%**) reach the depletion edge of the CB junction *before* they can recombine.
- The strong reverse-bias electric field at the CB junction **sweeps** these electrons into the collector region, where they exit as collector current.

**Step 4 — Base Current Formation**
- A small fraction of electrons (typically **1 – 5%**) recombine with holes in the base. To maintain charge neutrality, the base lead must supply replacement holes from the external circuit. This *replacement* current is the base current $I_B$.
- Since $I_B$ is small and $I_C$ is large, **current amplification** is achieved.

**Step 5 — KCL at the Transistor Terminal**
- The sum of currents entering equals the sum leaving:
  - $I_E = I_C + I_B$

## 2.2 The Four Operating Modes of a BJT

| Mode | EB Junction | CB Junction | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Active (Forward Active)** | Forward Biased | Reverse Biased | **Linear Amplification** |
| **Saturation** | Forward Biased | Forward Biased | **Switch ON** (Digital Logic LOW) |
| **Cutoff** | Reverse Biased | Reverse Biased | **Switch OFF** (Digital Logic HIGH) |
| **Inverse Active** | Reverse Biased | Forward Biased | Rarely used (poor performance) |

## 2.3 Current Components in the Active Region

For a more rigorous analysis, the emitter, base, and collector currents are decomposed:

- $I_{Ep}$ = Hole current crossing from base into emitter (minority — should be small for high $\alpha$).
- $I_{En}$ = Electron current injected from emitter into base (majority — should be large).
- $I_{Cn}$ = Electron current reaching the collector (the useful amplified signal).
- $I_{Cp}$ = Reverse saturation leakage current due to thermally generated holes.
- $I_{CBO}$ = Reverse saturation current of the CB junction with emitter open-circuited.
- $I_{CEO}$ = Leakage current from collector to emitter with base open-circuited.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the heart of every KTU Board question on transistor action.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_E = I_B + I_C$ | Kirchhoff's Current Law at BJT terminals | A |
| 2 | $\alpha_{dc} = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}$ | Common-base DC current gain (large-signal) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\alpha_{ac} = \dfrac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_E}\Big\vert_{V_{CB}=\text{const}}$ | Common-base AC (small-signal) current gain | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\beta_{dc} = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$ | Common-emitter DC current gain | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\beta_{ac} = \dfrac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_B}\Big\vert_{V_{CE}=\text{const}}$ | Common-emitter AC current gain ($h_{fe}$) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$ | Relationship between CE and CB gain | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{1 + \beta}$ | Inverse relationship | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\gamma = \dfrac{I_{En}}{I_E}$ | **Emitter Injection Efficiency** | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\alpha_T = \dfrac{I_{Cp}}{I_{En}}$ | **Base Transport Factor** | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T$ | Fundamental current gain equation | dimensionless |
| 11 | $I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CBO}$ | Ebers-Moll (CB config, active) | A |
| 12 | $I_C = \beta I_B + I_{CEO}$ | CE config collector current | A |
| 13 | $I_{CEO} = \dfrac{I_{CBO}}{1 - \alpha}$ | Leakage current amplification | A |
| 14 | $(1 + \beta) I_{CBO} = \beta I_{CEO}$ | Alternate form of leakage relation | A |
| 15 | $I_E = I_{ES} \left(e^{\dfrac{V_{BE}}{V_T}} - 1\right) - \alpha_R I_{CS} \left(e^{\dfrac{V_{BC}}{V_T}} - 1\right)$ | Ebers-Moll Equation (general) | A |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Transistor action is the bedrock of nearly every modern electronic system:

- **Analog Domain:** Audio amplifiers, RF amplifiers, operational amplifier input stages, oscillators, and radio receivers all rely on BJTs operating in the **forward active** region.
- **Digital Domain:** In saturation and cutoff, BJTs act as electronic switches forming the fundamental building blocks of TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic) gates — the forerunner to CMOS-based integrated circuits.
- **Power Electronics:** BJTs (and their evolved relatives, IGBTs) are used in motor drivers, power inverters, and voltage regulators.
- **Instrumentation:** Signal conditioning circuits in sensors, medical electronics (ECG amplifiers), and measurement devices.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between $\beta$ and $\alpha$

**Starting Point:** KCL at the BJT terminal.

$$
I_E = I_B + I_C
$$

**Step 1 — Divide both sides by $I_C$:**

$$
\frac{I_E}{I_C} = \frac{I_B}{I_C} + 1
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to express in terms of known gain definitions:**

Since $\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}$, we have $\dfrac{I_E}{I_C} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$.

Since $\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$, we have $\dfrac{I_B}{I_C} = \dfrac{1}{\beta}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
\frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\beta} + 1
$$

**Step 4 — Combine the right-hand side over a common denominator:**

$$
\frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1 + \beta}{\beta}
$$

**Step 5 — Invert both sides to isolate $\beta$:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\beta$ in terms of $\alpha$:**

$$
\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Since $\alpha \to 1$ (e.g., 0.99), a tiny change in $\alpha$ causes a *huge* change in $\beta$. For example, $\alpha = 0.99 \Rightarrow \beta = 99$, but $\alpha = 0.98 \Rightarrow \beta = 49$. This sensitivity is why $\alpha$ must be tightly controlled during transistor fabrication.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Derivation of $\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T$

**Step 1 — Define the Emitter Injection Efficiency $\gamma$:**

The ratio of the *useful* injected electron current to the *total* emitter current:

$$
\gamma = \frac{I_{En}}{I_E} = \frac{I_{En}}{I_{En} + I_{Ep}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Base Transport Factor $\alpha_T$:**

The fraction of electrons injected into the base that successfully *survive* recombination and reach the collector edge:

$$
\alpha_T = \frac{I_{Cn}}{I_{En}}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply $\gamma$ and $\alpha_T$:**

$$
\gamma \cdot \alpha_T = \left(\frac{I_{En}}{I_E}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{I_{Cn}}{I_{En}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $I_{En}$ (the electron current injected into the base):**

$$
\gamma \cdot \alpha_T = \frac{I_{Cn}}{I_E}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognise that the collector electron current $I_{Cn}$ *is* the collector current $I_C$ in the ideal case (ignoring leakage):**

$$
\gamma \cdot \alpha_T = \frac{I_C}{I_E} = \alpha
$$

$$
\boxed{\alpha = \gamma \cdot \alpha_T}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** To make $\alpha$ as close to 1 as possible, we need:
> 1. **High $\gamma$** → achieved by making the emitter *much* more heavily doped than the base (so $I_{En} \gg I_{Ep}$).
> 2. **High $\alpha_T$** → achieved by making the base *very thin* and lightly doped (so electrons have minimal chance to recombine).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Emitter Injection Efficiency in Terms of Doping

For an NPN transistor, the emitter injection efficiency is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{D_p \cdot n_E}{D_n \cdot p_B} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L_p}}
$$

Where:
- $D_n$ = electron diffusion coefficient in base
- $D_p$ = hole diffusion coefficient in emitter
- $n_E$ = equilibrium electron concentration in emitter $\approx N_{DE}$ (donor doping)
- $p_B$ = equilibrium hole concentration in base $\approx N_{AB}$ (acceptor doping)
- $W$ = effective base width
- $L_p$ = diffusion length of holes in emitter

**Simplification (assuming unity aspect ratio $\frac{W}{L_p} \to 0$ and unity diffusivity ratio $\frac{D_p}{D_n} \to 1$):**

$$
\gamma \approx \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{n_E}{p_B}} = \frac{p_B}{n_E + p_B}
$$

Since $n_E \gg p_B$ in a practical NPN transistor:

$$
\gamma \approx 1 - \frac{p_B}{n_E}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **mathematical justification for heavy emitter doping** — a key concept often asked in KTU viva and short-answer questions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Derivation of the Ebers-Moll Equation (Active Mode)

The Ebers-Moll model treats the BJT as **two coupled pn-junction diodes**. For the forward active mode (EB forward, CB reverse), only the forward-biased junction contributes.

**Step 1 — Write the diode equation for the forward-biased EB junction:**

$$
I_E = I_{ES} \left( e^{\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Since $V_{BE} \gg V_T$ in active mode, the $-1$ term is negligible:**

$$
I_E \approx I_{ES} \cdot e^{\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the collector current using $\alpha_F$ (forward alpha):**

$$
I_C = \alpha_F \cdot I_E + I_{CS} \left(1 - e^{\frac{V_{BC}}{V_T}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — In active mode, $V_{BC}$ is reverse-biased, so $e^{V_{BC}/V_T} \to 0$:**

$$
I_C = \alpha_F \cdot I_E - I_{CS}
$$

**Step 5 — Define $I_{CBO} = I_{CS}(1 - \alpha_F \alpha_R)$, leading to the simplified Ebers-Moll form:**

$$
I_C = \alpha_F I_E + I_{CBO}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $I_E = I_B + I_C$:**

$$
I_C = \alpha_F (I_B + I_C) + I_{CBO}
$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange to isolate $I_C$:**

$$
I_C (1 - \alpha_F) = \alpha_F I_B + I_{CBO}
$$

**Step 8 — Divide by $(1 - \alpha_F)$:**

$$
I_C = \frac{\alpha_F}{1 - \alpha_F} I_B + \frac{I_{CBO}}{1 - \alpha_F}
$$

**Step 9 — Recognise the substitutions $\beta_F = \dfrac{\alpha_F}{1 - \alpha_F}$ and $I_{CEO} = \dfrac{I_{CBO}}{1 - \alpha_F}$:**

$$
\boxed{I_C = \beta_F I_B + I_{CEO}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental input-output equation** of a BJT in the common-emitter configuration — frequently tested in KTU problems.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** For a BJT, $\beta_{dc} = 100$, $I_{B} = 20\ \mu A$, and $I_{CBO} = 1\ \mu A$. Calculate $I_C$, $I_E$, $\alpha$, and $I_{CEO}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $I_C$ using $I_C = \beta I_B + I_{CEO}$:**

But first we need $I_{CEO}$. Compute $\alpha$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta} = \frac{100}{101} \approx 0.9901
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_{CEO}$:**

$$
I_{CEO} = \frac{I_{CBO}}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{1\ \mu A}{1 - 0.9901} = \frac{1\ \mu A}{0.0099} \approx 101\ \mu A
$$

(Notice: $I_{CEO} = (\beta + 1) I_{CBO} = 101 \times 1\ \mu A = 101\ \mu A$ ✓)

**Step 3 — Compute $I_C$:**

$$
I_C = (100)(20\ \mu A) + 101\ \mu A = 2000\ \mu A + 101\ \mu A = 2101\ \mu A = 2.101\ mA
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $I_E$:**

$$
I_E = I_C + I_B = 2.101\ mA + 0.020\ mA = 2.121\ mA
$$

**Step 5 — Verify using $\alpha$:**

$$
I_C / I_E = 2.101 / 2.121 \approx 0.9906 \approx \alpha \ \checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> Always carry units through every step — KTU examiners deduct marks for sloppy unit handling in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Transistor Action (NPN Active Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Bias Source V_EE] -->|Forward Bias| EBJ[Emitter-Base Junction]
    EBJ -->|Injects electrons| BASE[Thin P-Type Base Region]
    BASE -->|95-99 percent reach| CBJ[Collector-Base Junction]
    CBJ -->|Reverse Biased by V_CC| SWEEP[Strong Electric Field Sweeps Electrons]
    SWEEP -->|Collector current I_C| COLL[Collector Terminal]
    BASE -->|1-5 percent recombine| RECOMB[Hole Recombination in Base]
    RECOMB -->|Base current I_B| BASETERM[Base Terminal]
    A -->|Total current I_E| EMIT[Emitter Terminal]
    COLL --> OUT[Output: Amplified Current I_C]
    BASETERM --> CONTROL[Control Input: Small I_B]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Current Components Inside a BJT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EMIT[Emitter Terminal I_E] --> SPLIT{Distribution of I_E}
    SPLIT -->|I_En Majority electrons| INTOBASE[Injected INTO Base]
    SPLIT -->|I_Ep Minority holes| INTOEMT[Back-injected INTO Emitter]
    INTOBASE --> TRAVERSE{Diffusion Across Base}
    TRAVERSE -->|I_Cn Arrives at CB edge| SWEEPCOLL[Swept into Collector as I_C]
    TRAVERSE -->|I_Bn Recombines in Base| HOLERECOMB[Combines with Holes]
    HOLERECOMB --> IB[Base Terminal Current I_B]
    SWEEPCOLL --> ICOLL[Collector Terminal Current I_C]
    INTOEMT --> IE[Returns as part of I_E]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Four Operating Modes of a BJT

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active: EB Forward + CB Reverse
    [*] --> Saturation: EB Forward + CB Forward
    [*] --> Cutoff: EB Reverse + CB Reverse
    Active --> Saturation: Increase I_B
    Saturation --> Active: Decrease I_B
    Active --> Cutoff: Remove I_B
    Cutoff --> Active: Apply I_B
    note right of Active: Linear Amplification Region
    note right of Saturation: Both Junctions ON Switch Closed
    note right of Cutoff: Both Junctions OFF Switch Open
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: BJT as an Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Input Signal Source v_in] -->|Applied to Base| BIASNET[DC Biasing Network V_BB]
    BIASNET --> BASE[Base Terminal of BJT]
    BASE --> BJT[NPN BJT in Active Mode]
    VCC[V_CC Supply] --> COLL[Collector Terminal]
    BJT --> COLL
    COLL --> LOAD[Load Resistor R_C]
    LOAD --> VOUT[Amplified Output v_out]
    EMIT[Emitter Terminal] --> GND[Ground or R_E]
    CTRL[Small Change in I_B] -->|Controls| BJT
    BJT -->|Produces Large Change| ICOLLECT[Change in I_C]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams use **block-level functional architecture** to map the signal flow and current partitioning within the BJT, which is the recommended approach for representing transistor action in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

---
