# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define an ideal MOS capacitor. List all the assumptions that distinguish an ideal MOS capacitor from a real one.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An ideal MOS capacitor is a Metal–Oxide–Semiconductor structure in which:
1. The work function difference between the metal gate and the semiconductor is zero ($\phi_{MS} = 0$).
2. The oxide contains no trapped charge ($Q_{ox} = 0$).
3. The oxide–semiconductor interface has no interface traps ($D_{it} = 0$).
4. The oxide is a perfect insulator (no DC current).
5. The semiconductor is uniformly doped with an ideal ohmic back contact.

In a real MOS capacitor, each of these assumptions is violated to some extent. **(1 mark per key point, total 3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the energy band diagram of an ideal MOS capacitor at equilibrium.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
At equilibrium with no applied gate bias, the ideal MOS capacitor has:
- A continuous **vacuum level** $E_{vac}$ across metal, oxide, and semiconductor.
- A single, constant **Fermi level** $E_F$ across the structure.
- **Flat energy bands** ($E_c, E_v$) in the semiconductor (no band bending) because $\phi_{MS} = 0$.
- A **rectangular barrier** in the oxide region corresponding to its 9 eV bandgap.

The diagram shows $\phi_M$ and $\phi_S$ both equal, so $E_F$ is at the same energy below $E_{vac}$ on both sides. **(2 marks for sketch, 1 mark for explanation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, 14 Marks]**

### Question A — 14 Marks

**Q.A (a)** With necessary diagrams, derive the energy band diagram of an **ideal MOS capacitor at equilibrium**. Discuss the role of vacuum level, electron affinity, and work function in the construction. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — State the assumptions of an ideal MOS capacitor (2 marks):**
1. $\phi_{MS} = \phi_M - \phi_S = 0$
2. $Q_{ox} = 0$, $D_{it} = 0$
3. Uniform doping, perfect ohmic back contact
4. Perfectly insulating oxide

**Step 2 — Identify the energy references (1 mark):**
- Vacuum level $E_{vac}$: universal reference, continuous across the structure.
- Electron affinity $\chi_{Si} = 4.05$ eV: $E_c = E_{vac} - \chi_{Si}$
- Work function: $\phi = (E_{vac} - E_F)/q$

**Step 3 — Construct the diagram layer by layer (3 marks):**
- **Metal:** Place $E_F^M$ at $\phi_M \cdot q$ below $E_{vac}$.
- **Oxide:** Mark $E_c^{ox} = E_{vac} - \chi_{ox}$ and $E_v^{ox} = E_c^{ox} - E_{g,ox}$ (9 eV wide gap).
- **Semiconductor:** $E_c = E_{vac} - \chi_{Si}$, $E_v = E_c - E_g = E_{vac} - \chi_{Si} - 1.12$ eV.
- **Fermi level alignment:** Since $\phi_M = \phi_S$, $E_F$ is at the same energy below $E_{vac}$ on both sides → **flat bands**.

**Step 4 — State the flat-band condition (1 mark):**
At equilibrium with $\phi_{MS} = 0$ and $V_G = 0$, the surface potential $\phi_s = 0$ and the energy bands in the semiconductor are perfectly flat.

---

**Q.A (b)** A p-type silicon substrate has acceptor concentration $N_a = 10^{16} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$. Calculate the bulk Fermi potential $\phi_F$ and the semiconductor work function $\phi_S$ assuming an ideal MOS structure with aluminum gate ($\phi_M = 4.1 \, \text{V}$). State whether the system is in the flat-band condition at equilibrium. **(7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $N_a = 10^{16} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$
- $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$ (Si at 300 K)
- $\chi_{Si} = 4.05 \, \text{eV}$, $E_g = 1.12 \, \text{eV}$
- $\phi_M = 4.1 \, \text{V}$ (Aluminum)
- $V_T = 0.0259 \, \text{V}$

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate $\phi_F$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_F &= V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_a}{n_i}\right) \\
&= 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) \\
&= 0.0259 \times \ln(6.67 \times 10^5) \\
&= 0.0259 \times 13.41 \\
&= 0.347 \, \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\phi_S$ (2 marks):**

For p-type Si, $E_F$ lies below $E_i$ by $\phi_F \cdot q$. Since $E_i$ is at midgap, $E_c - E_F = E_g/2 + \phi_F \cdot q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_S &= \chi_{Si} + \frac{E_g}{2q} + \phi_F \\
&= 4.05 + \frac{1.12}{2} + 0.347 \\
&= 4.05 + 0.56 + 0.347 \\
&= 4.957 \, \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $\phi_{MS}$ (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_{MS} &= \phi_M - \phi_S = 4.1 - 4.957 = -0.857 \, \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the flat-band condition (2 marks):**
Since $\phi_{MS} = -0.857 \, \text{V} \neq 0$, this is **NOT** an ideal MOS. The system is **not in flat-band** at equilibrium. To achieve flat-band, a gate bias of $V_{FB} = +0.857 \, \text{V}$ must be applied (assuming $Q_{ox} = 0$).

The negative $\phi_{MS}$ would cause the bands to bend **upward** at the surface (hole accumulation) at $V_G = 0$.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q.B (a)** Draw the energy band diagram of an ideal MOS capacitor with a p-type silicon substrate at equilibrium. Label all relevant energy levels ($E_{vac}$, $E_c$, $E_v$, $E_F$, $E_i$) and indicate the position of the intrinsic Fermi level, the bulk potential $\phi_F$, and the electron affinity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Diagram (4 marks):**
- Draw four regions: Metal (gate) | Oxide | Si bulk | Si back contact.
- $E_{vac}$: horizontal, continuous.
- Oxide: rectangular barrier with $E_c^{ox}$ and $E_v^{ox}$ indicated.
- Si: $E_c, E_v$ shown as **flat horizontal lines** (ideal case).
- $E_F$: single horizontal line passing through the metal, the oxide, and the semiconductor.
- $E_i$: horizontal dashed line in the semiconductor at midgap.
- Label $\phi_F = (E_i - E_F)/q$ in the bulk.

**Explanation (3 marks):**
1. **Vacuum level** $E_{vac}$ is the reference; the electron affinity of Si ($\chi_{Si} = 4.05$ eV) sets $E_c$ at $\chi_{Si}$ below $E_{vac}$.
2. The valence band $E_v$ is 1.12 eV below $E_c$.
3. The intrinsic level $E_i$ is at midgap, i.e., 0.56 eV below $E_c$.
4. For p-type Si, $E_F$ lies **below** $E_i$ by $\phi_F \cdot q = V_T \ln(N_a/n_i) \cdot q$.
5. Since $\phi_{MS} = 0$ (ideal), $E_F$ is at the same vertical position on both metal and semiconductor sides → **flat bands**.

---

**Q.B (b)** Compare the energy band diagrams of (i) an ideal MOS capacitor with $\phi_{MS} = 0$, and (ii) a real MOS capacitor with $\phi_{MS} > 0$ (p-type Si), both at equilibrium with no applied gate bias. What type of surface condition (accumulation, depletion, or inversion) is created in each case? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

| **Property** | **Case (i): Ideal, $\phi_{MS} = 0$** | **Case (ii): Real, $\phi_{MS} > 0$** |
|--------------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Fermi level position | $E_F^M = E_F^S$ (same energy) | $E_F^M$ is **higher** than $E_F^S$ in the diagram |
| Band shape in semiconductor | **Flat** ($E_c, E_v$ horizontal) | **Bend downward** at the surface |
| Surface potential $\phi_s$ | $0$ | Negative (bands bend down) |
| Charge region | None | **Depletion** of holes |
| Surface condition | **Flat-band** | **Depletion** (with $\phi_{MS} > 0$, holes are repelled) |

**Explanation (3 marks):**

**Case (i):** With $\phi_{MS} = 0$, the equilibrium Fermi level is at the same energy on both sides, and no band bending occurs. The semiconductor is in the **flat-band** condition. There is no space-charge region, and the surface concentration of holes equals the bulk concentration.

**Case (ii):** With $\phi_{MS} > 0$, the metal Fermi level is higher than the semiconductor Fermi level. At equilibrium, the Fermi levels must align, so the semiconductor bands bend **downward** at the surface to bring $E_F$ up at the surface. This downward bending corresponds to a **negative surface potential** ($\phi_s < 0$). The hole concentration at the surface drops below the bulk value, creating a **depletion region** of ionized acceptors. If $\vert\phi_s\vert$ were larger (e.g., $2\phi_F$), the surface would invert to n-type, but for the moderate $\phi_{MS} > 0$ case, the surface is typically in **depletion**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** Writing $\phi_M$ in eV and $\phi_{MS}$ in Volts in the same equation. Always convert consistently.
> 2. **Forgetting the +0.56 eV midgap offset:** When computing $\phi_S$ for a doped semiconductor, students often use only $\chi_{Si} + \phi_F$, missing the $E_g/2$ term for the position of $E_i$ within the gap.
> 3. **Confusing flat-band with equilibrium:** Flat-band is a *special* equilibrium state ($\phi_{MS} = 0$ AND $V_G = 0$). General equilibrium only requires $E_F$ = constant.
> 4. **Drawing the oxide as a flat line:** The oxide must be drawn as a *rectangular barrier* with its 9 eV bandgap, not as a flat band.
> 5. **Incorrect sign of $\phi_{MS}$:** $\phi_{MS} = \phi_M - \phi_S$. Sign errors here propagate into the entire analysis.
> 6. **Drawing band bending in the oxide:** The oxide bands are *always* horizontal in the ideal model (no charge, no field variation along the oxide except in the presence of oxide charge).
> 7. **Using $n_i = 10^{10}$ vs $1.5 \times 10^{10}$:** Be consistent with the given $n_i$ value for Si at 300 K (the more accurate value is $1.5 \times 10^{10} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$, but textbooks may use $1.0 \times 10^{10}$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM:**

- [ ] An **ideal MOS capacitor** has $\phi_{MS} = 0$, $Q_{ox} = 0$, $D_{it} = 0$, perfect insulator, uniform doping.
- [ ] The **vacuum level** $E_{vac}$ is the **universal, continuous** energy reference across the entire MOS structure.
- [ ] The **Fermi level** $E_F$ is **constant** (horizontal) at thermal equilibrium.
- [ ] The ideal MOS at equilibrium with $V_G = 0$ is in the **flat-band condition**: bands are perfectly horizontal in the semiconductor.
- [ ] **Work function:** $\phi = (E_{vac} - E_F)/q$ (in Volts) — measures how strongly a material holds its electrons.
- [ ] **Electron affinity of Si:** $\chi_{Si} = 4.05$ eV (fixed, material property).
- [ ] **Bandgap of Si:** $E_g = 1.12$ eV at 300 K; **bandgap of $\text{SiO}_2$:** $E_{g,ox} = 9$ eV (insulating).
- [ ] **Si/$\text{SiO}_2$ band offsets:** $\Delta E_c = 3.15$ eV, $\Delta E_v = 4.68$ eV.
- [ ] **Semiconductor work function for p-type:**
      $\phi_S = \chi_{Si} + E_g/2 + \phi_F$ where $\phi_F = V_T \ln(N_a/n_i)$.
- [ ] **Work function difference:** $\phi_{MS} = \phi_M - \phi_S$.
- [ ] **Ideal condition:** $\phi_{MS} = 0 \Rightarrow$ no band bending at equilibrium.
- [ ] **Real condition:** $\phi_{MS} \neq 0 \Rightarrow$ bands bend even at $V_G = 0$, producing accumulation, depletion, or weak inversion.
- [ ] **Surface potential:** $\phi_s = (E_i^{surface} - E_i^{bulk})/q$. For ideal MOS at equilibrium, $\phi_s = 0$.
- [ ] **Flat-band voltage for real MOS:** $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$. For ideal, $V_{FB} = 0$.
- [ ] **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/q = 25.9$ mV.
- [ ] The **oxide is always drawn as a rectangular barrier** in the band diagram, with a height of 3.15 eV above the Si conduction band (for $\text{SiO}_2$).
- [ ] **Intrinsic Fermi level $E_i$** is at midgap (0.56 eV below $E_c$) for Si at 300 K.
- [ ] **Three key steps to build the diagram:** (1) draw $E_{vac}$ continuous, (2) place $E_c$ at $\chi_{Si}$ below $E_{vac}$, (3) place $E_F$ at $\phi_S$ below $E_{vac}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accumulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Accumulation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Accumulation** is one of the three fundamental operating regimes of a **Metal–Oxide–Semiconductor (MOS) Capacitor** structure, the other two being **depletion** and **inversion**. In this regime, a gate bias of the *same polarity* as the majority carriers of the semiconductor substrate is applied, causing an **abundant pile-up (accumulation) of majority carriers** immediately beneath the oxide–semiconductor interface. For a **p-type silicon substrate**, accumulation is established by applying a **sufficiently negative gate voltage** ($V_{GB} < V_{FB}$, where $V_{FB}$ is the flat-band voltage). The negative charge on the metal gate electrostatically attracts the positively charged holes toward the Si–SiO₂ interface, producing an **excess concentration of majority carriers** at the surface. For an **n-type substrate**, accumulation is induced by a **sufficiently positive gate voltage** ($V_{GB} > V_{FB}$), which attracts electrons toward the interface. > [!IMPORTANT] > The term **"accumulation"** in solid-state device physics is *not* the same as charge build-up on a capacitor plate. It specifically refers to a **non-equilibrium excess of mobile majority carriers** forming a thin sheet of high conductivity at the semiconductor surface. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowd at the Stadium Gate" Imagine a **stadium** (the semiconductor bulk) filled with people — the **majority carriers** (holes for p-type). Outside the stadium is a **giant loudspeaker on the gate** playing an *announcement they love* (the gate voltage of attractive polarity). - **No announcement (flat band):** People are spread evenly throughout the stadium. Surface concentration = Bulk concentration. - **Loud, attractive announcement (accumulation):** People **rush toward the stadium gate (oxide interface)** to listen better. A dense crowd forms at the gate. - **Announcement they hate (depletion):** People **move away** from the gate, leaving a near-empty zone near the gate. - **Reversed polarity + loud volume (inversion):** A new crowd (minority carriers) gathers at the gate because they are summoned by the strong field. The "crowd thickness" at the gate in accumulation is **not** a depletion region but an *excess* layer, mathematically described by an **exponential decay** away from the interface over a characteristic length called the **Debye length** ($L_D$). ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Standard physical constants used throughout this module:** > - Thermal voltage: $\phi_t = kT/q = \mathbf{0.0259\ V}$ at $T = 300\ K$ > - Permittivity of free space: $\epsilon_0 = \mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-14}\ F/cm}$ > - Relative permittivity of silicon: $\epsilon_{rs} = \mathbf{11.7}$ > - Relative permittivity of SiO₂: $\epsilon_{rox} = \mathbf{3.9}$ > - Silicon permittivity: $\epsilon_s = \epsilon_{rs} \epsilon_0 = \mathbf{1.04 \times 10^{-12}\ F/cm}$ > - Oxide permittivity: $\epsilon_{ox} = \epsilon_{rox} \epsilon_0 = \mathbf{3.45 \times 10^{-13}\ F/cm}$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Conditions for Accumulation in a p-type MOS Capacitor The gate-to-substrate voltage $V_{GB}$ and the surface potential $\phi_s$ govern the regime: | Quantity | Symbol | Condition in Accumulation (p-type) | |----------|--------|-------------------------------------| | Gate Voltage | $V_{GB}$ | $V_{GB} < V_{FB}$ (negative relative to flat band) | | Surface Potential | $\phi_s$ | $\phi_s < 0$ (bands bend **upward** at surface) | | Semiconductor Charge | $Q_s$ | $Q_s > 0$ (excess holes) | | Metal Charge | $Q_M$ | $Q_M < 0$ (negative charge on gate) | | Oxide Field | $\mathscr{E}_{ox}$ | Points from semiconductor → gate | ## 2.2 The Three Operational Logic Steps **Step 1 — Apply gate bias of attractive polarity.** A negative $V_{GB}$ is applied to the metal gate of a p-type MOS capacitor. Negative charge builds up on the metal plate. **Step 2 — Electrostatic attraction of majority carriers.** The negative gate charge electrostatically pulls holes (the majority carriers in p-type) toward the Si–SiO₂ interface. The hole concentration at the surface $p(0)$ becomes **much greater** than the bulk doping $N_A$. **Step 3 — Charge neutrality and band bending.** The high surface concentration of holes creates a thin, highly conductive sheet. Energy bands **bend upward** toward the surface (since $E_i - E_F$ becomes more negative, meaning more p-type character). The electric field is confined to a very thin region (Debye length) and the depletion approximation fails completely. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---------|---------------| | 1 | $\phi_t = kT/q$ | Thermal voltage ≈ **0.0259 V** at 300 K | | 2 | $L_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_s \phi_t}{q N_A}}$ | **Debye length** — characteristic penetration depth of field in accumulation | | 3 | $p(0) = N_A \, e^{-\phi_s/\phi_t}$ | Surface hole concentration in p-type substrate | | 4 | $Q_s = -\sqrt{2\epsilon_s q N_A \phi_t \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\phi_s}{\phi_t}\right) - \dfrac{\phi_s}{\phi_t} - 1\right]}$ | **Total semiconductor charge per unit area** (general expression) | | 5 | $Q_s \approx -\sqrt{2\epsilon_s q N_A \phi_t}\,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\phi_s}{2\phi_t}\right)$ | **Accumulation approximation** (dominant exponential term) | | 6 | $\mathscr{E}_s = -\dfrac{Q_s}{\epsilon_s} = \dfrac{|Q_s|}{\epsilon_s}$ | Electric field at the Si–SiO₂ interface (pointing into oxide) | | 7 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | **Oxide capacitance per unit area** | | 8 | $C = \dfrac{C_{ox} C_s}{C_{ox} + C_s}$ | Series combination of oxide and semiconductor capacitances | | 9 | $C_{s,\text{acc}} \to \infty$ | Semiconductor capacitance in strong accumulation | | 10 | $\boxed{C_{\text{acc}} \approx C_{ox}}$ | **Total MOS capacitance in strong accumulation** | > [!IMPORTANT] > In the formula table, vertical bars in expressions are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. The minus sign in $Q_s$ for p-type produces a **positive** $Q_s$ because $\phi_s$ is negative in accumulation — the two negatives cancel. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The accumulation regime is the **foundation of MOS capacitor operation** in the following technologies: - **MOSFET channel formation** — although channels operate in inversion, understanding accumulation is required to grasp the band-bending model. - **Charge-coupled devices (CCDs)** — pixels store signal charge in potential wells created under accumulation biases. - **Non-volatile memory (Flash, EEPROM)** — programming/erasing involves moving charges in and out of the accumulation regime. - **MOS varactors** — used in RF tuning circuits; the accumulation region provides the highest capacitance density. - **CMOS digital logic** — the sub-threshold leakage of OFF-state transistors is governed by the weak-accumulation-to-weak-inversion transition. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Debye Length in a p-type Semiconductor We begin from **Poisson's equation** in one dimension inside the semiconductor: $$ \frac{d^{2}\phi}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\epsilon_s} $$ For a p-type semiconductor with the electrostatic potential $\phi$ referenced such that $\phi = 0$ in the bulk (intrinsic level offset cancelled), the local hole and electron concentrations are: $$ p(x) = N_A \, e^{-\phi(x)/\phi_t} $$ $$ n(x) = \frac{n_i^{2}}{N_A} \, e^{\phi(x)/\phi_t} $$ The total space-charge density (per unit volume) is: $$ \rho(x) = q \left[ p(x) - n(x) - N_A^{-} \right] = q \left[ N_A e^{-\phi/\phi_t} - \frac{n_i^{2}}{N_A}e^{\phi/\phi_t} - N_A \right] $$ > **Logic:** This accounts for mobile holes, mobile electrons, and the ionized acceptor background. Multiplying both sides of Poisson's equation by $d\phi/dx$ and integrating: $$ \int \frac{d\phi}{dx} \frac{d^{2}\phi}{dx^{2}} \, dx = -\frac{1}{\epsilon_s} \int \rho(\phi) \frac{d\phi}{dx} \, dx $$ $$ \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)^{2} = -\frac{1}{\epsilon_s} \int \rho(\phi) \, d\phi $$ The integral evaluates to: $$ -\frac{1}{\epsilon_s}\int q\left[N_A e^{-\phi/\phi_t} - \frac{n_i^{2}}{N_A} e^{\phi/\phi_t} - N_A\right] d\phi $$ $$ = \frac{q N_A \phi_t}{\epsilon_s}\left[e^{-\phi/\phi_t} + \frac{n_i^{2}}{N_A^{2}} e^{\phi/\phi_t} - 1\right] - \frac{q N_A \phi}{\epsilon_s} $$ Applying the boundary condition $\phi(\infty) = 0$ and $d\phi/dx \big|_{x=\infty} = 0$: $$ \left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)^{2} = \frac{2 q N_A \phi_t}{\epsilon_s}\left[e^{-\phi/\phi_t} + \frac{n_i^{2}}{N_A^{2}} e^{\phi/\phi_t} - 1 + \frac{\phi}{\phi_t}\right] $$ The **Debye length** is defined as the length scale over which $\phi$ decays exponentially in weak non-uniformity: $$ \boxed{L_D = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_s \phi_t}{q N_A}}} $$ > **Logic:** In accumulation ($\phi_s < 0$, $\vert\phi_s\vert \gg \phi_t$), the exponential term $e^{-\phi_s/\phi_t}$ dominates. The charge density decays over a distance $\sim L_D$ from the interface. For $N_A = 10^{15}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, $L_D \approx 130\ \text{nm}$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Surface Charge Density $Q_s$ in Accumulation Using Gauss's law at the oxide–semiconductor interface ($x = 0$): $$ Q_s = \epsilon_s \mathscr{E}_s = -\epsilon_s \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right|_{x=0} $$ From the integrated Poisson result (square-root form): $$ \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = -\sqrt{\frac{2 q N_A \phi_t}{\epsilon_s}} \sqrt{e^{-\phi_s/\phi_t} + \frac{n_i^{2}}{N_A^{2}} e^{\phi_s/\phi_t} - 1 + \frac{\phi_s}{\phi_t}} $$ Therefore: $$ Q_s = \sqrt{2 \epsilon_s q N_A \phi_t \left[e^{-\phi_s/\phi_t} + \frac{n_i^{2}}{N_A^{2}} e^{\phi_s/\phi_t} - 1 + \frac{\phi_s}{\phi_t}\right]} $$ For p-type in accumulation ($\phi_s < 0$, $\vert\phi_s\vert \gg \phi_t$), the term $e^{-\phi_s/\phi_t}$ dominates the bracket: $$ Q_s \approx \sqrt{2 \epsilon_s q N_A \phi_t \, e^{-\phi_s/\phi_t}} $$ $$ \boxed{Q_s \approx \sqrt{2 \epsilon_s q N_A \phi_t} \; e^{-\phi_s/2\phi_t}} $$ > **Logic:** Note that $\phi_s$ is **negative**, so $-\phi_s/2\phi_t > 0$, making the exponential grow large — a small negative $\phi_s$ produces a large positive $Q_s$. The charge density is **not** proportional to $\phi_s$ linearly (unlike depletion), but grows **exponentially**. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Potential Profile $\phi(x)$ in Accumulation In strong accumulation the hole term dominates. Poisson's equation reduces to: $$ \frac{d^{2}\phi}{dx^{2}} \approx \frac{q N_A}{\epsilon_s} e^{-\phi/\phi_t} $$ Using the substitution $u = d\phi/dx$ so that $u \, du/dx = (1/2) d(u^{2})/dx$: $$ \frac{1}{2}\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)^{2} = \frac{q N_A}{\epsilon_s} e^{-\phi/\phi_t} \frac{d\phi}{dx} $$ Integrating from the bulk ($\phi = 0$, $d\phi/dx = 0$) to a point $x$: $$ \left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)^{2} = \frac{2 q N_A \phi_t}{\epsilon_s}\left(1 - e^{-\phi/\phi_t}\right) $$ Taking the square root and using the negative-root branch (since $\phi$ is negative and $\phi$ increases with $x$): $$ -\frac{d\phi}{dx} = \sqrt{\frac{2 q N_A \phi_t}{\epsilon_s}\left(1 - e^{-\phi/\phi_t}\right)} $$ Separating variables and integrating from $0$ to $x$ (with $\phi(0) = \phi_s$): $$ \int_{\phi_s}^{\phi} \frac{d\phi'}{\sqrt{1 - e^{-\phi'/\phi_t}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 q N_A \phi_t}{\epsilon_s}} \int_{0}^{x} dx' $$ After substitution $u = e^{-\phi'/2\phi_t}$, the integral becomes: $$ \int \frac{2\phi_t \, du}{u \sqrt{1-u^{2}}} = 2\phi_t \, \text{arcsech}(u) + C $$ Evaluating the limits: $$ 2\phi_t \left[\text{arcsech}\!\left(e^{-\phi/2\phi_t}\right) - \text{arcsech}\!\left(e^{-\phi_s/2\phi_t}\right)\right] = x \sqrt{\frac{2 q N_A \phi_t}{\epsilon_s}} $$ Solving for $\phi(x)$: $$ \boxed{\phi(x) = -2\phi_t \ln\!\left[\text{sech}\!\left(\frac{x}{L_D\sqrt{2}} + \text{arcsech}\!\left(e^{-\phi_s/2\phi_t}\right)\right)\right]} $$ For $\phi_s \ll 0$, the hyperbolic secant approximation simplifies to: $$ \phi(x) \approx -2\phi_t \ln\!\left[\frac{1}{2}e^{-x/L_D}\right] = 2\phi_t \ln 2 - \frac{2\phi_t x}{L_D} $$ > **Logic:** This shows $\phi$ grows linearly (not exponentially!) away from the interface for small $\phi(x)$ — a key result distinguishing the **Boltzmann (accumulation) profile** from the **parabolic (depletion) profile**. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capacitance in Accumulation The **differential semiconductor capacitance** per unit area is: $$ C_s = \frac{d\vert Q_s \vert}{d\phi_s} $$ Differentiating the accumulation approximation $Q_s \approx \sqrt{2 \epsilon_s q N_A \phi_t}\, e^{-\phi_s/2\phi_t}$: $$ C_s = \frac{1}{2\phi_t}\sqrt{2 \epsilon_s q N_A \phi_t}\, e^{-\phi_s/2\phi_t} = \frac{Q_s}{2\phi_t} $$ Substituting $Q_s$ and using $L_D$: $$ C_s = \frac{\epsilon_s}{\sqrt{2}\, L_D} \, e^{-\phi_s/2\phi_t} $$ > **Logic:** As $\phi_s$ becomes more negative, $C_s$ **grows exponentially**, i.e., $C_s \to \infty$. The total MOS capacitance is the series combination of $C_{ox}$ and $C_s$: $$ C = \frac{C_{ox} C_s}{C_{ox} + C_s} $$ As $C_s \to \infty$: $$ \boxed{\lim_{C_s \to \infty} C = C_{ox} = \frac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}} $$ > **Logic:** In strong accumulation, the MOS structure behaves as a **pure oxide capacitor**. The semiconductor is so highly conductive at the surface that it acts like a "metal plate" — making the device electrically identical to a parallel-plate capacitor with oxide dielectric. ## 3.5 Summary of Derived Expressions | Quantity | Accumulation Expression | Behavior | |----------|-------------------------|----------| | Charge density $\rho(x)$ | $\rho(x) \approx q N_A \, e^{-\phi(x)/\phi_t}$ | Exponential in $\phi$ | | Surface charge $Q_s$ | $Q_s \approx \sqrt{2\epsilon_s q N_A \phi_t}\, e^{-\phi_s/2\phi_t}$ | Exponential in $\phi_s$ | | Potential $\phi(x)$ | $\phi(x) \approx \text{const} - 2\phi_t \, x/L_D$ | Linear far from interface | | Field $\mathscr{E}(x)$ | $\mathscr{E}(x) \approx 2\phi_t/L_D$ | Constant magnitude | | Capacitance $C$ | $C \approx C_{ox}$ | Independent of $V_{GB}$ in strong accumulation | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — MOS Capacitor in Accumulation Regime The following block-level functional architecture map illustrates the physical structure, charge distribution, band-bending, and circuit equivalent for the accumulation regime in a p-type MOS capacitor. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["METAL GATE
Negative Voltage Applied VGB less than VFB"] --> B["NEGATIVE CHARGE QM
Accumulated on Metal Plate"] B --> C["Thin Oxide Layer SiO2
Thickness tox"] C --> D["Si-SiO2 INTERFACE x = 0
Acts as Capacitor Plate 2"] D --> E["ACCUMULATION LAYER
Excess Holes p greater than NA
Thickness approx LD Debye Length"] E --> F["p-type Si BULK
Doping NA acceptors
Equilibrium Region"] E --> G["Charge Density rho greater than 0
Exponential Decay over LD"] E --> H["Surface Potential phiS less than 0
Bands Bend Upward"] E --> I["Electric Field ES points into Oxide
Magnitude Qs over epsS"] J["Series Capacitor Model"] --> K["Oxide Capacitance Cox
epsOx divided by tox"] J --> L["Semiconductor Capacitance Cs
Approaches Infinity"] K --> M["Total Capacitance C in accumulation
C equals Cox since Cs dominates"] L --> M ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Charge & Field Distribution in Accumulation ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Distance x = 0
Interface"] --> P2["x approx LD
Charge Falls to Bulk"] P2 --> P3["x greater than 3 LD
Bulk Equilibrium"] Q1["Qs at x=0
Positive Maximum"] --> Q2["Decay
q NA exp of negative phi by phiT"] Q2 --> Q3["Equals 0 in Bulk"] F1["E-field Magnitude
Maximum at Interface"] --> F2["E equals 2 phiT divided by LD
Nearly Constant"] F2 --> F3["E equals 0 in Bulk"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison of MOS Regimes (C-V Curve Context) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Gate Voltage VGB Sweep Negative to Positive"] --> Reg1["REGION 1: ACCUMULATION
VGB less than VFB
Holes Accumulate
C equals Cox (Maximum)"] Reg1 --> Reg2["REGION 2: DEPLETION
VFB less than VGB less than VTH
Holes Depleted
C Decreases"] Reg2 --> Reg3["REGION 3: INVERSION
VGB greater than VTH
Electrons Form Channel
HF-C Stays Low LF-C Recovers to Cox"] Reg1 --> Note1["Surface Potential phiS less than 0
Bands Bend Upward"] Reg2 --> Note2["Depletion Width W
Charge equals q NA W"] Reg3 --> Note3["Surface Potential phiS greater than phiF
Strong Inversion Condition"] style Reg1 fill:#90EE90 style Reg2 fill:#FFD700 style Reg3 fill:#FFA07A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Legend:** Green = Accumulation (our current focus), Yellow = Depletion, Orange/Pink = Inversion. The C-V curve of a real MOS capacitor is reconstructed from these three regions, with the **accumulation region** forming the high-capacitance plateau on the left side (for p-type substrate). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define the accumulation regime in a MOS capacitor. State the conditions on gate voltage and surface potential for accumulation in a p-type substrate.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Accumulation is the regime of MOS capacitor operation in which the gate voltage polarity attracts majority carriers to the oxide–semiconductor interface, forming a thin layer of excess majority carrier concentration. For a p-type substrate, accumulation occurs when $V_{GB} < V_{FB}$, producing a surface potential $\phi_s < 0$. The bands bend upward, and the semiconductor charge $Q_s > 0$ due to the excess holes. The MOS structure behaves like a parallel-plate capacitor with capacitance $C \approx C_{ox}$. (1+1+1 = 3 marks) ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is the Debye length? Why is it the relevant length scale in the accumulation regime?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Debye length $L_D = \sqrt{\epsilon_s \phi_t / (q N_A)}$ is the characteristic distance over which mobile charge carriers screen an external electric field in a semiconductor. In accumulation, the excess majority carrier concentration decays exponentially into the bulk with this length scale — typically 30–300 nm. Unlike the depletion regime (where width depends on the square root of surface potential), the accumulation charge profile is governed by Boltzmann statistics over the Debye length, making it the natural length scale for this regime. (1+2 = 3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] **(a)** Derive the expression for the surface charge density $Q_s$ in a p-type MOS capacitor under accumulation conditions. Clearly state all assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Governing equation (1 mark):** Starting from Poisson's equation in the semiconductor: $$ \frac{d^{2}\phi}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\epsilon_s} $$ **Step 2 — Charge density expression (1 mark):** For p-type, the dominant mobile carrier term gives: $$ \rho(x) = q\left[N_A e^{-\phi/\phi_t} - \frac{n_i^{2}}{N_A}e^{\phi/\phi_t} - N_A\right] $$ **Step 3 — First integration (1.5 marks):** Multiply by $d\phi/dx$ and integrate using the boundary condition $d\phi/dx \to 0$ as $\phi \to 0$: $$ \left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)^{2} = \frac{2 q N_A \phi_t}{\epsilon_s}\left[e^{-\phi/\phi_t} + \frac{n_i^{2}}{N_A^{2}} e^{\phi/\phi_t} + \frac{\phi}{\phi_t} - 1\right] $$ **Step 4 — Apply accumulation approximation (1.5 marks):** For $\phi_s \ll -\phi_t$ (strong accumulation), the $e^{-\phi_s/\phi_t}$ term dominates: $$ \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right|_{x=0} \approx -\sqrt{\frac{2 q N_A \phi_t}{\epsilon_s}}\, e^{-\phi_s/2\phi_t} $$ **Step 5 — Apply Gauss's law (1 mark):** $Q_s = -\epsilon_s \, d\phi/dx\big|_{x=0}$: $$ \boxed{Q_s \approx \sqrt{2 \epsilon_s q N_A \phi_t}\; e^{-\phi_s/2\phi_t}} $$ **Step 6 — State assumptions (1 mark):** (i) Boltzmann approximation, (ii) $\phi_s \ll -\phi_t$, (iii) mobile holes dominate, (iv) one-dimensional analysis. --- **(b)** A p-type silicon MOS capacitor has $N_A = 10^{16}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, oxide thickness $t_{ox} = 20\ \text{nm}$, and is biased in accumulation with $\phi_s = -0.4\ \text{V}$. Calculate (i) the Debye length, (ii) the surface charge density, and (iii) the total MOS capacitance. **(7 marks)** **Given:** $N_A = 10^{16}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, $t_{ox} = 20 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{cm}$, $\phi_s = -0.4\ \text{V}$, $\phi_t = 0.0259\ \text{V}$. **Part (i) — Debye length (2 marks):** $$ L_D = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_s \phi_t}{q N_A}} = \sqrt{\frac{(1.04 \times 10^{-12})(0.0259)}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10^{16})}} $$ $$ L_D = \sqrt{\frac{2.694 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{1.684 \times 10^{-11}}\ \text{cm} $$ $$ \boxed{L_D \approx 1.30 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm} = 1.30\ \mu\text{m}} $$ **Part (ii) — Surface charge density (3 marks):** $$ Q_s = \sqrt{2 \epsilon_s q N_A \phi_t}\; e^{-\phi_s/2\phi_t} $$ Compute the prefactor: $$ \sqrt{2 \times (1.04 \times 10^{-12}) \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (10^{16}) \times (0.0259)} $$ $$ = \sqrt{8.62 \times 10^{-17}} = 9.28 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{C/cm}^{2} $$ Exponential factor: $$ e^{-(-0.4)/(2 \times 0.0259)} = e^{7.722} \approx 2253 $$ $$ Q_s \approx 9.28 \times 10^{-9} \times 2253 = 2.09 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{C/cm}^{2} $$ $$ \boxed{Q_s \approx 2.09 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{C/cm}^{2}} $$ **Part (iii) — Total MOS capacitance (2 marks):** Since the device is in strong accumulation ($C_s \to \infty$): $$ C = C_{ox} = \frac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}}{20 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.725 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{F/cm}^{2} $$ $$ \boxed{C \approx 1.725 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{F/cm}^{2} = 172.5\ \text{nF/cm}^{2}} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** Explain with energy-band diagrams the three regimes of operation of a p-type MOS capacitor. Discuss in detail the accumulation regime. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A p-type MOS capacitor exhibits three distinct regimes as the gate voltage $V_{GB}$ is varied. **(1 mark for enumeration)** **1. Accumulation ($V_{GB} < V_{FB}$, $\phi_s < 0$):** Bands bend **upward** at the surface. The Fermi level at the surface moves closer to the valence band edge, increasing the hole concentration above $N_A$. The surface becomes a thin highly p-type layer. **(2 marks)** **2. Depletion ($V_{FB} < V_{GB} < V_{TH}$, $0 < \phi_s < \phi_F$):** Bands bend **downward** at the surface. Holes are pushed away, exposing a region of fixed negative ionized acceptors of width $W$. The depletion approximation yields $\phi_s = qN_A W^{2}/(2\epsilon_s)$. **(2 marks)** **3. Inversion ($V_{GB} > V_{TH}$, $\phi_s > \phi_F$):** Bands bend strongly downward. The intrinsic level $E_i$ at the surface drops below the Fermi level $E_F$, meaning the surface becomes n-type — minority carrier electrons form an inversion layer of density $n_s \approx N_A e^{(\phi_s - 2\phi_F)/\phi_t}$. **(2 marks)** --- **(b)** For an n-type silicon MOS capacitor with $N_D = 5 \times 10^{15}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ and $t_{ox} = 10\ \text{nm}$, calculate the surface electron concentration when $\phi_s = +0.3\ \text{V}$. Comment on the regime of operation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Calculate the bulk Fermi potential $\phi_F$ (2 marks):** $$ \phi_F = \phi_t \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{15}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right) $$ $$ \phi_F = 0.0259 \times \ln(3.33 \times 10^{5}) = 0.0259 \times 12.717 $$ $$ \boxed{\phi_F \approx 0.329\ \text{V}} $$ **Step 2 — Surface electron concentration formula (1.5 marks):** For an n-type substrate, the surface electron concentration is: $$ n(0) = N_D \, e^{\phi_s/\phi_t} $$ **Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (2 marks):** $$ n(0) = 5 \times 10^{15} \times e^{0.3/0.0259} = 5 \times 10^{15} \times e^{11.58} $$ $$ e^{11.58} \approx 1.087 \times 10^{5} $$ $$ \boxed{n(0) \approx 5.44 \times 10^{20}\ \text{cm}^{-3}} $$ **Step 4 — Comment on regime (1.5 marks):** For an n-type substrate, a **positive** $\phi_s$ causes electrons (majority carriers) to **accumulate** at the interface. Since $\phi_s = +0.3\ \text{V} \gg +\phi_t$ and positive, the device is in **strong accumulation**. The MOS capacitance will saturate at $C_{ox} = \epsilon_{ox}/t_{ox} = 3.45 \times 10^{-13} / (10 \times 10^{-7}) = 3.45 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{F/cm}^{2}$. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common pitfalls where students lose marks in this topic:** > 1. **Sign errors in $Q_s$** — Forgetting that for p-type in accumulation, $\phi_s$ is negative, making $Q_s$ positive. (–1 mark) > 2. **Confusing accumulation and depletion approximations** — In accumulation, the exponential term $e^{-\phi_s/\phi_t}$ dominates; in depletion, the linear $\phi_s/\phi_t$ term dominates. Mixing them yields wrong orders of magnitude. (–2 marks) > 3. **Writing $C = C_{ox}$ in depletion** — This is valid **only in strong accumulation**; in depletion $C < C_{ox}$ and is voltage-dependent. (–1 mark) > 4. **Forgetting the flat-band voltage** — Accumulation condition is $V_{GB} < V_{FB}$, not $V_{GB} < 0$. For MOS systems with work-function difference and oxide charge, $V_{FB} \neq 0$. (–1 mark) > 5. **Units confusion** — $\epsilon_s$ in F/cm and $N_A$ in cm⁻³; mixing SI and CGS gives answers off by factors of 10. (–1 mark) > 6. **Missing the Boltzmann factor** — Surface concentration in accumulation is $p(0) = N_A e^{-\phi_s/\phi_t}$, **not** $N_A + |\phi_s|$-type linear expression. (–2 marks) --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - ☐ **Accumulation** = Majority carriers pile up at the Si–SiO₂ interface due to attractive gate bias. - ☐ **p-type condition:** $V_{GB} < V_{FB}$, $\phi_s < 0$, $Q_s > 0$ (holes). - ☐ **n-type condition:** $V_{GB} > V_{FB}$, $\phi_s > 0$, $Q_s < 0$ (electrons). - ☐ **Debye length:** $L_D = \sqrt{\epsilon_s \phi_t / (q N_A)}$ — typical values 30 nm to 1 µm. - ☐ **Surface concentration:** $p(0) = N_A e^{-\phi_s/\phi_t}$ (Boltzmann statistics). - ☐ **$Q_s$ in accumulation:** $Q_s \approx \sqrt{2 \epsilon_s q N_A \phi_t}\, e^{-\phi_s/2\phi_t}$ — grows **exponentially** with $\vert\phi_s\vert$. - ☐ **Capacitance:** $C \approx C_{ox} = \epsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ in strong accumulation (frequency-independent). - ☐ **Bands bend upward** at the surface for p-type in accumulation (more p-type character). - ☐ **No depletion region** in accumulation — the depletion approximation **fails** here. - ☐ **Equilibrium** prevails — no current flows, no recombination-generation; Fermi level is flat throughout the device. - ☐ **Potential profile** decays linearly far from interface: $\phi(x) \approx 2\phi_t \ln 2 - 2\phi_t x/L_D$. - ☐ **Charge neutrality:** $Q_M + Q_s = 0$ (zero net charge on the MOS system). - ☐ **Sign convention:** $\phi_s$ measured from bulk; $E_i$ reference cancels. - ☐ **Real-world use:** MOS varactors, CCD pixels, Flash memory, sub-threshold MOSFET leakage analysis.

topics.title: Depletion and Inversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Depletion and Inversion # ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of a **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor (MOS) capacitor** (the foundational structure of every MOSFET, CMOS logic gate, and CCD image sensor), the terms **Depletion** and **Inversion** describe the two critical equilibrium/ non-equilibrium states of the semiconductor surface when an external gate bias $V_G$ is applied. - **Depletion Region:** The volume near the semiconductor-oxide interface that is completely devoid of mobile charge carriers (electrons in n-type, holes in p-type), containing only ionized dopant atoms (the depletion charge $Q_B$). It is created when the applied gate voltage repels the majority carriers away from the oxide-semiconductor interface. - **Inversion Layer:** A thin ($\approx 1\text{–}10\ \text{nm}$) sheet of minority carriers that forms a conducting channel immediately adjacent to the oxide-semiconductor interface once the gate voltage exceeds a critical threshold value $V_T$. The surface type is said to be "inverted" because the conductivity type of the surface has flipped (e.g., a p-type surface now behaves like n-type). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEAET412 Module 3):** The transition from Accumulation $\rightarrow$ Depletion $\rightarrow$ Inversion is the *entire operating principle* of a MOSFET. The inversion layer is what physically constitutes the "channel" through which drain current $I_D$ flows. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine the MOS structure as a **water reservoir with a controllable floodgate** (the gate electrode). - **No bias (Flat-band):** The water (carriers) is evenly distributed. The reservoir is calm. - **Small bias (Accumulation):** A weak attraction pulls water toward the gate-side wall of the reservoir, creating a dense layer of water at the wall. - **Moderate bias (Depletion):** The gate now *repels* the water. A wide region near the wall becomes completely empty of water, leaving only the fixed "mud pillars" (ionized dopant atoms) standing in the void. This empty stretch is the **depletion region**. - **Strong bias (Inversion):** The repulsion becomes so intense that water from the *opposite end* of the reservoir (minority carriers from the bulk) is sucked *through* the mud pillars to coat the wall with a *new* thin sheet of water. This new sheet — composed of a *different* type of water — is the **inversion layer**. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants to Memorize for KTU:** > - Silicon intrinsic carrier concentration at 300 K: $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ > - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{F/cm}$ > - Relative permittivity of silicon: $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7$ > - Relative permittivity of SiO₂: $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9$ ## 1.3 Why "Inversion" is the Magic Word in VLSI In modern CMOS technology, the formation of the inversion layer is what *turns the transistor ON*. Without inversion, no channel exists, and no current flows. The threshold voltage $V_T$ — the parameter that defines the switching threshold of billions of transistors on a microchip — is precisely the gate voltage at which the surface electron concentration equals the bulk hole concentration (or vice-versa). # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Regimes of the MOS Surface For a p-type silicon substrate with an n⁺ polysilicon gate, as we sweep $V_G$ from large negative to large positive values, the surface passes through three distinct regimes: | Regime | Gate Voltage $V_G$ | Surface Condition | Dominant Charge | Width | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Accumulation** | $V_G < V_{FB}$ | $p_s > p_{p0}$ (more holes piled up) | Mobile holes (majority) | $\approx 0$ | | **Depletion** | $V_{FB} < V_G < V_T$ | $p_s < p_{p0}$, $n_s \ll p_s$ | Fixed ionized acceptors $N_A^-$ | $x_d$ grows with $V_G$ | | **Inversion** | $V_G > V_T$ | $n_s > p_{p0}$ (surface type flipped) | Mobile electrons (minority) | $x_{d,\max} \approx \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\phi_s / qN_A}$ | Where: - $V_{FB}$ = **Flat-band voltage** (typically $-0.5$ to $-1.0\ \text{V}$ for n⁺-poly on p-Si) - $V_T$ = **Threshold voltage** (typically $0.4$ to $0.7\ \text{V}$ for modern CMOS) - $p_s$ = surface hole concentration, $n_s$ = surface electron concentration - $p_{p0} = N_A$ = bulk doping (acceptor) ## 2.2 Surface Potential $\phi_s$ and Its Critical Values The **surface potential** $\phi_s$ is the electrostatic potential at the oxide-semiconductor interface relative to the neutral bulk. It is the master variable that governs *all* MOS electrostatics. | Surface Condition | Surface Potential $\phi_s$ | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | Flat-band | $\phi_s = 0$ | No band bending; bulk-like carrier distribution | | Onset of depletion | $\phi_s = 0$ (start of transition) | Carriers exactly equal bulk values | | Onset of inversion | $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ | Surface electron density = bulk hole density | | Strong inversion | $\phi_s > 2\phi_F$ | $n_s > p_{p0}$; surface is strongly n-type | The **Fermi potential** of the p-type bulk is: $$\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why $2\phi_F$ and not $\phi_F$?** At $\phi_s = \phi_F$, the surface *intrinsic* condition is reached ($n_s = p_s = n_i$). To flip the surface type completely, the bands must bend by an *additional* $\phi_F$, giving a total band bending of $2\phi_F$ before inversion begins. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \frac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}} - \frac{qN_A x_{d0}}{C_{ox}}$ | Flat-band voltage | V | | 2 | $\phi_{MS} = \phi_M - \phi_S = \phi_M - \left(\chi_S + \frac{E_g}{2q} + \phi_F\right)$ | Metal-semiconductor work-function difference | V | | 3 | $C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$ | Oxide capacitance per unit area | F/cm² | | 4 | $Q_B = -qN_A x_d$ | Bulk depletion charge per unit area | C/cm² | | 5 | $x_d = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}\,\phi_s}{qN_A}}$ | Depletion region width | cm | | 6 | $x_{d,\max} = \sqrt{\frac{4\varepsilon_{si}\,\phi_F}{qN_A}}$ | Max depletion width at onset of strong inversion | cm | | 7 | $V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A\,(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$ | **Threshold voltage (long-channel)** | V | | 8 | $V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{4\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A\,\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$ | Equivalent form using $x_{d,\max}$ | V | | 9 | $n_s = n_i\,e^{q\phi_s / kT}$ | Surface electron density | cm⁻³ | | 10 | $p_s = n_i\,e^{-q\phi_s / kT}$ | Surface hole density | cm⁻³ | | 11 | $Q_n = -C_{ox}(V_G - V_T)$ | Inversion layer charge per unit area | C/cm² | | 12 | $Q_B = -\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A\,(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$ | Bulk charge including substrate bias | C/cm² | | 13 | $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | Body-effect coefficient | V$^{1/2}$ | | 14 | $V_T(V_{SB}) = V_{T0} + \gamma\left[\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right]$ | Threshold voltage with body effect | V | | 15 | $\tau_n = \frac{x_{d,\max}}{2 D_n}$ | Minority carrier generation lifetime in depletion | s | | 16 | $I_{gen} = \frac{q n_i W x_{d,\max}}{2 \tau_n}$ | Reverse-biased generation current per unit area | A/cm² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Equations 7, 8, 13, and 14 are **must-memorize** items for KTU Module 3 numerical problems. KTU examiners *love* asking for the threshold voltage derivation and the body-effect modification. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Threshold voltage tuning** is the primary design knob in CMOS fabrication. By adjusting $N_A$ (channel doping) and $t_{ox}$ (oxide thickness), IC designers precisely set $V_T$ to balance ON-current vs. OFF-current (leakage). - **Body effect (Eq. 14)** is the reason source-follower circuits and cascode stages have degraded transconductance — a critical consideration in analog CMOS design. - **The 2$\phi_F$ rule** sets the minimum surface potential for inversion and hence the minimum $V_T$ for any given process node. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Threshold Voltage $V_T$ of a Long-Channel MOS Capacitor We will derive the threshold voltage from first principles using **charge balance** in the MOS structure at the onset of strong inversion. ### Step 1 — Apply Gauss's Law to the Oxide and Semiconductor For a p-type substrate with gate voltage $V_G$ applied, Gauss's law applied to the oxide layer (containing no free charge) gives: $$V_{ox} = \frac{|Q_S|}{C_{ox}}$$ where $Q_S$ is the total semiconductor surface charge density (the sum of depletion charge $Q_B$ and inversion charge $Q_n$), and $V_{ox}$ is the voltage drop across the oxide. ### Step 2 — Write the Gate Voltage Equation Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) across the gate, oxide, and semiconductor: $$V_G = V_{FB} + V_{ox} + \phi_s$$ Substituting $V_{ox}$: $$V_G = V_{FB} + \frac{|Q_S|}{C_{ox}} + \phi_s$$ ### Step 3 — Define the Onset of Strong Inversion At strong inversion, $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ and the inversion charge is just starting to become significant. We approximate $|Q_S| \approx |Q_B|$ (depletion charge dominates at the threshold point). ### Step 4 — Express the Depletion Charge at Threshold The depletion region extends a distance $x_d$ into the p-type bulk, exposing ionized acceptors: $$Q_B = -q N_A x_d$$ where $x_d$ is determined by solving Poisson's equation in the depletion region with boundary conditions $\phi(0) = 2\phi_F$ at the surface and $\phi(x_d) = 0$ in the neutral bulk: $$\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2} = \frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}$$ Integrating twice with $E(x_d) = 0$ (neutral bulk): $$\phi(x) = \frac{qN_A}{2\varepsilon_{si}}(x_d - x)^2$$ Setting $\phi(0) = 2\phi_F$: $$2\phi_F = \frac{qN_A}{2\varepsilon_{si}} x_d^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_{d,\max} = \sqrt{\frac{4\varepsilon_{si}\phi_F}{qN_A}}$$ Therefore: $$\vert Q_{B,\max} \vert = qN_A \sqrt{\frac{4\varepsilon_{si}\phi_F}{qN_A}} = \sqrt{4\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A\,\phi_F}$$ ### Step 5 — Assemble the Threshold Voltage Substitute $\vert Q_{B,\max} \vert$ and $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ into the gate voltage equation, recognizing that at $V_G = V_T$: $$\boxed{\;V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{4\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A\,\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Algebraic Verification:** Using $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$, this can be equivalently written as: > $$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{t_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{ox}}\sqrt{4\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A\,\phi_F}$$ > This is the form KTU examiners prefer for numerical substitution problems. ## 3.2 Derivation: Surface Potential and Carrier Concentrations Starting from Boltzmann statistics, the electron and hole concentrations at the surface are: $$n_s = n_i\,e^{q(\phi_s - \phi_F)/kT}, \quad p_s = n_i\,e^{-q(\phi_s + \phi_F)/kT}$$ (These follow from the fact that the intrinsic Fermi level $E_i$ is displaced by $q\phi_s$ at the surface relative to the bulk.) **At flat-band ($\phi_s = 0$):** $$n_s = n_i\,e^{-q\phi_F/kT} = \frac{n_i^2}{N_A}, \quad p_s = n_i\,e^{q\phi_F/kT} = N_A$$ This correctly reproduces the bulk equilibrium values. ✓ **At onset of inversion ($\phi_s = 2\phi_F$):** $$n_s = n_i\,e^{q\phi_F/kT} = N_A, \quad p_s = n_i\,e^{-3q\phi_F/kT} = \frac{n_i^4}{N_A^3}$$ So $n_s = N_A$ and $p_s \ll N_A$ — the surface is now **n-type** despite being in a **p-type** substrate. This is the **inversion condition**. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A p-type Si MOS capacitor has $N_A = 10^{16}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, $t_{ox} = 10\ \text{nm}$, and an n⁺ polysilicon gate. Calculate the threshold voltage. Use $V_{FB} = -0.9\ \text{V}$, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$. **Step 1 — Compute Fermi potential:** $$\phi_F = \frac{0.0259}{1}\ln\left(\frac{10^{16}}{1.5\times10^{10}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(6.67\times10^5)$$ $$\phi_F = 0.0259 \times 13.41 = 0.347\ \text{V}$$ **Step 2 — Compute oxide capacitance:** $$C_{ox} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854\times10^{-14}}{10\times10^{-7}} = \frac{3.453\times10^{-13}}{10^{-6}} = 3.453\times10^{-7}\ \text{F/cm}^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute depletion term:** $$\sqrt{4\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A\,\phi_F} = \sqrt{4 \times 11.7 \times 8.854\times10^{-14} \times 1.6\times10^{-19} \times 10^{16} \times 0.347}$$ $$= \sqrt{4 \times 1.036\times10^{-12} \times 5.55\times10^{-4}}$$ $$= \sqrt{2.300\times10^{-15}} = 4.796\times10^{-8}\ \text{C/cm}^2$$ **Step 4 — Assemble $V_T$:** $$V_T = -0.9 + 2(0.347) + \frac{4.796\times10^{-8}}{3.453\times10^{-7}}$$ $$V_T = -0.9 + 0.694 + 0.139 = -0.067\ \text{V}$$ > [!NOTE] > This *negative* $V_T$ (a **depletion-mode** or *normally-on* device) occurs when the work-function difference $\phi_{MS}$ is very large and negative. In modern CMOS, ion implantation is used to adjust $N_A$ until $V_T \approx +0.5\ \text{V}$ (an *enhancement-mode* device). # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 MOS Band Diagram Across the Three Regimes The following Mermaid block renders the band-bending evolution as $V_G$ increases from negative to positive values. This is the diagram KTU examiners *always* expect in Part A/B answers. ```mermaid graph LR A["FLAT BAND
V_G = V_FB"] --> B["ACCUMULATION
V_G less than V_FB
Holes pile up
Bands bend UPWARD"] B --> C["DEPLETION
V_FB less than V_G less than V_T
Holes pushed away
Bands bend DOWNWARD
x_d grows with V_G"] C --> D["WEAK INVERSION
V_G near V_T
Few minority electrons
Band bending = 2 phi_F"] D --> E["STRONG INVERSION
V_G greater than V_T
Dense electron sheet
n_s greater than N_A
Inversion layer formed"] E --> F["DEEP DEPLETION
V_G ramped too fast
x_d exceeds x_d_max
No inversion yet
Used in CCDs"] style A fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333 style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style C fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 style D fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#993300 style E fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000 style F fill:#ccccff,stroke:#333399 ``` ## 4.2 Charge Distribution at the Onset of Strong Inversion The next diagram maps the physical charge layers in the MOS stack at the **threshold point** — the configuration that *defines* the threshold voltage. ```mermaid graph TB M["GATE METAL
n+ polysilicon
Charge +Q_G"] OX["SiO2 OXIDE
Thickness t_ox
Capacitance C_ox
NO free charge here
Fixed Q_ox trapped"] INV["INVERSION LAYER
Thickness approx 1 to 10 nm
Electron sheet charge Q_n
Mobile MINORITY carriers"] DEP["DEPLETION REGION
Width x_d_max
Fixed ionized acceptors N_A-
Charge Q_B negative"] BULK["NEUTRAL p-BULK
Doping N_A
Field E equals 0 here"] M -->|V_ox| OX OX -->|phi_s equals 2 phi_F| INV INV --> DEP DEP --> BULK style M fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333 style OX fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#003366 style INV fill:#ff6666,stroke:#990000 style DEP fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 style BULK fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366 ``` ## 4.3 The C-V Curve: Capacitance vs. Gate Voltage The high-frequency and low-frequency C-V characteristics are the most powerful experimental fingerprints of the depletion-to-inversion transition. ```mermaid graph LR A0["V_G very negative
ACCUMULATION
C equals C_ox
High capacitance"] --> A1 A1["V_G = V_FB
FLAT-BAND
C equals C_FB"] --> A2 A2["DEPLETION
C drops
C_s in series with C_ox
1 over C squared linear with V_G"] --> A3 A3["WEAK INVERSION
HF curve: C continues to drop
LF curve: C recovers toward C_ox"] --> A4 A4["V_G = V_T
STRONG INVERSION
HF curve: C_min plateau
LF curve: C returns to C_ox
Minority carriers can follow"] --> A5 A5["V_G greater than V_T
DEEP DEPLETION
if swept fast: C_ox-like
if swept slow: inversion follows"] style A0 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style A1 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333 style A2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 style A3 fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#993300 style A4 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000 style A5 fill:#ccccff,stroke:#333399 ``` ## 4.4 Functional Flow: From Gate Bias to Drain Current This flowchart shows how the *microscopic* depletion/inversion physics we studied maps onto the *macroscopic* transistor behavior in a MOSFET. ```mermaid graph TD P0["Gate bias V_G applied"] --> P1{"V_G compared to V_FB"} P1 -->|"V_G less than V_FB"| P2["ACCUMULATION
Holes at surface
No channel
OFF state"] P1 -->|"V_G greater than V_FB"| P3{"V_G compared to V_T"} P3 -->|"V_G less than V_T"| P4["DEPLETION
No mobile carriers
No channel
OFF state, I_D approx 0"] P3 -->|"V_G greater than V_T"| P5["INVERSION LAYER FORMS
Electron channel created
ON state"] P5 --> P6["Drain bias V_DS applied"] P6 --> P7["I_D flows through channel
I_D = mu C_ox W slash 2L times V_GS minus V_T squared"] P4 --> P8["Subthreshold leakage
I_D = I_0 exp V_GS slash n V_T
Small but nonzero"] P5 --> P9{"V_DS compared to V_GS minus V_T"} P9 -->|"V_DS small"| P10["TRIODE REGION
Channel uniform
I_D linear in V_DS"] P9 -->|"V_DS large"| P11["PINCH-OFF
Channel pinches at drain
SATURATION
I_D saturates"] style P2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style P4 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 style P5 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000 style P8 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#996600 style P10 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style P11 fill:#ccffff,stroke:#006666 ``` # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **Define the terms 'depletion region' and 'inversion layer' in a MOS capacitor. State the gate-voltage condition for the onset of strong inversion in a p-type substrate.** **Model Answer:** - **Depletion region:** The volume of the semiconductor adjacent to the oxide-semiconductor interface that has been swept free of mobile charge carriers by the applied gate voltage. It contains only the immobile ionized dopant atoms (acceptor ions $N_A^-$ for a p-substrate), and its width $x_d$ grows with the applied gate bias. - **Inversion layer:** A thin sheet of mobile minority carriers (electrons for a p-substrate) that forms at the oxide-semiconductor interface once the gate voltage exceeds the threshold voltage $V_T$. It constitutes the conducting channel in a MOSFET. - **Condition for onset of strong inversion:** The surface potential must reach $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$, where $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$ is the bulk Fermi potential. Equivalently, the surface electron concentration $n_s$ must equal the bulk hole concentration $N_A$. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall (KTU 2024):** Students often write $\phi_s = \phi_F$ for the inversion condition. This is the **intrinsic** surface condition, *not* inversion. The correct answer is $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **Distinguish between weak inversion and strong inversion. What is the threshold voltage $V_T$?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Weak Inversion | Strong Inversion | |---|---|---| | Surface potential | $\phi_F < \phi_s < 2\phi_F$ | $\phi_s \geq 2\phi_F$ | | Surface carrier | $n_i < n_s < N_A$ (minority) | $n_s \geq N_A$ | | Channel | Negligible conduction | Substantial channel | | Gate voltage | $V_T - \text{a few}\ \phi_T < V_G < V_T$ | $V_G \geq V_T$ | The **threshold voltage $V_T$** is defined as the gate voltage at which the surface electron concentration equals the bulk doping $N_A$, marking the onset of strong inversion. For a long-channel device: $$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{4\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A\,\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **(a)** Derive the expression for the threshold voltage $V_T$ of a MOS capacitor with a p-type silicon substrate and an n⁺ polysilicon gate. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the device in (a), the following parameters are given: $N_A = 5 \times 10^{16}\ \text{cm}^{-3},\ t_{ox} = 5\ \text{nm},\ V_{FB} = -1.0\ \text{V},\ n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$. Compute: (i) the Fermi potential $\phi_F$, (ii) the maximum depletion width $x_{d,\max}$ at threshold, (iii) the threshold voltage $V_T$, and (iv) the body-effect coefficient $\gamma$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): 1. **Assumption:** 1-D structure, equilibrium, no oxide charge variation, abrupt depletion approximation, onset of strong inversion defined by $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$. 2. **KVL across MOS structure:** $$V_G = V_{FB} + V_{ox} + \phi_s$$ 3. **Gauss's law across oxide:** $$V_{ox} = \frac{|Q_S|}{C_{ox}}, \quad C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$$ 4. **Poisson's equation in depletion region:** $$\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2} = \frac{qN_A}{\varepsilon_{si}}$$ 5. **Solution with $E(x_d) = 0$ and $\phi(x_d) = 0$:** $$\phi(x) = \frac{qN_A}{2\varepsilon_{si}}(x_d - x)^2$$ 6. **At threshold** ($\phi(0) = 2\phi_F$): $$2\phi_F = \frac{qN_A x_{d,\max}^2}{2\varepsilon_{si}} \Rightarrow x_{d,\max} = \sqrt{\frac{4\varepsilon_{si}\phi_F}{qN_A}}$$ 7. **Depletion charge at threshold:** $$Q_B = -qN_A x_{d,\max} = -\sqrt{4\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A\,\phi_F}$$ 8. **Substitute into KVL** with $|Q_S| \approx |Q_B|$ and $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$: $$\boxed{V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{4\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A\,\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}}$$ #### Model Solution to (b): **(i) Fermi potential:** $$\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.0259 \ln\left(\frac{5\times10^{16}}{1.5\times10^{10}}\right)$$ $$= 0.0259 \times \ln(3.33\times10^6) = 0.0259 \times 15.02 = 0.389\ \text{V}$$ **(ii) Maximum depletion width:** $$x_{d,\max} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 11.7 \times 8.854\times10^{-14} \times 0.389}{1.6\times10^{-19} \times 5\times10^{16}}}$$ $$= \sqrt{\frac{1.612\times10^{-12}}{8.0\times10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{2.015\times10^{-10}} = 1.42\times10^{-5}\ \text{cm} = 142\ \text{nm}$$ **(iii) Threshold voltage:** First, compute $C_{ox}$: $$C_{ox} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854\times10^{-14}}{5\times10^{-7}} = 6.906\times10^{-7}\ \text{F/cm}^2$$ Then: $$V_T = -1.0 + 2(0.389) + \frac{\sqrt{4 \times 11.7 \times 8.854\times10^{-14} \times 1.6\times10^{-19} \times 5\times10^{16} \times 0.389}}{6.906\times10^{-7}}$$ The numerator equals $qN_A x_{d,\max} = 1.6\times10^{-19} \times 5\times10^{16} \times 1.42\times10^{-5} = 1.136\times10^{-7}\ \text{C/cm}^2$. $$V_T = -1.0 + 0.778 + \frac{1.136\times10^{-7}}{6.906\times10^{-7}} = -1.0 + 0.778 + 0.164 = -0.058\ \text{V}$$ **(iv) Body-effect coefficient:** $$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A}}{C_{ox}} = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 11.7 \times 8.854\times10^{-14} \times 1.6\times10^{-19} \times 5\times10^{16}}}{6.906\times10^{-7}}$$ $$= \frac{\sqrt{1.658\times10^{-15}}}{6.906\times10^{-7}} = \frac{4.072\times10^{-8}}{6.906\times10^{-7}} = 0.0590\ \text{V}^{1/2}$$ --- ### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **(a)** Explain the charge distribution and band-bending in a p-type MOS capacitor under (i) accumulation, (ii) depletion, and (iii) strong inversion. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the body-effect equation: $V_T(V_{SB}) = V_{T0} + \gamma\left[\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right]$. Hence explain the significance of the body-effect coefficient $\gamma$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **(i) Accumulation** ($V_G < V_{FB}$): Holes are attracted to the oxide-semiconductor interface, creating a thin layer of majority-carrier charge. The bands bend **upward** at the surface; $E_F$ moves closer to the valence band edge $E_V$. No depletion region; surface hole concentration exceeds $N_A$. **(ii) Depletion** ($V_{FB} < V_G < V_T$): The positive gate voltage repels holes, exposing a region of width $x_d$ containing only ionized acceptors $N_A^-$. The bands bend **downward** at the surface. The depletion width $x_d$ increases with $V_G$ according to $x_d = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\phi_s / qN_A}$. **(iii) Strong Inversion** ($V_G > V_T$): The band bending exceeds $2\phi_F$; the intrinsic level $E_i$ at the surface drops below the bulk Fermi level $E_F$, making the surface effectively n-type. An electron inversion layer of thickness $\approx 1\text{–}10\ \text{nm}$ forms at the interface. The depletion width saturates at $x_{d,\max} = \sqrt{4\varepsilon_{si}\phi_F / qN_A}$. #### Model Solution to (b): Starting from the general threshold equation with a substrate-to-source bias $V_{SB}$ applied (reverse-biasing the source-substrate p-n junction): $$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A}}{C_{ox}}\,\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$$ Let $V_{T0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A}}{C_{ox}}\sqrt{2\phi_F}$ be the threshold with $V_{SB} = 0$, and define $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A}}{C_{ox}}$. Then: $$V_T(V_{SB}) = V_{T0} + \gamma\left[\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right]$$ **Significance of $\gamma$:** - It is a **figure of merit for the body effect** — a small $\gamma$ (small $N_A$, small $t_{ox}$) means the body has minimal influence on $V_T$, which is desirable in modern short-channel CMOS to suppress threshold roll-off. - It has units of $\text{V}^{1/2}$ and quantifies how many volts $V_T$ shifts per volt of $V_{SB}$. - A high $\gamma$ indicates the transistor is more sensitive to substrate bias, which is undesirable in analog design. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. In (a), do **not** confuse "depletion" with "depletion-mode device." Depletion is a *surface state*; depletion-mode is a *device type* (always-on). > 2. In the body-effect derivation, the term $\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ comes from the depletion charge formula $Q_B = -\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$. **Do not drop the $2\phi_F$ inside the square root** — it is *not* a typo. > 3. In numerical problems, **always include units** in intermediate steps. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit in long derivations. > 4. The maximum depletion width $x_{d,\max}$ is *evaluated* at the *onset* of strong inversion, not inside saturation. In saturation, the depletion region near the drain widens further (the *pinch-off* region), but $x_{d,\max}$ refers specifically to the channel-end value at $V_G = V_T$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:** - ☐ The four regimes of a p-type MOS capacitor (sweep $V_G$ from negative to positive): **Accumulation → Flat-band → Depletion → Inversion**. - ☐ Onset of strong inversion: $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$, equivalently $n_s = N_A$ at the surface. - ☐ Fermi potential of p-type bulk: $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$; at 300 K this is typically $0.3$ to $0.4\ \text{V}$ for $N_A \sim 10^{16}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$. - ☐ Depletion width formula: $x_d = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\phi_s / qN_A}$, maximum at $x_{d,\max} = \sqrt{4\varepsilon_{si}\phi_F / qN_A}$. - ☐ Oxide capacitance: $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox} = 3.9\varepsilon_0 / t_{ox}$ (F/cm² when $t_{ox}$ in cm). - ☐ Threshold voltage: $V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \sqrt{4\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A\,\phi_F}/C_{ox}$. - ☐ Body-effect coefficient: $\gamma = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\,qN_A}/C_{ox}$, with units $\text{V}^{1/2}$. - ☐ Body-effect equation: $V_T(V_{SB}) = V_{T0} + \gamma[\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}]$. - ☐ Inversion layer charge: $Q_n = -C_{ox}(V_G - V_T)$ — this is the cornerstone of MOSFET current equations. - ☐ $V_{FB}$ depends on $\phi_{MS}$, $Q_{ox}$, and any intentional doping profile adjustments. - ☐ The 2$\phi_F$ rule is a *minimum*; practical CMOS uses additional "halo" implants to *raise* $V_T$ for low-leakage operation. - ☐ In the **high-frequency** C-V curve, capacitance saturates at $C_{\min}$ in strong inversion; in the **low-frequency** (or quasi-static) curve, it returns to $C_{ox}$. - ☐ **Weak inversion (subthreshold)** conduction is exponential in $V_G$, not quadratic — critical for low-power analog design. - ☐ In deep depletion, the depletion width *exceeds* $x_{d,\max}$ because minority carriers cannot be generated fast enough — exploited in CCDs and MOS-C refresh circuits. - ☐ Memorize the $2\phi_F$ physical meaning: it is the total band bending needed to bring the surface intrinsic level $E_i$ *below* the bulk Fermi level $E_F$, flipping the surface type. - ☐ The depletion approximation ignores mobile charge in the depletion region — valid as long as $n_s, p_s \ll N_A$, which holds throughout depletion and only *just* at the onset of inversion.

topics.title: Surface Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Surface Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In solid-state device physics, the **Surface Potential** (denoted as $\phi_s$ or $\psi_s$) is defined as the **electrostatic potential difference between the surface of a semiconductor and its electrically neutral bulk (interior) region**, measured along the direction perpendicular to the surface.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\phi_s = \phi(0) - \phi_{bulk}$$

where $\phi(0)$ is the electrostatic potential at the semiconductor surface ($x = 0$) and $\phi_{bulk}$ is the potential deep inside the semiconductor (at $x \to \infty$) where the material is electrically neutral.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Surface potential is the *single most important variable* in the analysis of MOS capacitors and MOSFETs. It is the bridge that connects the applied gate voltage ($V_G$) to the induced channel charge ($Q_s$) and the depletion width ($W$). Every KTU question on MOS device analysis eventually reduces to solving for $\phi_s$.

## 1.2 Physical Sign Convention (Critical for KTU Board Exams)

The sign of $\phi_s$ determines the operational regime of the MOS structure:

| Sign of $\phi_s$ | Physical Condition | Charge State at Surface |
|---|---|---|
| $\phi_s = 0$ | Flat Band Condition | No net charge induced |
| $\phi_s > 0$ (p-type) | Hole Depletion / Inversion | Electrons attracted |
| $\phi_s < 0$ (p-type) | Hole Accumulation | Holes piled up |
| $\phi_s = \phi_{Fp}$ | Onset of Inversion (p-type) | Threshold condition |
| $\phi_s = 2\phi_{Fp}$ | Onset of Strong Inversion | Standard threshold |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference:** The bulk Fermi potential for a p-type semiconductor is given by $\phi_{Fp} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{N_a}{n_i}\right)$, and for n-type: $\phi_{Fn} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{N_d}{n_i}\right)$. These values are typically **positive** for p-type and **positive** (by convention) for n-type substrates.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam and Reservoir" Picture

Imagine a **large water reservoir** (the semiconductor bulk) connected to a sloped channel (the surface region). The surface potential is like the **height of the water at the dam wall** relative to the calm lake behind it:

- If water is **level** with the lake → **Flat Band** ($\phi_s = 0$)
- If the dam pushes water **upward** at the wall → **Accumulation** (majority carriers pile up)
- If the dam **sucks water down**, exposing the muddy lakebed → **Depletion**
- If the suction is so strong that water from the **far side (other carrier type)** rushes in to fill the void → **Inversion**

This analogy helps visualize why $\phi_s$ directly controls carrier concentration at the surface via the **Boltzmann relation**:

$$n(x) = n_i \, e^{q[\phi(x) - \phi_{bulk}]/kT}, \quad p(x) = n_i \, e^{-q[\phi(x) - \phi_{bulk}]/kT}$$

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Used

- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.380 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- **Electronic Charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- **Thermal Voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{0.02585 \, V} \approx \mathbf{26 \, mV}$
- **Permittivity of Free Space:** $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm
- **Silicon Relative Permittivity:** $\epsilon_{rs} = 11.8$
- **Intrinsic Carrier Concentration of Si:** $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Physics — How Surface Potential is Generated

The surface potential arises whenever an **external electric field** penetrates into a semiconductor. This field is most commonly created by:

1. **Metal–Oxide–Semiconductor (MOS) Gate Bias:** Applying a voltage $V_G$ to the gate metal.
2. **Schottky Barrier Contact:** Metal–semiconductor junction.
3. **p-n Junction Depletion Edge:** Built-in potential extending to a free surface.
4. **Work Function Difference:** Even at zero applied bias, $\phi_{MS} \neq 0$ creates an initial $\phi_s$.

## 2.2 The Poisson–Boltzmann Derivation Pathway (Step-by-Step Logic)

The analysis of surface potential follows a rigorous chain of logic:

**Step 1 — Poisson's Equation in 1D:**
$$\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\epsilon_s}$$

where $\rho(x)$ is the total space charge density at depth $x$ from the surface.

**Step 2 — Charge Density Formulation:**
$$\rho(x) = q\left[p(x) - n(x) + N_D^+ - N_A^-\right]$$

**Step 3 — Boltzmann Carrier Concentrations (in terms of $\phi$):**
$$p(x) = n_i \, e^{-q\phi(x)/kT}, \quad n(x) = n_i \, e^{q\phi(x)/kT}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $d\phi/dx$ and Integrate** (the key trick — first integral of Poisson's equation):

$$\int_0^{W} \frac{d\phi}{dx} \cdot \frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2} dx = -\frac{1}{\epsilon_s}\int_0^{W} \rho(\phi) \frac{d\phi}{dx} dx$$

This yields the **electric field** as a function of $\phi(x)$.

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**
- Deep in the bulk ($x \to \infty$): $\phi = \phi_{bulk}$, $E = 0$
- At the surface ($x = 0$): $\phi = \phi_{bulk} + \phi_s$, $E = E_s$

## 2.3 The Depletion Approximation (Most Common KTU Form)

For a **p-type substrate** with acceptor concentration $N_a$, under depletion conditions (where free carriers are negligible in the depletion region of width $W$):

$$\rho(x) \approx -qN_a \quad \text{for } 0 \le x \le W$$

**Integration yields the field at the surface:**

$$E_s = -\frac{qN_aW}{\epsilon_s}$$

**And the parabolic potential profile:**

$$\phi(x) = \phi_s\left(1 - \frac{x}{W}\right)^2$$

The most critical KTU formula connecting them:

$$\boxed{W = \sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_s \phi_s}{qN_a}}}$$

**Surface Charge per Unit Area (depletion approximation):**

$$\boxed{Q_s = -qN_aW = -\sqrt{2qN_a\epsilon_s\phi_s}}$$

## 2.4 Full (Non-Approximate) Electric Field Formula — The Key KTU Result

Without depletion approximation, the **first integration of Poisson's equation** for a p-type substrate gives:

$$E_s = \pm \frac{\sqrt{2}\cdot V_T}{\sqrt{\epsilon_s/qN_a}} \cdot F\left(\frac{\phi_s}{V_T}, \frac{n_i}{N_a}\right)$$

where the function $F$ is defined as:

$$F = \sqrt{e^{-\phi_s/V_T} + \frac{\phi_s}{V_T} - 1 + \frac{n_i^2}{N_a^2}\left(e^{\phi_s/V_T} - 1\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This function $F$ governs **ALL** regimes — accumulation, depletion, and inversion. KTU board questions often ask students to identify the dominant term in $F$ for a specific regime.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Regime Valid | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface Potential Definition | $\phi_s = \phi(0) - \phi_{bulk}$ | All | $\phi_s$ in volts |
| 2 | Bulk Fermi Potential (p-type) | $\phi_{Fp} = V_T \ln(N_a/n_i)$ | Equilibrium | $N_a, n_i$ |
| 3 | Depletion Width | $W = \sqrt{2\epsilon_s \phi_s / (qN_a)}$ | Depletion only | $W$ in cm |
| 4 | Surface Charge (depletion) | $Q_s = -\sqrt{2qN_a\epsilon_s \phi_s}$ | Depletion only | $Q_s$ in C/cm² |
| 5 | Surface Electric Field | $E_s = qN_aW/\epsilon_s$ | Depletion only | $E_s$ in V/cm |
| 6 | Threshold Surface Potential (p-sub) | $\phi_{s,th} = 2\phi_{Fp}$ | Strong inversion onset | Threshold |
| 7 | Flat Band Voltage | $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$ | $V_G$ for $\phi_s=0$ | $\phi_{MS}, Q_{ox}$ |
| 8 | Total Charge (general) | $Q_s = \mp \sqrt{2\epsilon_s N_a V_T \cdot F}$ | All (numerical) | $F$ from $F$-function |
| 9 | Inversion Charge | $Q_n \approx -(V_G - V_T)C_{ox}$ | Strong inversion | $V_T$ threshold |
| 10 | Boltzmann Carrier | $n_s = n_i e^{\phi_s/V_T}$ | All | At surface |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When solving for $W$ or $Q_s$, ALWAYS check whether the question specifies the depletion approximation. If "exact" or "general" is mentioned, you MUST use the $F$-function approach. Loss of method marks is the most common pitfall.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

The surface potential is the **master variable** in semiconductor device engineering. Its applications include:

- **MOSFET Threshold Voltage Design:** Engineers tune $\phi_s$ thresholds by controlling doping ($N_a$), oxide thickness ($t_{ox}$), and metal work functions.
- **Charge-Coupled Devices (CCDs):** Surface potential wells are used to store and transfer charge packets.
- **Non-Volatile Memory (Flash):** Floating-gate devices write/erase by modulating the local surface potential.
- **Biosensors & ISFETs:** Ion-Sensitive FETs transduce chemical potential into surface potential changes.
- **Solar Cell Surface Engineering:** Surface passivation reduces unwanted band bending ($\phi_s$).
