# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Assessment Note:** Part A consists of short-answer questions worth 3 marks each, drawn from across all modules. These test conceptual understanding, definitions, and one-line derivations. Answers should be concise (typically 4–6 lines).

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Question)]**

> *Define the threshold voltage $V_T$ of an enhancement-type MOSFET. What is the significance of the overdrive voltage $(V_{GS} - V_T)$ in the drain current equation?*

**Model Answer:**

The **threshold voltage $V_T$** of an enhancement-type n-channel MOSFET is defined as the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to induce (create) a conducting inversion layer (channel) of electrons at the surface of the p-type substrate beneath the gate oxide. For $V_{GS} < V_T$, no channel exists and $I_D = 0$. Typical values of $V_T$ for silicon n-channel enhancement MOSFETs range from **0.5 V to 2.0 V**.

The **overdrive voltage** (also called effective voltage or excess voltage) is defined as $V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_T$. It represents the *excess* gate drive above the threshold. In the saturation region, $I_{D,sat} \propto (V_{GS} - V_T)^2 = V_{ov}^2$, so the overdrive voltage **directly controls** the magnitude of the drain current and the transconductance $g_m$. In short, $V_{ov}$ is the *effective* voltage that controls MOSFET conduction.

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Question)]**

> *Distinguish between the linear (triode) region and the saturation region of operation of an enhancement-type MOSFET. State the boundary condition that separates the two regions.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Linear (Triode) Region | Saturation Region |
|---|---|---|
| **Condition** | $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_T$ | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_T$ |
| **Channel** | Continuous, uniform | Pinched off near drain |
| **$I_D$ vs. $V_{DS}$** | $I_D$ increases with $V_{DS}$ | $I_D$ constant (idealized) |
| **Behavior** | Voltage-controlled resistor | Voltage-controlled current source |
| **Equation** | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)[(V_{GS}-V_T)V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2]$ | $I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$ |

The **boundary condition** separating the two regions is the **pinch-off condition**:

$$
V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_T = V_{DS,sat}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Sub-part (a) typically tests derivation or theory, while (b) tests numerics or application.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the drain current equation of an n-channel enhancement-type MOSFET operating in the **linear (triode) region**. Clearly state all assumptions used.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** An n-channel enhancement MOSFET has the following parameters: $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 10$, $V_T = 1.5$ V, $V_{GS} = 5$ V. Calculate: (i) the saturation drain-to-source voltage $V_{DS,sat}$, (ii) the drain current in saturation $I_{D,sat}$, and (iii) the drain current when $V_{DS} = 1$ V (verify that the device is in the linear region).

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**[7 Marks — Derivation]**

**Assumptions:**
1. The device is biased in the **linear region**: $V_{GS} > V_T$ and $0 \le V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_T$.
2. **Gradual channel approximation (GCA)** is valid.
3. **Drift current** dominates; diffusion is neglected.
4. The channel is uniformly doped, and the threshold voltage is constant along the channel.

**Derivation:**

The induced inversion charge per unit area at a position $x$ along the channel (where the channel potential with respect to the source is $V(x)$) is:

$$
Q(x) = -C_{ox}\,[V_{GS} - V_T - V(x)]
$$

The total inversion charge in an elemental slice of width $W$ and length $dx$ is:

$$
dQ = Q(x)\,W\,dx = -C_{ox}\,W\,[V_{GS} - V_T - V(x)]\,dx
$$

The lateral electric field along the channel is $E(x) = -dV/dx$, and the electron drift velocity is $v(x) = \mu_n E(x) = -\mu_n\, dV/dx$.

The drain current (charge crossing a section per unit time) is:

$$
I_D = Q(x)\,W\,v(x) = \bigl(-C_{ox}[V_{GS}-V_T-V(x)]\bigr)\cdot W\cdot\bigl(-\mu_n \tfrac{dV}{dx}\bigr)
$$

The two negatives cancel:

$$
I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\,W\,[V_{GS} - V_T - V(x)]\,\frac{dV}{dx}
$$

Separating variables and integrating from $x=0$ ($V=0$, source) to $x=L$ ($V=V_{DS}$, drain):

$$
I_D \int_0^L dx = \mu_n C_{ox}\,W \int_0^{V_{DS}} [V_{GS} - V_T - V]\,dV
$$

The right-hand side evaluates to $(V_{GS} - V_T)\,V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_T)\,V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]\,}
$$

This is the **exact drain current equation in the linear (triode) region**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**[7 Marks — Numerical]**

**Given:**
- Process transconductance parameter: $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ A/V²
- Aspect ratio: $W/L = 10$
- Threshold voltage: $V_T = 1.5$ V
- Applied gate voltage: $V_{GS} = 5$ V

**(i) Saturation voltage:**

$$
V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_T = 5 - 1.5 = 3.5 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Drain current in saturation:**

$$
I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_T)^2
$$

$$
I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\,(50 \times 10^{-6})\,(10)\,(3.5)^2
$$

$$
I_{D,sat} = (250 \times 10^{-6})\,(12.25) = 3062.5 \times 10^{-6}\text{ A} = 3.0625 \text{ mA}
$$

**(iii) Drain current at $V_{DS} = 1$ V:**

First, check the region: $V_{DS} = 1$ V $< V_{DS,sat} = 3.5$ V, so the device **is in the linear region**. ✓

$$
I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_T)V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]
$$

$$
I_D = (50 \times 10^{-6})(10)\left[(3.5)(1) - \frac{1^2}{2}\right]
$$

$$
I_D = (500 \times 10^{-6})\left[3.5 - 0.5\right] = (500 \times 10^{-6})(3.0) = 1500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} = 1.5 \text{ mA}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $V_{DS,sat} = 3.5$ V, (ii) $I_{D,sat} = 3.0625$ mA, (iii) $I_D = 1.5$ mA at $V_{DS} = 1$ V.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the linear-region drain current equation, derive the **saturation-region** drain current equation. Explain the physical concept of **channel pinch-off** in your derivation.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** For a CMOS inverter design, the n-channel enhancement MOSFET must deliver a saturation current of $I_{D,sat} = 2$ mA when $V_{GS} = 3$ V and $V_T = 0.7$ V. If the process transconductance parameter is $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, determine the required aspect ratio $W/L$ and the resulting transconductance $g_m$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**[7 Marks — Derivation]**

In the linear region, the drain current is:

$$
I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_T)V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]
$$

As $V_{DS}$ increases, the local effective voltage at the drain end, $V_{GS} - V_T - V_{DS}$, decreases. **Pinch-off** occurs when this effective voltage at the drain becomes zero:

$$
V_{GS} - V_T - V_{DS} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_T
$$

**Physical meaning of pinch-off:** At the drain end, the gate field is just strong enough to support the inversion charge. Beyond $V_{DS,sat}$, the inversion layer vanishes near the drain, and additional $V_{DS}$ drops across a short depletion region. The voltage at the *start* of the pinch-off region remains clamped at $V_{DS,sat}$. Therefore, the drain current saturates.

Substituting $V_{DS} = V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_T$ into the linear equation:

$$
I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_T)^2 - \frac{(V_{GS} - V_T)^2}{2}\right]
$$

$$
I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,\frac{(V_{GS} - V_T)^2}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_T)^{2}\,}
$$

**Conclusion:** In saturation, the drain current is **independent of $V_{DS}$** and depends **quadratically** on the overdrive voltage $(V_{GS} - V_T)$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**[7 Marks — Numerical]**

**Given:**
- Required saturation current: $I_{D,sat} = 2$ mA $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ A
- Gate voltage: $V_{GS} = 3$ V
- Threshold voltage: $V_T = 0.7$ V
- Process transconductance parameter: $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ A/V²

**Step 1: Overdrive voltage**

$$
V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_T = 3 - 0.7 = 2.3 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $W/L$**

From the saturation equation:

$$
I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\,\mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,V_{ov}^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{W}{L} = \frac{2\,I_{D,sat}}{\mu_n C_{ox}\,V_{ov}^{2}} = \frac{2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})}{(100 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2.3)^2}
$$

$$
\frac{W}{L} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.29} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{5.29 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 7.56
$$

**Step 3: Transconductance $g_m$**

$$
g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_T) = (100 \times 10^{-6})(7.56)(2.3)
$$

$$
g_m = (756 \times 10^{-6})(2.3) = 1738.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/V} \approx 1.74 \text{ mA/V}
$$

**Alternative formula check:**

$$
g_m = \sqrt{2\,\mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\,I_{D,sat}} = \sqrt{2 \times (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times 7.56 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})}
$$

$$
g_m = \sqrt{3.024 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.739 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A/V} \approx 1.74 \text{ mA/V} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Answers:** $W/L \approx 7.56$, $g_m \approx 1.74$ mA/V.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Drain Current Equation Derivations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative signs during substitution (1–2 marks lost):** In Step 4 of the linear-region derivation, students often drop the two minus signs (one from $Q(x)$, one from the drift velocity) and end up with a negative current. **Always check the sign** of the final $I_D$ — it must be **positive** for a positive $V_{DS}$ and $V_{GS} - V_T > 0$.
>
> 2. **Confusing the boundary condition with the saturation condition (2 marks lost):** Students write "$V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_T$" as the condition for saturation. This is **the pinch-off boundary**, not the saturation region proper. The saturation region is $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_T$, with equality at the boundary.
>
> 3. **Dropping the $V_{DS}^2/2$ term in linear region (1 mark lost):** Unless the question explicitly says "small $V_{DS}$ approximation," the **exact** quadratic form is required.
>
> 4. **Unit inconsistency in numericals (1–2 marks lost):** If $\mu_n C_{ox}$ is given in $\mu$A/V², you must **convert to A/V²** before computing $I_D$ in amperes. Examiners deduct full marks if the final answer is off by a factor of $10^6$.
>
> 5. **Not stating the gradual channel approximation (1 mark lost):** Any MOSFET derivation in KTU must explicitly state the assumptions, especially GCA.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to verify the operating region in numericals (1 mark lost):** Before plugging into any region equation, **always check** whether $V_{DS}$ is above or below $V_{DS,sat}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — PEAET412 Module 3**

- [x] An **enhancement MOSFET** is *normally off*; conduction requires $V_{GS} > V_T$.
- [x] The **threshold voltage** $V_T$ is the minimum gate voltage to form the inversion layer.
- [x] The **overdrive voltage** is $V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_T$.
- [x] Three operating regions: **Cutoff** ($V_{GS} < V_T$), **Linear/Triode** ($V_{DS} < V_{ov}$), **Saturation** ($V_{DS} \ge V_{ov}$).
- [x] **Pinch-off** occurs when the channel charge at the drain end becomes zero: $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_T = V_{DS,sat}$.
- [x] **Linear-region equation (exact):** $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)[(V_{GS}-V_T)V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2]$.
- [x] **Linear-region equation (approximate, small $V_{DS}$):** $I_D \approx \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_T)V_{DS}$.
- [x] **Saturation-region equation:** $I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$.
- [x] **Compact form:** $I_{D,sat} = K_n(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$ where $K_n = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)$.
- [x] **Process transconductance parameter:** $K_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ (units: A/V²).
- [x] **Transconductance:** $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_T) = \sqrt{2 K_n I_{D,sat}}$.
- [x] **Channel resistance in linear region:** $r_{DS} = 1/[\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_T)]$.
- [x] In saturation, $I_D$ is **independent of $V_{DS}$** (idealized long-channel model).
- [x] Always **state the gradual channel approximation** and **drift-only** assumptions in derivations.
- [x] Always **verify the operating region** before applying either equation in numerical problems.
- [x] **Unit conversions:** $\mu$A/V² must be multiplied by $10^{-6}$ when computing in amperes.
- [x] **Aspect ratio $W/L$** is the primary design knob for both $I_{D,sat}$ and $g_m$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drain Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Drain Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Drain Characteristics** of a Field Effect Transistor (JFET or MOSFET) refer to the graphical and functional relationship between the **Drain Current ($I_D$)** flowing through the device and the **Drain-to-Source Voltage ($V_{DS}$)** applied across it, plotted as a family of curves for discrete, fixed values of the **Gate-to-Source Voltage ($V_{GS}$)**.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the drain characteristics are formally described as the **Output Characteristics** of the FET, and they constitute the primary DC characterization curve used to define the safe operating area (SOA) and biasing regions of the device. The curve family is plotted in the **$I_D$–$V_{DS}$ plane** and serves as the foundational reference for small-signal parameter extraction such as output resistance ($r_d$), transconductance ($g_m$), and amplification factor ($\mu$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Drain characteristics are exclusively a function of the FET family of devices. They are NOT plotted for BJT (where we plot output characteristics $I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$). Students must clearly distinguish between the two.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **flexible water hose connected to a tap** (the drain-source channel) and a **squeezing hand** (the gate electrode) controlling the flow:

- **$V_{DS}$** is analogous to the **water pressure** from the tap — increasing the pressure pushes more water through the hose.
- **$V_{GS}$** is analogous to the **squeeze pressure of the hand** — the harder you squeeze, the narrower the hose becomes, restricting the flow.
- **$I_D$** is the **actual water flow rate** (current) that emerges at the other end.

When the squeeze is mild (small $\vert V_{GS} \vert$), the hose is wide open and water flows easily — current rises sharply with pressure. When the squeeze is firm (large $\vert V_{GS} \vert$ near cut-off), the hose is almost completely choked, and almost no water flows regardless of pressure. This is precisely how a JFET/MOSFET behaves — the gate voltage *modulates* the conductive channel between source and drain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The FET is a **voltage-controlled device** — input is $V_{GS}$ (voltage), output is $I_D$ (current). This is the reverse of a BJT, which is current-controlled ($I_B$ controls $I_C$).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drain Current | $I_D$ | **mA** | Current flowing from drain to source |
| Drain-Source Voltage | $V_{DS}$ | **V** | Voltage across drain and source terminals |
| Gate-Source Voltage | $V_{GS}$ | **V** | Control voltage applied at the gate |
| Drain Current at $V_{GS}=0$ | $I_{DSS}$ | **mA** | Maximum drain current with gate shorted to source |
| Pinch-off Voltage | $V_P$ | **V** | $V_{GS}$ at which the channel is fully depleted |
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | **mS (milli-Siemens)** | Small-signal gain parameter |
| Dynamic Drain Resistance | $r_d$ | **k$\Omega$** | Output resistance in saturation region |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Regions of the Drain Characteristics

The drain characteristics of an n-channel JFET (or depletion-mode MOSFET) are partitioned into **three distinct operating regions**, each governed by a different physical mechanism:

#### Region 1 — Ohmic (Triode / Linear) Region
- **Condition:** $0 \le V_{DS} \le V_{DS(sat)}$, where $V_{DS(sat)} = V_{GS} - V_P$ (for n-channel, with $V_P < 0$ for JFET).
- **Physics:** The channel acts as a **voltage-controlled resistor**. The drain end of the channel is partially narrowed, but not yet pinched off.
- **Behavior:** $I_D$ increases approximately linearly with $V_{DS}$, behaving like an ohmic conductor whose resistance is set by $V_{GS}$.
- **Engineering Use:** Used in **analog switches, attenuators, and voltage-controlled resistors (VCRs)**.

#### Region 2 — Pinch-off / Saturation (Active) Region
- **Condition:** $V_{DS} \ge V_{DS(sat)}$ and $V_P \le V_{GS} \le 0$ (for n-channel JFET).
- **Physics:** A **depletion region** forms a "pinch-off point" near the drain end. Any additional $V_{DS}$ drops across this narrow pinch-off region, accelerating carriers through it.
- **Behavior:** $I_D$ becomes **nearly independent** of $V_{DS}$ and is controlled almost exclusively by $V_{GS}$. The curve plateaus — it is "saturated."
- **Engineering Use:** This is the **amplification region** — used in **small-signal amplifiers, RF front-ends, and source followers**.

#### Region 3 — Cut-off Region
- **Condition:** $V_{GS} \le V_P$ (i.e., gate is biased more negative than pinch-off for n-channel).
- **Physics:** The entire channel is **fully depleted** of mobile charge carriers. No conductive path exists between source and drain.
- **Behavior:** $I_D \approx 0$ (leakage currents in the order of nA may flow).
- **Engineering Use:** Used in **switching applications** — the FET is essentially **OFF**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** — they appear repeatedly in KTU university exams, model papers, and competitive exams like GATE.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Region of Validity | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shockley's Equation** | $I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{2}$ | Saturation / Pinch-off | Square-law dependence; foundation of FET analysis |
| 2 | On-state Drain Current | $I_D = I_{DSS}$ | Saturation, when $V_{GS} = 0$ | Maximum current for the device |
| 3 | Cut-off Condition | $I_D = 0$ | When $V_{GS} \le V_P$ | Full channel depletion |
| 4 | Saturation Voltage | $V_{DS(sat)} = V_{GS} - V_P$ | Boundary of Ohmic/Saturation | Defines onset of pinch-off |
| 5 | Ohmic Region Current | $I_D = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{V_P^{2}}\left[(V_{GS} - V_P)V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2}\right]$ | Ohmic / Triode | Quadratic in $V_{DS}$ |
| 6 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{V_{DS}} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{V_P}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$ | Saturation | Evaluated at the DC operating point |
| 7 | Transconductance at $V_{GS}=0$ | $g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$ | Saturation | Maximum $g_m$ |
| 8 | Dynamic Drain Resistance | $r_d = \dfrac{\partial V_{DS}}{\partial I_D}\bigg\vert_{V_{GS}}$ | Saturation | Very large in saturation (ideal: $\infty$) |
| 9 | Amplification Factor | $\mu = g_m \cdot r_d$ | Saturation | KTU frequently asks for $\mu$ |
| 10 | Channel Resistance (Ohmic) | $r_{DS(ON)} = \dfrac{1}{2 K (V_{GS} - V_P)}$ | Ohmic | Used for switch design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention for n-channel JFET:** $V_P$ is **negative**, $V_{GS}$ is between $V_P$ and $0$, $I_D$ and $I_{DSS}$ are **positive**. For p-channel devices, all polarities reverse.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Drain characteristics are the **backbone of analog IC design** and discrete circuit engineering:

- **Audio Amplifiers:** Class-A and Class-AB audio stages use JFETs biased in the **saturation region** for high input impedance and low noise.
- **RF Mixers and Oscillators:** The **non-linear transition** between ohmic and saturation regions is exploited for frequency translation.
- **Analog Switches (CD4066, 74HC4066):** FETs are driven hard into the **ohmic region** (ON) or **cut-off** (OFF) — drain characteristics define the on-resistance and off-leakage.
- **Current Sources and Active Loads:** In the saturation region, $I_D$ is largely independent of $V_{DS}$ — making the FET an excellent **constant-current source** (used inside op-amp output stages).
- **CMOS Logic:** Digital CMOS gates use MOSFETs in the **cut-off and triode regions** only — the saturation region is a brief transient state.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Shockley's Equation (Drain Current in Saturation)

We derive the **square-law** $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ relationship for an n-channel JFET in the saturation region using the **gradual channel approximation**.

**Step 1: Channel Geometry Setup**

Let the channel have:
- Length $L$ (between source and drain)
- Width $W$ (perpendicular to current flow)
- Doping concentration $N_D$ (donors per $cm^3$)
- Built-in channel half-thickness $a$
- Permittivity of semiconductor $\varepsilon_s$

**Step 2: Charge per Unit Length in the Channel**

At a position $y$ along the channel, the local depletion width $h(y)$ is determined by the **total reverse-bias voltage** between the gate and that point in the channel:

$$V(y) = V_{GS} - V(y)_{\text{drop along channel}}$$

The depletion region extends symmetrically from the top and bottom gates, giving a half-width:

$$h(y) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s \, [V_{GS} - V(y)]}{q N_D}}$$

**Step 3: Mobile Charge in the Channel Cross-Section**

The undepleted (conducting) half-thickness at position $y$ is $a - h(y)$, so the **mobile charge per unit area** is:

$$Q(y) = q N_D \, [a - h(y)]$$

**Step 4: Drain Current as Integral of Drift Velocity**

Current is the integral of charge density times drift velocity over the channel cross-section. Using $I_D = Q \cdot v_{d}$ and Ohm's law in differential form $v_d = \mu_n E = \mu_n \dfrac{dV}{dy}$:

$$I_D \, dy = \mu_n \, Q(y) \, dV$$

Substituting $Q(y)$:

$$I_D \, dy = \mu_n \, q N_D \left[a - \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s[V_{GS} - V(y)]}{q N_D}}\right] dV$$

**Step 5: Integrate Across the Channel**

Integrate from $y=0$ (source, $V=0$) to $y=L$ (drain, $V=V_{DS}$):

$$I_D \int_0^L dy = \mu_n q N_D a \int_0^{V_{DS}} dV - \mu_n q N_D \int_0^{V_{DS}} \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s [V_{GS}-V]}{q N_D}} \, dV$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the Integrals**

The first integral is straightforward. For the second, substitute $u = V_{GS} - V$:

$$I_D \cdot L = \mu_n q N_D a V_{DS} - \mu_n \sqrt{2 \varepsilon_s q N_D} \cdot \dfrac{2}{3}\left[(V_{GS} - V)^{3/2}\right]_{0}^{V_{DS}}$$

This gives the general expression. In the **saturation region**, pinch-off occurs when $h(L) = a$, i.e., when the channel is just fully depleted at the drain end. At this critical point:

$$V_{DS(sat)} = V_{GS} - V_P$$

**Step 7: Substitute the Saturation Condition**

Inserting $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_P$ into the integrated expression and defining $I_{DSS}$ as the current at $V_{GS} = 0$:

$$I_{DSS} = \dfrac{\mu_n q^{2} N_D^{2} a^{3} W}{3 \varepsilon_s L} = \dfrac{2 a \mu_n \varepsilon_s W}{3 L} \cdot \dfrac{V_P^{2}}{2 a^{2}}$$

The general result simplifies to **Shockley's Equation**:

$$\boxed{I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{2}}$$

This is the foundational result of JFET/MOSFET DC analysis.

### 3.2 Derivation of Transconductance ($g_m$)

**Step 1:** Start from Shockley's equation:

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Take the partial derivative with respect to $V_{GS}$, holding $V_{DS}$ constant (saturation condition):

$$g_m = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{V_{DS}} = 2 I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) \cdot \left(-\dfrac{1}{V_P}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$g_m = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{-V_P}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) = g_{m0}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$$

**Step 4:** When $V_{GS} = 0$:

$$g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$$

This is the **maximum transconductance** of the device, used for small-signal model construction.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Ohmic Region Channel Resistance

For very small $V_{DS}$ (deep in the ohmic region), Shockley's quadratic expression reduces to a linear one. The channel can be modeled as a **voltage-controlled resistor** $R_{DS}$:

$$R_{DS} = \dfrac{1}{2 K (V_{GS} - V_P)} \quad \text{where} \quad K = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox} W}{2L}$$

This is the **ON-resistance** of the FET when used as a switch — a critical parameter in power electronics and CMOS digital design.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Quick Numerical Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following worked example is calibrated to a **typical 2N3819 n-channel JFET** with $I_{DSS} = 10\,\text{mA}$ and $V_P = -4\,\text{V}$. These are the most commonly quoted textbook values.

**Example:** Find $I_D$, $g_m$, and $V_{DS(sat)}$ at $V_{GS} = -1.5\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1 — Drain Current:**

$$I_D = 10 \text{ mA} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{-1.5}{-4}\right)^{2} = 10 \times (1 - 0.375)^{2} = 10 \times (0.625)^{2}$$

$$I_D = 10 \times 0.3906 = 3.906 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — Transconductance:**

$$g_m = g_{m0}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) = \dfrac{2 \times 10}{4} \times 0.625 = 5 \times 0.625 = 3.125 \text{ mS}$$

**Step 3 — Saturation Voltage:**

$$V_{DS(sat)} = V_{GS} - V_P = -1.5 - (-4) = +2.5 \text{ V}$$

**Verification:** For the device to be in saturation, $V_{DS}$ must be $\ge 2.5\,\text{V}$. If a designer chooses $V_{DS} = 6\,\text{V}$, the device operates in the **flat plateau** of the drain characteristics, ideal for linear amplification.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Drain Characteristics Curve — Annotated Schematic

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical regions** of the drain characteristics curve and the transitions between them. Each node is purely alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid's parsing rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Apply VDS and VGS] --> B{VGS compared to VP}
    B -- VGS less than or equal to VP --> C[Cut-off Region]
    C --> C1[Channel fully depleted]
    C1 --> C2[ID approximately 0]
    B -- VGS between VP and 0 --> D{VDS compared to VDSsat}
    D -- VDS less than VDSsat --> E[Ohmic Region]
    E --> E1[Channel partially depleted]
    E1 --> E2[ID rises linearly with VDS]
    E2 --> E3[FET acts as VCR]
    D -- VDS greater than or equal to VDSsat --> F[Saturation Region]
    F --> F1[Pinch-off point at drain end]
    F1 --> F2[ID constant with VDS]
    F2 --> F3[ID controlled by VGS via Shockley]
    F3 --> F4[Amplification operation]
    A --> G[Family of curves plotted in ID vs VDS plane]
    G --> G1[Parameter VGS varies from 0 to VP]
    G1 --> G2[Bottom curve: VGS = VP, ID = 0]
    G1 --> G3[Top curve: VGS = 0, ID = IDSS]
    G1 --> G4[Spacing follows square law]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Drain-Characteristics Test Setup

The following diagram shows the **instrumentation architecture** used to experimentally obtain the drain characteristics in a Solid-State Devices laboratory (a common KTU lab experiment).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[DC Voltage Source VDD] --> r1[Current Limiting Resistor RD]
    r1 --> d1[Drain Terminal of JFET]
    d1 --> dev1[JFET or MOSFET Device Under Test]
    src2[DC Voltage Source VGG] --> g1[Gate Terminal of JFET]
    g1 --> dev1
    dev1 --> s1[Source Terminal grounded]
    s1 --> gnd1[Common Ground]
    m1[Ammeter measures ID] --> d1
    m2[Voltmeter measures VDS] --> d1
    m2 --> s1
    sw1[Step VGG to next value] --> src2
    sw1 --> rec1[Plot ID vs VDS curve for this VGS]
    rec1 --> sw1
```

### 4.3 Equivalent-Circuit Interpretation in the Saturation Region

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Saturation Region Equivalent Model] --> B[Input Side: Open Circuit]
    B --> B1[Gate is insulated: IG = 0]
    A --> C[Output Side: Current Source ID]
    C --> C1[gm times vgs in parallel with rd]
    C1 --> C2[ID is constant with respect to VDS]
    A --> D[Small-Signal Model: Voltage-Controlled Current Source]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The drain characteristics are the **experimental fingerprint** of the device. The three-region behavior (ohmic → saturation → cut-off) shown in the first diagram is the foundation of all subsequent FET amplifier and switching analyses in the KTU Solid State Devices syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define drain characteristics of a JFET. Mention the three regions of operation clearly.*

**Model Answer:**
The drain characteristics of a JFET are the curves showing the variation of drain current $I_D$ with drain-source voltage $V_{DS}$, plotted for fixed values of gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$. The three regions of operation are:
1. **Ohmic (Triode) region:** $0 \le V_{DS} \le V_{DS(sat)}$ — channel acts as a voltage-controlled resistor.
2. **Saturation (Active/Pinch-off) region:** $V_{DS} \ge V_{DS(sat)}$ — $I_D$ is nearly constant and controlled by $V_{GS}$.
3. **Cut-off region:** $V_{GS} \le V_P$ — channel is fully depleted, $I_D \approx 0$.

**[3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for region identification with conditions]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State Shockley's equation. What is the significance of $I_{DSS}$ and $V_P$?*

**Model Answer:**
Shockley's equation gives the drain current in the saturation (pinch-off) region:

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{2}$$

**Significance:**
- **$I_{DSS}$** (Drain current with Source Shorted): The maximum drain current that flows when $V_{GS} = 0$. It is a device parameter specified by the manufacturer.
- **$V_P$** (Pinch-off Voltage): The gate-source voltage at which the drain current becomes zero. For n-channel JFET, $V_P$ is **negative**.

**[3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 each for $I_{DSS}$ and $V_P$]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Always **state the operating region** before applying Shockley's equation. Marks are deducted if students use the saturation-region formula in the ohmic region.
> - **Sign conventions** for n-channel vs p-channel JFET are a frequent mark-loser. For n-channel: $V_P < 0$, $V_{GS} \le 0$, $I_D > 0$.
> - Do not confuse **pinch-off voltage $V_P$** with **saturation voltage $V_{DS(sat)}$**. They are different quantities.
> - Show **units** in every numerical answer. KTU examiners deduct marks for unitless answers.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** For an n-channel JFET, the pinch-off voltage is $V_P = -4\,\text{V}$ and $I_{DSS} = 12\,\text{mA}$. Determine the drain current $I_D$ and transconductance $g_m$ when $V_{GS} = -2\,\text{V}$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With the aid of a neatly labeled drain characteristics plot, explain the three regions of operation. Discuss why the saturation region is preferred for amplifier applications. **[7 marks]**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Apply Shockley's equation:**

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{2}$$

$$I_D = 12 \text{ mA} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{-2}{-4}\right)^{2} = 12 \times (1 - 0.5)^{2} = 12 \times 0.25$$

$$\boxed{I_D = 3 \text{ mA}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum transconductance $g_{m0}$:**

$$g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} = \dfrac{2 \times 12 \text{ mA}}{4 \text{ V}} = 6 \text{ mS}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the transconductance at the operating point:**

$$g_m = g_{m0}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) = 6 \text{ mS} \times (1 - 0.5) = 6 \times 0.5$$

$$\boxed{g_m = 3 \text{ mS}}$$

**Part (b):**

The drain characteristics plot consists of a family of curves in the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ plane, with $V_{GS}$ as a parameter stepped from $0$ (top curve, $I_D = I_{DSS}$) down to $V_P$ (bottom curve, $I_D = 0$).

The **three regions** are:
- **Ohmic region:** $V_{DS}$ is small; curves rise almost linearly. The FET behaves as a voltage-controlled resistor.
- **Saturation (active) region:** $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_P$; curves flatten. $I_D$ is controlled by $V_{GS}$ and is nearly independent of $V_{DS}$.
- **Cut-off region:** $V_{GS} \le V_P$; $I_D \approx 0$ for all $V_{DS}$.

**Why saturation region is preferred for amplifiers:**
- The **output resistance $r_d$ is very high** (approaching infinity in the ideal case), giving high voltage gain.
- The **transconductance $g_m$ is well-defined and constant** at the operating point, allowing linear small-signal analysis.
- The **flat plateau** ensures that variations in $V_{DS}$ (due to load or supply ripple) do not significantly affect $I_D$ — leading to **stable, distortion-free amplification**.

**[7 marks: 2 for plot description, 3 for region identification, 2 for amplifier justification]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for drain current $I_D$ in the saturation region of a JFET. State the assumptions clearly. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A JFET has $I_{DSS} = 8\,\text{mA}$ and $V_P = -3\,\text{V}$. For $V_{GS} = -1\,\text{V}$, find: (i) $I_D$, (ii) $g_m$, and (iii) the minimum $V_{DS}$ required to keep the JFET in saturation. **[7 marks]**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a):** Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. Gradual channel approximation (depletion width changes slowly along channel).
2. Uniform channel doping.
3. Drift current dominates (no diffusion in the channel).
4. Saturation condition: pinch-off just begins at the drain end.

**Derivation Outline (key steps):**

The mobile charge per unit area at position $y$ along the channel is:

$$Q(y) = q N_D \left[a - \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{GS} - V(y))}{q N_D}}\right]$$

The drain current is given by the charge-control integral:

$$I_D = \dfrac{\mu_n W}{L} \int_0^{V_{DS}} Q(y) \, dV$$

Performing the integration and applying the saturation boundary condition $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_P$:

$$\boxed{I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{2}}$$

where

$$I_{DSS} = \dfrac{\mu_n \varepsilon_s W V_P^{2}}{3 a L}$$

**Part (b):**

**(i) Drain Current:**

$$I_D = 8 \text{ mA} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{-1}{-3}\right)^{2} = 8 \times \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = 8 \times \dfrac{4}{9}$$

$$\boxed{I_D \approx 3.56 \text{ mA}}$$

**(ii) Transconductance:**

$$g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 \times 8 \text{ mA}}{3 \text{ V}} = 5.333 \text{ mS}$$

$$g_m = 5.333 \times \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right) = \boxed{3.56 \text{ mS}}$$

**(iii) Minimum $V_{DS}$ for saturation:**

$$V_{DS(sat)} = V_{GS} - V_P = -1 - (-3) = \boxed{+2 \text{ V}}$$

This is the boundary value; any $V_{DS} \ge 2\,\text{V}$ keeps the device in the saturation (active) region.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Drain Characteristics**

- **Definition:** $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ curves at constant $V_{GS}$ — also called **output characteristics** of a FET.
- **Three regions:** Ohmic (linear, $V_{DS} \le V_{DS(sat)}$), Saturation (active, $V_{DS} \ge V_{DS(sat)}$), Cut-off ($V_{GS} \le V_P$).
- **Shockley's Equation:** $I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{2}$ — **square-law device**.
- **Key parameters:** $I_{DSS}$ (max $I_D$ at $V_{GS}=0$), $V_P$ (cut-off $V_{GS}$), $g_m$ (transconductance), $r_d$ (output resistance).
- **Transconductance:** $g_m = g_{m0}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$.
- **Saturation voltage:** $V_{DS(sat)} = V_{GS} - V_P$.
- **Sign convention (n-channel JFET):** $V_P$ is **negative**, $V_{GS}$ is between $V_P$ and $0$, $I_D$ is **positive**.
- **Curve spacing:** Curves are **NOT linearly spaced** in $V_{GS}$ — they follow the **square law**.
- **Amplification region:** Saturation (active) region is used for linear amplifiers due to high $r_d$ and well-defined $g_m$.
- **Switching regions:** Cut-off (OFF state) and Ohmic (ON state with low $R_{DS(ON)}$) are used for digital/analog switching.
- **FET vs BJT:** FET is **voltage-controlled**, BJT is **current-controlled**; FET has **higher input impedance** but **lower transconductance per mW** than BJT.
- **Channel-length modulation:** Real FETs show a slight upward slope in the saturation region (Early effect analogue) — quantified by the parameter $\lambda$.
- **Exam tip:** Always specify the **operating region** before writing Shockley's equation. Marks are reserved for this in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transfer Characteristic** of a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET) is defined as the functional relationship between the **Drain Current ($I_D$)** flowing through the device and the **Gate-to-Source Voltage ($V_{GS}$)** applied at the input terminal, with the Drain-to-Source Voltage ($V_{DS}$) held as a constant parameter.

In the language of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Solid State Devices** syllabus, the transfer characteristic (often called the $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ curve) is the most fundamental "signature graph" of a MOSFET. It is the direct electrical manifestation of the field-effect mechanism: the gate terminal, separated from the channel by the thin **gate-oxide layer** (typically $\text{SiO}_2$ with thickness $t_{ox}$ on the order of a few **nanometers**), electrostatically **induces or modulates** a conductive channel between the source and the drain.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Faucet with Voltage Knob"

Imagine a **garden water tap**:
- The **gate voltage ($V_{GS}$)** is the **rotating handle** of the tap.
- The **drain current ($I_D$)** is the **flow of water** out of the tap.
- The **threshold voltage ($V_{th}$)** is the **stiffness of the handle** — you must rotate it a minimum amount before *any* water flows.
- Once the handle is turned past the stiffness threshold, water flows gently at first (the **subthreshold / weak-inversion** regime), then transitions into a **smooth, predictable flow** (the **saturation regime**) where the handle's position is roughly proportional to the square of the flow rate.

So, the transfer characteristic is essentially the **"handle position vs. water flow"** curve of the transistor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The transfer characteristic isolates the *input control* behavior of the MOSFET from its *output* behavior. It is the curve you consult when designing analog amplifiers (to compute the **transconductance $g_m$**), digital inverters (to determine switching thresholds), and biasing networks.

### 1.2 Why the Transfer Characteristic Matters in Solid-State Electronics

| Engineering Domain | Why Transfer Characteristic is Used |
|---|---|
| **Analog IC Design** | Determines small-signal gain ($g_m = \partial I_D / \partial V_{GS}$) |
| **Digital CMOS Logic** | Defines the switching threshold and noise margins of inverters |
| **RF Power Amplifiers** | Sets the bias point and linearity class (A, AB, C) |
| **Memory (Flash, DRAM)** | Governs program/erase and charge-storage behavior |
| **Sensor Front-Ends** | Bio/chemical FET sensors translate analyte binding into $\Delta V_{th}$ |

### 1.3 Physical Setup of a Typical n-Channel Enhancement MOSFET

For the rest of this study note, we will focus on the **n-channel enhancement-mode MOSFET (NMOS)**, the most commonly analyzed device in Module 3.

- **Substrate / Body:** p-type silicon (lightly doped, $N_A \approx 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}$)
- **Source & Drain:** two heavily doped n$^+$ regions ($N_D \approx 10^{20} \text{ cm}^{-3}$)
- **Gate:** heavily doped polysilicon or metal, separated by a thin **gate oxide** ($\text{SiO}_2$) of thickness $t_{ox}$
- **Channel length:** $L$ (modern CMOS: $\sim$ **10–30 nm**)
- **Channel width:** $W$

The transfer characteristic is obtained by:
1. Grounding the source ($V_S = 0$) and the body ($V_{B} = 0$).
2. Holding the drain at a fixed $V_{DS} > 0$ (usually in saturation, $V_{DS} > V_{GS} - V_{th}$).
3. Sweeping the gate voltage $V_{GS}$ from $0$ up through the threshold and beyond.
4. Recording $I_D$ at each step.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Regimes of the Transfer Characteristic

As $V_{GS}$ is swept from $0 \rightarrow V_{th} \rightarrow \text{high positive values}$, the MOSFET passes through three distinct physical regimes. Each regime has its own governing equation, and together they form the complete transfer curve.

#### Regime 1 — Subthreshold (Weak Inversion) Region
- **Condition:** $V_{GS} < V_{th}$
- **Physics:** No inversion layer has formed yet. The drain current is carried by the **minority carrier diffusion** between source and drain — analogous to a bipolar transistor in the sense that $I_D$ depends exponentially on $V_{GS}$.
- **Current magnitude:** Extremely small, typically **nA to µA** per micrometer of width.
- **Why it matters:** Critical for **low-power / subthreshold logic** (used in IoT and energy-harvesting circuits) and for understanding **leakage currents** in modern nanometer CMOS.

#### Regime 2 — Triode (Linear / Ohmic) Region
- **Condition:** $V_{GS} \geq V_{th}$ **and** $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$ (equivalently $V_{DS} < V_{ov}$ where $V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ is the **overdrive voltage**).
- **Physics:** A continuous inversion layer (channel) bridges source and drain. The channel behaves like a **voltage-controlled resistor**, with resistance inversely proportional to $V_{ov}$.
- **Current magnitude:** Increases approximately linearly with $V_{DS}$.

#### Regime 3 — Saturation (Active) Region
- **Condition:** $V_{GS} \geq V_{th}$ **and** $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th} = V_{ov}$
- **Physics:** The channel is **pinched off** near the drain end. Any further increase in $V_{DS}$ is dropped across the depleted pinch-off region, not the channel itself. As a result, $I_D$ becomes (ideally) **independent of $V_{DS}$** — hence the name "saturation." The current is controlled *only* by $V_{GS}$, making this the regime where the MOSFET behaves as a true **voltage-controlled current source**.
- **Current magnitude:** Proportional to the **square** of the overdrive voltage $V_{ov}^2$.

### 2.2 The Threshold Voltage ($V_{th}$) — The "Turn-On" Parameter

The threshold voltage is the minimum $V_{GS}$ required to form a strong inversion layer at the $\text{Si–SiO}_2$ interface. For an n-channel MOSFET on a p-type substrate with source and body grounded:

$$
V_{th} \;=\; V_{FB} \;+\; 2\phi_F \;+\; \frac{\sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_{si}\,q\,N_A\,(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}
$$

where:
- $V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \frac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}}$ is the **flat-band voltage** (work-function difference minus fixed oxide charge effect).
- $\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ is the **Fermi potential** of the p-type body.
- $C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ is the **gate-oxide capacitance per unit area**.
- $\varepsilon_{si} \approx 11.7\,\varepsilon_0$ and $\varepsilon_{ox} \approx 3.9\,\varepsilon_0$ are the **permittivities of silicon and silicon dioxide**, respectively.
- $Q_{ox}$ is the fixed oxide charge per unit area.
- $\phi_{MS}$ is the metal–semiconductor work-function difference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Body Effect (Back-Gate Effect):** If the body is biased at a negative voltage $V_{SB} > 0$ (with respect to the source), $V_{th}$ increases. The modified threshold becomes:
> $$V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$
> where $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_{si}\,q\,N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ is the **body-effect coefficient** (typically **0.3 to 1.0 $\sqrt{\text{V}}$**).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every key equation** you will need for transfer-characteristic problems in the KTU 2024 Scheme University Examination. **Memorize the boundary conditions as carefully as the equations themselves.**

| # | Quantity / Regime | Governing Equation | Boundary Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gate-oxide capacitance | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | $t_{ox}$ fixed by process | $\text{F/cm}^2$ |
| 2 | Overdrive voltage | $V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | $V_{ov} \geq 0$ for strong inversion | V |
| 3 | Process transconductance | $k_n' = \mu_n \, C_{ox}$ | $\mu_n$ = electron mobility ($\approx 1350 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$ for bulk Si) | $\text{A/V}^2$ |
| 4 | Conduction parameter | $k_n = k_n' \cdot \dfrac{W}{L}$ | $W/L$ = aspect ratio | $\text{A/V}^2$ |
| 5 | $I_D$ in Subthreshold | $I_D = I_{D0}\,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{nV_T}\right)\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$ | $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, $V_T = kT/q$ | A |
| 6 | $I_D$ in Triode | $I_D = k_n'\,\dfrac{W}{L}\!\left[V_{ov}V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | $V_{ov} \geq 0$, $V_{DS} < V_{ov}$ | A |
| 7 | $I_D$ in Saturation (ideal, no channel-length modulation) | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}\,k_n'\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,V_{ov}^{\,2}$ | $V_{ov} \geq 0$, $V_{DS} \geq V_{ov}$ | A |
| 8 | $I_D$ in Saturation (with channel-length modulation $\lambda$) | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}\,k_n'\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,V_{ov}^{\,2}\,(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ | $\lambda$ is empirical, $\sim 0.01$–$0.1 \text{ V}^{-1}$ | A |
| 9 | DC Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{V_{DS}} = k_n'\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,V_{ov} = \sqrt{2\,k_n'\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,I_D}$ | Saturation regime | S (siemens) |
| 10 | Body-effect coefficient | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_{si}\,q\,N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | p-type body | $\sqrt{\text{V}}$ |
| 11 | Threshold with body effect | $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | $V_{SB} \geq 0$ for NMOS | V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common LaTeX Pitfall in Tables:** The absolute-value and divide bars used in formulas above have been written using `\vert` and `\mid` to prevent breaking the markdown table layout. Do not type the raw vertical pipe `|` inside table cells.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Transfer Characteristic

- **Amplifier design:** The slope of the transfer curve at the operating point gives the **transconductance $g_m$**, which sets the small-signal voltage gain as $A_v = -g_m R_D$ (common-source amplifier).
- **Bias-point selection:** Designers choose a quiescent $V_{GS}$ such that the device operates in saturation, ensuring a high output resistance.
- **Process monitoring:** In fabrication, measuring $I_D$ vs. $V_{GS}$ is a standard test to extract $V_{th}$, $k_n'$, and $\lambda$ for yield control.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Drain Current in the Triode Region

> **Goal:** Starting from the fundamental charge-sheet model of the inversion layer, derive $I_D$ as a function of $V_{GS}$ and $V_{DS}$ in the triode region.

**Step 1 — Inversion-layer (sheet) charge density.**
At a position $y$ along the channel (measured from the source), the local gate-to-channel voltage is $V_{GC}(y) = V_{GS} - V(y)$, where $V(y)$ is the local channel potential due to the applied $V_{DS}$. The induced inversion charge per unit area is:

$$
Q_n(y) \;=\; -C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{th}\right] \;=\; -C_{ox}\left[V_{ov} - V(y)\right]
$$

The negative sign indicates electrons. For $V(y) < V_{ov}$, the channel is still strongly inverted.

**Step 2 — Drift current through a slice of the channel.**
The current carried by a slice of channel of width $W$ and differential length $dy$ is governed by the **drift equation** $dI = -\mu_n Q_n(y)\,\mathcal{E}(y)\,W\,dy$, where the lateral electric field is $\mathcal{E}(y) = -dV/dy$.

$$
I_D \,dy \;=\; \mu_n W\,C_{ox}\!\left[V_{ov} - V(y)\right]\,dV
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from source ($y=0$, $V=0$) to drain ($y=L$, $V=V_{DS}$).**
Assuming the gradual-channel approximation (channel potential varies slowly), $I_D$ is constant along $y$:

$$
\int_{0}^{L} I_D \, dy \;=\; \int_{0}^{V_{DS}} \mu_n W\,C_{ox}\!\left[V_{ov} - V(y)\right] dV
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_D \cdot L \;=\; \mu_n C_{ox}\,W\!\left[V_{ov}\,V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $I_D$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_D \;=\; \mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\!\left[V_{ov}\,V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2}\right] \;=\; k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\!\left[V_{ov}\,V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **triode-region drain current equation** — it is the first key result that appears in KTU board problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Drain Current in the Saturation Region (and the $V_{ov}^{\,2}$ Law)

**Step 1 — Identify the boundary between triode and saturation.**
Saturation begins when the channel just pinches off at the drain end. At the drain ($y = L$), the local effective voltage $V_{ov} - V(L)$ must drop to zero:

$$
V_{ov} - V_{DS,\,sat} \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V_{DS,\,sat} \;=\; V_{ov}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $V_{DS} = V_{ov}$ into the triode equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{D,sat} \;=\; k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\!\left[V_{ov}\cdot V_{ov} - \frac{V_{ov}^{\,2}}{2}\right] \;=\; k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\!\left[V_{ov}^{\,2} - \frac{V_{ov}^{\,2}}{2}\right] \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,V_{ov}^{\,2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the famous **square-law transfer characteristic** in saturation:

$$
\boxed{\,I_{D,sat} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The transfer characteristic is therefore a **parabola** when plotted as $I_D$ vs. $V_{GS}$ in saturation. The **square-root relationship** $V_{GS} = V_{th} + \sqrt{2I_D L / (k_n' W)}$ is often used to size transistors for a given bias current.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transconductance $g_m$ in Saturation

**Step 1 — Take the partial derivative of $I_{D,sat}$ with respect to $V_{GS}$, holding $V_{DS}$ fixed.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_m \;&=\; \frac{\partial I_{D,sat}}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{V_{DS}} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial V_{GS}}\!\left[\frac{1}{2}\,k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^{2}\right] \\[4pt]
&=\; k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_{th}) \;=\; k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,V_{ov}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express $g_m$ in terms of the bias current $I_D$** (useful when $I_D$ is known but $V_{ov}$ is not):

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_m \;=\; \sqrt{2\,k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,I_D} \;=\; \frac{2\,I_D}{V_{ov}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The transconductance scales **linearly** with $V_{ov}$ and as the **square root** of the bias current. This is why high-gain analog stages typically operate at a high $V_{ov}$ — and why weak-inversion (subthreshold) circuits are preferred for ultra-low-power transconductance stages, where $g_m/I_D$ is maximized.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Body-Effect Modification of $V_{th}$

**Step 1 — Start from charge neutrality in the MOS structure with a substrate bias $V_{SB} > 0$.**
The surface potential at strong inversion must now satisfy:

$$
\phi_s \;=\; 2\phi_F + V_{SB}
$$

**Step 2 — The depletion-layer charge per unit area becomes:**

$$
Q_B \;=\; -\sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_{si}\,q\,N_A\,(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}
$$

**Step 3 — The gate voltage needed to reach strong inversion (i.e., $V_{th}$) increases by an amount:**

$$
\Delta V_{th} \;=\; \frac{|Q_B| - |Q_{B0}|}{C_{ox}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_{si}\,q\,N_A}}{C_{ox}}\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Combining with the zero-bias threshold $V_{th0}$:**

$$
V_{th} \;=\; V_{th0} \;+\; \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right), \qquad \gamma \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_{si}\,q\,N_A}}{C_{ox}}
$$

This body-effect term shifts the entire transfer curve to the **right** along the $V_{GS}$ axis as $V_{SB}$ increases.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** An n-channel MOSFET has $k_n' = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 50$, $V_{th} = 0.7$ V, and is biased at $V_{GS} = 1.7$ V with $V_{DS} = 3$ V. Calculate the drain current $I_D$, the transconductance $g_m$, and the overdrive voltage $V_{ov}$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ov} \;&=\; V_{GS} - V_{th} \;=\; 1.7 - 0.7 \;=\; 1.0 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
I_D \;&=\; \frac{1}{2}\,k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,V_{ov}^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,(100 \times 10^{-6})\,(50)\,(1.0)^2 \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} \;=\; 2.5 \text{ mA} \\[4pt]
g_m \;&=\; k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,V_{ov} \;=\; (100 \times 10^{-6})\,(50)\,(1.0) \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ S} \;=\; 5 \text{ mS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $V_{DS} = 3 \text{ V} > V_{ov} = 1 \text{ V}$ ⇒ the device is indeed in saturation, validating the use of the square-law equation. ✓

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — MOSFET Transfer-Characteristic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["INPUT: V_GS Sweep from 0 to High Positive Value"]
    node2["Gate-Oxide Capacitance C_ox Modulates Channel Potential"]
    node3{"V_GS less than V_th ?"}
    node4["Subthreshold Region: I_D = I_D0 * exp (V_GS - V_th) / n V_T"]
    node5{"V_DS less than V_GS minus V_th ?"}
    node6["Triode Region: I_D = k_n-prime W over L times V_ov V_DS minus V_DS squared over 2"]
    node7["Saturation Region: I_D = 0.5 times k_n-prime W over L times V_ov squared"]
    node8["OUTPUT: I_D vs V_GS Transfer Curve Plotted"]
    node9["Side Parameter Extracted: g_m = k_n-prime W over L times V_ov"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 -- YES --> node4
    node3 -- NO --> node5
    node5 -- YES --> node6
    node5 -- NO --> node7
    node4 --> node8
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node8
    node7 --> node9
```

### 4.2 Qualitative Transfer-Characteristic Plot (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ax1["V_GS Axis"] --> ax2["I_D Axis Vertical"]
    p1["Point A: Origin, V_GS = 0, I_D = 0, No Channel"]
    p2["Point B: V_GS = V_th, Threshold of Strong Inversion"]
    p3["Point C: Triode Knee, V_DS = V_ov"]
    p4["Point D: Saturation Plateau, I_D = 0.5 k_n-prime W over L V_ov squared"]
    p5["Point E: High V_GS, Square Law Growth"]

    p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 --> p5
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of the MOSFET as a Voltage-Controlled Current Source

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    gate["Gate Terminal receives V_GS"]
    oxide["Gate Oxide SiO2 acts as Capacitive Coupling"]
    channel["Inversion Channel forms at Si-SiO2 interface"]
    source["Source Terminal injects electrons"]
    drain["Drain Terminal collects I_D proportional to V_ov squared"]
    body["Body Terminal applies V_SB causing Body Effect shift in V_th"]

    gate --> oxide --> channel
    source --> channel --> drain
    body -. modulates .-> channel
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid diagrams map the **logical flow** of the transfer-characteristic mechanism. They are used here in place of physical cross-sectional drawings, which would require bitmap figures outside the text-rendering capability of this format.
