## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]`
**Define the transfer characteristic of a MOSFET. What are the three regions of operation visible on this curve?**

**Model Answer:**
The transfer characteristic of a MOSFET is the graphical (or analytical) relationship between the **drain current $I_D$** and the **gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$**, measured at a fixed drain-to-source voltage $V_{DS}$. The three regions visible on the curve are:
1. **Subthreshold (cut-off) region** — $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, where $I_D$ is exponentially small.
2. **Triode (linear / ohmic) region** — $V_{GS} \geq V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{ov}$, where $I_D$ rises approximately linearly with $V_{DS}$.
3. **Saturation (active) region** — $V_{GS} \geq V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{ov}$, where $I_D$ follows the **square law** $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2}k_n'(W/L)V_{ov}^{\,2}$. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**What is the significance of the transconductance $g_m$ of a MOSFET? Write its expression in saturation.**

**Model Answer:**
The transconductance $g_m$ quantifies the **efficiency with which the input gate voltage controls the output drain current** — it is the slope $\partial I_D / \partial V_{GS}$ of the transfer characteristic at the operating (bias) point. It is the central parameter in determining the **small-signal voltage gain** of analog amplifiers ($A_v = -g_m R_D$ for a common-source stage).

In saturation, with $V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$:

$$
g_m \;=\; k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,V_{ov} \;=\; \sqrt{2\,k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,I_D} \;=\; \frac{2\,I_D}{V_{ov}}
$$

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**Q3.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **drain current $I_D$** of an n-channel enhancement MOSFET in the **triode region** starting from the gradual-channel approximation. State clearly all the assumptions made. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An n-channel MOSFET has the following parameters: $k_n' = 50 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W = 10 \,\mu\text{m}$, $L = 1 \,\mu\text{m}$, $V_{th} = 0.8$ V, $\lambda = 0.02 \text{ V}^{-1}$. The device is biased at $V_{GS} = 2.0$ V and $V_{DS} = 5.0$ V. Calculate:
1. The overdrive voltage $V_{ov}$
2. The drain current $I_D$ (including channel-length modulation)
3. The output resistance $r_o$
4. The transconductance $g_m$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**
*(See Section 3.1 above for the full derivation. Key checkpoints expected by the examiner:)*

- **Assumption 1:** Gradual-channel approximation — $V_{GS} - V(y) \approx \text{const}$ across channel depth.
- **Assumption 2:** Drift current only (diffusion neglected in the channel).
- **Assumption 3:** Mobility $\mu_n$ constant (no velocity saturation).
- **Inversion charge:** $Q_n(y) = -C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V(y) - V_{th}\right]$
- **Drift current in slice:** $dI_D = \mu_n W\,C_{ox}\!\left[V_{ov} - V(y)\right] dV$
- **Integration yields:**

$$
I_D \;=\; k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\!\left[V_{ov}\,V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2}\right]
$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ov} \;&=\; V_{GS} - V_{th} \;=\; 2.0 - 0.8 \;=\; 1.2 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
\text{Since } V_{DS} = 5.0 \text{ V} \;&>\; V_{ov} = 1.2 \text{ V}, \text{ the device is in SATURATION.} \\[4pt]
I_D \;&=\; \frac{1}{2}\,k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,V_{ov}^{\,2}\,(1 + \lambda V_{DS}) \\[4pt]
&=\; \frac{1}{2}\,(50 \times 10^{-6})\!\left(\frac{10}{1}\right)\!(1.2)^{2}\!(1 + 0.02 \times 5.0) \\[4pt]
&=\; \frac{1}{2}\,(50 \times 10^{-6})(10)(1.44)(1.10) \\[4pt]
&=\; (3.96 \times 10^{-4})(1.10) \;=\; 4.36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} \;=\; 0.436 \text{ mA} \\[4pt]
r_o \;&=\; \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} \;=\; \frac{1}{(0.02)(4.36 \times 10^{-4})} \;=\; 1.15 \times 10^{5} \,\Omega \;=\; 115 \text{ k}\Omega \\[4pt]
g_m \;&=\; k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,V_{ov} \;=\; (50 \times 10^{-6})(10)(1.2) \;=\; 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S} \;=\; 0.6 \text{ mS}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**Q4.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]`

**(a)** Define **threshold voltage** for a MOSFET. Derive the expression for $V_{th}$ including the **body effect**. Discuss how the transfer characteristic shifts when the body is reverse-biased. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An n-channel MOSFET is biased with $V_{GS} = 1.5$ V, $V_{SB} = 2$ V, and has parameters: $V_{th0} = 0.6$ V, $\gamma = 0.4 \,\sqrt{\text{V}}$, $2\phi_F = 0.7$ V, $k_n' = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 25$. If $V_{DS} = 3$ V:
1. Compute the effective threshold voltage $V_{th}$.
2. Compute the overdrive voltage $V_{ov}$.
3. Compute the drain current $I_D$ in saturation.
4. If the device is now re-biased at $V_{SB} = 0$ V with all other voltages unchanged, by what factor does the drain current change? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**
*(See Section 3.4 above for the derivation. Examiner checkpoints:)*

- **Definition:** $V_{th}$ is the minimum $V_{GS}$ required to create a strong-inversion layer at the $\text{Si–SiO}_2$ interface, given zero substrate bias.
- **Body-effect derivation:** $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$, with $\gamma = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}/C_{ox}$.
- **Curve shift:** Increasing $V_{SB}$ raises $V_{th}$, shifting the entire transfer curve to the **right** (toward higher $V_{GS}$). For a fixed $V_{GS}$, the current $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2}k_n'(W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ therefore **decreases**.

**Model Solution to (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{th} \;&=\; V_{th0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right) \\[4pt]
&=\; 0.6 + 0.4\!\left(\sqrt{0.7 + 2} - \sqrt{0.7}\right) \\[4pt]
&=\; 0.6 + 0.4\!\left(\sqrt{2.7} - \sqrt{0.7}\right) \\[4pt]
&=\; 0.6 + 0.4\,(1.6432 - 0.8367) \;=\; 0.6 + 0.4(0.8065) \\[4pt]
&=\; 0.6 + 0.3226 \;=\; 0.9226 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
V_{ov} \;&=\; V_{GS} - V_{th} \;=\; 1.5 - 0.9226 \;=\; 0.5774 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
I_D \;&=\; \frac{1}{2}\,k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\,V_{ov}^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(100 \times 10^{-6})(25)(0.5774)^{2} \\[4pt]
&=\; (1.25 \times 10^{-3})(0.3334) \;=\; 4.17 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} \;=\; 0.417 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 4 — Re-bias with $V_{SB} = 0$:**
$V_{th} = V_{th0} = 0.6$ V; $V_{ov}' = 1.5 - 0.6 = 0.9$ V; $I_D' = \tfrac{1}{2}(100\times10^{-6})(25)(0.9)^2 = 1.0125 \times 10^{-3}$ A $= 1.013$ mA.

$$
\text{Factor} \;=\; \frac{I_D'}{I_D} \;=\; \frac{1.013}{0.417} \;\approx\; 2.43 \;\Rightarrow\; I_D' \approx 2.43 \times I_D
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks on Transfer-Characteristic Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check the saturation condition.** Always verify $V_{DS} \geq V_{ov}$ *before* using the square-law equation. The Board examiner will deduct **2 marks** outright if you plug into the wrong regime's formula.
> 2. **Mixing up $V_{ov}$ and $V_{th}$.** $V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$, not $V_{GS}$ alone. This single substitution error cascades into wrong $I_D$, wrong $g_m$, and wrong $r_o$.
> 3. **Dropping the $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ factor.** If the question states $\lambda \neq 0$, you must include channel-length modulation. Ignoring it loses **1 to 2 marks** in part (b) numerical questions.
> 4. **Unit inconsistencies in $k_n'$.** A common $k_n'$ value is $100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$. Forgetting to convert $\mu$A to A yields currents off by a factor of $10^6$.
> 5. **Body-effect sign error.** For an **n-channel** device, $V_{SB} \geq 0$ *increases* $V_{th}$. The opposite is true for p-channel devices. Examiners watch for this carefully.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Transfer Characteristics of MOSFETs (Module 3, PEAET412)**

- ☐ **Definition:** $I_D$ vs. $V_{GS}$ curve at fixed $V_{DS}$ — the "input-control" signature of the MOSFET.
- ☐ **Three regions:** Subthreshold ($I_D \propto e^{V_{GS}/V_T}$), Triode (linear), Saturation (square law).
- ☐ **Master equations:**
  - Triode: $I_D = k_n'(W/L)\left[V_{ov}V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2\right]$
  - Saturation: $I_D = \tfrac{1}{2}k_n'(W/L)V_{ov}^2(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$
- ☐ **Overdrive voltage:** $V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ — the controlling variable in saturation.
- ☐ **Saturation condition:** $V_{DS} \geq V_{ov}$ — *check this first* in every numerical.
- ☐ **Transconductance in saturation:** $g_m = k_n'(W/L)V_{ov} = \sqrt{2 k_n'(W/L) I_D} = 2 I_D / V_{ov}$.
- ☐ **Output resistance:** $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$ (channel-length modulation).
- ☐ **Threshold voltage with body effect:** $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$; **body effect shifts the transfer curve rightward** for NMOS as $V_{SB}$ increases.
- ☐ **Body-effect coefficient:** $\gamma = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}/C_{ox}$, units $\sqrt{\text{V}}$.
- ☐ **Subthreshold swing** governs leakage; for ideal MOSFET, the minimum is $60$ mV/decade at room temperature.
- ☐ **Engineering uses:** Analog gain ($g_m$), digital switching threshold (curve midpoint), low-power subthreshold logic, sensor transduction.
- ☐ **Common examiner traps:** regime mis-identification, dropped $\lambda$ term, $k_n'$ unit conversion, sign of body effect.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOSFET Scaling - Need for Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** MOSFET Scaling - Need for Scaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **MOSFET Scaling** is the systematic, geometric, and electrical miniaturization of Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET) dimensions and operating parameters to enhance **transistor density**, **switching speed**, and **functional performance per unit silicon area**, while progressively reducing **power consumption** and **cost-per-function**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme, MOSFET scaling refers to the deliberate reduction of the **physical dimensions** of the transistor — primarily the **channel length ($L$)**, **channel width ($W$)**, and **gate oxide thickness ($t_{ox}$)** — coupled with proportional adjustment of **doping concentrations** and **supply voltages ($V_{DD}$)**. The discipline is governed by the foundational observation of **Gordon Moore (1965)**, now universally known as **Moore's Law**, which empirically predicted that the number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every **18 to 24 months**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of an integrated circuit as a city, and MOSFETs as the buildings within it.**

Imagine you are the urban planner of a fast-growing metropolitan city. As the population grows, you face two competing pressures:
1. **More people (functionality) need to fit into the same land area (silicon die).**
2. **Each building (transistor) must perform its job faster (switching speed) and consume less electricity (power).**

Your solution? **Build smaller, smarter, more energy-efficient skyscrapers.** You shrink each apartment (channel), but you build *upward* (vertical stacking — 3D FinFETs) and *inward* (denser doping). You also rewire the city's power grid to deliver just enough voltage (lower $V_{DD}$) so the wires do not overheat. This is precisely what **MOSFET scaling** achieves in semiconductor engineering.

## 1.3 Core Metrics Driving the Need for Scaling

The following performance indices form the **four pillars** of VLSI scaling, all of which must be satisfied simultaneously:

| Strategic Goal | Engineering Metric | Why It Matters |
|----------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Higher Density** | More transistors per cm² | Lower cost per function, greater functionality |
| **Higher Speed** | Reduced gate delay ($\tau_d$) | Faster computation, higher clock frequencies |
| **Lower Power** | Reduced dynamic + static power | Longer battery life, less thermal stress |
| **Better Functionality** | Integration of complex subsystems | System-on-Chip (SoC) capability |

## 1.4 The Moore's Law Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Moore's Law (1965):** The number of transistors per chip approximately **doubles every 24 months**, made possible by linear dimensional scaling of ~**0.7× per generation** (i.e., $\sqrt{2}$ area reduction = $\times 2$ density).

For over five decades, this empirical trend has dictated the **International Technology Roadmap for Semiconductors (ITRS)** and its successor, the **International Roadmap for Devices and Systems (IRDS)**, defining node targets from the **10 µm** generation (1970s) down to the present sub-**3 nm** FinFET era.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need to Scale? — The Five Engineering Drivers

The necessity for MOSFET scaling is not arbitrary; it stems from a tightly coupled set of physical and economic demands:

### 2.1.1 Driver 1 — Increase in Transistor Density (Economic Imperative)
- The **cost per transistor** has historically decreased by ~**30% per generation**.
- A smaller transistor means **more devices on a single die** for the same fabrication cost.
- This enables **larger memory arrays** and **more complex logic cores** (e.g., multi-billion-transistor CPUs/GPUs).

### 2.1.2 Driver 2 — Higher Switching Speed (Performance Imperative)
- Gate delay is given by $\tau_d \approx C_g \cdot V_{DD} / I_{D,sat}$.
- When $L$ and $W$ are reduced by factor $\kappa$, the channel resistance falls, while $C_g$ also falls proportionally.
- The **net result** is a faster intrinsic switching speed — essential for high-frequency digital and RF applications.

### 2.1.3 Driver 3 — Lower Power Dissipation (Thermal/Battery Imperative)
- Dynamic power: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$.
- Reducing $V_{DD}$ and $C$ quadratically/cubically reduces power.
- Critical for **mobile devices, IoT, and edge-AI hardware**.

### 2.1.4 Driver 4 — Improved Functional Integration (System Imperative)
- Scaling enables the realization of **complete systems on a single chip** — the SoC paradigm.
- Mixed-signal, RF, memory, and logic co-integration is only feasible through aggressive scaling.

### 2.1.5 Driver 5 — Reduction of Short-Channel Degradation (Historical Imperative)
- As lateral dimensions shrink without scaling vertical dimensions, **short-channel effects (SCEs)** become severe: 
  - **Threshold voltage roll-off**
  - **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)**
  - **Channel-length modulation**
  - **Hot-carrier effects**
  - **Sub-threshold leakage**
- Scaling forces a *holistic* adjustment of doping and supply voltage to **suppress SCEs**.

## 2.2 The Scaling Parameter ($\kappa > 1$)

Let $\kappa$ be the **scaling factor**, defined such that all linear dimensions are reduced by $1/\kappa$. Then:

- New channel length: $L' = L / \kappa$
- New channel width: $W' = W / \kappa$
- New oxide thickness: $t_{ox}' = t_{ox} / \kappa$

## 2.3 Two Canonical Scaling Methodologies

### 2.3.1 Constant Electric Field Scaling (Full Scaling — Dennard, 1974)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Also called **Dennard Scaling**. Both linear dimensions AND supply voltage scale by $1/\kappa$, keeping the internal electric field **constant**.

### 2.3.2 Constant Voltage Scaling (Voltage-Limited Scaling)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Linear dimensions scale by $1/\kappa$, but the supply voltage is **held constant** ($V_{DD}' = V_{DD}$). The internal electric field therefore *increases* by $\kappa$. This is the actual industry practice due to **interface and noise-margin constraints**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **must-memorize cheat sheet** for KTU 2024 board examinations. Every row is a guaranteed mark zone.

| # | Physical Quantity | Symbol | Constant Field Scaling (Full) | Constant Voltage Scaling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Channel length | $L$ | $L' = L / \kappa$ | $L' = L / \kappa$ |
| 2 | Channel width | $W$ | $W' = W / \kappa$ | $W' = W / \kappa$ |
| 3 | Gate oxide thickness | $t_{ox}$ | $t_{ox}' = t_{ox} / \kappa$ | $t_{ox}' = t_{ox} / \kappa$ |
| 4 | Substrate doping | $N_A$ | $N_A' = \kappa \cdot N_A$ | $N_A' = \kappa \cdot N_A$ |
| 5 | Supply voltage | $V_{DD}$ | $V_{DD}' = V_{DD} / \kappa$ | $V_{DD}' = V_{DD}$ |
| 6 | Threshold voltage | $V_T$ | $V_T' = V_T / \kappa$ | $V_T' \approx V_T$ |
| 7 | Oxide capacitance / area | $C_{ox}$ | $C_{ox}' = \kappa \cdot C_{ox}$ | $C_{ox}' = \kappa \cdot C_{ox}$ |
| 8 | Gate capacitance | $C_g$ | $C_g' = C_g / \kappa$ | $C_g' = C_g / \kappa$ |
| 9 | Saturation drain current | $I_{D,sat}$ | $I_{D,sat}' = I_{D,sat} / \kappa$ | $I_{D,sat}' = \kappa \cdot I_{D,sat}$ |
| 10 | Channel resistance | $R_{ch}$ | $R_{ch}' = R_{ch}$ | $R_{ch}' = R_{ch} / \kappa$ |
| 11 | Intrinsic delay | $\tau_d$ | $\tau_d' = \tau_d / \kappa$ | $\tau_d' = \tau_d / \kappa$ |
| 12 | Power dissipation / device | $P$ | $P' = P / \kappa^2$ | $P' = P / \kappa$ |
| 13 | Power-delay product (PDP) | $P \cdot \tau_d$ | $PDP' = PDP / \kappa^3$ | $PDP' = PDP / \kappa^2$ |
| 14 | Power density | $P/A$ | $P/A \rightarrow$ **unchanged** | $P/A \rightarrow \kappa \cdot (P/A)$ |
| 15 | Current density | $J$ | $J' = J$ | $J' = \kappa^2 \cdot J$ |
| 16 | Internal electric field | $\mathcal{E}$ | $\mathcal{E}' = \mathcal{E}$ | $\mathcal{E}' = \kappa \cdot \mathcal{E}$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These scaling laws directly influence:
- **Foundry process node design** (TSMC N3, Samsung 3GAE, Intel 20A)
- **Standard-cell library characterization** in EDA tools (Cadence, Synopsys)
- **Power-aware synthesis and clock-tree optimization**
- **Reliability-aware design for hot-carrier injection (HCI) and NBTI**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gate Capacitance Under Scaling

The **gate capacitance** (the dominant capacitive load driven by the MOSFET) is given by the parallel-plate capacitor equation:

$$C_g = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} \cdot W \cdot L$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the scaling relations for $W$, $L$, and $t_{ox}$:**

$$C_g' = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}/\kappa} \cdot (W/\kappa) \cdot (L/\kappa)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the numerator and denominator:**

$$C_g' = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox} \cdot \kappa}{t_{ox}} \cdot \frac{W \cdot L}{\kappa^2}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the $\kappa$ factors:**

$$C_g' = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L}{t_{ox} \cdot \kappa}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the original $C_g$:**

$$\boxed{C_g' = \frac{C_g}{\kappa}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Gate capacitance shrinks by $\kappa$, so each scaled transistor drives a *smaller* load, accelerating switching — *provided* the wiring capacitance (which does not scale the same way) does not dominate.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Oxide Capacitance Per Unit Area Under Scaling

The oxide capacitance per unit area is:

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$$

**Step 1 — Apply $t_{ox}' = t_{ox}/\kappa$:**

$$C_{ox}' = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}/\kappa}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$\boxed{C_{ox}' = \kappa \cdot C_{ox}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** A thinner oxide gives *higher* $C_{ox}$, which improves transconductance and drive current — but only if gate leakage (tunneling) can be tolerated. This is why **high-$\kappa$ dielectrics** (e.g., $\text{HfO}_2$) replaced $\text{SiO}_2$ at the 45 nm node.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Saturation Drain Current Under Constant Field Scaling

The classical long-channel saturation current is:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_T)^2$$

**Step 1 — Substitute scaled quantities** ($W \rightarrow W/\kappa$, $L \rightarrow L/\kappa$, $C_{ox} \rightarrow \kappa C_{ox}$, $V_T \rightarrow V_T/\kappa$, $V_{GS} \approx V_{DD}/\kappa$):

$$I_{D,sat}' = \frac{\mu_n (\kappa C_{ox})}{2} \cdot \frac{W/\kappa}{L/\kappa} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{\kappa} - \frac{V_T}{\kappa}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the $W/L$ term (ratios are invariant):**

$$\frac{W/\kappa}{L/\kappa} = \frac{W}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the $(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$ term (factor $1/\kappa$ outside parentheses):**

$$\left(\frac{V_{DD} - V_T}{\kappa}\right)^2 = \frac{(V_{DD} - V_T)^2}{\kappa^2}$$

**Step 4 — Combine all factors:**

$$I_{D,sat}' = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_T)^2 \cdot \frac{\kappa \cdot 1}{\kappa^2}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the original current:**

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat}' = \frac{I_{D,sat}}{\kappa}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Dissipation Per Transistor

The dynamic power of a CMOS gate (per switching event) is:

$$P = \alpha \cdot C_g \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

**Step 1 — For Constant Field Scaling**, substitute $C_g \rightarrow C_g/\kappa$, $V_{DD} \rightarrow V_{DD}/\kappa$, and note that the *clock frequency scales* as $f' = \kappa f$ (because delay $\tau$ is shorter by $\kappa$):

$$P' = \alpha \cdot \frac{C_g}{\kappa} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{\kappa}\right)^2 \cdot (\kappa f)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the powers of $\kappa$ in the denominator and numerator:**

$$P' = \alpha \cdot C_g \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f \cdot \frac{\kappa}{\kappa^3}$$

$$\boxed{P' = \frac{P}{\kappa^2}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Power per device falls by $\kappa^2$ — this is the *miracle* of Dennard scaling that broke down around 2005 (the so-called **"Power Wall"**), forcing the industry to abandon pure constant-field scaling and adopt **multi-core architectures** instead.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Power-Delay Product (PDP) — Figure of Merit

The Power-Delay Product measures the **energy consumed per switching event**:

$$PDP = P \cdot \tau_d = \alpha C_g V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 1 — Substitute scaled values for Constant Field Scaling:**

$$PDP' = \alpha \cdot \frac{C_g}{\kappa} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{\kappa}\right)^2 = \alpha C_g V_{DD}^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\kappa^3}$$

$$\boxed{PDP' = \frac{PDP}{\kappa^3}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Constant-field scaling is *uniquely* attractive because PDP improves *cubically* — the most powerful single metric in VLSI design.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Constant Voltage Scaling Variant of $I_{D,sat}$

Under **constant voltage scaling**, only dimensions and doping scale, but $V_{DD}$ and $V_T$ are unchanged.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the saturation current equation:**

$$I_{D,sat}' = \frac{\mu_n (\kappa C_{ox})}{2} \cdot \frac{W/\kappa}{L/\kappa} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_T)^2$$

**Step 2 — Cancel $W/L$ ratio invariance:**

$$I_{D,sat}' = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_T)^2 \cdot \kappa$$

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat}' = \kappa \cdot I_{D,sat}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** The current *increases* by $\kappa$, which combined with reduced $C_g$ gives faster delay — but the *power density* rises sharply (table entry #14), creating severe **reliability and thermal** problems. This is why modern processes scale $V_{DD}$ downward but at a much slower rate than dimensions.

## 3.7 The Intrinsic Delay Derivation

Intrinsic gate delay (CV/I metric) is:

$$\tau_d = \frac{C_g \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{D,sat}}$$

**Step 1 — Apply Constant Field scaling:**

$$\tau_d' = \frac{(C_g/\kappa)(V_{DD}/\kappa)}{I_{D,sat}/\kappa} = \frac{C_g \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{D,sat}} \cdot \frac{1/\kappa^2}{1/\kappa} = \frac{\tau_d}{\kappa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_d' = \frac{\tau_d}{\kappa}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Constant Voltage scaling:**

$$\tau_d' = \frac{(C_g/\kappa)(V_{DD})}{\kappa \cdot I_{D,sat}} = \frac{\tau_d}{\kappa}$$

The delay improvement is *identical* in both schemes — but the cost (in power density) is drastically different.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The "Five Drivers of MOSFET Scaling" Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["MOSFET SCALING - WHY IS IT NEEDED"] --> B["PERFORMANCE DRIVERS"]
    A --> C["ECONOMIC DRIVERS"]
    A --> D["POWER DRIVERS"]
    A --> E["INTEGRATION DRIVERS"]
    A --> F["RELIABILITY DRIVERS"]
    B --> B1["Faster switching speed"]
    B --> B2["Lower gate delay tau_d"]
    B --> B3["Higher clock frequency f"]
    C --> C1["Lower cost per transistor"]
    C --> C2["More devices per die"]
    C --> C3["Larger memory and logic"]
    D --> D1["Lower dynamic power Pdyn"]
    D --> D2["Reduced static leakage"]
    D --> D3["Lower PDP and EDP"]
    E --> E1["System on Chip SoC"]
    E --> E2["Mixed signal integration"]
    E --> E3["3D FinFET GAA stacking"]
    F --> F1["Suppress short channel effects"]
    F --> F2["Reduce DIBL and VT rolloff"]
    F --> F3["Mitigate hot carrier injection"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Comparison of Scaling Methodologies

```mermaid
graph LR
    S["START - MOSFET Parameter P"] --> Q{"Scaling Strategy?"}
    Q --> R1["CONSTANT FIELD SCALING - Dennard 1974"]
    Q --> R2["CONSTANT VOLTAGE SCALING - Voltage Limited"]
    R1 --> N1["L W tox scale by 1 over kappa"]
    R1 --> N2["VDD and VT scale by 1 over kappa"]
    R1 --> N3["Doping NA scales by kappa"]
    R1 --> O1["E field UNCHANGED"]
    R1 --> O2["Power per device P divided by kappa squared"]
    R1 --> O3["PDP improves by kappa cubed"]
    R1 --> OUT1["IDEAL BUT NOT SUSTAINABLE - Power wall at 90nm"]
    R2 --> M1["L W tox scale by 1 over kappa"]
    R2 --> M2["VDD HELD CONSTANT"]
    R2 --> M3["Doping NA scales by kappa"]
    R2 --> P1["E field INCREASES by kappa"]
    R2 --> P2["Power per device P divided by kappa"]
    P2 --> P3["Power density INCREASES by kappa"]
    R2 --> OUT2["INDUSTRY-COMPATIBLE - dominant in modern nodes"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of a Scaled MOSFET

```mermaid
graph TD
    DEV["Scaled MOSFET Device"] --> G["GATE STACK - high k dielectric plus metal gate"]
    DEV --> C["CHANNEL - lightly doped or undoped Fin"]
    DEV --> S["SOURCE - heavily doped S/D"]
    DEV --> D["DRAIN - heavily doped S/D"]
    DEV --> B["BODY - retrograde well"]
    G --> G1["tox scaled by 1 over kappa"]
    G --> G2["Cox increased by kappa"]
    C --> C1["L scaled by 1 over kappa"]
    C --> C2["W scaled by 1 over kappa"]
    S --> S1["NA increased by kappa"]
    D --> D1["Shallow junction depth"]
    B --> B1["Halo or pocket implants"]
    DEV --> EFF["OUTPUT EFFECTS"]
    EFF --> EFF1["IDS scaled by 1 over kappa or kappa"]
    EFF --> EFF2["tau_d scaled by 1 over kappa"]
    EFF --> EFF3["PDP scaled by 1 over kappa cubed"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Scaling Trade-off Triangle (The VLSI Designer's Trilemma)

```mermaid
graph TD
    T["SCALING TRADE-OFF TRIANGLE"] --> P["PERFORMANCE - speed"]
    T --> PW["POWER - energy per operation"]
    T --> A["AREA - transistor density"]
    P --> N1["Favored by smaller L and W"]
    PW --> N2["Favored by lower VDD"]
    A --> N3["Favored by 1 over kappa in all dimensions"]
    N1 --> LIM["Cannot simultaneously maximize all three beyond physical limits"]
    N2 --> LIM
    N3 --> LIM
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Concise, definition-based or formula-substitution questions.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define MOSFET scaling. List any two key driving forces that necessitate the continuous scaling of MOSFETs in modern VLSI technology.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
MOSFET scaling is the systematic reduction of the physical dimensions of a MOSFET — primarily channel length $L$, channel width $W$, and gate oxide thickness $t_{ox}$ — along with proportional adjustment of doping concentration $N_A$ and supply voltage $V_{DD}$, with the goal of increasing **transistor density**, **switching speed**, and **functional complexity** per unit chip area while reducing **power dissipation** and **cost per function**.

**Two driving forces (1 mark):**
1. **Performance:** Reduced intrinsic gate delay $\tau_d$, enabling higher clock frequencies.
2. **Economics:** Lower cost per transistor, enabling integration of more functionality on the same silicon die.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Moore's Law. Explain how dimensional scaling of $\sqrt{2}$ per side leads to a doubling of transistor density every technology generation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Moore's Law (2 marks):**
Moore's Law, formulated by **Gordon Moore (1965)**, states that the number of transistors per integrated circuit approximately **doubles every 18 to 24 months** due to the continuous miniaturization of device dimensions.

**Dimensional argument (1 mark):**
If linear dimensions are scaled by a factor of $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ per side, then:

$$\text{Area}_{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{L}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \times \left(\frac{W}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{LW}{2} = \frac{\text{Area}_{\text{old}}}{2}$$

Therefore, **two transistors** can fit in the area previously occupied by **one**, doubling the density — the very essence of Moore's Law.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** A 14-mark question is split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two full alternative questions are provided below for internal choice.

---

### ❖ Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Constant Electric Field Scaling** (Full Scaling) proposed by Dennard. With the help of a scaling factor $\kappa$, derive the scaling relationships for **gate capacitance ($C_g$)**, **saturation drain current ($I_{D,sat}$)**, and **power dissipation per device ($P$)**. State the resulting expressions for the **power-delay product (PDP)** and the **internal electric field ($\mathcal{E}$)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of Constant Field Scaling (2 marks):**
Introduced by **Robert Dennard (1974)**, Constant Field Scaling (also called **Full Scaling**) simultaneously reduces:
- All linear dimensions ($L$, $W$, $t_{ox}$) by $1/\kappa$
- All voltages ($V_{DD}$, $V_T$) by $1/\kappa$
- Increases substrate doping $N_A$ by $\kappa$

The defining feature is that the **internal electric field $\mathcal{E} = V_{DD}/L$ remains constant** from one technology node to the next. This preserves long-channel device physics, including mobility and velocity saturation behaviour.

**Step 2 — Derivation of $C_g$ (1.5 marks):**

$$C_g = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} \cdot W \cdot L$$

Substituting $W \rightarrow W/\kappa$, $L \rightarrow L/\kappa$, $t_{ox} \rightarrow t_{ox}/\kappa$:

$$C_g' = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}/\kappa} \cdot \frac{W}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{L}{\kappa} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} \cdot WL \cdot \frac{\kappa}{\kappa^2} = \frac{C_g}{\kappa}$$

$$\boxed{C_g' = \frac{C_g}{\kappa}}$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of $I_{D,sat}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_T)^2$$

Substituting $C_{ox} \rightarrow \kappa C_{ox}$, $W \rightarrow W/\kappa$, $L \rightarrow L/\kappa$, $V_{DD} \rightarrow V_{DD}/\kappa$, $V_T \rightarrow V_T/\kappa$:

$$I_{D,sat}' = \frac{\mu_n (\kappa C_{ox})}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{(V_{GS} - V_T)^2}{\kappa^2} = \frac{I_{D,sat}}{\kappa}$$

$$\boxed{I_{D,sat}' = \frac{I_{D,sat}}{\kappa}}$$

**Step 4 — Derivation of $P$ (1 mark):**

$$P = \alpha C_g V_{DD}^2 f \quad \Rightarrow \quad P' = \alpha \cdot \frac{C_g}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}^2}{\kappa^2} \cdot (\kappa f) = \frac{P}{\kappa^2}$$

$$\boxed{P' = \frac{P}{\kappa^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Results (1 mark):**
- Power-delay product: $PDP' = P' \cdot \tau_d' = (P/\kappa^2)(\tau_d/\kappa) = PDP / \kappa^3$
- Internal electric field: $\mathcal{E}' = V_{DD}'/L' = (V_{DD}/\kappa)/(L/\kappa) = V_{DD}/L = \mathcal{E}$ (unchanged)

---

**(b)** Compare **Constant Voltage Scaling** with **Constant Field Scaling**. Show that the **power density increases by a factor of $\kappa$** under constant voltage scaling, and explain why this limits the practical scaling of $V_{DD}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Constant Voltage Scaling (2 marks):**
In Constant Voltage Scaling, only the **linear dimensions** ($L$, $W$, $t_{ox}$) and **doping concentration** $N_A$ are scaled, while the **supply voltage $V_{DD}$ is held fixed**. This approach is the *de facto* industry choice because:
- TTL-compatible interfaces demand standard voltage levels (historically 5 V, then 3.3 V, 1.8 V, 1.2 V, etc.).
- Noise margins of digital logic degrade if $V_{DD}$ is reduced too aggressively.
- The threshold voltage $V_T$ cannot be scaled arbitrarily without exploding **subthreshold leakage**.

**Step 2 — Compute the power density ratio (2.5 marks):**
Power per device under constant voltage scaling: $P' = P/\kappa$ (from $C_g' = C_g/\kappa$ and $V_{DD}' = V_{DD}$).
Device area: $A' = (L/\kappa)(W/\kappa) = A/\kappa^2$.

Therefore:

$$\frac{P'}{A'} = \frac{P/\kappa}{A/\kappa^2} = \kappa \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Power density increases by factor } \kappa}$$

**Step 3 — Practical implications (2.5 marks):**
1. **Thermal runaway:** Each square centimetre of silicon must dissipate $\kappa$ times more heat, requiring expensive cooling solutions.
2. **Reliability degradation:** Higher fields accelerate **hot-carrier injection (HCI)**, **NBTI**, and **time-dependent dielectric breakdown (TDDB)**.
3. **Sub-threshold leakage:** With $V_T$ approximately constant, the ratio $V_{DD}/V_T$ becomes unfavourable, causing exponential growth in **off-state leakage current** $I_{off}$.
4. **Conclusion:** This is why modern technology nodes (sub-90 nm) abandoned pure constant-voltage scaling and introduced **$V_{DD}$ scaling, multi-$\kappa$ dielectrics, multi-threshold devices, and FinFET/GAA architectures** to balance performance, power, and area.

---

### ❖ Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** List the **five major benefits** of MOSFET scaling. Derive the relationship showing that the **intrinsic gate delay reduces by $1/\kappa$** under constant field scaling. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Five Major Benefits (3 marks):**
1. **Higher transistor density** — more functionality per die.
2. **Lower intrinsic gate delay** — faster switching speed.
3. **Lower power dissipation per device** — better energy efficiency.
4. **Lower cost per function** — economic scalability.
5. **Improved integration of complex SoCs** — system-level benefits.

**Derivation of Intrinsic Gate Delay (4 marks):**

The intrinsic delay is given by the CV/I figure of merit:

$$\tau_d = \frac{C_g \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{D,sat}}$$

**Substitution of scaled values:**

$$\tau_d' = \frac{C_g' \cdot V_{DD}'}{I_{D,sat}'} = \frac{(C_g/\kappa)(V_{DD}/\kappa)}{I_{D,sat}/\kappa}$$

**Simplification:**

$$\tau_d' = \frac{C_g \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{D,sat}} \cdot \frac{1/\kappa^2}{1/\kappa} = \frac{\tau_d}{\kappa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_d' = \frac{\tau_d}{\kappa}}$$

**Engineering insight:** This is the *speed dividend* of Dennard scaling — every technology generation was historically ~30% faster.

---

**(b)** A MOSFET in a **180 nm process** has $L = 180$ nm, $W = 1\,\mu$m, $t_{ox} = 4$ nm, $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, and $N_A = 10^{17}$ cm$^{-3}$. It is scaled to a **90 nm process** using **constant field scaling**. Determine the new values of $L$, $W$, $t_{ox}$, $V_{DD}$, $N_A$, and the percentage reduction in **gate capacitance** and **power dissipation**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the scaling factor (1 mark):**
$$\kappa = \frac{180 \text{ nm}}{90 \text{ nm}} = 2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the scaling rules (3 marks):**

$$L' = \frac{L}{\kappa} = \frac{180}{2} = 90 \text{ nm}$$

$$W' = \frac{W}{\kappa} = \frac{1\,\mu\text{m}}{2} = 0.5\,\mu\text{m} = 500 \text{ nm}$$

$$t_{ox}' = \frac{t_{ox}}{\kappa} = \frac{4 \text{ nm}}{2} = 2 \text{ nm}$$

$$V_{DD}' = \frac{V_{DD}}{\kappa} = \frac{1.8}{2} = 0.9 \text{ V}$$

$$N_A' = \kappa \cdot N_A = 2 \times 10^{17} = 2 \times 10^{17} \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Gate capacitance reduction (1.5 marks):**
$$C_g' = \frac{C_g}{\kappa} = \frac{C_g}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Reduction} = \frac{C_g - C_g/2}{C_g} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**Step 4 — Power dissipation reduction (1.5 marks):**
$$P' = \frac{P}{\kappa^2} = \frac{P}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Reduction} = \frac{P - P/4}{P} \times 100\% = 75\%$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Parameter | 180 nm | 90 nm | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L$ | 180 nm | 90 nm | $-50\%$ |
| $W$ | 1 µm | 0.5 µm | $-50\%$ |
| $t_{ox}$ | 4 nm | 2 nm | $-50\%$ |
| $V_{DD}$ | 1.8 V | 0.9 V | $-50\%$ |
| $N_A$ | $10^{17}$ cm$^{-3}$ | $2 \times 10^{17}$ cm$^{-3}$ | $+100\%$ |
| $C_g$ | $C_g$ | $C_g/2$ | $-50\%$ |
| $P$ | $P$ | $P/4$ | $-75\%$ |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in MOSFET Scaling Problems — Mark Loss Zones:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the clock-frequency dependence of power.** When computing $P'$, students often write $P' = P/\kappa^3$ instead of $P/\kappa^2$. Remember: dynamic power $P = \alpha C V^2 f$, and $f$ also scales by $\kappa$ in Dennard scaling — leading to a net $1/\kappa^2$.
> 2. **Confusing $C_{ox}$ (per unit area) with $C_g$ (total).** $C_{ox}$ **increases** by $\kappa$ (thinner oxide), but $C_g = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$ **decreases** by $\kappa$ (smaller area). Many students mark both the same way — instant loss of 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing the two scaling schemes.** Mixing constant-field and constant-voltage results in a single derivation is a guaranteed 3-mark deduction. Always declare the scheme at the start of your answer.
> 4. **Ignoring that $V_T$ does not scale exactly** in real processes. In constant-voltage scaling, $V_T$ is *approximately* constant — but technically, due to the body-effect term, it shifts slightly. Examiners love to test this subtlety.
> 5. **Forgetting the saturation current derivation requires all four substitutions** ($C_{ox}$, $W/L$, and $(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$). Missing any one of them gives the wrong exponent of $\kappa$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullet Points — Read Before Every Exam:**

- **MOSFET scaling** is the systematic miniaturization of device dimensions to improve density, speed, and power efficiency.
- The fundamental driver is **Moore's Law** (~2× transistors per chip every 18–24 months).
- The scaling factor is $\kappa$ — all linear dimensions reduce by $1/\kappa$.
- **Two canonical schemes:** **Constant Field (Dennard)** and **Constant Voltage**.
- In **constant field scaling**: $L, W, t_{ox}, V_{DD}, V_T \rightarrow 1/\kappa$ ; $N_A, C_{ox} \rightarrow \kappa$ ; $I_{D,sat} \rightarrow 1/\kappa$ ; $C_g \rightarrow 1/\kappa$ ; $P \rightarrow 1/\kappa^2$ ; $\tau_d \rightarrow 1/\kappa$ ; $PDP \rightarrow 1/\kappa^3$ ; **electric field $\mathcal{E}$ unchanged**.
- In **constant voltage scaling**: $V_{DD}$ is **frozen**; electric field rises by $\kappa$; **power density rises by $\kappa$** — this is the chief limitation.
- **Five benefits of scaling:** higher density, lower delay, lower power, lower cost, better integration.
- **Five short-channel effects that scaling mitigates:** $V_T$ roll-off, DIBL, punch-through, hot-carrier injection, subthreshold leakage.
- The **Dennard Power Wall** (broken around 2005, ~90 nm node) ended constant-field scaling — modern nodes use **multi-threshold, multi-$V_{DD}$, FinFET/GAA, and 3D stacking**.
- The **CV/I delay metric** is the most fundamental figure of merit for speed.
- The **power-delay product (PDP)** is the energy per switching event — the cleanest "energy efficiency" metric.
- **Modern relevance:** Today, scaling is no longer purely geometric — it is achieved through **strain engineering, high-$\kappa$ metal gates, FinFETs, Gate-All-Around (GAA) nanosheets, and 3D chiplet stacking**.
- **Memorize the master formula sheet (Section 2.4)** — it covers ~70% of all marks in this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constant Voltage Scaling and Constant Field Scaling. Sub-threshold Conduction in MOS. Short Channel Effects in MOSFETs - Channel Length Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Constant Voltage Scaling and Constant Field Scaling. Sub-threshold Conduction in MOS. Short Channel Effects in MOSFETs - Channel Length Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 MOSFET Scaling Theory — The Foundation of Modern VLSI

The relentless drive toward **miniaturization** in integrated circuits (Moore's Law) is governed by two principal scaling theories proposed by **Dennard et al. (1974)**. These scaling rules define how device dimensions, supply voltages, and doping concentrations must be modified to maintain reliable transistor operation as the channel length shrinks into the deep-submicron regime.

### 1.2 Constant Voltage Scaling (CVS) — "Shrink the Chip, Keep the Plug"

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Constant Voltage Scaling (CVS)** is a scaling methodology in which all physical dimensions of the MOSFET (channel length **L**, channel width **W**, gate oxide thickness **t_ox**, and junction depths) are scaled by a factor **1/S** (where **S > 1**), while the **supply voltage V_DD and threshold voltage V_TH are held constant**. The doping concentration **N_A** is increased by a factor **S** to maintain proper threshold voltage.

#### Conceptual Analogy: The Garden Hose
Imagine a water pipe (the MOSFET channel) being uniformly squeezed smaller in all dimensions while the water pressure from the pump (V_DD) remains unchanged. The water (current) flow becomes more turbulent, the walls experience more stress (higher electric fields), and the pump works harder (higher power density). This is exactly what happens in CVS — the electric field inside the transistor **increases**, leading to reliability problems like **hot carrier injection** and **oxide breakdown**.

### 1.3 Constant Field Scaling (CFS) — "Shrink Everything Proportionally"

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Constant Field Scaling (CFS)**, also called **Full Scaling** or **Dennard Scaling**, is a methodology in which all physical dimensions **and** all voltages (V_DD, V_TH) are scaled by the same factor **1/S**, while the doping concentration is increased by a factor **S**. The result is that the **internal electric field remains invariant** under scaling.

#### Conceptual Analogy: The Architectural Blueprint
Think of a building and its scaled-down architectural model made of the same material. The model looks identical in proportions, the wind force on each surface relative to its size is the same, and the foundation stress is identical. CFS achieves this ideal — the physics of operation remains unchanged because field patterns, current densities, and power densities all stay constant.

### 1.4 Sub-Threshold Conduction — The "Phantom Current"

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Definition**
> **Sub-threshold conduction** (or **weak inversion conduction**) is the leakage current that flows between the source and drain of a MOSFET when the gate-to-source voltage **V_GS is below the threshold voltage V_TH**. In an ideal device, I_DS would be zero for V_GS < V_TH, but in reality, the Boltzmann distribution of carrier energies allows a small exponential current to flow.

**The standard thermal voltage at T = 300 K is V_T = kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV**, often rounded to **26 mV** in KTU problems.

### 1.5 Channel Length Modulation (CLM) — The "Elastic Channel"

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Definition**
> **Channel Length Modulation (CLM)** is a **short-channel effect** in which the effective electrical channel length **L_eff = L − ΔL** decreases as the drain-to-source voltage **V_DS** increases beyond the saturation voltage **V_Dsat**. The depletion region at the drain end widens, "eating" into the channel, which causes the saturation drain current to increase slightly with V_DS instead of remaining ideally constant.

The phenomenon is quantified by the **channel length modulation parameter λ** (with units of **V⁻¹**), and the related **Early voltage V_A = 1/λ**.

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> CLM is one of the most important short-channel effects because it determines the **output resistance r_o** of the MOSFET, which directly affects the **intrinsic gain (g_m × r_o)** of analog circuits and the **sub-threshold slope** behavior in digital design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Scaling Parameters: A Systematic Approach

Let **S > 1** be the dimensionless scaling factor. All device and circuit parameters are transformed as follows:

### Table 2.1 — Master Scaling Comparison Table (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| Parameter | Symbol | Constant Voltage Scaling (CVS) | Constant Field Scaling (CFS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Channel Length | L | L / S | L / S |
| Channel Width | W | W / S | W / S |
| Gate Oxide Thickness | t_ox | t_ox / S | t_ox / S |
| Supply Voltage | V_DD | V_DD (constant) | V_DD / S |
| Threshold Voltage | V_TH | V_TH (constant) | V_TH / S |
| Doping Concentration | N_A | S · N_A | S · N_A |
| Gate Oxide Capacitance per unit area | C_ox | S · C_ox | S · C_ox |
| Electric Field | E | S · E (increases!) | E (constant) |
| Drain Current | I_DS | S · I_DS | I_DS / S |
| Power Dissipation | P = V_DD · I_DS | S · P | P / S² |
| Power Density | P / Area | S³ · (P / Area) | P / Area (constant) |
| Capacitance | C | C / S² | C / S² |
| Delay Time | τ | τ / S | τ / S |
| Power-Delay Product | PDP = P · τ | PDP (constant) | PDP / S³ |

> [!NOTE] **Why Two Tables?**
> KTU problems frequently ask students to **derive** these scaling factors. Memorize the underlying logic: when a parameter has units involving dimensions, it scales as **(1/S)^(number of dimension terms)**; when it has units involving voltage, it scales by the voltage rule; current uses the **MOSFET I-V equation** as the starting point.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of CVS and CFS Derivation

### Step 1: Geometric Scaling
- All linear dimensions (L, W, t_ox, junction depth x_j) scale as **1/S**.
- This means **Area** scales as **1/S²** and **Volume** as **1/S³**.

### Step 2: Voltage Scaling
- **CVS:** V_DD, V_TH unchanged → internal fields (V/L) increase by S.
- **CFS:** V_DD, V_TH scale as **1/S** → internal fields (V/L) remain constant.

### Step 3: Doping Scaling
- To preserve the threshold voltage relationship, charge density must scale inversely with dimension. Since the depletion width scales as **1/S**, the doping **N_A** must scale as **S** to maintain the same depletion charge.

### Step 4: Capacitance Scaling
$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} \rightarrow \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}/S} = S \cdot C_{ox}$$
The total gate capacitance **C_g = C_ox · W · L** scales as:
$$C_{ox} \cdot \frac{W}{S} \cdot \frac{L}{S} = \frac{C_g}{S^2}$$

### Step 5: Current Scaling (using saturation I-V)
$$I_{DS} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CVS** was the **de facto industry standard until ~1990** because it preserved TTL-compatible voltage levels (5 V). It is now abandoned because the high fields cause oxide breakdown (typically E_ox > 10 MV/cm).
- **CFS** is the conceptual basis for modern **low-power CMOS design** (used from ~1990 to the present, with modified versions). It enables the dramatic reduction in power consumption of every new technology node.
- **Sub-threshold conduction** is the **dominant leakage mechanism** in modern chips at 90 nm and below. It is the reason why **mobile devices sleep** and why **dark silicon** (a portion of a chip that must remain powered off to stay within thermal budget) exists.
- **Channel length modulation** limits the **DC gain** of analog amplifiers. Designers combat it using **cascode** and **longer-channel** devices.

---

## 2.4 Sub-Threshold Conduction — Detailed Theoretical Framework

In weak inversion, the surface potential **ψ_s** is approximately linear in V_GS, and the inversion layer charge **Q_n** is governed by the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$Q_n \propto \exp\left(\frac{\psi_s}{V_T}\right)$$

The sub-threshold drain current exhibits an **exponential** dependence on V_GS:

$$I_{DS} = I_0 \, \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right) \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

where:
- **I_0** is a process-dependent pre-exponential factor
- **n = 1 + C_d/C_ox** is the **sub-threshold ideality factor** (typically 1.0 to 1.5)
- **C_d** is the depletion capacitance
- **V_T = kT/q** is the thermal voltage

### The Sub-Threshold Slope (S-slope)

The **sub-threshold swing S** is defined as the change in V_GS required to change I_DS by one decade (factor of 10):

$$S = \frac{d V_{GS}}{d(\log_{10} I_{DS})} = n V_T \ln(10) \approx 2.3 \, n V_T$$

At T = 300 K, the **theoretical minimum** is **S_min = 2.3 × 25.85 mV ≈ 60 mV/decade** (when n = 1).

## 2.5 Channel Length Modulation — Mathematical Framework

In saturation, the pinch-off point occurs at the drain end. As V_DS increases, the depletion region at the drain widens by **ΔL**, reducing the effective channel length:

$$L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$$

The modified saturation current is:

$$I_{DS} = I_{DS,sat} \left(1 + \lambda V_{DS}\right)$$

where **I_DS,sat** is the ideal saturation current (without CLM) and **λ** is the channel length modulation parameter. The output resistance is:

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{DS}} = \frac{V_A}{I_{DS}}$$

### Empirical Relation for λ

$$\lambda = \frac{\Delta L}{L \cdot V_{DS}} \quad \text{and roughly} \quad \lambda \propto \frac{1}{L}$$

This means **shorter channels suffer more from CLM** — a critical concern for nanoscale design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Drain Current Scaling in CVS

**Starting Point — Saturation Current Equation:**

$$I_{DS} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

**Step 1:** Apply dimensional scaling. W → W/S, L → L/S, so W/L is **unchanged**.

**Step 2:** Apply C_ox scaling. C_ox = ε_ox/t_ox, with t_ox → t_ox/S:

$$C_{ox,new} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}/S} = S \cdot C_{ox}$$

**Step 3:** Apply voltage scaling (CVS: V_GS and V_TH unchanged):

$$(V_{GS,new} - V_{TH,new})^2 = (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 \quad \text{(unchanged)}$$

**Step 4:** Combine all effects:

$$I_{DS,new} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n (S \cdot C_{ox}) \frac{W/S}{L/S} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

**Step 5:** Simplify:

$$I_{DS,new} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 \cdot S$$

$$\boxed{I_{DS,new} = S \cdot I_{DS}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Drain Current Scaling in CFS

**Step 1:** Apply dimensional scaling: W → W/S, L → L/S → W/L unchanged.

**Step 2:** Apply C_ox scaling: C_ox → S · C_ox (same as CVS).

**Step 3:** Apply voltage scaling (CFS: V_GS, V_TH → V/S):

$$(V_{GS,new} - V_{TH,new})^2 = \left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{S}\right)^2 = \frac{(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2}{S^2}$$

**Step 4:** Combine all effects:

$$I_{DS,new} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n (S \cdot C_{ox}) \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2}{S^2}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify:

$$I_{DS,new} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 \cdot \frac{1}{S}$$

$$\boxed{I_{DS,new} = \frac{I_{DS}}{S}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Power Dissipation Scaling

**Static Power Dissipation** (ignoring dynamic power for simplicity):

$$P = V_{DD} \cdot I_{DS}$$

**For CVS:**

$$P_{new} = V_{DD} \cdot (S \cdot I_{DS}) = S \cdot P$$

**For CFS:**

$$P_{new} = \frac{V_{DD}}{S} \cdot \frac{I_{DS}}{S} = \frac{P}{S^2}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Power Density (Critical Reliability Metric)

**Power Density** = Power per unit silicon area:

$$\text{Power Density} = \frac{P}{\text{Area}} = \frac{V_{DD} \cdot I_{DS}}{W \cdot L}$$

**For CVS** (Area → Area/S²):

$$\text{PD}_{new} = \frac{S \cdot P}{\text{Area}/S^2} = S^3 \cdot \frac{P}{\text{Area}}$$

This **cubic increase** in power density is the fundamental reason CVS was abandoned — the chip would literally melt!

**For CFS:**

$$\text{PD}_{new} = \frac{P/S^2}{\text{Area}/S^2} = \frac{P}{\text{Area}} \quad \text{(CONSTANT)}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Propagation Delay Scaling

The propagation delay through a CMOS gate is approximately:

$$\tau = \frac{C_g \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{DS}}$$

**For CVS:**

$$\tau_{new} = \frac{(C_g/S^2) \cdot V_{DD}}{S \cdot I_{DS}} = \frac{\tau}{S}$$

**For CFS:**

$$\tau_{new} = \frac{(C_g/S^2) \cdot (V_{DD}/S)}{I_{DS}/S} = \frac{\tau}{S}$$

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight:** Both CVS and CFS give the same **delay improvement of 1/S**, but CFS achieves it with much less power penalty. This is why CFS became the dominant scaling paradigm.

## 3.6 Derivation: Power-Delay Product (Figure of Merit)

**For CVS:**

$$\text{PDP}_{new} = P_{new} \cdot \tau_{new} = (S \cdot P) \cdot \frac{\tau}{S} = P \cdot \tau \quad \text{(unchanged)}$$

**For CFS:**

$$\text{PDP}_{new} = \frac{P}{S^2} \cdot \frac{\tau}{S} = \frac{P \cdot \tau}{S^3}$$

CFS improves the power-delay product by **S³**, making it dramatically more energy-efficient.

## 3.7 Derivation: Sub-Threshold Current Expression

Starting from the drift-diffusion equation in weak inversion:

$$I_{DS} = -\mu_n W Q_n(x) \frac{d\psi_s}{dx}$$

where Q_n(x) is the inversion charge per unit area at position x along the channel.

**Step 1:** In sub-threshold, the inversion charge follows Boltzmann statistics:

$$Q_n(x) \approx \frac{Q_{dep}}{\exp\left(\frac{V_{GB} - V_{TH}}{nV_T}\right) - 1} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{\psi_s(x) - \psi_{s,0}}{V_T}\right)$$

For simplicity, we use the standard textbook result:

$$Q_n(x) \propto \exp\left(\frac{\psi_s(x)}{V_T}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substituting and integrating along the channel from source (x=0) to drain (x=L):

$$I_{DS} = \mu_n \frac{W}{L} \sqrt{\frac{q\varepsilon_{si}N_A}{2\psi_s}} V_T^2 \exp\left(\frac{\psi_s - \psi_{s,ref}}{V_T}\right) \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging in terms of measurable terminal voltages:

$$\boxed{I_{DS} = I_0 \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{nV_T}\right)\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]}$$

For **V_DS > 3V_T** (≈ 75 mV at room temperature), the term in brackets approaches 1, and:

$$I_{DS} \approx I_0 \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{nV_T}\right)$$

## 3.8 Derivation: Sub-Threshold Swing (S-slope)

Starting from the sub-threshold current:

$$I_{DS} = I_0 \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS}}{nV_T}\right)$$

Taking the logarithm:

$$\log_{10}(I_{DS}) = \log_{10}(I_0) + \frac{V_{GS}}{nV_T \ln(10)}$$

Differentiating with respect to V_GS:

$$\frac{d(\log_{10} I_{DS})}{d V_{GS}} = \frac{1}{nV_T \ln(10)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{S = nV_T \ln(10) \approx 2.3 \cdot nV_T}$$

At 300 K with n = 1.5: **S ≈ 2.3 × 1.5 × 25.85 mV ≈ 89 mV/decade**

## 3.9 Derivation: Channel Length Modulation Effect

**Step 1:** In saturation, the pinch-off point is at distance **L − ΔL** from the source, where ΔL is the width of the drain depletion region extending into the channel.

**Step 2:** From depletion region theory:

$$\Delta L = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}(V_{DS} - V_{Dsat})}{qN_A}}$$

**Step 3:** The effective channel length becomes L_eff = L − ΔL, so the saturation current increases:

$$I_{DS} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L - \Delta L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

**Step 4:** For small ΔL compared to L, applying a Taylor expansion:

$$\frac{1}{L - \Delta L} \approx \frac{1}{L}\left(1 + \frac{\Delta L}{L}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Defining the channel length modulation parameter:

$$\lambda = \frac{\Delta L}{L \cdot V_{DS}} = \frac{1}{L V_{DS}} \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}(V_{DS} - V_{Dsat})}{qN_A}}$$

We obtain the compact form:

$$\boxed{I_{DS} = I_{DS,sat}\left(1 + \lambda V_{DS}\right)}$$

**Step 6:** The output resistance is:

$$r_o = \left(\frac{\partial I_{DS}}{\partial V_{DS}}\right)^{-1} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{DS,sat}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
