# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — Definition)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
Define **velocity saturation** in semiconductors. Mention the two key material parameters that determine the onset of velocity saturation.

### Model Answer (3 marks):

> Velocity saturation is the high-field transport phenomenon in semiconductors where the drift velocity of charge carriers approaches a constant maximum value, called the **saturation velocity ($v_{sat}$)**, and no longer increases proportionally with the applied electric field.
>
> The two key material parameters are:
> 1. **Low-field mobility ($\mu$)** — determines the slope of the $v_d$ vs $E$ curve in the ohmic regime.
> 2. **Saturation velocity ($v_{sat}$)** — the asymptotic maximum value the carrier velocity can reach at very high fields.
>
> The onset is governed by the **critical electric field** $E_{crit} = v_{sat} / \mu$.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the two parameters, 1 mark for the $E_{crit}$ expression.)*

---

### Question 2 (Numerical/Conceptual)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
For silicon at 300 K, the electron low-field mobility is 1350 cm²/V·s and the saturation velocity is $1.0 \times 10^7$ cm/s. Calculate the critical electric field for electrons.

### Model Answer (3 marks):

> The critical field is defined as the field at which the low-field linear extrapolation reaches $v_{sat}$:
>
> $$E_{crit} = \frac{v_{sat}}{\mu_n}$$
>
> Substituting the given values:
>
> $$E_{crit} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^7 \text{ cm/s}}{1350 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}}$$
>
> $$E_{crit} = 7.41 \times 10^3 \text{ V/cm}$$
>
> $$\boxed{E_{crit} \approx 7.4 \times 10^3 \text{ V/cm}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> Common mistakes students make on velocity-saturation problems:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (e.g., mixing nm with cm). Always convert $L$ to cm when $v_{sat}$ is in cm/s.
> 2. **Confusing $v_{sat}$ with thermal velocity** ($v_{th} \approx 10^7$ cm/s is similar in magnitude, but they are *different* — $v_{th}$ is random, $v_{sat}$ is the directional drift limit).
> 3. **Using the long-channel square-law formula** even in a velocity-saturated scenario. Always check: is $E = V_{DS}/L > E_{crit}$? If yes → use the linear velocity-saturated formula.
> 4. **Skipping the $g_m$ derivation** when asked. KTU examiners allocate ~3 marks specifically for the $g_m$ expression.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., "the entire channel is in saturation, mobility degradation is neglected, $V_{DS} > V_{DS,sat}$").

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full Derivation of Velocity-Saturated MOSFET Current

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted from Past Paper]**

(a) **[7 marks]** Starting from the fundamental relationship between drift velocity and electric field, derive the empirical Caughey–Thomas relation:

$$
v_d(E) = \frac{\mu E}{1 + \mu E / v_{sat}}
$$

Show clearly that it reduces to (i) $v_d = \mu E$ at low fields and (ii) $v_d = v_{sat}$ at high fields. Define and compute the critical field $E_{crit}$ for Si electrons.

(b) **[7 marks]** Consider a short-channel n-MOSFET with channel length $L = 90$ nm, width $W = 1\ \mu$m, oxide capacitance $C_{ox} = 1.4 \times 10^{-6}$ F/cm², threshold voltage $V_{TH} = 0.3$ V, $v_{sat} = 1.0 \times 10^7$ cm/s, and gate overdrive $V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 0.7$ V. The device operates in the velocity-saturation regime.

(i) Calculate the saturation drain current $I_{D,sat}$.
(ii) Calculate the transconductance $g_m$.
(iii) Calculate the transit time $t_{tr}$ and the cutoff frequency $f_T$.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Derivation of the Caughey–Thomas relation:**

**Step 1: Define the physical model.**

In a real semiconductor, as $E$ increases, two competing effects occur:
- The applied field *pushes* carriers (gain kinetic energy).
- Optical-phonon scattering *resists* this motion.

The drift velocity is the steady-state result of these two effects. Empirically, Caughey and Thomas proposed the form:

$$
v_d(E) = \frac{\mu_0 E}{(1 + (\mu_0 E / v_{sat})^{\beta})^{1/\beta}}
$$

For simplicity in KTU-level analysis, the parameter $\beta = 1$ is used, giving:

$$
v_d(E) = \frac{\mu_0 E}{1 + \mu_0 E / v_{sat}} \quad \blacksquare \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 2: Low-field limit check.**

When $E$ is very small, $\mu_0 E / v_{sat} \ll 1$, so the denominator $\to 1$:

$$
v_d \to \mu_0 E \quad \blacksquare \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

This is the **ohmic / low-field** relation.

**Step 3: High-field limit check.**

When $E$ is very large, $\mu_0 E / v_{sat} \gg 1$, so the denominator $\approx \mu_0 E / v_{sat}$:

$$
v_d \to \frac{\mu_0 E}{\mu_0 E / v_{sat}} = v_{sat} \quad \blacksquare \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

This is the **velocity saturation** limit.

**Step 4: Define and compute the critical field for Si.**

The critical field is the value of $E$ at which $v_d = v_{sat}/2$ (the "half-saturation" point):

$$
\frac{v_{sat}}{2} = \frac{\mu_0 E_{crit}}{1 + \mu_0 E_{crit}/v_{sat}}
$$

Solving:

$$
\frac{v_{sat}}{2}(1 + \mu_0 E_{crit}/v_{sat}) = \mu_0 E_{crit}
$$

$$
\frac{v_{sat}}{2} + \frac{\mu_0 E_{crit}}{2} = \mu_0 E_{crit}
$$

$$
\frac{v_{sat}}{2} = \frac{\mu_0 E_{crit}}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{crit} = \frac{v_{sat}}{\mu_0}} \quad \blacksquare \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

Numerical computation for Si (electrons, $\mu_0 = 1350$ cm²/V·s, $v_{sat} = 1 \times 10^7$ cm/s):

$$
E_{crit} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^7}{1350} = 7.41 \times 10^3 \text{ V/cm} \quad \blacksquare \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $L = 90$ nm $= 9 \times 10^{-6}$ cm; $W = 1\ \mu$m $= 10^{-4}$ cm; $C_{ox} = 1.4 \times 10^{-6}$ F/cm²; $V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 0.7$ V; $v_{sat} = 1 \times 10^7$ cm/s.

**Check that velocity saturation dominates:**

Average field:

$$
E = \frac{V_{DS}}{L} \gg \frac{0.7}{9 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 7.8 \times 10^4 \text{ V/cm}
$$

Since $E \gg E_{crit} = 7.4 \times 10^3$ V/cm, the entire channel is velocity-saturated. ✓

**(i) Saturation drain current [3 marks]:**

$$
I_{D,sat} = W \cdot C_{ox} \cdot v_{sat} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{TH})
$$

$$
I_{D,sat} = (10^{-4}\ \text{cm}) \times (1.4 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{F/cm}^2) \times (1.0 \times 10^7\ \text{cm/s}) \times (0.7\ \text{V})
$$

$$
I_{D,sat} = (10^{-4})(1.4 \times 10^{-6})(10^7)(0.7)
$$

$$
I_{D,sat} = 9.8 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{A} = 0.98\ \text{mA} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**(ii) Transconductance [2 marks]:**

$$
g_m = \frac{\partial I_{D,sat}}{\partial V_{GS}} = W C_{ox} v_{sat}
$$

$$
g_m = (10^{-4})(1.4 \times 10^{-6})(10^7) = 1.4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{S} = 1.4\ \text{mS} \quad \blacksquare
$$

> Notice: $g_m$ is **independent of $V_{GS}$** — a hallmark of velocity saturation.

**(iii) Transit time and cutoff frequency [2 marks]:**

$$
t_{tr} = \frac{L}{v_{sat}} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{cm}}{10^7\ \text{cm/s}} = 9 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{s} = 0.9\ \text{ps}
$$

$$
f_T = \frac{1}{2\pi t_{tr}} = \frac{v_{sat}}{2\pi L} = \frac{10^7}{2\pi \times 9 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.77 \times 10^{11}\ \text{Hz} \approx 177\ \text{GHz} \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Full Question

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

(a) **[7 marks]** Explain with a neat sketch (describe in words / flowchart) the physical mechanism responsible for velocity saturation in silicon. Why does GaAs exhibit a peak velocity followed by a decrease, while Si does not? Name the effect that exploits this GaAs behavior.

(b) **[7 marks]** A Si n-MOSFET has $L = 65$ nm, $W = 2\ \mu$m, $C_{ox} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ F/cm², $V_{TH} = 0.25$ V, and $V_{GS} = 1.0$ V. Given $v_{sat} = 1.0 \times 10^7$ cm/s and $\mu_n = 1350$ cm²/V·s, determine:
(i) The long-channel saturation current (square-law).
(ii) The velocity-saturated saturation current.
(iii) The percentage deviation of the long-channel value from the velocity-saturated value. Comment on which model is more appropriate for $L = 65$ nm.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Physical mechanism of velocity saturation in Si [4 marks]:**

In silicon, the conduction band has a **central minimum** and **six equivalent satellite valleys** (the X-valleys, located along the $\langle 100 \rangle$ directions of the Brillouin zone). At low electric fields, electrons occupy the low-effective-mass valleys and have a high mobility. As the electric field increases:

1. Electrons gain kinetic energy from the field.
2. When their energy exceeds ~0.5 eV, they undergo **intervalley scattering** — being scattered from the low-mass valley to the higher-mass satellite valleys.
3. In the satellite valleys, the effective mass is larger and the mobility is lower.
4. Simultaneously, the electrons emit **optical phonons** very frequently, which dissipates the energy gained from the field.
5. A **steady state** is established where energy gain from the field equals energy loss to the lattice.
6. The average drift velocity plateaus at the **saturation velocity $v_{sat} \approx 1.0 \times 10^7$ cm/s**.

> **Sketch (text description):** Plot $v_d$ (y-axis) vs $E$ (x-axis, log scale). The curve rises linearly with slope $\mu$ at low $E$, then bends and flattens, approaching $v_{sat}$ as a horizontal asymptote.

**Si vs GaAs behavior [2 marks]:**

- **Silicon**: the satellite valleys are at a relatively **high energy** (~1.1 eV above the central valley). Most electrons remain in the low-mass valley. There is **no overshoot** in the $v_d$–$E$ curve. Saturation is monotonic.
- **Gallium Arsenide**: the central $\Gamma$ valley has a very low effective mass ($0.067 m_0$) and the satellite L-valleys are at a much **lower offset energy** (~0.29 eV). At low fields, electrons are in $\Gamma$ and have a very high velocity. As $E$ increases past ~3.5 kV/cm, electrons transfer to the L-valleys (effective mass $1.2 m_0$), where mobility is low. The **velocity actually decreases** with increasing $E$ — this is the **negative differential mobility (NDM)** region.

**Name the effect [1 mark]:** The NDM region in GaAs is exploited in the **Gunn Effect** (used in **Gunn diodes** for microwave oscillation).

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $L = 65$ nm $= 6.5 \times 10^{-6}$ cm; $W = 2\ \mu$m $= 2 \times 10^{-4}$ cm; $C_{ox} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ F/cm²; $V_{TH} = 0.25$ V; $V_{GS} = 1.0$ V; $V_{GS} - V_{TH} = 0.75$ V.

**Check field regime:** Critical field $E_{crit} = v_{sat}/\mu_n = 10^7/1350 = 7.4 \times 10^3$ V/cm. For a typical $V_{DS} = 1$ V:

$$
E = \frac{V_{DS}}{L} = \frac{1}{6.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.54 \times 10^5 \text{ V/cm} \gg E_{crit}
$$

So the device is **deep in the velocity-saturated regime**.

**(i) Long-channel (square-law) current [3 marks]:**

$$
I_{D,sat}^{long} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{2} \times 1350 \times (2.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{6.5 \times 10^{-6}} \times (0.75)^2
$$

$$
= 0.5 \times 1350 \times (2.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times 30.77 \times 0.5625
$$

$$
= 0.5 \times 1350 \times (2.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times 17.31
$$

$$
= 0.5 \times 0.04673
$$

$$
= 0.02336 \text{ A} \approx 23.36 \text{ mA} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**(ii) Velocity-saturated current [3 marks]:**

$$
I_{D,sat}^{vel} = W C_{ox} v_{sat} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})
$$

$$
= (2 \times 10^{-4}) \times (2.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1.0 \times 10^7) \times 0.75
$$

$$
= (2 \times 10^{-4})(2.0 \times 10^{-6})(10^7)(0.75)
$$

$$
= 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 3.0 \text{ mA} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**(iii) Percentage deviation and comment [1 mark]:**

$$
\% \text{ deviation} = \frac{|I_{D}^{long} - I_{D}^{vel}|}{I_{D}^{vel}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{|23.36 - 3.0|}{3.0} \times 100 = \frac{20.36}{3.0} \times 100 \approx 678.7\%
$$

> The long-channel formula **overestimates** the current by nearly **7×** (or ~679%). This shows that for $L = 65$ nm, the **velocity-saturated model is essential**, and the long-channel square-law is completely inappropriate. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition**: At high $E$, carrier drift velocity saturates at $v_{sat}$ — a material constant.
- **Low-field equation**: $v_d = \mu E$ (ohmic, linear).
- **High-field equation**: $v_d = v_{sat}$ (independent of $E$).
- **Empirical (Caughey–Thomas) formula**:
  $$v_d = \frac{\mu E}{1 + \mu E / v_{sat}}$$
- **Critical field**: $E_{crit} = v_{sat} / \mu$ (knee of the $v_d$–$E$ curve).
- **Standard Si values**: $v_{sat,n} = 1.0 \times 10^7$ cm/s; $v_{sat,p} = 0.6 \times 10^7$ cm/s; $\mu_n = 1350$ cm²/V·s; $\mu_p = 480$ cm²/V·s.
- **MOSFET velocity-saturated current**: $I_{D,sat} = W C_{ox} v_{sat} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$ — **linear** in $V_{GS}$.
- **Long-channel (no velocity sat.) current**: $I_{D,sat} = \tfrac{1}{2} \mu C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$ — **quadratic** in $V_{GS}$.
- **Transconductance (velocity sat.)**: $g_m = W C_{ox} v_{sat}$ — **constant**, bias-independent.
- **Transit time**: $t_{tr} = L / v_{sat}$.
- **Cutoff frequency**: $f_T = v_{sat} / (2\pi L)$.
- **Physical cause**: intervalley scattering + strong optical-phonon emission.
- **GaAs special behavior**: peak velocity then **negative differential mobility (NDM)** → **Gunn Effect** → Gunn diodes for microwaves.
- **Hot-carrier concerns**: even with $v_d$ saturated, carriers gain large kinetic energy → **HCI** (hot-carrier injection) and **impact ionization** at the drain end of short MOSFETs.
- **Critical field check**: compute $E = V_{DS}/L$; if $E \gg E_{crit} \approx 7.4 \times 10^3$ V/cm for Si, **always use the velocity-saturated formula** (especially in nanometer-scale problems).
- **Engineering impact**: velocity saturation limits the high-frequency and high-current performance of modern CMOS — determines $f_T$, $f_{max}$, and the maximum usable $I_{on}$ in digital logic.
- **Unit conversions**: $L$ in cm (when $v_{sat}$ is in cm/s), $C_{ox}$ in F/cm², $W$ in cm → $I_D$ in A.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threshold Voltage Variations and Hot Carrier Effects. FinFET - Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Threshold Voltage Variations and Hot Carrier Effects. FinFET - Structure ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Threshold Voltage Variations **Threshold Voltage (V_T)** is the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to create a conducting channel between the source and drain terminals of a MOSFET. However, in real nanoscale devices, the threshold voltage does not remain constant — it is **modulated by several physical and geometric effects**. The major sources of threshold voltage variation in short-channel MOSFETs are: - **Body Effect (Substrate Bias Effect)** – Variation of V_T with source-to-body bias (V_SB). - **Short Channel Effect (SCE)** – Reduction in V_T as channel length L decreases below a critical value. - **Narrow Width Effect (NWE)** – Increase in V_T when the channel width W becomes very small. - **Drain Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)** – Threshold voltage reduction due to high drain bias. - **Threshold Voltage Roll-off** – The combined dependence of V_T on channel length. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** As MOSFETs are aggressively scaled down to sub-100 nm regimes, threshold voltage variation becomes a critical reliability and performance concern. KTU 2024 scheme treats this as a high-weightage concept under short-channel device physics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constant to Remember:** The body-effect coefficient (gamma, **γ**) typically lies in the range **0.3 V^½ to 0.6 V^½** for silicon MOSFETs at room temperature (**T = 300 K**), and the thermal voltage **V_T = kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV** at 300 K. #### Conceptual Analogy Imagine a **water dam** controlling flow through a channel: - The **gate voltage** is the height of the dam's gate. - **Threshold voltage** is the minimum water pressure (voltage) needed to lift the gate and allow water (current) to flow. - Now, if the **dam's wall (channel length) becomes too short**, water leaks even when the gate is partially closed (DIBL effect). - If the **dam is built on soft soil (body bias)**, the foundation shifts and the required water pressure changes (body effect). - If the **dam is too narrow (narrow width)**, the water finds it harder to flow, requiring more pressure (V_T increases). This makes it crystal clear that V_T is not a static design parameter but a **function of geometry and bias conditions**. ### 1.2 Hot Carrier Effects (HCE) **Hot Carrier Effect (HCE)** is a reliability degradation phenomenon in short-channel MOSFETs where carriers (electrons or holes) acquire very high kinetic energy ("hot") from the strong lateral electric field near the drain end, leading to device parameter degradation over time. These energetic "hot" carriers can: - **Inject into the gate oxide** (trapped charges shift V_T). - **Generate interface states** at the Si-SiO₂ boundary (mobility degradation). - **Cause impact ionization**, producing electron-hole pairs and substrate current. > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** A "hot carrier" is any carrier whose energy is significantly greater than the thermal equilibrium energy (kT), typically exceeding the Si-SiO₂ energy barrier of **Φ_B ≈ 3.1 eV** for electrons. #### Conceptual Analogy Think of cars speeding down a narrow highway (channel): - Near the **braking zone (drain end)**, the electric field is like a sharp speed bump. - Cars that come in too fast (**hot carriers**) jump the divider and crash into the wall (**gate oxide**), damaging it. - The **wall (oxide)** gets dented over time, weakening its insulating property. - Eventually, the highway becomes unsafe (**device failure**), and the road's traffic rules become harder to enforce (**V_T shift, mobility degradation**). ### 1.3 FinFET - Structure A **FinFET** (Fin Field-Effect Transistor) is a **multi-gate, non-planar (3D) MOSFET** in which the conducting channel is a thin vertical "fin" of silicon, and the gate electrode wraps around the fin from two or three sides, providing excellent electrostatic control over the channel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Structural Feature:** The fin width (W_fin) defines the effective silicon body thickness. Because the gate controls the channel from multiple sides, the channel is **fully depleted** at much smaller dimensions, dramatically reducing short-channel effects. #### Conceptual Analogy A **FinFET** is like a **vertical sandwich**: - The **bread slices** are the gate electrodes (multi-gate control). - The **filling** is a thin silicon fin (the channel). - The sandwich's **grip is much firmer** than a single top-contact, just as multiple gates control the channel far better than a single planar gate in traditional MOSFETs. This multi-gate "grip" allows FinFETs to scale down to gate lengths of **7 nm, 5 nm, and even 3 nm** in modern CMOS technology nodes. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Threshold Voltage Variations — Detailed Mechanisms #### (a) Body Effect (Substrate Bias Effect) When a reverse body-to-source bias (V_SB > 0) is applied, the depletion region widens, requiring a higher gate voltage to form the channel. **Physical Reason:** - Increased V_SB widens the depletion region under the channel. - More negative bulk charge (Q_B) must be compensated by the gate. - Result: V_T **increases** with V_SB. **Operational Logic:** - Source is grounded (V_S = 0). - Body is biased at negative voltage (V_B = −V_SB). - The additional reverse bias enhances the depletion charge. #### (b) Short Channel Effect (SCE) on V_T As channel length L decreases, the source and drain depletion regions occupy a larger fraction of the channel, and the gate loses control. **Mechanisms contributing to SCE:** 1. **Charge sharing** between gate, source, and drain. 2. **DIBL** (Drain Induced Barrier Lowering). 3. **Punch-through** (at extreme scaling). #### (c) Narrow Width Effect (NWE) When channel width W is small, the gate field fringing at the edges induces extra depletion charge, requiring higher V_T. **Mechanism:** - Depletion regions from the **bird's beak regions** (at the STI/field oxide edges) extend into the channel. - Additional charge must be supported by the gate → V_T **increases**. #### (d) DIBL (Drain Induced Barrier Lowering) When V_DS is high, the drain depletion region extends toward the source, lowering the source-to-channel potential barrier. - Reduces the energy barrier Φ_B at the source. - Effective V_T **decreases** linearly with V_DS. - Particularly severe in sub-100 nm devices. #### (e) Threshold Voltage Roll-off The combined effect of all short-channel phenomena manifests as a **V_T vs. L** curve that drops sharply below a critical length (L_min). ### 2.2 Hot Carrier Effects — Detailed Physics **Generation of Hot Carriers:** 1. **High Lateral Field:** Near the drain pinch-off region, the electric field E_y can reach **10⁵ – 10⁶ V/cm**. 2. **Energy Gain:** Carriers gain kinetic energy between collisions. 3. **Impact Ionization:** If energy exceeds **E_g = 1.12 eV** (Si bandgap), electron-hole pairs are created. 4. **Injection:** Hot electrons overcome the **Si-SiO₂ barrier (3.1 eV)** and inject into the gate oxide. 5. **Damage:** Trapped charge and interface states shift V_T and degrade mobility. **Substrate Current Model (Lucky Electron Model):** $$I_{sub} = C_1 \cdot I_D \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{\phi_B}{q \lambda E_m}\right)$$ Where: - **I_D** = drain current, - **φ_B** = barrier height (~3.1 eV for electrons), - **λ** = mean free path of hot electrons (~7.8 nm in Si), - **E_m** = maximum lateral electric field, - **C_1** = empirical constant. **Empirical Approximation (commonly used in KTU problems):** $$I_{sub} \approx C \cdot I_D \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B}{V_{DS}}\right)$$ Where B and C are technology-dependent constants. ### 2.3 FinFET — Structural Analysis **Key Structural Parameters:** - **Fin Width (W_fin or T_si):** Body thickness of the silicon fin. Determines short-channel control. - **Fin Height (H_fin):** Vertical extent of the fin; contributes to effective channel width. - **Gate Length (L_g):** Length of the gate electrode along the fin. - **Effective Channel Width:** For a FinFET with N fins: $$W_{eff} = 2 \cdot N \cdot H_{fin} + N \cdot W_{fin}$$ (The factor 2 comes from the two side gates; top gate is also active in tri-gate.) **Types of Multi-Gate FETs:** 1. **Double-Gate (DG) MOSFET** – Gate on two sides. 2. **FinFET / Tri-Gate** – Gate on three sides (two sides + top). 3. **Gate-All-Around (GAA)** – Gate completely surrounds the channel. 4. **Nanowire FET** – Ultimate GAA with circular cross-section. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Application | |---|---------|---------------------------| | 1 | $V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | Body effect on threshold voltage | | 2 | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | Body-effect coefficient | | 3 | $\Delta V_T^{SCE} = \dfrac{qN_A W_{Dm}}{C_{ox}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2W_D}{r_j}} - 1\right]$ | Short-channel charge sharing | | 4 | $V_T^{DIBL} = V_T^{long} - \eta \cdot V_{DS}$ | DIBL-induced V_T reduction | | 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{3}{4}\dfrac{X_{dep}}{L_{eff}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{ox}}{X_{dep}}$ | DIBL coefficient (approximate) | | 6 | $I_{sub} = C \cdot I_D \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{B}{V_{DS}}\right)$ | Substrate current (HCE) | | 7 | $E_m = \dfrac{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}{l}$ | Maximum lateral electric field near drain | | 8 | $l = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\epsilon_s}{qN_A}(V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat})}$ | Effective length of velocity saturation region | | 9 | $W_{eff} = 2N \cdot H_{fin} + N \cdot W_{fin}$ | Effective channel width of FinFET | | 10 | $\beta_{n} = \mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W_{eff}}{L}(V_{GS} - V_T)$ | Drain current in linear region (using W_eff for FinFET) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In KTU board exams, problems on body effect, DIBL, and substrate current are the most common. Always carry the numerical values of thermal voltage (**V_T = 25.85 mV at 300 K**), Si bandgap (**E_g = 1.12 eV**), and oxide barrier height (**Φ_B = 3.1 eV**) by heart. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Threshold Voltage Control:** Critical for **low-power VLSI design** (mobile processors, IoT). - **Hot Carrier Reliability:** Determines **device lifetime** in automotive and aerospace electronics. - **FinFET Technology:** Powers modern CPUs (Intel's **10 nm, 7 nm, 4 nm** nodes), Apple M-series chips, and Samsung Exynos processors. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Body Effect on Threshold Voltage **Starting Point — Threshold Voltage Definition:** $$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$$ This is the threshold voltage at **V_SB = 0** (zero body bias). **Step 1 — Recognize the bulk charge term:** At equilibrium, the bulk charge per unit area is: $$Q_B = -\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F)}$$ The threshold voltage includes the term: $$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F - \dfrac{Q_B}{C_{ox}}$$ **Step 2 — Introduce source-to-body bias V_SB:** When V_SB > 0 is applied, the surface potential needed to reach strong inversion increases. The bulk charge becomes: $$Q_B' = -\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the new threshold voltage:** $$V_T' = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + V_{SB} - \dfrac{Q_B'}{C_{ox}}$$ Substituting Q_B': $$V_T' = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + V_{SB} + \dfrac{\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$ **Step 4 — Subtract the original V_T to isolate the body-effect term:** The original V_T at V_SB = 0 is: $$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$ Subtracting: $$V_T' - V_T = V_{SB} + \dfrac{1}{C_{ox}}\left[\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})} - \sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F)}\right]$$ But typically the V_SB term on the right is absorbed, and the body-effect equation is written compactly as: $$\boxed{V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)}$$ Where the body-effect coefficient is: $$\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A}}{C_{ox}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Short-Channel Effect on V_T (Charge Sharing Model) **Assumption:** The depletion charge under the gate is shared between the gate, source, and drain. **Step 1 — Define the geometric charge-sharing fraction:** Let **f** be the fraction of depletion charge controlled by the gate. For a long channel, f ≈ 1. For short channel, f < 1. $$f = 1 - \dfrac{r_j}{L}\left[\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2W_D}{r_j}} - 1\right]$$ Where: - r_j = junction depth, - W_D = depletion width, - L = channel length. **Step 2 — Effective bulk charge controlled by gate:** $$Q_B^{eff} = f \cdot Q_B = f \cdot \sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$ **Step 3 — Effective threshold voltage:** $$V_T^{SCE} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{f \cdot \sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$ **Step 4 — The V_T reduction due to SCE:** $$\Delta V_T = V_T^{long} - V_T^{SCE} = (1 - f) \cdot \gamma \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$$ **Conclusion:** As L decreases, f decreases (charge sharing increases), so V_T reduces. This is the **threshold voltage roll-off**. ### 3.3 Derivation: DIBL Coefficient η **Starting Point:** The drain potential penetrates into the channel by a distance proportional to the depletion width X_dep. $$\Delta \Phi_S \approx \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \dfrac{X_{dep}}{L_{eff}} \cdot V_{DS}$$ This represents the barrier lowering at the source. **Threshold voltage shift due to DIBL:** $$\Delta V_T^{DIBL} = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \dfrac{X_{dep}}{L_{eff}} \cdot V_{DS}$$ Comparing with the empirical form $V_T = V_T^{long} - \eta V_{DS}$: $$\eta = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \dfrac{X_{dep}}{L_{eff}}$$ This is the **DIBL coefficient**, which grows as L_eff decreases — explaining why DIBL is severe in short-channel devices. ### 3.4 Derivation: Substrate Current in HCE (Lucky Electron Model) **Step 1 — Probability of "lucky" carrier gaining enough energy:** The probability that an electron gains sufficient energy to traverse distance d without collision (no phonon scattering) is: $$P = \exp\left(-\dfrac{d}{\lambda}\right)$$ Where λ is the mean free path. **Step 2 — Hot carrier generation rate:** The number of hot electrons per unit time is proportional to: $$G \propto n \cdot E_m \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Phi_B}{q\lambda E_m}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Substrate current (each impact-ionized electron generates one hole that flows to substrate):** $$I_{sub} = I_D \cdot \alpha \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Phi_B}{q\lambda E_m}\right)$$ Where α is the impact-ionization coefficient. **Step 4 — Approximation for E_m:** For a device in saturation: $$E_m \approx \dfrac{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}{l}$$ So the final form: $$I_{sub} = C \cdot I_D \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{B}{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}\right)$$ This is the form most often cited in KTU numerical problems. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When Does Each Effect Dominate? | Effect | Channel Length Range | Dominant Region | Impact on V_T | |--------|---------------------|-----------------|----------------| | Body Effect | All L | Below threshold | V_T increases with V_SB | | Short Channel Effect | L < 1 µm | Throughout channel | V_T decreases | | Narrow Width Effect | W < 1 µm | Channel edges | V_T increases | | DIBL | L < 250 nm | Drain end | V_T decreases linearly with V_DS | | Hot Carrier Effect | L < 500 nm | Drain pinch-off | V_T shifts (Δ), mobility degradation | | FinFET Mitigation | All L (multi-gate) | Bulk of channel | All above effects significantly suppressed | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 FinFET 3D Structure (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional block-level architecture** of a FinFET, showing its key structural components and the multi-gate electrostatic control. ```mermaid graph TD A[SOURCE TERMINAL] --> B[Silicon Fin Channel
Vertical 3D Body] B --> C[DRAIN TERMINAL] D[GATE OXIDE Layer 1] --> E[Left Side Gate] D --> F[Top Gate Tri Gate] D --> G[Right Side Gate] E --> H[Effective Channel Control] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Strong Electrostatic Coupling] I --> J[Reduced Short Channel Effects] I --> K[Suppressed DIBL] I --> L[Lower Subthreshold Leakage] M[Fin Width W fin] --> N[Body Thickness] M --> O[Full Depletion Control] P[Fin Height H fin] --> Q[Effective Channel Width] P --> R[Drive Current Capacity] ``` ### 4.2 Hot Carrier Effect — Degradation Flow ```mermaid graph TD A[High VDS Applied] --> B[Strong Lateral E field Near Drain] B --> C[Electrons Gain Kinetic Energy] C --> D{Energy Exceeds 3.1 eV?} D -->|Yes| E[Electron Injection into Gate Oxide] D -->|No| F[Normal Drift] E --> G[Trapped Charges in Oxide] E --> H[Interface State Generation] G --> I[Threshold Voltage Shift] H --> J[Mobility Degradation] I --> K[Device Performance Degradation] J --> K K --> L[Reduced Device Lifetime] M[Energy Exceeds 1.12 eV] --> N[Impact Ionization] N --> O[Substrate Current I sub] O --> P[Reliability Monitoring Metric] ``` ### 4.3 Threshold Voltage Variation — Factor Interaction Map ```mermaid graph TD A[Threshold Voltage VT] --> B[Body Effect] A --> C[Short Channel Effect] A --> D[Narrow Width Effect] A --> E[DIBL] A --> F[Hot Carrier Injection] B --> B1[VSB Increases VT] C --> C1[Small L Reduces VT] D --> D1[Small W Increases VT] E --> E1[High VDS Reduces VT] F --> F1[Trapped Charge Shifts VT] B1 --> G[Effective Threshold] C1 --> G D1 --> G E1 --> G F1 --> G G --> H[Final Operating VT] I[FinFET Multi Gate Design] --> J[Suppresses SCE DIBL HCE] J --> H ``` ### 4.4 Comparison: Planar MOSFET vs FinFET (Schematic Block View) | Feature | Planar MOSFET | FinFET | |---------|---------------|--------| | Channel Geometry | 2D horizontal | 3D vertical fin | | Gate Control | Single top gate | 2 or 3 sides (multi-gate) | | Body Thickness | Doping-controlled | Geometric (W_fin) | | Short-Channel Control | Moderate | Excellent | | DIBL Suppression | Poor at <100 nm | Strong | | Subthreshold Slope | ~70 mV/dec (limit) | Approaches 60 mV/dec | | Drive Current per Area | Limited | High (3D W_eff) | | Scalability Limit | ~22 nm (practical) | <3 nm (current) | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "Hot Carrier Effect" in short-channel MOSFETs. Mention the two primary damage mechanisms caused by hot carriers.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Hot Carrier Effect (HCE) is the phenomenon in short-channel MOSFETs where carriers (electrons/holes) acquire very high kinetic energy from the strong lateral electric field near the drain end of the channel, gaining energy significantly greater than the thermal energy (kT). When their energy exceeds the Si-SiO₂ barrier height (Φ_B ≈ **3.1 eV** for electrons), they can inject into the gate oxide and cause device degradation. - **Two Damage Mechanisms (1.5 Marks):** 1. **Trapped Charge Build-up in Gate Oxide:** Hot carriers get trapped in the SiO₂ layer, causing a shift in threshold voltage (V_T) over time. 2. **Interface State Generation at Si-SiO₂ Boundary:** Hot carriers break Si-H bonds at the interface, creating dangling bonds (P_b centers) that degrade carrier mobility and increase subthreshold leakage. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is a FinFET? State any two advantages of FinFET over conventional planar MOSFET.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1.5 Marks):** A FinFET is a **non-planar, multi-gate MOSFET** in which the conducting channel is a thin vertical silicon "fin" of width W_fin, and the gate electrode wraps around the fin from two sides (double-gate FinFET) or three sides (tri-gate FinFET), providing superior electrostatic control over the channel. - **Two Advantages (1.5 Marks):** 1. **Superior Short-Channel Control:** Because the fin is fully depleted and the gate controls the channel from multiple sides, short-channel effects (DIBL, threshold voltage roll-off) are drastically reduced. 2. **Higher Drive Current per Unit Area:** The 3D structure allows multiple fins to be placed in parallel, increasing effective channel width and drive current without increasing the footprint. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Threshold Voltage Variations** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the threshold voltage of a MOSFET including the **body effect (substrate bias effect)**. A silicon n-MOSFET has the following parameters: N_A = 10¹⁶ cm⁻³, t_ox = 20 nm, φ_F = 0.35 V. Calculate the body-effect coefficient γ. Given that V_SB = 1 V, find the shift in threshold voltage. (7 Marks) **(b)** Explain the **short-channel effect (SCE)** and **DIBL** mechanisms in nanoscale MOSFETs. Derive the expression for the **DIBL coefficient η** in terms of depletion width X_dep and effective channel length L_eff. (7 Marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Body Effect on V_T (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Threshold Voltage at Zero Body Bias (2 Marks):** At V_SB = 0, the threshold voltage is: $$V_{T0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$ The third term comes from the bulk depletion charge per unit area: $$Q_B = -\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F)}$$ **Step 2 — Effect of Applying V_SB (2 Marks):** When V_SB > 0 (reverse body bias), the depletion region widens, and the surface potential for strong inversion becomes (2φ_F + V_SB). The new bulk charge is: $$Q_B' = -\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$ The new threshold voltage is: $$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + V_{SB} - \dfrac{Q_B'}{C_{ox}}$$ $$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + V_{SB} + \dfrac{\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$ **Step 3 — Final Body-Effect Equation (1 Mark):** Subtracting V_T0: $$V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$ Where: $$\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A}}{C_{ox}}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical Calculation (2 Marks):** Given: - N_A = 10¹⁶ cm⁻³ = 10²² m⁻³, - t_ox = 20 nm = 20 × 10⁻⁹ m, - φ_F = 0.35 V, - ε_s = 11.7 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m ≈ 1.036 × 10⁻¹⁰ F/m, - ε_ox = 3.9 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m ≈ 3.45 × 10⁻¹¹ F/m, - C_ox = ε_ox / t_ox = 3.45 × 10⁻¹¹ / 20 × 10⁻⁹ = 1.725 × 10⁻³ F/m². Computing γ: $$\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.036 \times 10^{-10} \times 10^{22}}}{1.725 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$= \dfrac{\sqrt{3.315 \times 10^{-7}}}{1.725 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{5.76 \times 10^{-4}}{1.725 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.334 \text{ V}^{1/2}$$ Shift in V_T for V_SB = 1 V: $$\Delta V_T = 0.334 \left(\sqrt{2 \times 0.35 + 1} - \sqrt{2 \times 0.35}\right)$$ $$= 0.334 \left(\sqrt{1.7} - \sqrt{0.7}\right) = 0.334 (1.304 - 0.837) = 0.334 \times 0.467 \approx 0.156 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta V_T \approx 156 \text{ mV}}$$ --- **(b) Short-Channel Effect and DIBL (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Short-Channel Effect Explanation (2 Marks):** As channel length L decreases, the depletion regions of source and drain junctions occupy a significant fraction of the channel. The gate loses exclusive control over the channel charge because some of the depletion charge is "shared" with the source and drain. This **charge sharing** reduces the threshold voltage as L shrinks. The phenomenon is known as the **Short Channel Effect (SCE)**. **Step 2 — DIBL Explanation (2 Marks):** Drain Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL) is a short-channel effect where a high drain voltage V_DS lowers the source-to-channel potential barrier. When V_DS is large, the drain depletion region extends toward the source, reducing the barrier height Φ_B at the source. This causes an additional reduction in V_T beyond what SCE alone predicts, and is particularly severe for L < 250 nm. **Step 3 — Derivation of DIBL Coefficient η (2 Marks):** The penetration of drain potential into the channel can be modeled as a 2D Poisson's equation. Using the depletion approximation, the surface potential at the source is reduced by: $$\Delta \Phi_S = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \dfrac{X_{dep}}{L_{eff}} \cdot V_{DS}$$ The corresponding threshold voltage shift is: $$\Delta V_T^{DIBL} = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \dfrac{X_{dep}}{L_{eff}} \cdot V_{DS}$$ Comparing with the empirical form V_T = V_T^{long} − η·V_{DS}: $$\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \dfrac{X_{dep}}{L_{eff}}}$$ **Step 4 — Significance (1 Mark):** As L_eff shrinks, η grows, leading to severe V_T roll-off and increased off-state leakage. This is the primary motivation for moving from planar MOSFETs to multi-gate devices like FinFETs. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Hot Carrier Effects and FinFET** **(a)** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the **Hot Carrier Effect (HCE)** in short-channel MOSFETs. Derive the expression for **substrate current** using the **Lucky Electron Model** and discuss the dependence of I_sub on V_DS. (7 Marks) **(b)** Draw and explain the **3D structure of a FinFET**. Compare FinFET with conventional planar MOSFET in terms of (i) short-channel control, (ii) subthreshold slope, (iii) drive current, and (iv) scalability. (7 Marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Hot Carrier Effect and Substrate Current (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Physical Origin of HCE (2 Marks):** In a short-channel MOSFET operating in saturation, the drain end experiences a very high lateral electric field (10⁵–10⁶ V/cm). Electrons drifting from source to drain gain significant kinetic energy between collisions, becoming "hot carriers." When their energy exceeds: - **E_g = 1.12 eV** (Si bandgap) → **Impact ionization** occurs, generating electron-hole pairs. - **Φ_B = 3.1 eV** (Si-SiO₂ barrier) → **Hot carrier injection** into the gate oxide. **Step 2 — Energy Band Diagram Explanation (2 Marks):** At the drain end: - The conduction band bends sharply downward due to high V_DS. - Hot electrons at the drain end have energy above the SiO₂ conduction band edge. - These electrons can tunnel/inject into the oxide (gate injection). - Holes generated by impact ionization flow to the substrate, forming **substrate current I_sub**. **Step 3 — Lucky Electron Model Derivation (2 Marks):** According to the Lucky Electron Model, an electron becomes "lucky" (gains enough energy without collision) with probability: $$P = \exp\left(-\dfrac{d}{\lambda}\right)$$ The substrate current (proportional to the number of impact-ionizing electrons) is: $$I_{sub} = C_1 \cdot I_D \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Phi_B}{q\lambda E_m}\right)$$ Where E_m is the maximum lateral electric field at the drain: $$E_m = \dfrac{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}{l}, \quad l = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\epsilon_s(V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat})}{qN_A}}$$ For simplicity, the empirical form used in KTU problems is: $$I_{sub} \approx C \cdot I_D \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{B}{V_{DS}}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Dependence on V_DS (1 Mark):** I_sub increases sharply with V_DS due to the exponential term. It peaks at V_DS ≈ 1.5 × V_DD and then decreases. This bell-shaped behavior is the most important signature of HCE. --- **(b) FinFET Structure and Comparison (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — 3D Structure of FinFET (2 Marks):** A FinFET consists of: - A vertical **silicon fin** of width W_fin and height H_fin, etched from a silicon-on-insulator (SOI) or bulk-Si substrate. - A **gate oxide** (typically high-κ) wrapping around the fin. - A **metal gate** (typically TiN, work-function metal) covering the top and both sides of the fin. - **Source** and **drain** regions at the two ends of the fin. The fin is fully depleted at zero gate bias because W_fin is small (typically 5–15 nm). **Step 2 — Working Principle (1 Mark):** When V_GS > V_T, inversion layers form on the **two side surfaces** (and optionally the top surface for tri-gate). The multi-side gate control ensures: - Excellent electrostatic integrity. - Subthreshold slope close to ideal 60 mV/dec. - Very low off-state leakage. **Step 3 — Comparison with Planar MOSFET (4 Marks):** | Feature | Planar MOSFET | FinFET | |---------|---------------|--------| | (i) Short-Channel Control | Poor at L < 100 nm | Excellent (multi-gate fully depletes body) | | (ii) Subthreshold Slope | Limited to ~70 mV/dec | Approaches 60 mV/dec | | (iii) Drive Current | Limited by W | High (3D W_eff = 2NH_fin + NW_fin) | | (iv) Scalability | Practical limit ~22 nm | Scalable to <5 nm (currently in production at 3 nm) | **Conclusion:** FinFETs provide superior performance, lower power, and better scalability, which is why they have replaced planar MOSFETs in all leading-edge CMOS technologies from Intel, TSMC, Samsung, and Apple since 2014. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > 1. **Sign Confusion in Body Effect:** Many students forget that V_SB is the **source-to-body bias**, not body-to-source. For an n-MOSFET with body at ground and source at +V, V_SB is **negative**, and V_T actually **decreases** (forward body bias). > 2. **Mixing Up φ_F and 2φ_F:** The threshold voltage uses **2φ_F** (surface potential at strong inversion), not φ_F. > 3. **Forgetting to convert units in HCE problems:** λ, l, and X_dep are in **nm**, while V_DS is in **V** and E_m in **V/cm**. Always use consistent SI units. > 4. **In FinFET questions, students often forget the factor of 2** in the effective width formula (W_eff = 2N·H_fin + N·W_fin). The factor 2 represents the two side gates. > 5. **Hot Carrier Effect is not the same as the Body Effect.** HCE is a **reliability/degradation** mechanism, while body effect is a **bias-dependent** V_T shift. Examiners often test this distinction. > 6. **In DIBL derivations,** students forget that η is **dimensionless** and the formula should be presented in a way that shows the proper physical scaling with L_eff. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Threshold Voltage (V_T)** is not a constant; it varies with **V_SB (body effect), L (SCE), W (NWE), and V_DS (DIBL).** - **Body-effect coefficient γ** is given by $\gamma = \sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A}/C_{ox}$, typically **0.3 to 0.6 V^½**. - **SCE and DIBL** are the dominant V_T variation mechanisms in **sub-100 nm** devices. - **DIBL coefficient:** $\eta = \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \dfrac{X_{dep}}{L_{eff}}$ — grows rapidly as L_eff shrinks. - **Hot Carrier Effect** is a **reliability issue** arising from energetic carriers near the drain in short-channel MOSFETs. - **Two main damage mechanisms:** (1) Oxide trapped charge, (2) Interface state generation. - **Substrate current** follows: $I_{sub} \approx C \cdot I_D \cdot \exp(-B/V_{DS})$ — peaks at moderate V_DS. - **Barrier height Φ_B = 3.1 eV** (Si-SiO₂ for electrons), **3.8 eV** for holes. - **FinFET** uses a **vertical silicon fin** with **multi-gate (2 or 3 sides)** control. - **Effective width of FinFET:** $W_{eff} = 2N \cdot H_{fin} + N \cdot W_{fin}$ (factor 2 = two side gates). - **FinFET advantages:** Better SCE suppression, near-ideal subthreshold slope, higher drive current, scalable to **sub-5 nm nodes**. - **Modern CMOS technology** (Intel 4, TSMC N3, Samsung 3GAE) is built on **FinFET or GAA architectures** — multi-gate is the future (and present) of MOSFET scaling. - **Mitigation of HCE:** Use **lightly doped drain (LDD)**, **HALO implants**, and **multi-gate structures** (FinFET, GAA). - **Mean free path** of hot electrons in Si at 300 K: **λ ≈ 7.8 nm** — a key constant in HCE derivations. - **Thermal voltage** at 300 K: **V_T = kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV** — used universally in semiconductor physics.

topics.title: Operation and Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET412 - SOLID STATE DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Operation and Advantages # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Junction Field Effect Transistor (JFET) A **Junction Field Effect Transistor (JFET)** is a three-terminal, **unipolar**, voltage-controlled semiconductor device in which the current conduction between the source and drain terminals is controlled by the electric field produced by the voltage applied at the gate terminal. Unlike the Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT), the JFET uses **only one type of charge carrier** (either electrons in n-channel or holes in p-channel) for conduction, hence the prefix *unipolar*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The JFET is the foundational FET device. The 2024 scheme emphasizes understanding its *operation* (channel pinch-off and saturation) and enumerating its *advantages* over conventional BJTs in low-noise, high-input-impedance analog applications. ### Formal Terminal Definitions | Terminal | Symbol | Function | |----------|--------|----------| | Source | S | Terminal through which majority carriers *enter* the channel | | Drain | D | Terminal through which majority carriers *leave* the channel | | Gate | G | Reverse-biased PN junction that controls channel conductivity | > [!NOTE] > For an **n-channel JFET**, current flows from **Drain → Source** (conventional), carried by **electrons**. The Gate is formed by a **p-type** region diffused into the n-type channel. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Garden Hose Imagine a **flexible rubber garden hose** carrying water (representing current) under pressure from a pump (the drain–source voltage $V_{DS}$). The water flow rate is the **drain current** $I_D$. Now place your **thumb** on the hose from above (representing the **gate**). The harder you press (higher reverse bias on the gate), the more you **squeeze the hose**, narrowing the effective cross-section and **reducing the water flow**. - **No thumb pressure (VGS = 0):** Maximum water flow (maximum $I_{DSS}$). - **Increasing thumb pressure (VGS more negative for n-channel):** Flow decreases. - **Thumb pressed hard enough (VGS = Vp):** Hose is fully pinched — **water flow stops** (cutoff, $I_D = 0$). This mechanical analogy maps **1-to-1** with the electrostatic *depletion* action of the reverse-biased gate junction on the conducting channel. > [!TIP] > Always remember: **The JFET is "normally-ON"** — current flows with $V_{GS} = 0$. To turn it OFF, you must *reverse-bias* the gate–channel junction. This is the single most distinguishing fact from an E-MOSFET. --- ## 1.3 Physical Construction (Conceptual) For an **n-channel JFET**: $$\text{n-type substrate (channel)} \;\;\; \leftarrow \text{current flows here} \;\;\; \text{with diffused p+ gates on either side}$$ Ohmic contacts are made at the two ends of the n-channel (Source and Drain), and a single contact is made on top of the p+ region (Gate). The p-n junctions between the gate and the channel are normally **reverse-biased** in operation. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants for Silicon JFETs (must memorize):** > - Built-in potential of gate junction: $V_{bi} \approx \mathbf{0.7 \; V}$ > - Relative permittivity of Si: $\varepsilon_r = \mathbf{11.7}$ > - Thermal voltage at 300 K: $V_T = \mathbf{25.85 \; mV}$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle: The Depletion Mechanism When a reverse-bias voltage $V_{GS}$ is applied between Gate and Source, the depletion regions of the two p+n junctions (top and bottom gates) **widen and intrude into the n-channel**. This reduces the effective conducting cross-section and hence the channel conductance. Define the **pinch-off voltage** $V_P$ (sometimes denoted $V_{GS(off)}$) as the value of $V_{GS}$ at which the two depletion regions just meet, completely closing the channel. ### Step-by-Step Logic of Conduction Control 1. **At $V_{GS} = 0$:** Depletion regions have a small built-in width. Channel is fully open. A small $V_{DS}$ causes a current $I_{DSS}$ to flow. 2. **As $V_{GS}$ becomes more negative (for n-channel):** The reverse-bias widens the depletion region. The conducting channel narrows. **Drain current $I_D$ decreases monotonically.** 3. **At $V_{GS} = V_P$ (a negative quantity for n-channel):** Depletion regions merge. The channel is **pinched off**. $I_D = 0$. 4. **Operating range:** $V_P \le V_{GS} \le 0$ for an n-channel JFET. ## 2.2 The Three Operating Regions of the JFET | Region | Condition | Physical Meaning | |--------|-----------|------------------| | **Ohmic / Triode** | $0 \le V_{DS} \le V_{DS,sat}$ | Channel acts like a voltage-controlled resistor | | **Saturation (Pinch-off)** | $V_{DS} \ge V_{DS,sat}$ | Channel is pinched at the drain end; current is nearly constant | | **Cutoff** | $V_{GS} \le V_P$ | No conduction; $I_D = 0$ | Where the **saturation drain voltage** is: $$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_P$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table. Every KTU board question on JFET will need at least one of these relations. | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---------|-------------|------| | 1 | $I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$ | **Shockley's Equation** (saturation region) | A | | 2 | $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_P$ | Pinch-off / saturation drain voltage | V | | 3 | $g_m = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\Big\vert_{V_{DS}} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$ | **Transconductance** $g_m$ | Siemens (S) | | 4 | $g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$ | Maximum transconductance (at $V_{GS}=0$) | S | | 5 | $g_m = g_{m0}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$ | Transconductance in terms of $g_{m0}$ | S | | 6 | $r_d = \dfrac{\partial V_{DS}}{\partial I_D}\Big\vert_{V_{GS}}$ | Drain (output) resistance | Ω | | 7 | $I_D = I_{DSS} \cdot \dfrac{V_{DS}}{V_{DS,sat}}$ for $V_{DS} \le V_{DS,sat}$ | **Ohmic region** current | A | | 8 | $\mu = g_m \cdot r_d$ | **Amplification factor** (dimensionless) | — | > [!CAUTION] > **Sign convention trap:** $V_P$ is **negative** for n-channel and **positive** for p-channel. Many students drop the sign and lose a full mark. The standard *Shockley* form uses the signed value of $V_P$ in the denominator. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Low-noise audio preamplifiers** — JFETs have inherently low thermal noise due to majority-carrier conduction. - **High-impedance electrometers and oscilloscope input stages** — input resistance $\ge 10^{10}\;\Omega$. - **RF mixers and analog switches** — used in vintage broadcast equipment. - **Source followers (buffers)** — provide high input impedance with current gain. - **Constant-current sources** — by biasing at a fixed $V_{GS}$, $I_D$ becomes independent of $V_{DS}$. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Shockley's Equation (Saturation Region) We derive the famous $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ relation from the gradual-channel approximation. ### Assumptions - The channel is a uniformly doped n-type slab of width $2a$, donor density $N_D$. - The gate is heavily doped p+ ($N_A \gg N_D$), so the depletion region extends **entirely into the n-side**. - The channel is *gradually* varying (1-D Poisson along the channel length $L$). ### Step 1 — Built-in Potential from Doping $$ V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right) $$ This is the built-in potential of the p+n junction at equilibrium. ### Step 2 — Depletion Width into the n-Side For a one-sided abrupt junction, applying reverse bias $V_R = V_{bi} - V_{GS}$ (the reverse voltage is the sum of built-in plus applied), the depletion width extending into the n-side is: $$ w(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2\,\varepsilon_s\,(V_{bi} - V_{GS})}{q\,N_D}} $$ where $x$ is the position along the channel and $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of silicon. ### Step 3 — Effective Half-Channel Height The original conducting half-height is $a$. The depletion removes $w(x)$ on each side, leaving: $$ h(x) = a - w(x) $$ ### Step 4 — Channel Conductance (Ohm's Law Locally) The drain current is the integrated drift current across the reduced cross-section $2h(x)\cdot Z$ (where $Z$ is the channel width into the page). The electric field along the channel is $E(x) = -\dfrac{dV}{dx}$. Therefore: $$ I_D = 2 q N_D Z\, h(x)\, \mu_n\, E(x) = -2 q N_D Z\, h(x)\, \mu_n\, \frac{dV(x)}{dx} $$ ### Step 5 — Boundary Conditions - At the **source** end ($x = 0$): $V(0) = 0$, $h(0) = a - w(0)$. - At the **drain** end ($x = L$): $V(L) = V_{DS}$. The channel is just pinched-off when the depletion regions from top and bottom gates meet, i.e., when $h = 0$: $$ w \big\vert_{\text{pinch-off}} = a \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{bi} - V_{GS} = \frac{q N_D a^2}{2\varepsilon_s} \equiv -V_P $$ Solving for the pinch-off voltage: $$ V_P = V_{bi} - \frac{q N_D a^2}{2\varepsilon_s} \quad (\text{negative for n-channel}) $$ ### Step 6 — Integration over Channel Length Integrating the differential form of Ohm's law from source to the **pinch-off point** (where the channel closes at $V_{DS} = V_{DS,sat}$): $$ \int_0^L I_D \, dx = 2 q N_D Z \mu_n \int_{V_{GS}}^{V_{DS,sat}} h(V)\, dV $$ Carrying out the algebra (substituting $w$ from Step 2) and using the pinch-off condition yields, after simplification: $$ I_{DSS} = \frac{q^2 N_D^{\,2} \mu_n Z a^3}{\varepsilon_s L} $$ This is the maximum current at $V_{GS} = 0$. ### Step 7 — Final Form: Shockley's Square Law Combining all terms into a single compact expression gives the **fundamental JFET I-V relation**: $$ \boxed{\;I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{\!2}\;} $$ This is **Shockley's Equation**, the single most important equation in JFET analysis. It states that drain current varies as the **square** of the effective gate voltage. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Transconductance Starting from Shockley's equation, differentiate with respect to $V_{GS}$ at constant $V_{DS}$: $$ g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = 2 I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)\!\left(-\frac{1}{V_P}\right) $$ Rearranging: $$ \boxed{\;g_m = g_{m0}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)\;} \quad \text{where} \quad g_{m0} = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} $$ ### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style) > **Problem:** A silicon n-channel JFET has $I_{DSS} = 10\;\text{mA}$ and $V_P = -4\;\text{V}$. Find (i) $I_D$ at $V_{GS} = -2\;\text{V}$, and (ii) $g_m$ at the same bias. **Solution (i):** $$ I_D = 10 \;\text{mA}\!\left(1 - \frac{-2}{-4}\right)^{\!2} = 10\!\left(1 - 0.5\right)^{\!2}\;\text{mA} = 10 \times 0.25 = 2.5\;\text{mA} $$ **Solution (ii):** $$ g_{m0} = \frac{2 \times 10\;\text{mA}}{4\;\text{V}} = 5\;\text{mS} $$ $$ g_m = 5\;\text{mS}\!\left(1 - \frac{-2}{-4}\right) = 5 \times 0.5 = 2.5\;\text{mS} $$ > [!TIP] > **Short-cut check:** $g_m = g_{m0}\sqrt{I_D/I_{DSS}}$ — useful when you know the operating current rather than the bias voltage. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 JFET Physical Cross-Section (n-channel) The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the structural anatomy of an n-channel JFET — the gate depletion regions, source/drain ohmic contacts, and current path. ```mermaid graph LR A[Drain Terminal D]:::term B[n+ Ohmic Contact]:::contact C[Top p+ Gate Region]:::gate D[Depletion Region Top]:::dep E[n-type Channel - Current Path]:::chan F[Depletion Region Bottom]:::dep G[Bottom p+ Gate Region]:::gate H[n+ Ohmic Contact]:::contact I[Source Terminal S]:::term J[Gate Terminal G]:::term A --- B B --- C C --- D D --- E E --- F F --- G G --- H H --- I C --- J G --- J classDef term fill:#222,stroke:#FFD700,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef contact fill:#0a3,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef gate fill:#a36,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef dep fill:#fc6,stroke:#a36,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef chan fill:#69f,stroke:#fff,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Operation Flow: Pinch-Off Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Stage 1: VGS = 0V
Channel fully open
ID = IDSS"]:::stage1 S2["Stage 2: VGS = -1V
Depletion regions widen slightly
ID decreases"]:::stage2 S3["Stage 3: VGS = -2V
Channel narrows further
ID = IDSS/4 approx"]:::stage3 S4["Stage 4: VGS = VP
Depletion regions meet
ID = 0 - Pinch Off"]:::stage4 S5["Stage 5: VGS beyond VP
No conduction
JFET OFF"]:::stage5 S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 classDef stage1 fill:#9f9,stroke:#060,color:#000 classDef stage2 fill:#cf9,stroke:#060,color:#000 classDef stage3 fill:#fc9,stroke:#a60,color:#000 classDef stage4 fill:#f96,stroke:#a30,color:#000 classDef stage5 fill:#f66,stroke:#a00,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture: JFET as a Voltage-Controlled Current Source ```mermaid graph TB INPUT["Input Signal: VGS
Control Voltage"]:::input CTRL["Gate - Source Junction
Reverse Biased"]:::ctrl CHAN["Conducting Channel
Effective Width h x"]:::chan VDS["Drain Source Bias: VDS"]:::bias OUTPUT["Output: Drain Current ID"]:::output LOAD["External Load RL"]:::load INPUT --> CTRL CTRL -->|Modulates width| CHAN VDS -->|Drives carriers| CHAN CHAN --> OUTPUT OUTPUT --> LOAD LOAD -->|Voltage drop| OUTPUT classDef input fill:#69f,stroke:#003,color:#fff classDef ctrl fill:#fc6,stroke:#a60,color:#000 classDef chan fill:#9f9,stroke:#060,color:#000 classDef bias fill:#c9f,stroke:#609,color:#000 classDef output fill:#f96,stroke:#a30,color:#000 classDef load fill:#ccc,stroke:#666,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the architecture:** The **gate** is an *isolated* control port — virtually no DC current flows into it (only the leakage of a reverse-biased junction). Hence the JFET presents a **very high input impedance**, which is the key engineering advantage exploited in instrumentation. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List any four advantages of a JFET over a BJT.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Very high input impedance** (typically $10^8$ to $10^{12}\;\Omega$) because the gate is reverse-biased, whereas a BJT base draws significant current. 2. **Lower noise figure** — majority-carrier conduction eliminates shot-noise associated with minority-carrier injection in BJTs. 3. **Better thermal stability** — JFETs are less prone to thermal runaway since drain current *decreases* with temperature (negative temperature coefficient above a certain point). 4. **Voltage-controlled device** — easier to bias and integrate into linear analog circuits; no gate current required for control. 5. *Bonus point:* Square-law transfer characteristic makes JFETs useful in **mixers and modulators** (multiplication of two signals). --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Why is a JFET called a "normally-ON" device? What is meant by pinch-off voltage?** **Model Answer:** A JFET is called **normally-ON** because with **zero gate-source voltage** ($V_{GS} = 0$), a conducting channel exists between source and drain, and a finite drain current $I_{DSS}$ flows. Unlike an enhancement MOSFET, no gate bias is required to *create* the channel — it is inherent in the doping. The **pinch-off voltage** $V_P$ (also denoted $V_{GS(off)}$) is defined as the reverse gate-source voltage at which the depletion regions around the gate junctions **completely close** the conducting channel, reducing the drain current to **zero**. For an n-channel JFET, $V_P$ is **negative**; for a p-channel JFET, $V_P$ is **positive**. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **Sign of $V_P$:** Forgetting the negative sign in $V_{GS}/V_P$ for an n-channel device causes the bracket to evaluate to *more than 1* and gives a **physically impossible negative $I_D$**. Always cross-check the result. > - **Region confusion:** Students often blindly apply Shockley's equation in the **ohmic/triode region**. Shockley's equation is **valid only in saturation** ($V_{DS} \ge V_{DS,sat}$). In the ohmic region, use $I_D = (2 I_{DSS}/V_P^2)\cdot[(V_{GS}-V_P)V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2]$. > - **Units of $g_m$:** Forgetting to convert mA/V to Siemens leads to off-by-$10^3$ errors in amplifier design. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]` **(a)** With the help of a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the construction and **operation of an n-channel JFET**. Discuss the effect of increasing reverse gate bias on the channel and define the **pinch-off voltage**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive **Shockley's equation** relating drain current $I_D$ to gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$ in the saturation region. A JFET has $I_{DSS} = 12\;\text{mA}$ and $V_P = -3\;\text{V}$. Calculate the drain current and transconductance at $V_{GS} = -1\;\text{V}$. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Construction and Operation:** An n-channel JFET consists of a **lightly-doped n-type silicon bar** with **two heavily-doped p+ regions** diffused on opposite sides (top and bottom). Ohmic contacts are taken from the two ends of the n-bar (Source S and Drain D) and from the combined p+ regions (Gate G). Internally, the two p+ gates are connected. **Operation:** - With $V_{GS} = 0$ and a small $V_{DS}$ applied, electrons flow from S to D, establishing a current $I_{DSS}$. - The gate-channel p+n junctions are at zero bias (only built-in field exists), so the depletion regions are very thin. - When a **reverse bias** $V_{GS} < 0$ is applied, the depletion regions widen and **intrude into the n-channel**, effectively narrowing it. - The drain current $I_D$ therefore **decreases** as $V_{GS}$ becomes more negative. - At $V_{GS} = V_P$, the two depletion regions **meet** at the centre, closing the channel — this is **pinch-off**, and $I_D = 0$. A labelled cross-section (already shown in Section 4.1) is mandatory for full marks. **Part (b) — Derivation:** Refer to the **complete step-by-step derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. The final result is: $$ I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{\!2} $$ **Numerical Computation:** Given: $I_{DSS} = 12\;\text{mA}$, $V_P = -3\;\text{V}$, $V_{GS} = -1\;\text{V}$. $$ I_D = 12\!\left(1 - \frac{-1}{-3}\right)^{\!2} = 12 \times \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{\!2}\;\text{mA} = 12 \times \frac{4}{9} = \frac{48}{9} = 5.33\;\text{mA} $$ Transconductance: $$ g_{m0} = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} = \frac{2 \times 12\;\text{mA}}{3\;\text{V}} = 8\;\text{mS} $$ $$ g_m = g_{m0}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) = 8\;\text{mS} \times \frac{2}{3} = 5.33\;\text{mS} $$ > [!TIP] > Cross-verify: $g_m = g_{m0}\sqrt{I_D/I_{DSS}} = 8\;\text{mS} \cdot \sqrt{5.33/12} = 8 \times 0.6667 = 5.33\;\text{mS}$ ✓ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the **I-V characteristics** of a JFET in the **ohmic** and **saturation (pinch-off)** regions, with a sketch of the output characteristic curves. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of JFETs. Compare a JFET with a BJT in terms of input impedance, noise performance, and thermal stability. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — I-V Characteristics:** The output (drain) characteristics of a JFET plot $I_D$ versus $V_{DS}$ for various fixed values of $V_{GS}$. - **Ohmic Region** (small $V_{DS}$): The channel behaves as a **voltage-controlled resistor**. For a given $V_{GS}$, $I_D$ rises **linearly** with $V_{DS}$ initially. The relationship is: $$ I_D = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{V_P^{\,2}}\left[(V_{GS} - V_P)V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{\,2}}{2}\right] $$ - **Saturation (Pinch-off) Region** (for $V_{DS} \ge V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_P$): The channel is pinched near the drain end. Further increase in $V_{DS}$ has *almost no effect* on $I_D$, and the curve **flattens**. Current is governed by Shockley's equation: $$ I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{\!2} $$ - **Breakdown Region:** If $V_{DS}$ exceeds the breakdown voltage $V_{(BR)DS}$, avalanche breakdown of the gate-channel junction occurs and $I_D$ shoots up sharply — this region must be **avoided** in design. A family of curves (for $V_{GS} = 0, -1, -2, -3, \ldots$ V) shows the locus where the transition between ohmic and saturation occurs — this is the **boundary parabola** $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_P$. **Part (b) — Advantages, Disadvantages, and BJT Comparison:** **Advantages of JFET:** 1. **High input impedance** ($10^8 - 10^{12}\;\Omega$). 2. **Low noise** — useful in RF and audio pre-amps. 3. **Voltage-controlled** — simple biasing, no gate current. 4. **Good thermal stability** — negative temperature coefficient at high current. 5. **Smaller size** than equivalent BJT — easier to integrate. **Disadvantages of JFET:** 1. **Low gain-bandwidth product** compared to BJTs. 2. **Slower switching** — junction capacitances limit high-frequency use. 3. **Normally-ON** — fails *short* (passes current) if gate drive fails, complicating fail-safe design. 4. **Lower transconductance** for a given device area. 5. **Sensitive to static discharge** at the gate (very high input impedance means voltages can build up and punch through the junction). **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | JFET | BJT | |-----------|------|-----| | Input impedance | Very high ($10^8$ to $10^{12}\;\Omega$) | Low (a few kΩ) | | Noise | Low (majority carrier) | Higher (minority carrier, shot noise) | | Thermal stability | Excellent (negative $dI_D/dT$) | Poor — prone to thermal runaway | | Control variable | Voltage ($V_{GS}$) | Current ($I_B$) | | Polarity | Unipolar | Bipolar | --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density revision checklist for JFET Operation and Advantages (Module 4):** - ☐ JFET is a **unipolar, voltage-controlled, normally-ON** device with three terminals: **Source, Drain, Gate**. - ☐ Conduction is by **majority carriers only** — electrons in n-channel, holes in p-channel. - ☐ The gate-channel junction is **always reverse-biased** in normal operation — this gives high input impedance. - ☐ **Pinch-off voltage $V_P$** is the gate voltage that *just closes* the channel. $I_D = 0$ at $V_{GS} = V_P$. - ☐ **Shockley's Equation:** $I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$ — valid in **saturation region** only. - ☐ Three operating regions: **Ohmic (triode), Saturation (active), Cutoff**. - ☐ $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_P$ is the boundary between ohmic and saturation. - ☐ **Transconductance** $g_m = g_{m0}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$, with $g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$. - ☐ **Sign convention:** $V_P$ is **negative** for n-channel, **positive** for p-channel. Always include the sign in calculations. - ☐ Key **advantages over BJT:** high $Z_{in}$, low noise, voltage control, thermal stability, smaller size. - ☐ Key **disadvantages:** normally-ON (fail-short), lower $g_m$, slower than MOSFETs in switching, ESD sensitive. - ☐ Applications: **electrometer inputs, source followers, RF mixers, analog switches, constant-current sources**. - ☐ The device is **OFF** when $V_{GS} \le V_P$ (for n-channel) and **ON** when $V_P < V_{GS} \le 0$. - ☐ Never apply forward bias to the gate — it destroys the input-impedance advantage and can cause latch-up. - ☐ For high-frequency design, consider the **gate-source capacitance** $C_{gs}$ and **gate-drain capacitance** $C_{gd}$ as the dominant speed limiters.
